This isn't a game, it is an experience.
It doesn't hold your hand, for better or for worse.
Yet, even while it spits in your face and takes away more than it gives, you will feel even more accomplishment afterwards. Bloody, tattered, emaciated, but still alive.

Posted: November 24
Game gets repetitive really fast. Boss fights are super easy if you know how the game works. The only thing that makes this game challenging at all is that the game doesn't tell you what anything in the game is for or how it works, which is one of the worst ways to increase the difficulty of your games. It's not hard to drive a car, but if you take a car onto the interstate without knowing what any of the controls do, of course you're going to have a hard time. The game uses some familiar mechanics from other games like leveling up to bait you into making the wrong decisions because apparently the people at Fromsoft can do anything but code an actual difficult fight. You get encumbered at half your equip capacity, which the game doesn't tell you. Most games the capacity is the point at which you become encumbered. Once you learn how things work, most of the bosses die in three or four hits and you're pretty much invincible. A one trick pony.

I don't know why I thought this would be different than Elden Ring, which I refunded after about an hour or so of playing. That game relies on input lag to make the fights harder, but still on my first playthrough of Elden Ring and being a complete noob I nearly killed the boss on the horse on my first try fresh out of the gate. At least Dark Souls Remastered has ultrawide support (how does that make sense?) and the controls are responsive and not laggy so it is technically a better game.

I think the only reason people play these games is to see if they can do it. Let me save you the trouble. Yes you can. It will just be monotonous and repetitive whether you steamroll all the fights having figured out the game or you play the same boss over and over trying to defeat it. You can do it, but whether you will want to depends on whether or not you have better games you want to play.

Aside from the gameplay, one other thing that stands out as really bad is the grammar of some of the dialog, particularly when they're speaking Early Modern English. They constantly use the third-person singular simple present conjugation of verbs for the second-person. I've seen clips of later Souls games and this hasn't changed. An example:

"But cometh thee not for the grave of Sir Artorias?"

which should be "But comest thou not for the grave of Sir Artorias?"

The way it is written in the game it's the equivalent of saying "But it comes to you not..." as thee is used as an object and not the subject, and cometh is the wrong conjugation. Except for a few verbs, this is pretty much what happens across the board. Strangely enough, in certain places where they do use the second person singular simple present conjugation it is where the imperative should be used which is the same as the bare infinitive. Go figure. Another abomination is:

"Thou dost cometh."

As in Present-Day English, when using an auxiliary verb with a verb, the auxiliary verb is conjugated as normal and the verb it's modifying is the bare infinitive. You say "He comes" or "He does come" not "He does comes." And again, as always they're using the wrong conjugation of come so even if you drop the auxiliary verb it's still wrong. And all these examples are from just one of the many dialogues that use this language.

I know this is a strange thing to be critical of, and I know this is a Japanese studio, but maybe don't attempt to use archaic language for the dialogue. If you're not familiar with Early Modern English, you probably won't notice but if you are it's a bit immersion breaking as these characters are clearly not all making a conscious choice to use improper grammar.

As far as the story, it never made me care about it. The story is as crucial to this game as a story is to a mindless action movie. It's there because it would be pretty stupid not to have it, but it's just an illusion of a story, in the end this is just a series of boss battles. You spend almost all of your time either in a boss battle or trying to get to the next one. I do have to say the opening sequence for this game is 100 times better than the one for Elden Ring. At least this one explains the set up quite a bit more while the Elden Ring one was just a list of character names you're supposed to care about without knowing who the heck they are. This one does list a few names but the list makes sense in its context and isn't a borderline parody like the Elden Ring one.

Posted: November 23
There is something about this game that keeps you wanting to go back. It's really smartly designed. In a way, it's both simple and complex, it's vague and dark, but when you beat it, you realize it was so clear and bright.

The story is amazing, leaves a lot to think about, without being flooded with tons of cinematics and dialogue.

On cinematics, it has one of the best opening cinematic ever, so badass. I found an upscaled version of it on YT I watch it from time to time

The way New Game Plus loops into the story is something I experienced for the first time in this game, so smart, I felt that journey myself, understood it better, and wanted to go at it again

If you're new to the souls-like genre, this game can be frustrating if you're not patient. I know that the reputation is that these games are hard, and sure, they can be challenging, but think of them as they need problem-solving rather than get gud. There is always an easier way to tackle the obstacle at hand, you just need to find it.

The remastered version is a great upgrade, it runs smoothly with no issues and better anti-aliasing. I found it to be more visually appealing than DS2 Scholar of the First Sin.

Posted: November 23
Dark Souls isn’t just a game—it’s a landmark in gaming history, a brutal, beautiful journey that reshaped what players expect from challenge, world-building, and storytelling. Even today, more than a decade later, it stands tall as one of the greatest games ever made.

From the moment you awaken in the Undead Asylum, Dark Souls pulls you into a world that feels ancient, broken, and completely alive. Lordran is unlike any fantasy setting before it: interconnected, mysterious, and built with such care that every shortcut you unlock feels like discovering a secret passage in a living labyrinth. The level design is legendary—the way Firelink Shrine sits at the heart of everything is pure genius.

But what truly sets Dark Souls apart is how it tells its story. Nothing is handed to you. There are no forced cutscenes, no walls of exposition. Instead, the game whispers its lore through item descriptions, crumbling architecture, and haunting NPC encounters. You’re not just playing through a narrative—you’re piecing together the ruins of a fallen world, and the sense of discovery is unmatched.

The combat is deliberate, weighty, and immensely satisfying. Every swing matters. Every dodge, every block, every parry feels crucial. The game punishes carelessness but rewards patience and observation. When you finally defeat a boss that once seemed impossible—whether it’s the Capra Demon in that tiny room or the breathtaking duel with Ornstein and Smough—it feels like a personal victory you earned through pure determination.

The atmosphere is unforgettable. From the eerie stillness of Anor Londo to the horrors lurking in Blighttown, Dark Souls creates emotion through environment better than almost any game. The music only arrives at key moments, making every boss theme hit with dramatic weight. And the characters—Solaire, Siegmeyer, Gwynevere, Artorias—are iconic parts of gaming culture for a reason.

Dark Souls is difficult, yes, but its difficulty has purpose. It teaches resilience. It rewards curiosity. It respects the player enough to let them struggle, adapt, and ultimately triumph.

In the end, Dark Souls is more than a game—it’s an experience. A journey of hardship, mystery, triumph, and discovery. A masterpiece that continues to inspire countless games and millions of players. And even today, returning to Lordran feels like coming home to a legend.

A true classic, forever etched into gaming history.

A satisfying, but often frustrating experience. I agree with another reviewer suggesting it falls off after Ornstein and Smough. That may be because I went to do all the side content before finishing the game. But I enjoyed my time in punishing Lordran. Some bosses are ridiculously unfair, and the lack of inventory direction will screw over anyone not consulting a guide, but overall the satisfaction of persevering against all odds make this a worthwhile playthrough. Please take advantage of the discord to find others for jolly cooperation should you feel weary and hollow. And never be afraid to sleep on it. Frustration and impatience will only get you punished. Die with zen. And then win.

Posted: November 19
Played II and III but that was a while ago. You've all heard about the difficulty and it's all true. You will be punished time and again. Frustration levels will be off the scale. Most of the enemies will seem to be a couple of levels above you and the icing on the cake is you are really given very little to go on as regards where you go and who is friend or foe. There is no pause and the game is very cute when it comes to farming for drops and resources. That's a lot of negative things to consider. Just as well then that the pluses far outweigh the negatives. The best level design you will find anywhere. There are multiple layers to this game and whether you try to rush or take your time is up to you. I'd take your time. Assuming you actually complete it when the final credits roll you will be very sad.

Posted: November 15
The second Souls game. While this remaster is quite underwhelming because the developers could've just spent more time and money into making a proper PC port, it did actually fix many of the problems the original PC release had (and it also fixed Blighttown on consoles). This is also the only way to experience the game legally nowadays.
The Estus Flask is an improvement over the grass system that Demon's Souls had, as now you're not forced to spend all those precious souls on healing items. It makes the game a bit less easy as you can only have 5/10/15/20 of them depending on your bonfire level, but at least you get them for free, almost (holding more requires spending humanities at each bonfire, unless you run back to one that has been kindled already).
Many of the bosses in this game are memorable. In my DS2 review I didn't even mention the bosses at all, because I completely forgot about them. In DS2, they're only memorable because of how uniquely broken they are. In DS1 though, they're memorable because of their unique designs (except the copy-pasted ones like the Asylum Demons). Sif is a sword wielding wolf that limps when he'd bled enough. Gwyn is a fallen lord with a gigantic flaming sword and a melancholic theme, fighting in an ashen cave. Artorias is a man with a broken hand that still manages to beat you to a pulp with his flipping attacks. Centipede Demon is a huge centipede monster with flexible arms in a lava pit. Ceaseless Discharge is... well, he's Ceaseless Discharge.
This is the only game other than DS2 in the series where you can upgrade armour pieces. Not exactly sure if this is a good thing as it makes armour essentially underpowered by default, though there are many many options and they decided to compensate with this so that you can wear whatever gear you'd like but still have decent protection.
The storyline is a little bit hard to follow as it's not exactly continuous, you have the option to go whichever way you want, and that can make you forget things. The protagonist is also a mute. They've always been mute. You build your knowledge about the world through NPCs the bosses (and especially their souls), and item descriptions. Each item has its backstory detailing its users, where they were commonly used, etc. Some of the characters' motives are up to open interpretation. Whatever happens to Solaire in Lost Izalith, to Big Hat Logan in the The Duke's Archives, what Lautrec and Patches want, and so on.
Other than that, it's massively broken. Backstabs are broken, hitboxes are broken, the poise system is annoying, and many of the weapons and magic are way too overpowered. I am one of those people who bait players into gesturing, then I one-shot them with a Dark Bead (i.e. the shotgun diarrhea spell). If that doesn't work, I equip my Chaos Zweihander and my fully upgraded Giant's set. This should say a lot.
The game is way too grey. The sun shines, the sky is blue, yet it's still so dark. I know that this is because of the atmosphere, but DS2 still did it the best.
The player character is not ambidextrous. Holding a shield in your left hand initiates an attack no matter what, and using a spear for example in your left hand causes you to block, unless you use heavy attacks. This is as dumb as it can get, especially because you only have 2 slots for each hand, making inventory management more difficult.
3.6/5, not great, not terrible.

You know, I once saw a Video about Dark Souls and how it helped the person in the video cope with their depression and just how life goes. I actually have a similar story and it might be dumb to share it in a steam review, but i want to reach people who maybe have faced similar things.

It was September 2024, Life was going downhill fast, Health reasons (nearly died because of a fever), School was difficult as hell, and my best friend decided to nearly ditch me, and replace me with another guy who wanted to do things to me, which I cant tell you in a steam review. I didnt have much to live for, But that was also the Time I started playing Dark Souls, and I dont know what it was, but the game helped me cope insanely with all of it, I always hear people saying "if you are having a hard time just stop caring" or "fate has its ways" but I gotta tell you if it hadnt been for this Game i dont think I would still be as mentally stable as I am right now.

Now lets go into the details why it helped me. I think Dark souls has something insanely peaceful about itself, sure it is about defeating bosses, but it also shows humanity in a dying world, a state which reflected my mental state at that time, but the "dont give up there is a hope" mentality really helped me. Fast forward to this day, I am (more or less) healthy, Schools booming and my best friend realized what she did was wrong and now we are at the top of the platonic friendship iceberg.

So this review might be biased, but I gotta say this might be one of if not my favourite game of all time, I'm gonna take the Dark souls Mentally with me, until the last day of my Life.

thanks a lot for reading guys .And remember :"Don't you dare go hollow"

Dark Souls 1 is the most immersed I have ever felt in the world of a video game, even more so than modern day open world games. It is a masterpiece of environment and level design. Every location sticks with you. Everything about this game's world was made consciously and intentionally. I'd bet most fans of this game could make a map of it from memory with next to no missing parts. The slow and methodical combat, the management of weapon durability and humanities, the genuine threat that the standard enemies pose, the iconic sound design, all of it comes together to make a game that truly feels like an adventure in a way that nothing else I have ever played has, including the Dark Souls sequels and the spiritual successor Elden Ring. No open world game will ever amaze me as much as the grand, purposefully made spiderweb that is Lordran. No action game will ever frighten me as much as having the knife i was relying on break in the middle of the catacombs. This game does have its fair share of old-game jank, but that could never overshadow how unique and real the experience feels. Play Dark Souls. Just don't try to 100% it. That is not fun.

Posted: November 11
Dark Souls Remastered isn’t just a game it’s a rite of passage. Equal parts masterpiece and madness, it dares you to suffer, learn, and triumph. one death at a time.

GAMEPLAY : The core gameplay loop is addicting every swing, roll, and parry feels heavy and meaningful. The tension of exploring each area keeps you on edge. But it’s also undeniably janky, weird hitboxes, stiff animations, and some awkward camera angles can make dying feel unfair rather than deserved.

LEVEL AND WORLD DESIGN : The interconnected world is still one of the best in gaming. Firelink Shrine connects to everywhere, and discovering shortcuts feels genuinely rewarding. Even after years, that “aha” moment when you find a loop back to safety hits hard.

PERFORMANCE : The Remastered edition smooths out the experience 60 FPS and better lighting make Lordran look sharper, but don’t expect a modern glow-up. It’s still a product of its time, just less muddy and more stable.

SOUND & ATMOSPHERE : The silence between boss fights and the eerie ambient sounds make every footstep feel important. The music... when it hits... hits hard.

VERDICT : Dark Souls Remastered is both a reminder of how far game design has come and how much it owes to this brutal masterpiece. It’s clunky, it’s punishing, but it’s unforgettable.

Personal score : 9/10

Actual score : 7.8/10

Dark Souls is for a very specific type of player. That player should be patient, calculating, determined, and detail-conscious. Only by paying close attention to every single detail, from item placement to enemy behavior, can you start to unwind what is unquestionably one of the greatest untold stories in gaming history. You must force the world to yield up its secrets, or be condemned to wallow in ignorance. There is a masterclass in storytelling occurring here that has taken a decade to unpack.

All that said, the game actively tries to find ways to make you quit, and largely succeeds not due to the difficulty of enemy attack patterns or crazy timing, but rather an endless slog of ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that makes the simplest tasks an exercise in fortitude. In that regard the gameplay is more akin to arcade platformers, in spirit if not in execution. Prepare to run back through gauntlets to get to every boss battle, for boss battles that are as much about luck as skill, for an unforgiving combat system, and a constant battle between sunk-cost thinking and the question, "could I be playing something that doesn't make me scream and throw my controller?" Yes, yes you could. Still, even if it's not my cup of tea in comparison to its later entries, there is a lot of genuinely great material here that walked so Dark Souls 3 could run.

Posted: November 4
DARK SOULS has a lot of glaring issues that make this game NOT FUN.
The worst offenders:
No. 1: forced PvP (Player invasions). It's a ♥♥♥♥ design choice that should have been patched out. Later games offer the choice to play offline or semi-offline to prevent player invasions from happening when you don't want them, but NPC invasions (equally annoying) remain. Hopefully FromSoftware will ditch this antiquated crap mechanic once and for all.
No. 2: gank boss fights. That is just bad game design. And there's a lot here, the worst offender being the infamous Ornstein and Smough.

Now the remaster updates the graphics that were rather poor when this came out, but the worst issues remain - hence it gets the thumb down. There are better "Souls-like" titles out there, MORTAL SHELL is much more fair and a superior title, and both LORDS OF THE FALLEN games are far superior. Sorry FromSoftware fan boys, I will not be joining your circle jerk.

Posted: November 4
I read a comment which referred to DS1 as 'lightning in a bottle' and there's no better way to describe it.
This is the game that began a lifelong obsession for a lot of souls fans, and there truly is no other game like it.
You will hate it, you will hate yourself, you will delete and reinstall it over, and over, and over again (if you're anything like me.)
But what I wouldn't give to be able to go back and play this again for the very first time - it even outshines Elden Ring in some ways as far as I'm concerned, but perhaps I'm biased simply because I fell so deeply in love with this game.
If i were to make any recommendations for first timers, do yourself a favor and only play the remastered edition - skip Prepare To Die, it's unneccessary for anyone who isn't an OG oldhead looking to reminisce about the good old days - clunks, 30FPS and all.
If you've only played Elden Ring, you might look at this game as being 'mid', but if you really delve into it, you'll discover the essence which truly made games like Elden Ring (and other FROMSOFT titles) such masterpieces in the first place.
Dark Souls 1 is quite possibly in my top 5 games of all time, and that's even above Elden Ring, which is legitimately a masterpiece in its own right. Play this game before you die (IRL).

Posted: November 3
I thoroughly enjoy RPG games, especially dungeon crawlers. The souls serious, especially Dark Souls, is a step above all other games. As opposed to many other games where you become pretty powerful early on, and have access to the best gear, this is not the case here. The game is extremely challenging, and requires you to develop the correct strategy and not just spam the hit button like many hack and slash games. It is truly rewarding when defeating certain enemies or completing an area.

Story wise, it is hard to understand it, as you have to read many item descriptions. There are some good videos out there outlining the story and the background but it is very tough to understand from the game. Also, don't expect having a clear direction on what to do and the game world is pretty open, but there are tough areas which you'll understand you have to come back to when your character is more powerful.

Overall, excellent game world design, enemies, and combat (I only do melee with some ranged and never used spells).

Posted: November 3
Dark Souls Remastered is a masterpiece of challenge, atmosphere, and interconnected world design. The world design is immaculate — It's a game that rewards patience and persistence like few others. It's a game that really brings you back and then makes you wish you were never there to experience it in the first place. It's not for the faint of heart but the absolute most enjoyable thing about the game besides the nostalgia is that you get to see the progress the studio made over the years, from Sif being The Red Wolf of Radagon and so on. It's absolutely amazing to see how things have changed since their first title and for that alone I'd recommend the game a thousand times over. Besides Blighttown being the true final boss of Dark Souls it is a masterpiece.

[9/10] Astounding. The game that started a whole new genre. FromSoftware were on the verge of something great with Demon's Souls but it wasn't until Dark Souls that they pretty much got it perfected. Dark Souls has a couple of rough spots where time constraints left a couple of areas underdeveloped. However, overall, what this game achieves is staggering. In all their SoulsBorne games since, they still have not quite captured the magic of the level design in Dark Souls.

With Dark Souls, FromSoftware nailed the formula for challenging third person role playing adventure games. The high challenge presented does give you a great feeling of achievement when you finally overcome the difficulty and push through to the end. Coupled with a deep and fascinating lore (that can be difficult at times to understand just from playing the game) gives a wonderful dark fantasy vibe. The breadth of weapons, armor and character builds also gives the game a large amount of replay-ability and the opportunity to role play.

Dark Souls – My Journey to 100% Completion

After around 90 hours of playtime, I finally managed to 100% complete Dark Souls and earn every single achievement.
It wasn’t just a challenge of skill — it was an emotional journey full of pain and sorrow. Not the pain of the gameplay… but the pain of the story.

From the very beginning, when our first savior — the one who gave us hope and freed us from the Asylum — eventually went hollow, and there was nothing we could do to save him…
To watching Sif, both of his heartbreaking cutscenes…
To the sacrifice of Artorias, fighting the darkness alone…
To the tears of the Duke’s Archives couple, whose fate was nothing but tragedy…
To the betrayal of Seath the Scaleless,
and the fall of Big Hat Logan, who, despite all his wisdom, lost himself to madness.

And also, to all the others not mentioned by name but forever remembered —
Solaire, Andre, the Giant Blacksmith, the Giant Archer at the tower, Princess Gwynevere, and so many more who made this journey unforgettable.

I still can’t forget Dusk, taken away and trapped in the abyss…
Or Siegmeyer, whose brave heart led him to die by his daughter’s hand…
Or The Fair Lady, frail and suffering, still worrying for her sister —
a sister who gave up everything to protect her.

There’s so much to say about this world, and only those who truly explore it can understand its depth.
Dark Souls isn’t just a game — it’s a story about despair, sacrifice, and fleeting hope.
Finishing it didn’t just give me achievements; it left me with memories and emotions I won’t ever forget.


ps: Thank you, Miyazaki — for designing this game.
ps2: Screw whoever designed Seath the Scaleless — having to cut his tail nearly made me lose my mind.

Dark Souls was my 2nd FromSoftware game that I have finished; Bloodborne was my first. Dark Souls was also my first FromSoft game that I played. I now have 104 hrs on this game and am on new game +3. The world building is immersive. the gameplay is especially responsive. Even inputs you mistakenly pressed are taken. Quest lines, albeit linear, are rewarding with wonderful interactions with the little amount of Jolly Cooperation (friendship for you non players) shining in the world of dark. And even then, you are constantly reminded that you are alone. You are the ONLY one who can save this world. Boss designs are unique, for the most part, and they all have challenging attack patterns to learn but you almost always have a fair chance, especially if you use spells, summons, and items to help. While I have discussed the good about the game, now i'll discuss the not so good. The lock on camera is frustrating. I can't tell you how many times I locked on and forgot how limited my attacks were and died as a result. I also believe the sense of direction is often times missing. On my first playthrough, I had absolutely no idea where to go. Many times I would run my head against a brick wall, thinking the game is just supposed to be that infuriating. After a few hours though, you understand the level design enough to find your way. But what if the player can't? I couldn't. I looked up a playthrough and followed it for 3 hours until I saw a comment that encouraged players to experience the game from mistakes. From Software has mostly improved this issue with their games (especially so with ELDEN RING). Many have mentioned this but, the second half of this game seems a bit rushed. FROM HERE WILL BE SPOILERS FOR THE STORY OF DARK SOULS!!



I would definitely recommend Dark Souls. Although frustratingly challenging, the reward is immediate once you overcome these difficulties. This game is the gateway drug to your souls-like addiction waiting to come. Humorous friends, sad departures, badass builds (and stupid naked builds on the other end that one shot the biggest bosses), and beautiful music await if you decide to try this game. 8.7/10

P.S.
Amazing chest ahead

Есть несколько категорий людей:
- Олды, которые прошли ДС еще в 2011-2012;
- те, кто наслушались рассказов олдов о том, какая ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ это игра, попробовали, сдохли на самом начале игры, но сочли это ахуенной новаторской механикой, так и не прошли игру но начали умазывать олдов подключившись к их хвалебным одам;
- те, кто безопасно прошли ДС на ютубе и тоже рассказывают как это ахуенно;
- ньюфаги, которые играют в новые соулсы, но не играли в ДС, но при этом говорят, как в ДС все ахуенно и жалуются на те или иные механики новых соулсов ДАЖЕ ПОНЯТИЯ НЕ ИМЕЯ, ЧТО ЭТИ ЖЕ МЕХАНИКИ ПРИДУМАНЫ ЕЩЕ В ДС И РАБОТАЮТ ЗДЕСЬ ЕЩЕ ХУЖЕ;
- и наконец ньюфаги, которые играют в новые соулсы и нашли в себе силы справиться с ДС и понимают, насколько это устаревшая игра, которая должна остаться в 2011.
К последнему типу отношусь я.

Культовая ли игра? Однозначно.

Стоит ли ее пройти? Да. Один раз в жизни однозначно. Вы начнете понимать, почему соулсы работают так, как работают и глупое нытье по типу "в лайс оф пи мечем задеваешь стены и это бесит" или "в вучанг слишком медленные хилки и подъемы после того как тебя сбили с ног" должно свестись на нет.

Лучший ли это соулс? Нет, однозначно нет. Прародитель, да. Культ, да. Но те же Вучанг, АИ ЛИМИТ и Лайс оф Пи обошли ДС во всем, довев все первоначальные механики до совершенства и улучшив то, что можно улучшить. Отказаться от того, что бесит полностью, правда, получилось только у Вуконга, ну да ладно.

В остальном я могу только представить, как горели седалища у людей в 2011, когда еще не было гайдов, чиз-видео, фандомов и люди проходили игру вслепую на своих горьких ошибках... RIP

Кстати, если будете искать видео в ютубе по тому или иному босу готовьтесь к тому, что ничего полезного в 90% случаев вы тоже не найдете, потому что большинство видео заливали непонятно кто и как прошедшие игру, типы либо оверперекачанные идут против боссов, либо вся "стратегия" сводится к тому, что чел просто надел тяжелый доспех и после каждого удара юзает хилку. Да и вообще большей половине кто что-то говорит о ДС не верьте - достаточно взглянуть на ачивки, чтобы понять, что большая половина людей игру не прошла даже до половины.

TIPS для новичнов:
- если в интернете написано, что какой-то условный драконий меч самый имбовый в игре - это не означает, что это правда. Найдите свое оружие для себя, уникального которое подходило бы всем, не существует.
- имбового билда не существует - качайте своего персонажа как вы видите и хотите. Если кто-то все вкачивает в силу - совсем не значит, что этот стиль игры подойдет вам. К примеру, я даже здоровье особо не прокачивал, у меня ХП не доходит даже до тысячи и мне этого более чем достаточно. вместо этого я максимально качаю ловкость и быстрое оружие.
- не гонитесь за утраченными душами. Готовьтесь к тому, что у вас сгорят 10к...20к...30к.... Временами даже несколько раз подряд. И попытки их вернуть затянут в вас в петлю. Иногда важнее сосредоточиться на том, чтобы пройти какой-то определенный этап в игре, чем сохранить души. Для желающих мест для фарма хватает.

Don't let my low hours on Steam deceive you, this is my most favorite game ever made. (I have done multiple playthroughs on my Playstation and put tons of hours into this game.)

Dark Souls is in my humble opinion, the most masterfully crafted game ever conceived, and this is even with the second half of the game put into hindsight. I'm a happy guy, but I've heard of a lot of people who've played this game and it's literally saved them from deciding to move on from this world. I wholeheartedly believe them because the message of not giving up hope and "going hallow" is masterful storytelling.

Dark Souls is without a doubt, my most favorite game of all time.

Over a decade ago at this point, I got Dark Souls: Prepare To Die Edition for free on my Xbox 360 through their Games For Gold program. I was probably like 12 or 13 at the time, and I got absolutely filtered. After a few hours of playing and getting stuck on Taurus Demon, I decided that I'm just not good enough for these kinds of games. I wasn't having fun, I wasn't fully grasping the mechanics of the game, and when I did beat a boss or overcome a challenge, the joy of winning didn't even come close to making up for the pain of losing over and over and over again. So I put the game down and ignored every other release that claimed to be like it, because I had simply decided that I wasn't good enough at video games to enjoy those titles. And for that period of my life, I was probably right.

Fast forward to 2022, when Elden Ring released. My dad had it on PS4 and wanted to gameshare it with me while I was at college. I said why not, since i didn't have to pay any money for it. So I tried it, and while I never beat it, I did get a good 80 hours in and defeated many bosses that in previous years I never would have had the patience or skill to tackle. Godrick, Draconic Tree Sentinel, Morgott, even pre-nerf Starscourge Radahn. I only stopped playing because something else came along and grabbed my attention, and I forgot all about it.

Now, 3 years later, I finally decided to try Dark Souls again. And I had a wonderful time with this game.
The world, the exploration, the music, the bosses, everything was fantastic. There's something really special about this game and I can absolutely understand why it is still critically acclaimed all these years later. I even really enjoyed the second half of the game, which people seem to think is awful in comparison to the first half. And the DLC? Somehow even better. Artorias was the best fight in this game for sure, and beating him was all the more rewarding once I crafted his greatsword. I had a great time with this one from beginning to end, and I can see myself revisiting it some day in the future. If you enjoy the later fromsoft titles but have never played this one, I absolutely recommend it.

Whatever good things you've heard about DS1, it's true. This ♥♥♥♥ bangs, it's peak. If that's all you need to hear, go play it right now, you won't regret it. But I like talking so I'm gonna keep doing it.

The best things about this game are the level design, atmosphere, and boss fights. The darkness, despair, and emptiness of the world really seep into every nook and cranny in a way that I personally don't think the other games have been able to replicate. You just want to spend time in it and try to learn all you can about the world around you. You quickly become attached to any ray of light in the game, which are often the friendly NPCs, and want to protect that light. The combat, though sluggish compared to the modern Elden Ring, has a rhythm that you get used to quickly and even if it pisses you off, you learn to love it.

The first half of Dark Souls is incredible, nearly flawless in my opinion. However, to be fair, the second half does have a drop in quality. I believe this was mainly due to constraints on the team making it. Some of the areas in the second half trade creative, difficult enemies for simply throwing large groups of enemies at you or having some sort of gimmick for the area that leaves something to be desired. The bosses of these areas are still enjoyable but it's the mobs you have to get through that strained my patience at times.

Despite this, I still thoroughly enjoyed the entirety of the game and can see myself replaying it to explore builds or get achievements I missed. Seriously, if you're interested, just get it. It's one of the best for a reason.

Posted: October 24
Honestly, after all these years, Dark Souls: The First is the best Souls they've ever made.
Having played every single Souls games, Bloodborne, Sekiro and Elden Ring, I can say that although they've streamlined and improved many ideas with subsequent games, they've never managed to capture the lightning in a bottle that is Dark Souls again.

The main two things that make this game truly great for me are the level design and progression.
No Souls game comes close to how actually cool the Dark Souls interconnected world is and how satisfying it is to explore, even after dozens of playthroughs. Well, I guess Shadow of the Erdtree kind of attempts to do something simillar but in open world, but it's just not the same. I never had that feeling of my mind expanding after riding that damn elevator from Undead Parish back to Firelink Shrine for the second time, even though every From game is doing this shortcut design time and time again, and in my opinion failing at recapturing that same feeling.

And progression? Idk man, it feels like Dark Souls had that sweet spot of mechanics between Demon's Souls' rawness and modern Souls' streamlining. Armor actually makes a lot of difference and you can feel it. Stamina and health are a lot more important. Overall leveling feels more impactful where you actually feel your power growing with every allocated point. Especially after Elden Ring with its weird leveling break points.

The Remaster? It's good. My only gripe with it would be that stupid bonfire in Catacombs. It really wasn't all that necessary, nobody died to Pinwheel ever (except Jerma, but he's a tired old man, so don't be harsh), and they should've put it directly after Pinwheel if they really wanted a checkpoint there. But it's not that critical. Just don't sit on it before getting the Lord Vessel. Other than that the price is an absolute rip-off, never buy this at full price.

A Timeless Masterpiece of Challenge and Atmosphere

Dark Souls Remastered is more than just a game. It is a triumph of game design, an experience that challenges, rewards, and sticks with you long after you put down the controller. From the very first step into the undead world, you are immersed in a beautifully bleak universe that feels alive, dangerous, and utterly captivating. Every location, from the haunting ruins of Undead Burg to the oppressive halls of Anor Londo, is meticulously crafted, dripping with atmosphere and story hidden in every corner.

The combat is precise, weighty, and deeply satisfying. Every swing of a sword, every roll and parry, demands attention and skill. Enemies are not obstacles to be trivially destroyed but tests of patience, timing, and strategy. The satisfaction of finally overcoming a tough boss or navigating a perilous area is unmatched in gaming. Dark Souls does not hold your hand, but it trusts you enough to learn, adapt, and grow.

Remastered brings this classic to modern standards beautifully. The improved visuals, higher frame rate, and smoother gameplay make the world even more immersive. Despite these updates, the game retains every ounce of the tension, mystery, and danger that made the original unforgettable. The subtle storytelling, environmental cues, and item descriptions are genius in their restraint, rewarding those who pay attention and think critically.

Multiplayer, in its indirect form through messages, summoning, and invasions, adds layers of depth and unpredictability, creating moments of shared triumph and heartbreak that feel organic and memorable. The world feels alive not because it shouts at you, but because every design choice, from the smallest enemy placement to the sprawling interconnected levels, is deliberate and meaningful.

Dark Souls Remastered is not just a game to play. It is a journey to experience. It is punishing, yes, but it is fair, beautiful, and endlessly rewarding. Few games dare to trust their players as much, and even fewer succeed with such elegance. Every victory here feels hard-earned, every discovery feels profound, and every challenge leaves you wanting more.

If you love games that demand your full attention, respect your intelligence, and deliver an experience that is haunting, exhilarating, and unforgettable, Dark Souls Remastered is a masterpiece that deserves your time, patience, and admiration.

There aren't many things that I can say about Dark Souls that haven't already been said. Not only has its existence ushered in and inspired a new genre of action game, but its own name has become a metric by which to judge other games' difficulties.

What I will say is that in spite of its reputation as a game for masochists, Dark Souls is a game about discovery, resolve, persistence, and overcoming struggle. At times the atmosphere may feel too hostile, or the situation too dire, but what you'll find is that humanity persists in places and conditions you wouldn't expect, both within in the game, and within your actions as the player.

I may have been late to the party, but I loved Dark Souls for the journey it took me on, and the analog it provides towards struggle and persistence in life. It might not be the type of game for everyone, but if you find yourself interested, I don't think I could give a better recommendation.

Posted: October 16
I can't quite remember how many times I've beaten this game, but I still like to come back to it from time to time, it's just damned good.

The worldbuilding is interesting, exploring (while sometimes daunting) is very rewarding, the game branches in so many places and you'd be surprised where you end up after stumbling around for a while.
The combat can be quite challenging at the start and I think that will be the biggest hurdle.

A huge tip I didn't discover until my last playthrough is that locking onto enemies as the game teaches you will in most cases handicap you further as you don't get full access to your motion (when locked on, you can only sprint straight forward and roll in 4 directions, while without lock on you can sprint in any direction aswell you always roll where your stick points). Once you learn to manually aim your attacks instead, all enemies will be alot easier, trust me on this.

Anyhow, I am a strong believer of going in blind in this game, it will be the absolute best experience.
There are many tools to handle disadvantageous situations, use them, figure them out and once you're finished. Be calm, patient and Praise the sun.

This game is good not because it is hard, It good because of its amazing level design and Interesting world to explore that actually rewards you for exploring. It rewards you for prevailing in its challenge. Every bonfire lit, every boss fallen feels earned. No quest markers, No maps, No hand holding It is you and your skill alone. It trust you that you are smart enough to figure it out and you will eventually. Of all games that I have ever played and known nothing have ever surpass the interconnected world of this masterpiece.

If you want to play this game for the first time this is my tip .
1. Play it blind never look at any guide don't spoil your self the fun and challenge.
2. Do not pick the thief class or the master key in the character creation in your first play through.
3. Resistant stats does not matter don't level it up.
4. Explore the world, talk to NPCs and read item description of every item you pick up they are the guide and the clues.

I'm only just delving into souls games but so far this one was the most mysterious, the most nerve racking and rewarding experience, So many times i would mutter to myself "Surely there is a bonfire around this corner" It's that constant fight or flight which makes the world and your actions feel meaningful, And when you start to understand the mechanics DAMN COMBAT IS SATISFYING, Once you become a more formidable force against your foes, The exploration really opens up, and so does your internal perspective, One minute you feel like a god, the next minute you are being squished by god like a bug, That feeling is unmatched
I went into this game not knowing what to expect, And i marched out a man, a man who now hits every single chest before opening it.
I really wouldn't consider Dark Souls as hard as people say it is, It's not exactly a hard game but it requires a few things to succeed, Patience is the biggest one, The ability to concede and say "i died because i did X wrong" will help you MASSIVELY, And thinking laterally, it's astonishing how many things you WILL miss in this game, I had to slap myself a few times for all the backtracking i did only to realise that little door in the corner i missed was actually a massive secret area with an entire optional boss battle and multiple powerful items, I could go on forever about this game and I'm only just scratching the surface with these games

English version (french below) :

Just to clarify, I haven’t played the original version, so I can’t compare it with the remastered edition.
Dark Souls is a unique experience, it’s almost more of an exploration game than an action game, with a world that’s beautifully designed. Believe me, you’ll get plenty of surprises.

The game looks fantastic, in my opinion, even better than the third one, though that’s subjective.
There are lots of varied areas with distinct colors and very different atmospheres. The bosses are also really well-designed, although you can feel the age of the game, especially if you’re coming from Elden Ring or Dark Souls III (it might be a bit of a shock at first).

The game is much less difficult than the general opinion suggests. Yes, it’s challenging, for sure, but it’s far from impossible. You can also summon NPCs or other players if you want to enjoy the game without getting stuck.
Just like Dark Souls III, it would be a shame to miss out on the beauty and the unique experience the game offers just because of its difficulty.
The DLC is also really fun, and it’s included in this edition, so make sure to enjoy it!

For completionists, 100% is doable, but it requires replaying multiple runs to get all the special weapons (and all the endings), plus a bit of farming for items, which you can also get in multiplayer if you prefer.
One little tip: if you fight the final boss and defeat it, you won’t be able to go back and complete any content you missed. Don’t make the same mistake I did : do the DLC before facing the final boss.

Enjoy the game, it’s a reference for many other games, even today. :-)



French version :

Je précise que je n'ai pas joué à la version originale, donc je ne pourrai pas comparer avec cette version.
Dark Souls est particulier, c'est presque plus un jeu d'exploration qu'un jeu d'action, avec un monde particulièrement bien designé, croyez-moi vous aurez des surprises.
Le jeu est très beau, selon moi plus beau que le 3e même si c'est subjectif. Plein de zones variées, avec des couleurs bien marquées et des ambiances très différentes. On a aussi des boss aux design très sympas, même si on sent l'âge du jeu, surtout comparé à Elden Ring ou Dark Souls III (si vous venez de ces jeux, ça va probablement vous faire un petit choc)

Le jeu est bien moins dur que ce que l'opinion générale veut faire croire. Oui, c'est dur, c'est clair, mais c'est tout sauf impossible, et vous pouvez également invoquer des PNJ ou même d'autres joueurs si jamais vous voulez profiter du jeu et que vous vous sentez bloqué(e). Tout comme Dark Souls III, ce serait dommage de se priver de la beauté du jeu et de l'expérience unique qu'il propose à cause de sa difficulté.
Je précise aussi que le DLC est vraiment sympa, et inclus dans cette édition donc profitez en !

Pour les complétistes, le 100% est faisable, mais il nécessite de recommencer plusieurs parties pour avoir toutes les armes spéciales (et toutes les fins), et aussi un peu de farm pour des objets, que vous pouvez aussi avoir en multijoueur si vous préférez.
Petit dernier conseil : si vous affrontez le boss final et que vous le battez, vous ne pourrez plus retourner en arrière pour faire le reste du contenu que vous avez raté donc ne faites pas comme moi, et faites le DLC avant d'aller affronter le boss final.

Profitez du jeu, c'est une référence pour beaucoup de jeux qui sortent encore aujourd'hui :-)

A very good game that will truly test your resolve. The combat is deeply satisfying, requiring players to carefully find openings to attack — opportunities that are not always obvious. Proper spacing and timing are highly rewarded. The level design stands among the best in the action RPG genre, with interconnected locations that offer multiple paths for progression. This design naturally encourages players to create their own playthrough rules and limitations, inspiring challenge runs such as SL1, no-death, or no-hit.

If you think you have patience, or if you want to learn it, Dark Souls: Remastered will be your teacher. Every mistake is a lesson, every victory hard-earned. Few games reward persistence the way this one does — so step into Lordran and see how far your resolve can truly take you. Don’t you dare go hollow.

I can't believe how long I had this on my backlog of things to play but I don't think other games will get any better than this gem.
The world is so cohesive and well laid out together I'm really surprised how connected everything was. The characters were lovely sort, with few exceptions of course but so are people in real life. Not everyone will like you and neither will you like everyone.
There's very special charm to the atmosphere and games made back then. You get bit of everything varying from giant dragon arse to giantess curvy lady booba.. I mean From toxic sludge of sewers and weird underground demons to this charming almost angelic presence.. Right!
Coming from newer souls games I noticed many improvements made over the years but that didn't stop me from enjoying this one. Definitively worth it on 50% off discount.
You will not understand or even see many things in first playthrough.. there's so many depending things on when and what you do that this game has huge potential for replayability and of course continuation of suffering. But you will come back from this game like from a gym.. sore.. defeated.. but you will come for more and each time coming back stronger and more determined!

And with that said.. don't you dare go hollow..

Of the 4 "souls" branded Fromsoft games, DS1 is the best. The connected world and level design shines. Similar to Demons Souls, the level design provides the majority of the challenge while the bosses are relatively easy, but require traversing an area before they can be fought. The early to mid game areas are all incredible. Undead Parish, Blightown, Anor Londo, Painted World, and even The Depths are all great areas with well designed bosses. Had the entire game kept up the quality of these areas, it would pretty easily be the greatest game ever made.

Unfortunately, the game has some problems with some of the final areas and bosses. Tomb of the Giants is an all around miserable experience. Lost Izalith is clearly rushed and has enemy spam, though I personally think its still a decent area. It's bosses sour the experience though. The final boss, Gwyn, is too fast for the engine, making him either parry cheese or an incredibly tough boss with a long runback.

Fortunately, the included DLC has some of the best areas in the game, in addition to having 3 of the best 4 bosses in the game. These areas are meant to be late game, and counter the otherwise lackluster experience nicely.

I recommend this game to everyone who's ok with a little challenge (and can accept that parts of the game are innately unfair)

It was a fun run. Of course, many mechanics now feel a bit outdated, compared to DS3 or Elden Ring. I became proud of myself because I finished the original game back then, but even now, after I have much more soulslike experience, I died a lot. Big hugs to the two archers in Anor Londo and two lovely puppies near Capra!

The game plays much better than DS2, in my opinion. It is not clunky, and I felt "at home."

I think this game has much more "Dark Souls moments" than the 3rd part and Elden Ring, which is a bit annoying now. Back then, the game was more like a challenge, but after playing many soulslike games, the game no longer serves as the benchmark of being good.

Having said that, focusing less on just finishing the game allowed me to dive deeper into the world, dialogues, and lore. The game has much more connections between NPCs and some items than it may seem at first glance. The background of the DLC is amazing.

By the way, the DLC part is so hidden that I finished the game without even seeing that I missed something. That is very unfortunate, because I am not a huge fan of NG+. Make sure you go through it before the endgame, while I am on my second try.

I also never thought that Anor Londo is basically a real place in Italy, which motivates me to visit it. I suggest you check videos on YouTube about the real places from Dark Souls.

In the end, I am very happy to be able to play amazing classics with the updated graphics. Works great on the Steam Deck! Cloud saves are there, too (hello DS3). Clear recommendation: worth the full price.

Posted: October 6
Elden Ring is one of my favorite games, and I wanted to finally play Dark Souls to better understand the genre. Because DS1's good qualities are already so well documented, this review will focus on the reasons I don't recommend it.

Looking back on my time with Dark Souls, I struggle to remember a moment it was both tough and fair simultaneously. The levels and bosses are tests of knowledge far more than skill. Before you have the needed knowledge for an encounter - it's unfair. Once you've learned the tricks - Dark Souls is surprisingly easy. The manner in which you gain the knowledge to progress is a compounding problem. You either look stuff up (which cheapens the experience), or you perform hundreds of long and tedious runbacks, unsure if you're even in the right area - to the point runbacks become the very large majority of your playtime.

Many people consider the spiraling, interconnected level designs to be DS1's strongest feature - discovering your first shortcut back to Firelink Shrine is mindbending. But this level design means that a lot of time is spent rerunning shortcuts, instead of bonfires being more plentiful and closer to objectives. It ends up like a party trick that's cool the first couple times you see it, but comes at the cost of dramatically worse gameplay. When the runbacks are already painful, further lengthening them contributes to the player learning to stop engaging with enemies and just sprint past. The enemies and levels turn from imposing and sympathetic to laughable and ignorable.

As I overcame more of DS1's challenges, my sense of accomplishment waned - and in its place grew annoyance. Maybe this speaks to the quality of the front half of the game compared to the back, or maybe I just spent all my patience. My annoyance peaked when fighting the final non-dlc boss, who fell down 6 times to parries in my first attempt. I was just deflated. I couldn't believe that was the climax of what many hold as the best game of all time.

I appreciate Dark Souls for its innovation, boldness and its role in bringing challenge back into broader gaming. Playing it has helped me understand just how incredibly far FromSoftware have come in terms of boss design, art direction and subtle quality of life improvements. It also reminded me why I eventually put Elden Ring down - reasons including the general imbalance, sprinting runbacks, and the inaccessibility of it's wonderful story and lore.

Go into a different world and master it and decide it's fate. Classic Fromsoft formula that, once again, works perfectly well. The atmosphere in this one is unmatched though. Where other games are more directly about combat or puzzles or tactile feedback, Dark Souls uses spaces/environmental factors in many different ways. Capra Demon is tight square spaces, Moonlight Butterfly long straight ones, Taurus Demon is running and climbing. Ornstein and Smough is about pillars, Four Kings is lack of frame of reference. The only real problem have with this game is the Bed of Chaos boss, because it doesn't really work as I believe it was intended. Instead of being a changing map as you fight it, instead it becomes tedious running back. Seeing them use virtual spaces in this way and the way they cared so much about building an engrossing world really impressed me.

Posted: October 3
This is peak FromSoftware. The ultimate Dark Souls experience, an absolute must-play. Don't get me wrong, they have improved in a multitude of aspects since DS1, but the map design is simply unmatched and it delivers an experience so interesting that no other game caused this feeling in me. And this is truly the main selling point of the game for me, along with the (oftentimes) challenging difficulty (DS3 is far too easy compared to DS1).

THE BAD
This game falls shorts in a lot of aspects that are very dear to me:
1. Controls lacks fluidity (like all other games from 2010, but you get used to it);
2. There's only way of playing it: melee (sword + shield). I made a mage that was so weak, that I had to switch to melee before half of the game because pure caster mage progression is dire.
3. Quests are, except maybe a couple, forgettable. Quite unfair to do the comparison but DS3 has so much more memorable lines and quests that DS1 looks basic.

THE GOOD
1. Map design: superb metroidvania-like map that obliges you to memorize it (since there is no fast-travel until mid-to-late game). Get to know that map and its shortcuts have been so fun and rewarding to me that when I acquired fast-travel I kinda refused to use it.
2. Combat: even though, controls are a bit clunky, but once you get past the oddities, combat is top-notch without a doubt. I've seen newly released games that have a worse combat that DS1 has.
3. DLC is bizarrely good. Some of the best bosses in any game are here. Absolutely memorable (maybe because the amount of times I died?). Ambience is in the perfect tone and story is compelling
4. Writing is good! I judge the story-telling to be Miyazaki's "Achilles' heel", but I did liked DS1 story. Elden, for example, requires you to read so many item descriptions that, to my taste, is tedious to say the least. DS1 follows more of a direct and standard way of telling stories.

Dark Souls is widely considered one of the best games of all time, and I have to agree.

The gameplay is challenging yet fair. It's extremely satisfying to finally defeat a boss after spending numerous attempts learning its attack patterns. Even regular enemies can pose a threat, which keeps you on your toes throughout the entire game. There is a variety of viable builds, so you can try different playstyles to find out what you like best.

The many areas of the game world are interconnected to an impressive degree, and player exploration is heavily rewarded with hidden shortcuts and powerful items. Even after playing through the game three times for all achievements, I wasn't bored by the end of it.

The lore is very interesting, and is mainly explained through item descriptions and NPC dialogue. I think that this vague style of storytelling works perfectly within the setting of the game, and gives the player a reason to search for hidden items even if they don't plan to use them.

Overall, Dark Souls is a must-play. It doesn't go on sale often anymore, but it's well worth even full price. I'd rate it 10/10.

Posted: September 30
The pacing, the combat, the atmosphere- all designed to make you feel small and insignificant, which makes your hard-fought victories feel all the more large and rewarding by comparison.

The developers have designed a game world that makes the player character feel so lost and alone that the player behind the controller is made to feel the same. Every fight is a battle. Every death is a punishment. And yet, it is through this looping pilgrimage of "challenge -> defeat -> learning -> overcoming," that the player is made to realize that the true obstacle of this game comes not from the enemies, bosses, traps, or tricks, but from within the player's own mind: the shackles of self-doubt, self-critique, and self-imposed limitation that we so often put upon ourselves without realizing when faced with difficult situations that feel insurmountable.

It is ultimately a test of the player's own resolve: Can you calm your emotions after a difficult defeat and try again with a clear mind? Can you regroup your sense of dedication and purpose after multiple deaths in a row? Can you suppress the distraction of failure, the anger and disappointment that comes with loss, and refocus instead on learning, on developing strategy, on seeing through an enemy's strengths to reveal their weaknesses and capitalize upon them?

This game is known for being difficult, but that difficultly is frequently fair and balanced. The game becomes more difficult, at times approaching impossible, when the player enters a situation without being prepared, without carefully observing their surroundings, without appropriately respecting a new enemy who's lair you have so rudely and haphazardly tread upon. The game rewards patience, focus, and attention to detail. And it punishes without mercy those who rush, who panic, and who fail to respect the challenge in front of them.



The world design is, to my mind, still unmatched in the modern game era. A harsh, unforgiving, interconnected landscape that unravels in all directions as you explore, and folds back on itself in unsuspecting ways when you least expect a shortcut.

The level design is full of secrets, hidden loot, and illusory walls, protected by well placed enemies who all demand your respect when facing them. A rewarding experience for those who like to explore. For a game with a unconventional jump system, it offers some excellent (sometimes frustrating) 3D platforming sections. Every zone feels unique and alive with the history and the decay of a fallen age.

The music and sound design are great. The game doesn't overwhelm you with constant music to create a mood. Sometimes the darkest and most suppressing moods are evoked through silence and your own thoughts. Sometimes the most inviting sound is the music of a safe zone that you are so rarely rewarded with.

The boss designs are still, to me, some of the most iconic in the franchise. There are many larger, more complicated, more demanding bosses that have come in later games, but I still find myself thinking of so many of these bosses with awe and respect.

Combat. What's there left to say? This game redefined the action combat genre so effectively that is spawned a new "souls-like" genre of it's own. The stamina based combat system, the wide variety of weapon choices and builds, the flexibility to shift between lock-on and free combat, from single handed to two handed, from melee to range to magic. The game offers extensive freedom to make your own choices on how to level your character, even if that means making mistakes that you'll regret and have to learn to live with.



Truly moving art does't just offer a one-way glimpse into the artist's vision, it offers a reflection of the observer within the art. It reaches out, touches the observer, changes them. This game is excellent, but far from perfect. A "flawed masterpiece" by many accounts. And yet, do we not all have more to learn, more to understand, more to appreciate, more to strive for? Regardless of its flaws, this game still influences the medium to this day- a bellwether by which similar games will always be compared.

I cannot recommend this game enough. It is a game I continue to go back to. It is a game that has helped me through difficult times in my own life. One that has taught me lessons that transcend the video game medium. That sometimes the most well learned lessons come from failure. That sometimes the only way to achieve is to dust off and try again. And that true failure is only a certainty when one completely gives up.

Edit: My bad I picked deprived

Edit: Okay, at the 5 hour mark. This can be a fun game if you play it in chunks, not allowing yourself to be pushed too far. The semi-open world can be confusing when navigating the main story, but makes up for it with beautiful sights and atmospheres akin to experiencing Skyrim again for the first time. Combat leaves a lot to be desired as it is quite 'janky' and 'weighted', but if you master timing and patience, you can abuse openings. Parrying is not something I've bothered with yet. Unfortunately the early game is quite unforgiving, with ambushes, claustrophobic encounters and enemy respawns all contributing to the death of your mind. So far I'm enjoying the game but I could see myself getting stuck enough to give up entirely at some point.

Edit: Nearly 50 hours in. I suck at admitting I'm wrong - but damn was I wrong. Dark Souls is looking to be one of my all-time favourites. The learning curve is steep but rewarding - the world is complex and interlinked, with rich storylines and iconic characters - each boss battle teaches you a lesson, and my favourite part of this game is how it is one big allegory for depression. Dark Souls will not handhold you through defeating bosses or learning game mechanics. You will die and die over again until you learn. It isn't all perfect; the second half of this game is plagued by walking, absurdly huge areas and occasionally some busted RNG. But you make it through eventually. Yeet.

Positives [+] and Negatives [-]
+ Challenging and rewarding combat as intended
+ Epic OST
+ Ethereal atmosphere no other game can match
+ Great boss design
+ Good level design
+ Cool character/NPC designs
- Mid to late game is kind of a slog

This is not my first Souls game, but this is my first Dark Souls game. Just like the previous Souls games I played, I expected more of the same. It was, but it wasn't.

I did love the music, the vibes, the combat, the challenge. But there were some parts where I had to push through, not because of the challenge, but because of how bored I got. I know this game is considered a masterpiece and the atmosphere cannot be replicated by any Soulslike. I know this is an old game and the one of the first of its kind so expect me to not enjoy some parts because future games has improved the Souls experience.

It's just that the overworld isn't really interactive for me to stay interested and not get bored. Like the ones I played, Dark Souls do have NPC's with questlines, but I found most of them boring. The NPC's do have cool character design, though.

I love the level design for the most part. It's really thought out and I like how it intertwines back to Firelink Shrine. "Interconnected Map" is what it's called, I think. You have several paths, but they all end in returning to Firelink Shrine either by a literal path back to it or a bonfire warp.

There's not really much to talk about combat if you're familiar with Souls games, so that's why this is the last thing I will talk about. I enjoyed it for the most part. As this is Dark Souls 1, most of the enemies are predictable and not challenging. There are still some fast-swinging enemies and little trolls here and there to fool the player, but they're not that aggressive that you can stop them before it gets bad.

On my very first playthrough of Dark Souls, I killed the Bell Gargoyles, rang the bell, and returned to Firelink Shrine. I talked to the Crestfallen Warrior, who told me about the spooky, scary place called Blighttown.

Naturally, I had no idea where to go.

I didn’t realize there was a staircase leading to New Londo Ruins. Otherwise, I would have gone there.
But to my misfortune, I did know about the staircase leading down into the graveyard, because I had already died there fighting skeletons.

So I went into the Catacombs, thinking I was heading to Blighttown.

One thing led to another. I ended up in the Tomb of the Giants. I told Patches I was a cleric, so he attacked me. I killed him. Somehow, I also killed Pinwheel.

No Skull Lantern. No light. Just pure darkness.

Eventually, I reached Nito’s fog gate but couldn’t enter. So, blindly and broken, I made my way all the way back to Firelink Shrine.

After returning to Firelink, I finally discovered the path to New Londo. I explored the area, only to get absolutely wrecked by the ghosts.
Then I didn’t touch the game for months.

But eventually… I came back. And I fell in love with it.

You don’t play Dark Souls. You survive it. Then you understand it. Then you love it.

This is my third FromSoftware game, after Elden Ring (my first) and Bloodborne. I expected there to be rough edges and I expected there to be a lack of the QoL features implemented since this game's release, but I still wasn't prepared for Dark Souls. I tried to play it like Elden Ring, but Dark Souls is a much slower game and requires a more methodical approach. After some time I adjusted to this new style of play and began to enjoy my experience... mostly.

See, Dark Souls can very much be an exercise in frustration: cheese--or straight-up poorly-designed--bosses; wonky hit boxes; narrow corridors that you will clank your weapons against countless times; agonizingly slow recovery animations; long, boring boss runbacks, or runbacks littered with enemy ganks; uncooperative camera; etc. Very often, Dark Souls is more a test of your patience than your skills as a player. The difficulty is surmountable, but the horsesh*t? Depends on how much you're willing to forgive.

If you are of a forgiving nature, you'll be treated to an addictive, engrossing adventure. The world of Dark Souls is as mysterious and wondrous as it is deadly. Exploration is encouraged and is often rewarded, either with a shortcut or a rare item or even a NPC/merchant. The intricacy of the map will reveal itself as you quickly become familiar with every route, creating a network to and from every area in the game by the time you're ready to fight the final boss. The storytelling is typically implicit, leaving you to connect the clues and uncover the secrets of this world; you very much get out what you put in to the lore and narrative of Dark Souls. Similarly, the game won't usually explicitly state the solution to a problem, letting you discover it for yourself through context clues or, again, exploration. Dark Souls doesn't hold your hand; instead, it allows you the satisfaction of conquering it through cleverness and skill.

This is all to say, if, like me, you enjoyed Elden Ring and want to play some of the earlier games in hopes of capturing that same feeling, it's worth it. There is a genuine kernel of brilliance here (that would later, in my opinion, be improved upon). But be warned! Encompassing that kernel of brilliance is a load of bollocks that may or may not discourage you. It nearly discouraged me, but I kept at it and I am glad I did. Now I can pat myself on the back for experiencing Dark Souls... right before I uninstall it and never play it again.

8/10 - Great

There is much to say about this entry in the Dark Souls series; it simply is a must-play for those who want to dip their toes in it. The game-play is similar to the other games, but you begin to understand some of the decisions evolved or retired in the subsequent entries. The boss fights here are memorable and challenging, and so are many of the characters you encounter throughout the world. The combat is fantastic, and there are still many players still engaging in PvP. You would understand that some systems and its visual fidelity is dated, but there is still beauty and a quaint simplicity to it. It allows you to fill it's space with the imagination.

Ultimately, I would recommend this if you enjoy Souls games. If you come from the later entries, you will find a play-through of this game refreshing and enlightening in the context of the series.

Posted: September 12
Dark Souls succeeds precisely because it's a game that encourages failure. Other games deal with failure in pathetic or childish ways: they lower the difficulty for you, make it impossible to lose, or reset your progress if you fail too many times.
Dark Souls is one of the few games that tells you: No, you didn’t do it right, but that’s okay. Try as many times as you want, but you need to do it right.
In our society, we are terrified of failure. People who fail are heroes, and people who are successful and wealthy should be ashamed for it. In a culture where everyone gets the same medal regardless of their accomplishments, it’s easy to get depressed when you don’t have any motivation to succeed.
But Dark Souls shows you that there are still ways to challenge yourself to be a better version of you wherever you go, whatever you do.

This is actually my first review, so bear with me. I completed 100% of Dark Souls a while ago, and looking back, it’s easily one of the best RPGs I’ve ever played. When I first tried it, I gave up after Blighttown, but coming back six months later completely changed my perspective. The constant deaths, learning from mistakes, and slow but steady progress are what make it so rewarding. Maybe it sounds funny, but this game didn’t just shape me as a gamer - it shaped me as a person too.

Combat is deliberate and punishing. Some bosses can be clunky and frustrating, turning fights into a real struggle, but the standout battles, like Ornstein and Smough, Artorias the Abysswalker or Manus - are unforgettable. That duo fight (O&S), especially back in 2011 was incredible - managing two completely different threats at once was a brilliant test of skill and strategy. These moments make everything else worth it.

The world of Lordran is essentially a metroidvania, interconnected and full of shortcuts, secrets, and hidden paths. Exploration feels meaningful, and even after over hundred of hours, there’s always something new to discover. The second half of the game feels a bit rushed and less inspired than the first; some areas reuse enemies and bosses, which reduces the sense of uniqueness and discovery. That said, the game has incredible re-playability - the range of builds, challenges, and ways to approach combat feels endless, and it cleverly breaks the sense of linearity most RPGs have.

The DLC is outstanding, adding some of the best areas and boss fights in the game.

The story and world are steeped in darkness. Lordran isn’t just a setting - it’s a place where decay, despair, and fading light are part of every step you take. Piecing the story together yourself through items, dialogue, and environmental storytelling makes the sense of looming, almost oppressive darkness even more impactful. Every player who wants to truly feel like a gamer should play the Souls series - don’t make excuses about difficulty or not being skilled enough, just dive in.

Overall, Dark Souls is challenging, immersive, and incredibly rewarding. It’s one of the best RPGs ever, a game that teaches patience, skill, and persistence, and offers some of the most memorable experiences in gaming.

9/10 - I’d fall and rise again, over and over.

Posted: September 10
A genre defining game and one of the best to do it.

Everyone knows the difficult but never unfair nature of the boss fights are one of the most outstanding aspects of this game and the overall series, but what really makes the game for me is the level design and world building. The enemies are perfectly placed to give you a good challenge to overcome and teach you fundamental lessons about how to play the game. Item placements are also well placed to incentivize you to explore the entirety of the area and reward you greatly for it with powerful items, spells, consumables, etc. The atmosphere in this game is unrivaled, and the obscure lore creates intrigue. The lore being described through vague pieces of dialogue but moreso item descriptions and environmental clues within the game itself make the story of the game fascinating and very interesting to uncover piece by piece.

My only gripe with this game is that the last third of the game became a bit lackluster. Understandably, each of the areas after acquiring the Lordvessel had a certain gimmick about it to navigate, so a more prolonged experience in each of these areas would have been overly frustrating. Despite that, the level design still felt like it fell just a little short of where it should be. Notably, Demon Ruins and Lost Izalith are very sparse and empty areas, and the Bed of Chaos of course is the worst gimmick boss in the entire series.

Overall, this is still one of the best games I've ever played even if the more modern entries of the franchise outdo every individual aspect of this one. Dark Souls 1 is much greater than the sum of its parts and will always hold a special place for me when it comes to video games. Maybe I wouldn't recommend this for any beginner into the souls series, preferring Dark Souls 3 or Elden Ring, but this is a great game to play to see where it all began.

Dark Souls 1 has that "it factor" more than any other game in the series. It doesn't have the best combat (Sekiro), or the best bosses (DS3), or the best build variety (Elden Ring), but it does have the best sense of exploration and, by extent, level design. The first half of the game is a constant drip of eureka moments as you unveil the interconnectedness of the world and return to Firelink Shrine from every direction.

The game unfortunately drops off substantially after you get the Lordvessel. Areas become disjointed, the Lord bosses are all either simplistic, frustrating, or both, and areas like Lost Izalith and Tomb of the Giants are way more irritating than they are fun.

Gwyn sort of claws back some lost ground by being a fast and fun fight, but even he is a bit of a let down with his low health and lack of danger outside of his grab attack.

Dark Souls is far from perfect, but it says a lot that I like it as much as I do as a player who prioritizes mechanical complexity above all else.

Playing Dark Souls 1 on PC was my second play through of the game, and the first souls game ever played in 2021. An incredible game, difficult bosses, and an amazing story.
Humanity, a fragile balance that dances in between life and death.
The final chapter, the final curtain call. Life is violently beautiful, and much like Dark Souls these little deaths make us who we are.
Dark souls makes getting over it with Bennett Foddy look like child’s play. The mechanics, the timing when fighting enemies, and story are all an interwoven cacophony of “what the hell did I get myself into, but lets keep going”. This game is one of the best, most difficult, AND mechanically challenging games I have ever played/seen. This game has substance, merit, it has a good story, a well oiled machine that kept me entranced. All while being diverse and beautifully connected in the games overall concept as well as the map and how the world breathes.
Life is a fragile thing. It’s a balance of the little deaths that teach us to work harder, improve on our skills, and never give up. The concept of the soul in this game is this tiny sprite, the incorporeal thing that defines us, and puts us in the category of humans. Fragile, small, always looking for that one thing to belong to. We are not gods, we do not tower 20 feet tall and wave magic wands, but the soul In Dark Souls is our magic and what defines us as an un-dead warrior.
So sit at the bonfire, hang your coat, fill your estus and praise the sun dear un-dead warrior. Your time has come.
