This is where the cracks in fromsoft's formula are really starting to show for me. It's still a good game but I have a few complaints:

So much RUNNING this whole game you're running non-stop whether its running to Andre to level up a weapon, running between bonfires because you can't teleport to specific ones or the runbacks man the runbacks in this game bro.

The ability to roll into attacks with i-frames also seems to be greatly reduced in this game compared to ds3. Obviously ds1 is much slower than newer fromsoft titles but I would have thought the standard of rolling into rather than away from attacks would have been more prevalent in ds1.

It feels to me that in ds1 the difficulty is not from the bosses having outlandish movesets or enemy placement. A lot of the time the difficulty was just from frustration. ★☆☆☆☆ Dark Souls: Worst Game Ever Made

I don’t know who thought this was a good idea, but Dark Souls 1 is basically a torture simulator disguised as a “game.” You don’t play it—you suffer through it. Every five seconds some random skeleton, slime, or flying demon the size of a building smacks you into the dirt, and the game laughs in your face with its “YOU DIED” screen. Wow, thanks for reminding me, FromSoftware.

The controls feel like you’re piloting a refrigerator on roller skates, the camera has a personal vendetta against you (especially in Blighttown, where it should come with a seizure warning), and don’t even get me started on the bosses. Half of them are just giant blobs of “swing and hope you survive,” and the other half are unfair tag-team wrestlers who gank you in a corner.

Oh, and the story? Ha. There is no story. You get a two-minute cutscene about fire or something, and then the rest of the “lore” is buried in item descriptions that no sane person is going to read.

Everyone who says “the difficulty is fair” is lying. It’s not “fair.” It’s pure RNG nonsense, trial and error, and punishment for having the audacity to pick up a controller.

If I wanted to spend hours bashing my head against a wall while some sadistic developer laughs at me, I’d just go to math class. Dark Souls 1 isn’t a video game—it’s psychological warfare.

Final Verdict: 0/10, should be classified as cruel and unusual punishment.

Posted: August 29
Where to even begin. Dark Souls is such a unique game. It's known mainly for its difficulty, and yeah, that is one of its defining aspects, but this game is so much more than that. I can't just tell you. You have to play to understand it.

But since this is a review, I gotta praise it for its merits. One of the things that impressed me the most about this game is the level/area design. Dark Souls' world is already extremely interesting and immersive from an artistic/storytelling perspective, and the way the map was designed elevates that even further.

This is a non-linear game, so you're free (and encouraged) to explore levels in the order you want. It's not an open world, so there are limits, but you are given comfortably ample choices. There are plenty of secrets, and you'll be finding some pretty cool stuff if you look hard enough. Backtracking is minimal, not just because of good checkpoint placement, but also thanks to the cleverly designed shortcuts you'll find around the world. Finally, enemies and obstacles have really good placement, especially in the earlier areas. You'll have a fantastic time learning how to prepare to traverse each area, strategizing along the way.

Bossfights are the most known aspect of Dark Souls, and for good reason. Each boss is extremely memorable and very well designed. They are challenging, but never in an artificial way (with few exceptions *cough cough* Bed of Chaos *cough*). The game will teach you very early on that tackling bosses is never about brute force or pure unadulterated skill: it's about being smart. Learn their weaknesses, find better equipment, summon a friend. You'll die a lot, but each death will bring you closer to victory.

The graphics, atmosphere, and soundtrack come together to make this game an completely unique experience. You'll be immersed by the bleak, melancholic world of Lordran, one that still shines with small lights of hope across its world. The story is told through small pieces of lore scattered all across the game, meaning that you'll need to search the environment and pay plenty of attention to its clues to fully understand this world. (... or you can just watch Youtube videos about the lore. No one will blame you for that xD)

But even if you don't get the bigger picture, the main message carried by every single aspect of this game is clear enough: don't go hollow. Don't ever give up.

The game is not flawless, though! The main problem of Dark Souls is the fact that it's technically an unfinished game. You'll not notice this during the first half, as the developers made the wise decision of making the first impression as good as possible. But once you get past Anor Londo... the quality of the level design drops significantly. The later bossfights are also not as good. Still, they're not bad to an unplayable extent, so going through the last areas to achieve the ending is still a good compromise.

So... Yeah, this is Dark Souls. I find it tough to explain this game. So please, give it a try, even if it looks too hard. It's definitely worth it!

9/10

So what makes it difficult is:

1) The developers like to trick you with environmental traps, but if you expect danger behind literally every doorway and on every ceiling, you can probably avoid most of them.

2) Enemies will perform completely unexpected moves which may sometimes one-shot you, but if you have seen it a couple times you can easily avoid it.

3) You can theoretically brick your save by upgrading worthless weapons since there are limited upgrade materials and grinding souls is absurdly tedious. Technically some enemies can drop upgrade materials but they are too rare to be a reliable source. Most people would be Googling everything in this game though so this is probably not a real issue.

4) Dying in a boss fight and running back to the boss can be a slog and become really frustrating if you just want to go to bed or whatever. Lies of P makes your souls drop outside the boss door which I think was an excellent choice.


But it's all worth it because exploring and discovering new things is really neat and this is also the only piece of media I've consumed with a character named 'Petrus' right there in Firelink Shrine.

Pero por sobre todo, lo que más llega es su sutil historia, contada solo para aquel que quiera escuchar y conectar los hilos; engañosa, ambigua y desgarradora. Obviando -cuesta hacerlo- su ya conocido declive en la segunda mitad (remontada parcialmente por el hermoso Artorias of the Abyss), Dark Souls es una imperdible odisea sobre la desesperanza y la perseverancia.

🔥 “They didn’t just remaster a game—they reforged a legend in fire.” 🔥

Dark Souls: Remastered isn’t just a glow-up—it’s a resurrection. The game that birthed a genre, ruined friendships, awakened masochists, and taught an entire generation to praise the sun—has returned in glorious, remastered agony.

If the Prepare to Die Edition was the cursed blade that cut you down until you became something more…
Remastered is that same blade—sharpened, polished, and dipped in obsidian drip.

⚔️ Combat That Carves You into a Champion

Still slow. Still methodical. Still utterly ruthless.
Every swing of your weapon, every mistimed roll, every greedy Estus sip—punished.
But when you overcome? When you learn the dance?
You don’t just win—you ascend.

🛡️ Parried a Black Knight for the first time?
🧠 Discovered the shortcut in Undead Burg?
🔥 Finally solo’d Ornstein & Smough?
Those aren’t just milestones.
They’re core memories etched into your soul.

🏰 Lordran: The Forever World

You will never forget this place.
Not because of graphics (though the remaster makes every lava-soaked, abyss-drenched, moss-covered stone shine).
But because Lordran is the best-designed world in gaming history.

Everything connects.
Everything means something.
You’ll spiral down into Blighttown, claw your way through Sen’s Fortress, fall into the Abyss, rise above the clouds in Anor Londo—and it all fits together like some twisted, perfect jigsaw of pain.

🧠 Lore Whispers in Every Ruin

Dark Souls doesn’t explain. It shows.
You’ll read an item description and suddenly realize an NPC’s entire tragic backstory.
You’ll find a sword that belonged to a king you never met.
You’ll watch a knight stare silently at the sun, and it’ll break your heart.

It’s myth-making through mystery—and it never holds your hand.
You’re not a hero. You’re not special.
You’re just one more cursed soul in a land of the damned.
And yet… you push forward.

🎮 Remastered? Oh, They Knew What to Fix.

🧈 Smooth 60 FPS? Finally.

🕸️ Better Blighttown performance? Bless the devs.

🎮 Controller support that doesn’t fight back? Peak.

💀 PvP and co-op improvements? Now you can die to invaders in 4K!

This is the definitive way to experience the game that changed everything.
A faithful preservation with just the right amount of modernization—without sacrificing an ounce of soul.

💀 Final Verdict: 10/10 – The Flame Never Dies

Dark Souls: Remastered is not just a game.
It is a legend polished, a test of will, a rite of passage for every gamer who dares to embrace pain and turn it into progress.

You’ll die.
You’ll rage.
You’ll uninstall.
Then you’ll come crawling back, because deep down…
you know you were never hollow.

“You Died.”
Yes. And I’ll die again.
With a smile.

🛐 Praise the Sun. Prepare to Cry. Git Gud Forever. ☀️

Posted: August 25
My first exposure to any type of souls game was 14 years ago with the original PS3 release of Dark Souls and it is probably my all time favorite game so I may sound a little bit biased here. I recently picked up the Remaster on Steam after not touching the game in over 10 years, and it is just as much of a masterpiece as I remember it.

Positives:

Literally everything related to the melee combat, magic and ranged combat in general is just the meh easy mode version of the game.

The lore is absolutely SSS-tier, but to get a complete picture takes a lot of putting together a puzzle of lots of tiny disconnected pieces, so someone could play the entire game and have no idea what the actual story behind anything is.

The world design with all the connecting paths and incredible atmosphere is among the best ever created in video game history.

Everything in the world have a story to tell. Imagine something as simple as your Estus Flask (healing flask for those who have never touched a Dark Souls game) have actual lore behind it, I won't spoil any of the actual lore behind it, but it's all tied to the source of your immortality and the reason you come back each time you fall.

Even small things like item placement is deliberate. You pick up a new sword off a corpse, that corpse have some significance as it symbolizes someone who came before you and failed. You find an armor set in a chest, yep you guessed it, you can track it to someone in the lore and why they left it there in one way or another. You find a key somewhere, yep, you betcha there is some piece of lore as to why it's there and who brought it there.

This applies to all From Software's games, Dark Souls is no exception. The game gives you no directions, it encourages exploration, it reward exploration, it encourages learning and adapting, and it demands learning and adapting. This is by far one of the biggest strengths of the game alongside the world, lore and atmosphere.


There are a few minor things which could come across as a little slow and janky for someone who started with Elden Ring or Dark Souls 3. I personally have no issues with any of it, but it's worth pointing out, as it does require a little bit of an adjustment period.

The final few zones are a bit rushed and show some signs of them running out of time as the deadlines loomed. A lot of people complain about Lost Izalith, but that one is not bad at all in the Remaster, those who played it on release know it used to be so much worse with the huge aggro range the headless dragon mobs had and how many more of them there were blocking your path, all of this has been toned down to no longer be an issue. The only zone that still feels like it's badly designed in the Remaster is the Tomb of the Giants, simply because there is nothing interesting there, it's pitch black darkness and that's about it.


Dark Souls is a masterpiece. It does have some smaller flaws, but just because of how great the rest of the game is, those flaws are not very noticeable. It is the game that introduced a lot of people to this type of game, and even if Demon's Souls came first, this is the one that gained the popularity and it's the one that stands as the progenitor to all the souls-like games you find on the market these days.

Should you buy it? 100% you should. It's one of the few games that would actually be worth the price at 80€/$80 if it was released today. It is a unique experience, and no game, not even the other From Software games have managed to recreate the exceptional world and atmosphere of Dark Souls.

One of the most beautiful game I have ever played.
You are being thrown into brutal world which wont forigve you any mistake you make. But it rewards your patience and strenght not to give up.

The Good
One of the biggest highlights of this game is its harsh yet stunning world design. Each location feels uniquely crafted and seamlessly connected to the others. The way areas intertwine is a masterpiece rarely seen in modern games. More often than not, you’ll find yourself shocked by how cleverly everything is linked and this sense of discovery becomes one of the greatest rewards for exploring the map. Combined with the beautiful music, it immerses you in an incredible world, despite its darker tone.
The main story may seem simple, but it’s the lore where this game truly shines. It feels like everything you see each ruin, each area carries deeper meaning and hidden history.
The cherry on top is definitely the NPCs. While your interactions with them are often minimalistic and cryptic, they are perfectly polished and masterfully realized. Their subtle dialogue and presence leave a lasting impression.
Story is quite simple, but lore is where this game shines. It feels like everything you see, each ruin or each area you visit it has its deeper meaning. Cherry on top are definitly NPCs. While interactions with them are often minimalistic and leave you with tons of questions, they are perfectly polished and mastefully realized.

The Bad
The weakest part of the game is that it can sometimes be too punishing, especially in terms of bonfire placement and a few locations that feel intentionally stretched to exhaust the player. Also, the latter half of the game loses some of its magic; certain areas seem rushed and lack the design quality of the first half.

Beside that, this game is perfect and worth sinking in for a long time. While in comparion to other games that FS made later it may feel outdated, it still offers one of the most amazing journey and stunning expirience.

DARK SOULS review

Combat – 5/10
The combat is solid but basic. It gets the job done without the flashy moves of Dark Souls 3, but it’s not overly simple either, just good.

Bosses – 7/10
Dark Souls 1 has some pretty solid bosses. None of them felt overly difficult or unfair; every fight seemed possible as long as I kept improving. That’s what makes it such a good introduction for new players, it challenges you without ever feeling hopeless. Compared to DS2, I never felt like giving up here. The bosses do their job well, teaching the player the core mechanics while still being fun and memorable.

World/Level Design – 6/10
Dark Souls 1 has some of the best interconnected level design in the series. Those moments where you realise everything links together are incredible, almost like exploring a giant, living city. It feels smart and rewarding to navigate. That said, compared to the later games, the world can feel underwhelming. It’s not bad by any means, just overshadowed by the larger, more ambitious worlds that came after.

Atmosphere – 8.5/10
Dark Souls 1 nails its atmosphere. From the music to the environments, everything builds a sense of immersion that kept me hooked. The vibe of this game is unmatched, it’s the only one in the series, compared to 2, that fully pulled me into its world. I’d even say it’s some of the best atmosphere in the entire series. Dark Souls 3 felt weaker in this department (see my DS3 review for more context), but here it’s top-notch and a big part of why the game is so memorable.

Difficulty – 6/10
The difficulty strikes a good balance, never too hard, never too easy. It’s tough enough to feel rewarding but not overwhelming, making it perfect for first-timers who want the Dark Souls experience without being thrown straight into something insane.

Replay-ability – 5/10
Dark Souls 1 is fun to play again thanks to its strong core loop and the variety of builds you can try. That said, the replay value is limited by only having two endings, so there isn’t much incentive to run through multiple times just for story variation. The gameplay itself carries the replay factor, but compared to later entries with more endings and content, it feels a bit thin.

Performance/Controls – 5/10
Movement can feel a bit clunky at times, which can be frustrating in tight fights. That said, the game performs well on PC, with stable frame rates and minimal technical issues. It’s functional, but the controls aren’t as smooth or refined as in the later entries.

Overall Score – 6/10
Dark Souls 1 is a solid foundation for the series. It introduces the world, mechanics, and bosses in a way that’s fair and engaging, especially for newcomers. While some aspects feel simpler or less polished than the later games, the atmosphere and interconnected world make it a memorable experience.

Posted: August 21
Rating: 9/10


Disclaimer: From the Fromsoft title I have beaten Elden Ring and Sekiro. This was my first Dark Souls game.

Overall, I am extremely impressed with how much they accomplished and their lofty vision for the very first entry into the series and in creating an entire new genre of video games. I was expecting way more jank considering the experimental nature of the game. However the game is so enticing and invites you to dig deeper into the mechanics and uncover all the secrets you can find.

Brief complaints: Only two. First, (and this is likely my fault for bringing assumptions from Elden Ring) I felt like I was blindsided with some decisions I made with NPCs that permanently made the game more difficult for me without being able to fix these errors. For example, I was left with no way to recover the central Firelink Shrine due to making 2 stupid mistakes. As I write this however, permanent hardcore consequences is a neat idea that most games don't have the balls to do so perhaps it is not so bad after all. Second, and more egregious, I personally felt like the game did an amazing job at slowly making the game more difficult until you hit a massive difficulty spike at the very end of the game. This was extremely frustrating as I felt like the game broke it's own rules of engagement. I don't mind a difficult final boss (absolutely loved the ending to Sekiro) but the last 2 bosses in this game felt so out of line to the rest of the game as to make it very jarring. Ending the game made me feel glad it was over when I wish I had felt a sense of accomplishment as in Sekiro and Elden Ring.

None of these complaints are enough to not give this at least a 9/10. Overall extremely impressed with the vision Fromsoft has had from day 1. Cannot wait to play the next two installments in the trilogy.

Some simply good things:

+ Insanely beautiful Dark fantasy aesthetic
+ Difficulty so high its almost liberating
+ Once you get the hold of gameplay and combat mechanics, other games start to feel almost disappointing

And some bad things with a silver lining:

- Story and quests are only explained though vague dialogue and item descriptions, no journal notes no nothing to keep track of what has happened or what should be done next
+ By not holding your hand you have the full liberty to go wherever you want, and it will always benefit you in some way ( okay sometimes the benefit is to learn that you should not have gone somewhere yet :D )
+ Since nothing is shown or explained, and the game throws hidden pathways and illusory walls, player is encouraged to explore the world deeper than whats on the surface.

- Doesn't explain many of the gameplay mechanics
+ Feels exciting to learn something after 10 hours of playing
- If comparing to later released games in the series, the mechanics do feel somewhat outdated and clumsy

- There are clearly areas that just did not get polished properly before release
+ There are areas that are absolutely beautiful

If you're considering playing this game, I would most certainly recommend checking out Dark Souls 3 first, then if you're left hungry for more after that, then try this out.

Um jogo maravilhosamente difícil, com a história que eu mais me empolgo de ouvir e as melhores mecânicas de combate e gameplay.

Dark Souls é definitivamente um jogo difícil, mas com uma ótima proposta — e o melhor: ele entrega tudo que promete. A direção artística é maravilhosa, as cutscenes são empolgantes e, como já mencionei, a história é uma das melhores que já vi — por mais que não seja contada como em um jogo qualquer.
Gosto muito da trilha sonora desse jogo, mas sinto que ela falta na maioria da obra. Você realmente só percebe que tem algo tocando em Firelink Shrine e quando enfrentando algum boss, o que é um desperdício de mostrar ao mundo que a música importa também para gerar o clima. O único momento do jogo em que concordo de não ter uma trilha sonora é em Kiln of the First Flame. Uma área muito pesada se comparar com o resto do jogo. Tudo caminhou até aquele momento. Somando o silêncio e os inimigos que antecedem com o tão falado Gwyn, Lord of Cinder, e sua impactante música tema, funciona perfeitamente para deixar o jogador no mínimo emocionado.
Também gosto muito como sempre se aprofundam em personagens secundários e criam mistérios aleatórios, dando importância para cada detalhe. Isso num jogo de 2011 é surpreendente!

Porém, por mais que eu ame essa obra-prima, infelizmente sei e não posso recomendar para todo mundo. A galera que pede modo fácil no Elden Ring nunca conseguiria suportar os bugs e a hit box desse jogo, sobretudo a dificuldade. E por falar em dificuldade, tenho que mencionar que a partir do momento que você aprende a dar parry e faz uma build decente, o jogo fica muito mais de boa. Essa sensação só retorna no NG+, quando você enfrenta chefes novamente e pensa: “eu já passei desse boss antes, se eu perdi agora, a culpa é do jogo” — algo que acontece com frequência.

Sou um player casual, normalmente jogo para zerar e confesso que evito jogos difíceis, mas esse aqui vou até platinar. Por mais que eu já tenha dado minha opinião, ainda assim recomendo que todo mundo dê uma chance para esse jogo, considerando que, se você em algum momento desistir de jogar porque é difícil ou algo do tipo, o jogo te venceu. É um jogo sobre resiliência e força de vontade do jogador, que sempre te recompensa por tentar e explorar.

I know how revered and iconic this game is. I tried--I really did--but I honestly would not recommend Dark Souls I.
The Positives
Graphics still hold up quite well for its age
Atmosphere is very unique and detailed. Cold, bizarre, mysterious
Probably has the best level design out of any souls-like I've played
There is so much frustration, tension and epiphanies while getting to grips with this map. It's so hyper labyrinthine and interconnected. Masterfully done
The Reasons I stopped playing
The combat is too slow, hollow and repetitive
After playing so many modern souls-titles, dipping into this is just awful. Half the combat is you fighting against the camera and the skinty corridors around you and the other half is mindlessly hacking at hordes of enemies that have janky hitboxes
Boss fights are just... not fun at all
I got through maybe 5 main story bosses and a couple optional ones and I've hated every single one. Instead of the endorphin rush from finally beating a boss you've been stuck on for a while, there's just this numb, dull feeling, "oh okay, on to the next bit I guess".
I got stuck on a particular boss and just couldn't be bothered to slog through it
Had so much fun exploring this new area but then got thwamped a few times on the boss with its boring but hard to read moveset and bloated HP. Not a problem, you just keep trying until you beat it right? That's the fun of these games. I stopped and realised that I don't have that drive to beat it. I don't want to. The excellent exploration parts of this game aren't worth the slog.
I understand the above might sound a little infantile, but I have played and loved pretty much every souls-like out there and just found these mechanics too archaic and sluggish. I would have loved to keep pushing through to see how cool the other areas were but I just couldn't

If you were too young to play DS1 when it came out and are wondering whether you should get it or not... I'd say to probably give it a skip but still hold respect.

Posted: August 18
Dark Souls is the first (technically 2nd) souls game made by the renowned studio FromSoftware back in 2011. It is a game that has inspired so many other Souls-like games that I had to at least give it a try. I never played this game at launch so I'm immune to the blinding effects of nostalgia.

Starting with the good aspects, there are a bunch of weapons, armor and playstyles/builds to choose. You can grab these from random merchants (you can kill them too for their stuff), pick them off the corpses of dead heroes and even chop some off from the tails of certain enemies. I was surprised that you can cheese the drake sword simply by shooting 30 arrows at its tail from underneath a bridge to acquire this powerful early game weapon. Heck, you can rob the zweihander from a corpse by waltzing into a graveyard at Firelink Shrine. You can talk to blacksmiths who can upgrade your weapons with special elemental effects or even transform them into unique boss weapons after defeating the boss and stealing his soul. Towards the mid game, I chose to go all in on strength and endurance, wearing the absolutely GOATED Havel's armor set, the grass-crest shield for stamina and the zweihander for the massive damage. It was dopamine inducing to clear almost all enemies with a single swing and to kill bosses in less than 2 minutes but the weight of my equipment and the speed of this sword would bite me in the ass throughout the entire run. If you want to switch to a different build, you can simply grind souls to level up in other aspects like dexterity, intelligence or faith since they are also strong in their own ways.

The design of the game was awesome, not only does the world building have the best vibe of dystopian corruption, depression and helplessness (SCREW YOU BLIGHT TOWN) but you can circle around and go to so many different places from the central hub of Firelink Shrine. The enemies were simple but also an extreme pain in the ass when they got you with a stupid ambush or inflicted some god awful status effect like poison, toxic or curse. The first two were the most annoying since they would last like 5-10 minutes and will kill you simply from draining your flasks over time if you don't get it cured or rest at the bonfire. The bosses were simple and boring. They had at most 5-10 different combat moves, no second phase and easy to cheese once you've learned their technique. Some bosses like the Iron golem were funny (you can make this dude trip and fall off a bridge) while others were annoying, repetitive and dealt half your health despite wearing the tankiest armor in the game (♥♥♥♥ YOU IN PARTICULAR ORNSTEIN AND SMOUGH). One of the worst aspects of this game are the hit boxes and movement/attacking animations of both myself and the bosses. I can be cheesing a boss by staying underneath or behind him and suddenly when the boss reaches forward for a grab combo, I get snatched somehow, dealing almost my entire health bar. Or when I go for a swing after dodging the boss's attack, my character pulls an XCOM 99% hit chance attack fail and swing 90 degrees to the side for no reason despite locking on. Combine that with super slow timing to swing, getting hit because I can't move, taking a short nap before getting up to recover and then dying because I took my sweet time to lick the viscous maple syrup from my flask to save myself all while gayly moaning like I'm getting jacked off, I got frustrated way too much fighting these jokers. Also, bonfires would be strategically placed a mile away from the boss room so you'd have to rush past every single enemy you've already killed, hoping you don't end up wasting flasks before having to reattempt the boss again. The worst example of this is when I fought the twink at Anor Londo, pushing me all the way back to the unlit bonfire at the very beginning (you can't teleport out of there) and having to walk through the cathedral rafters just to reach an elevator on the other side of the map (I'm so glad statues of Marika are a thing in Elden Ring).

I've heard from many people that this game taught them patience or would give them satisfaction after finally defeating a difficult boss. For me, this game brings me suffering and beating it just relieves the symptoms; no satisfaction, no celebration, just the peaceful bliss of my death bed. As someone who was initially introduced into the souls genre through Elden Ring, playing Dark Souls wasn't the most enjoyable experience. It felt like taking a moment to look at the old family album of your Grandpa during his glory days, understanding the traits that made him awesome, and then reverting back to reality of helping him walk around with his arthritis and having to change his loaded diaper. Overall, I'd give this game a decent 7.5/10. Experiencing the amazing chest made me motivated to praising the sun a lot more these days.

Posted: August 18
As far as the remaster goes it's fine, it has keyboard and mouse prompts which is a big thumbs up and supports refresh rates higher than 60hz unlike Elden Ring (seriously wtf).

In terms of the game itself the logical part of me should absolutely hate it, there are so many obtuse and bad design decisions resulting in a terrible new player experience. There are whole sections of the game that feel designed from the ground up with the intention of being as annoying as possible. I could not imagine playing this game to completion when it came out without the use of a guide, items and areas that are integral to progression can be easily missed or are purposely hidden in places you would never think to check, and good luck accessing the DLC without looking it up.

The biggest problem in the souls franchise will always be the boss runback in my eyes, and this game displays it at it's very worst. The average difficulty of bosses is much lower compared to later entries, however expect to familiarize yourself with the 1-2 minute stretch leading to each one as you'll be spending more time trekking back than actually fighting the boss itself. It's a cheap way of making death more punishing, makes every boss in the game more annoying, and shows no respect for the players time. As I've fallen in love with these games I've learned to deal with it, but it will always be the biggest scar on this franchise, Elden Ring is proof that these games are more fun without it, good riddance.

Despite all of these criticisms I do still love this game, It's a testament to how souls gameplay is lightning in a bottle, it is such an addictingly satisfying formula that even in possibly it's clunkiest depiction I'm still absolutely obsessed with it. This combat system is so unique in the fact that progression feels earned rather than owed to the player, in so many games after killing a boss I feel that I won not because I'm good at the game but because I was a high level or stocked up on potions before hand, progression in Dark Souls almost never feels that way, victory almost always comes from good execution. It's a franchise that doesn't owe the player the credits at the end, you have to go accomplish it, and that makes everything so much more rewarding.

As time has gone on the franchise has drifted further from the level design of this game and it's a real shame, there are so many moments when opening new shortcuts that contextualize the scale of things and sell the interconnectedness of the world, newer entries like DS3 or ER manage to do this with distant views and set pieces which works great and is very impressive, but I would love to see a new entry that could use the strengths of both types of design.

Overall it's a very unique and addicting yet flawed experience that's an acquired taste, I wouldn't recommend this to anyone as their first souls game, Dark Souls 3 or Elden Ring are much more palatable in comparison. With any prior souls experience however there is so much to love here, the series has spent the past decade distancing itself from this games clunk and lack of quality of life features, but there's something really special about this atmosphere and level design that the newer games lack and I hope to see a return to form in the future.

I keep reading reviews about how clunky and difficult it is, as compared to modern Souls games. And I just think that's unfair, given the games age. I even read a review about the lack of story. So I wanted to do the game justice in a fair review.

First off, this game is a remaster of a game already well past its prime. But during that prime, this game was a cult classic that has given birth to, practically, its own genre. Demon Souls was first, yes, but Dark Souls put in the legwork for FromSoftware's reputation.
That said, you can expect things to be less refined.
It isn't a remake, unlike the PS5 version of Demon Souls. Even so, I'd argue it aged fairly well, with only minor things that could be considered negative. Camera is definitely always been a problem with this game, especially in tight quarters, but it's workable.

For anyone new to this series/style of gaming... IT. IS. HARD. It's not supposed to be easy. Everything is out to get you, and even minor mistakes can cost you a lot. However, as you improve at the games core mechanics, learn to recognize traps, and familiarize yourself with the level designs, you'll find you have less trouble as you go on.

The games art style is glorious. I can't find another word to describe it. The backdrops are beautiful, the layer upon layers of the level design adds flavor and variety, and the enemies and bosses range from intriguing to awe-inspiring.

As far as the story goes, if you're new to this type of story telling, the lore is rich but hidden. You have to really go out of your way to truly understand what's going on. From item descriptions to NPC questlines (that can end in a variety of ways), there is a TON of lore, but very little of it is actually spoon fed to you. But it's a very intense story, with a lot of "cliff hangers" or things left to your own imagination or head/canon.

All in all, it was a very unique experience in its time, and earned its place in the world. It's still WELL worth the play, and I hope many more people can come to appreciate the intricacies of the game. If anything can help you lose your pride as a gamer, its this title. Get ready to be humbled.

Posted: August 13
Dark Souls Remastered is pure perfection. The methodical combat, the unforgiving difficulty, the haunting silence that permeates every corner of Lordran, it all creates something transcendent. This isn't just a game; it's a meditation on perseverance.

What keeps me coming back after hundreds of hours isn't just the masterful level design or the brutal-but-fair combat. It's the comfort that comes with mastery, the way your relationship with this world evolves from terror to understanding to genuine affection. You learn to read the language of Lordran, every enemy placement tells a story, every shortcut feels like a revelation, every hidden passage rewards your curiosity.

And yet, even now, there's always something new to discover. A tucked-away item you somehow missed, an NPC questline with another tragic twist, a secret that makes you question everything you thought you knew about this world. The depth is staggering.

*"Whatever you do, do not crack and go hollow. Lest my time on you go wasted"* - Quelana of Izalith speaks these words from her perch on a pillar in Blighttown. She only reveals herself to those dedicated enough, a hidden teacher who believed in your potential when you didn't believe in yourself.

This game changed what I expect from interactive entertainment. Nothing else comes close.

Posted: August 12
The game that started it all for me.

I always feared playing Dark Souls because I felt I would never be good enough at it and not be able to finish it. But I could not have been more wrong. As I'm writing this review, I've finished Dark Souls 1-3, Demon Souls, Sekiro, Bloodborne (my fav!) and Elden Ring.

Playing Dark Souls made me a better player. I learned what patience is. I learned to conquer my feeling that a game should always allow you to do things your way; this is somewhat true for this game, but there were situations where I could have used my brain rather than hitting a wall for 4h 1/2 (I look at you Ornstein & Smough). Sometimes, you have to go with the flow, and that's a very valuable skill to have in real life: be able to determine when it's valuable to fight, and when it's valuable to open up your mind to other solutions.

The level design is probably one of my favorite of any game. This world just "makes sense" compared to other titles that sometimes feel overly convoluted in how the parts of the levels connect together. The verticality of Dark Souls is just memorable and very well done.

Fights are mostly fair, challenging, a bit quirky sometimes from a 2025 perspective, but I think it's part of the experience, and I don't mean that in a "get good" way, but in a "there is something to recognize in this approach". If you want to play an unfair game, play Demon Souls.

Highly recommended. Pray the Sun!

overall rating - 8.5
story - 9
map - 9
gameplay - 8

The map design makes it feel like the world is really connected. Every path/shortcut is eventually linked to each other. The level design is probably the hardest out of any dark souls games since it feels like they want the player to fall off ledges or die in some way.
The bosses in this game are probably the most easy of the 3 dark souls titles (i only find the dlc boss Manus hard). However, the controls do make the game harder though since combat feels slow, sluggish. For example, you can't spam dodge since there's a recovery delay. Most of these problems are understandable though since this is an old game.

Thing(s) I hate about the game:
- bonfire travel is not unlocked until halfway through the game, so you have to run back and forth between maps which is a waste of time
- running back to fight a boss is very tedious and time-consuming since most bonfire placement is somewhat far away, even if you had unlocked shortcuts (ex: Lost Izalith bonfire)

A masterpiece game that created an entire genre.
Dark souls is a hard game, that will test your determination and your strenght, and that is a part of why this game is so good. Every time you fail, you will learn something valuable, you will get better, you will grow, until you are able to overcome what at one point seemed as something impossible to beat.

The exploration in this game feels amazing, and it is incredibly rewarding. Every area is completely different from one another and you will never know what to expect and how or what will kill you next.


Dark souls will make you feel accomplished with its exploration, for beating its bosses, for learning its excellent mechanics, and even for upgrading that weapon that you so much like.
You can build your character whoever you want, and you can create a goal to use specific armors and weapons.

The game will force you to learn bosse's attack patterns, their strenghts, their weaknesses, and with every try you will get better.
Its mechanic of losing your souls (currency), when you die once, makes you to be more careful on your second chance, and if you die again before recovering them it will tell you a very important message; that you still have something to learn and improve! And with some resolve and consistency, you will do it.

Dark souls will teach you to never lose hope and to never give up!

Everything in this game is incredibly well designed, enemies, BOSSES, world building, weaponry, spells. It is a very rewarding experience.

The only reason someone would give a negative review would be because they need to "git gud",
Seriously, thats how good this game is.

Posted: August 6
Dark Souls is a game that I can´t really describe with traditional labels. If judged solely by the quality and polish of individual aspects it will falter: the infamous runbacks, a catalogue of shoddy hitboxes, scarce resources that discourage experimentation and an overall heavily punishing gameplay loop that makes mistakes be paid with a players most precious resource: time. Not even the bosses are safe as most of them are rather easy and honestly boring to fight, courtesy of their basic movesets and/or reliance on 1 gimmick which more often than not only adds frustration to these already unremarkable fights. The same can be said about level design, enough has been said about the likes of Lost Izalith or Tomb of the Giants to show that some areas of the game are universally despised for legitimate reasons. Reasons that made me give up on this game several times over the years I owned it.

But that - along with every single line of code within the game´s files - is all tied together. What makes Dark Souls special to me are the absurd lenghts the game goes to connect every single aspect found within its world, all the way to giving up on it. Life, death, rebirth, humanity: the bleak fantasy world of Lordran provides a staggeringly authentic experience that mimics real life´s struggles while inmersing you in its own rules. The conflicts, situations and people you get involved with as you get deeper in the rabbit hole are as mundane as they can be surreal, a contradiction best epitomized by my falling in love with a giant human dragon hybrid who´s supposed to be a boss (I love you with every fiber of my being, Priscilla. I will never hurt you).

It is this unique connection to reality that kept me coming back, that kept me from going Hollow and now has me fondly remembering even the least enjoyable episodes of my playing experience. As dated,frustrating and even boring as this game can get, these low points accompany and even compliment the games highest peaks and result in possibly the greatest example of something being more than the sum of its parts. Dark Souls is a game that I recommend to absolutely everyone, no matter if they like it or not, no matter how long it takes them to finishing it, they even finish it.


Except enemy attacks going through walls and other solid surfaces; no amount of introspection or justification, no matter how profound, on the part of the devs will ever make attacks going through walls acceptable in my eyes.

I'm glad I started the Dark Souls series with the first entry, because it also seems the most basic, mechanically speaking. Not saying it was easy, but the combat system for this game was fairly straightforward and definitely felt easy to learn yet hard to master. Despite this, I really liked it. Bosses, for the most part, felt pretty fair to fight in this game. The game hints at what kind of enemies you are about to face with items placed before you encounter them, and even so, you don't need these items most of the time.

I was told about the rushed second half of this game before playing, and it absolutely shows. Especially in Lost Izalith. And even then, I didn't HATE it... but it was definitely not as strong as the first half. The bosses were certainly my favorite part of this game. Walking into a fight and mentally asking myself, "What is this thing going to do that I haven't seen before and how am I going to counter it?" was really engaging as it encourages looking at past behavior from boss interactions and learning what you did wrong and how you can you learn to not get punished as hard or learning what you did right and how you can do it more effectively and consistently.

I think Dark Souls 1 is a good gateway into the series, but I also understand the veteran player's frustrations with the later parts of the game. Still a solid 8/10 game imo

Posted: August 4
It's Dark Souls™. It's a classic, a masterpiece, a must-play. Dark Souls™ does things that no other FromSoftware title does. Its world is interconnected and nonlinear. Cooperation is more important than in any of the other games. It expects you to figure out its mechanics by yourself, though there's no shame in using the wiki. The first 2/3rds of this game are brutally good, S&O might as well be the final boss cause everything after that is super easy, though a little frustrating at some points (mainly just the BoC fight). Still a lot of fun regardless.

As somebody who recently beat the original version, I'd say the remaster itself can be hit or miss, though mostly hit. The lighting in select areas looks kinda bad compared to the original, but all of the effects and materials generally look better with a few exceptions. The quality of life changes are welcome, too. Unfortunately the remaster doesn't really do anything to fix the original game's problems, which would have been really nice to see.

I've read that people may have issues with cheaters in PvP, even potentially ruining people's saves, so multiplayer probably needs better protective measures to ensure things like that don't happen. Personally, I've not encountered such issues (as of now) and there are mods out there to mitigate some of that.

Unlike Demon's Souls, which could have done with a simple remaster, I think Dark Souls™ would really benefit from a proper ground-up remake. Finishing content that was originally cut, polishing areas that needed more time, and further fleshing out the excellent mechanics and world the game already had. I'd be more than happy with such a remake as long as it stuck to the original's artistic vision.

Overall, Dark Souls™: Remastered is a solid 8/10, a must-buy, probably my favorite out of FromSoftware's soulsborne games, and maybe even one of my favorite games of all time.

I played Dark Souls on every platform (X360, PS3, PS4), and finally now on PC. I’ve probably spent thousands of hours on this game. I can’t stop recommending it — it was my first “soulslike” before that term even existed. It’s a game that doesn’t hold your hand. Many times, you feel completely lost and alone in that fantasy world, not knowing where to go or what to do. You have to piece together the lore through the art design, item descriptions, and pure interpretation. Which presents us with this rich storyline without happy endings — a good metaphor for the cycle of life, something that never ends, always goes on.

For me, it’s a 10/10 — a revolutionary game for its time, and one that will forever be remembered in the history of video games.

DESCRIÇÃO

Darksouls Remastered é um jogão, ponto final. Dificilmente você verá alguém falando mau dele. E com motivos. E desde já adianto: COMPRE!

Cada centavo vale a pena. Você adquire um mindset resiliente pois o jogo não é para brincadeira. Zerei o jogo 5 vezes com as mais variadas builds. Nenhuma é mais forte ou mais fraca. Mesmo a build de mago é tranquilo também.

O jogo é semi-mundo aberto. Um mapa interconectado e muito bem reaproveitado. Com detalhes lindos e uma história incrível.

Bosses são bem feitos. Cada um com sua própria forma de te chatear. Nenhum estilo de jogo é fixo em Darksouls. Alguns exigem parry, outros exigem dano mágico. No Entanto você pode derrotar todos os bosses da forma que quiser, alguns com mais dificuldades que outro dependendo da build.


A trilha sonora eu particularmente acho na média. Algumas são bem marcantes como a do último Boss. Ainda mais dado o contexto. Outras são boas, mas não tão boas quanto em Nier: Automata ou outros jogos Soulslike como Ender Lilies.


O que não falta é variedade de armas em Darksouls. E muito menos de items gerais. Acredito que eu tenha terminado o jogo com mais de 100 armas únicas. Além disso, cada boss com exceção de alguns dropam uma alma única para forjar uma espada assinatura do boss. Não desperdice essas almas se quiser a conquista "Honra de cavaleiro". Em especial a do Zif, cujo você irá precisar de 3.

As armas tem duas particularidades. Além disso algumas tem habilidades especiais com magia ou dano em área.


A história é bem fragmentada, porém é fácil de entender quando você começa a ler os itens. Em especial alguns items únicos como alma de bosses e lords. Além de algumas armas únicas como Rapieira de Velka

Para mim é um 10 sem dúvidas.


O jogo remasted já tem a DLC inclusa, portanto basta olhar ai no youtube para ver como ativa, pois é bem específico.


Compre. Vale cada centavo. Mesmo morando neste país cujo um único jogo equivalente a quase um terço de um salário mínimo, compre.

I started my journey with the Dark Souls series through the third installment, which impressed me enough to go back to the roots and experience the original — this time in its Remastered form.

The game’s strongest asset is, without a doubt, its world design, built around interconnectivity. Locations loop back into each other in intelligent, rewarding ways, making exploration feel satisfying and purposeful. Discovering shortcuts or hidden paths adds a layer of depth rarely seen in modern level design.

Although the game shows its age and feels a bit clunky — especially when it comes to combat mechanics and controls — the first half is an absolute masterpiece. The pacing, atmosphere, and environmental storytelling are top-notch. However, after defeating the iconic duo Ornstein and Smough, the overall quality noticeably declines. It feels like the second half of the game was rushed, as if the developers were eager to wrap things up quickly. Level design becomes less inspired, and some areas feel half-baked.

Thankfully, one boss redeems a lot — Artorias. He’s easily one of the best-designed bosses in the entire Souls franchise and a real highlight of the experience.

I’d rate the first half 8/10, while the whole game lands at a 6/10. It's a solid soulslike experience that I still enjoyed overall — but after Ornstein and Smough, I simply wanted to push through and finish it.

I think that it's really cool that Miyazaki and the FromSoft team have seen the success that they have. This game is prohibitively unique, so the fact that it was able to launch the studio into a household name is something that deserves to be celebrated. This game still found loving fans who sing this game's praises despite its notoriously cryptic story and mechanics. Like everyone else, I heard those praises, and wanted to give it a shot. And walking away from it, I have one suggestion: Do not play this game. If you try other souls likes and you really can't get enough of them, then yeah, this could be worth playing. But otherwise, save yourself the blood pressure and play the modern titles that don't suffer from the same insane design choices. If you like this game, I'm not trying to take that away from you, but I strongly believe that other games accomplish what this game tries to without being this outdated.
The phrase "tough but fair" is often used to describe this game. If you die, it's your fault, and you need to improve and do better. That can certainly be true, but a majority of the time, it simply isn't. This game is explicitly unfair. It's not even difficult. On an armorless run, O&S took me 5 tries, and nearly every other boss took less than that with the exceptional few taking an extra couple of tries. That's fine, of course, but I point this out to illustrate exactly 𝘩𝘰𝘸 this game is "hard." If I was just fighting these bosses, this game wouldn't have been 50 hours. So what was I doing? Runbacks. I don't mean exploring, or fighting NPC's, or anything like that. I mean running the same length of the map over and over again to get to the boss. Fighting the NPC's along the way isn't worth it because the game can be so punishing and you'll get more souls faster if you skip them and go straight to the next objective. They'll try to stop you as you run past, but if you hold sprint and move in a straight line through them they'll always miss you. So you don't even "dodge" or avoid the NPC's, you treat them like pillars to run around. That's not hard, that's tedious - and in my experience, downright miserable. The stuff outside of this can be good, the art direction and enemy designs can really carry the atmosphere in a lot of areas. Granted, those areas seem to deliberately set themselves up to trick the player or bait them into dying, but that illustrates what this game's mission statement is: ♥♥♥♥ you.
This game hates the player. You are cold, weak, and afraid and nobody is coming to save you. That's what this game is trying to make you understand. Piecing the story together is basically an ARG and experimentation is punished severely by the terrible controls and the enemy design. The multitudes of mechanics at play are deliberately obscured from the player. Most of the people I've talked to about this game didn't make it past their first area because Firelink Shrine goes to 3 different regions and two of them are borderline unplayable to new players that have no equipment. A lot of people wander into the "wrong" place. That freedom would be interesting if it didn't also require the player to have the knowledge of several playthroughs to meaningfully act on. Basilisks can soft lock the game. NPC's can be hit once and will permanently kill the player on sight and chase them to the ends of the Earth with no hope of recourse, even after respawning (save an incredibly specific NPC's cryptic dialogue option that costs a lot of souls). The map has dead ends that lead nowhere, traps that you can only find after dying to them, there's a boss that will literally un-light your bonfire. The fastest path back to him is to walk a different region of the map and then fast travel back to that boss, on a path that has 0 enemies to stop you. Even if you had to treat NPC's like they exist, which you really don't, this runback would still be a solitary 3 minute walk to a different section of the map so that you can fast travel to another section of the map - all for the immense joy of running back and forth past projectiles against a tentacled twink with a gimmick.
He's not the only bad boss, roughly half of the bosses are laughably awful. Boring, uninspired movesets that are easily avoided and beaten with awkward AI. Or there's the Bed of Chaos, who isn't really a "boss fight," just a few crystals you need to hit to win against random instakills and bad hitboxes. Seath literally cannot be beaten if you approach him from the most direct path. You need to walk on literal invisible cliffs working against the worst physics engine I have ever seen to find him. Once you do, he's a cakewalk. There's more that I could say, but I'm trying to explain what the bulk of this game is - a Sisyphean walking simulator to bosses that are often coma-inducing, while working against deliberately obscure and unfair mechanics and enemies using horrible controls. All in service of a narrative that doesn't make sense without a mountain of external context or mutliple playthroughs. ♥♥♥♥ this game.

Posted: July 27
Well....

Dark Souls 1 was for me like the one disappointing restaurant visit, where everybody told you it would be great, just for you to find so many flaws that you just can‘t enjoy it.

I was looking forward to playing this game. I love the genre. I liked Elden Ring, Sekiro, and I love Dark Souls 3 and Lies of P (In fact, those are two of my all-time favourite games).
So I was hyped when I read all the reviews about Dark Souls, calling it a masterpiece.
The beginning was great. Undead Burg and the Asylum are two great locations with good enemy placement, great design and overall okay bosses. But my journey took a nosedive afterwards. Partly it was because in search of the Depths I stumbled into the Catacombs and the Tomb of Giants - two locations you are supposed to visit later in the game. However, since I was crushing the Boss on my first try, I did not realize that I was in the wrong place. I wasted a lot of time there until I learned that I did not need to go there yet and instead had to go to a completely different location.

Soon followed the next nosedive of my enjoyment - the Capra Demon. The Boss itself is okay: He's got a well telegraphed attack pattern and slow movement speed. The problem is the arena itself and the fact that you also face two little dogs, who will bodyblock you on every opportunity they get.
This boss is exemplary for some problems I had with the game. All in all, those can be described as jank. In this battle, I had numerous times where my camera would just glitch out of the arena, so I could not see myself. Dying to a boss because I made a mistake does not frustrate me. I cannot say the same thing about dying because of being hindered by the game itself. So when I finally defeated the boss, I was not feeling any form of accomplishment. I was just glad it was over.

Another annoyance that happened to me multiple times: Bad hitboxes.
Yes, I know. And if you played any soulsborn games, you know as well. ALL of the FromSoftware games have weird hitboxes. But in this game‘s case, it‘s on another level. I was hit through walls, leading to me falling to my death and losing many souls. And I stopped counting how often I was hit by attacks that clearly did not connect with me at all.

In this game, there are a few different locations, and funnily enough, most people will agree that almost all of them are horrible. The big exception obviously being Anor Londo - which I absolutely agree with because it was by far the most fun I had with the game. Blighttown, the Tomb of Giants and Lost Izalith however, are just some of the locations which really are bad. And there is the next, and my personal biggest problem I had with the game: I did not feel like my time was well spent, but instead wasted. Let me explain:

In this game, contrary to Elden Ring, Dark Souls 3 and many other soulslikes, bonfires are placed much more spaciously. Only on very rare occasions you will find Bonfires in front of or behind the boss arenas. This however, means you will have to traverse the locations after EVERY SINGLE TIME you died to a boss. Sometimes this is not a big problem. But in Blighttown, for example, this means that you will have to walk through a toxic lake, which forces you to walk slowly. I don‘t mind starting the boss fight being poisoned. But I do mind having to walk for about 2 minutes while facing no enemy, just to get back into the fight. This led to me, again, being glad when the fights were over for all the wrong reasons.

I do not dislike this game because it is hard. I knew that before, and in fact, this is the souls game where I collected by far the least deaths. But when I killed Gwyn on my third or fourth try I did not feel any kind of accomplishment. I just felt empty. I was glad that it was over, but I was also not because I was waiting for the moment when I would finally see why people call this game a masterpiece. I was not glad because I finally beat the boss; I was glad because I finally did not have to play the game anymore.

What saddens me most: Anor Londo is a great location. You do feel like you‘re playing a good game. Ornstein and Smough are probably the most infamous Bosses of Dark Souls 1 and rightfully so. The Fight was a blast. But seeing how the game could be fun made me even more sad somehow.

Do I regret playing the game? No. No, I really don‘t, because it‘s interesting to see where the franchise started (with Demon Souls being exclusive to console). But I cannot recommend it either, as I cannot say that I had a great time.

Finally played it and I have to admit it's absolutely an impressive entry into the series.

It obviously has it's flaws, but does many things right. For example:
- world building (best in the series)
- itemization / diversity
- combat / stamina management / general pace
- upgrade paths
- art style / story / game length

I tried going in as blind as I could and obviously missed some bonfires / secrets that would've helped enjoying some areas a little bit more. I looked them up afterwards. Here are some I missed:







It's sad to see that future installments didn't invest in creating an interconnected world like ds1 did.
Speaking of future installments, I now played all of them and one thing that I really don't understand is the hate ds2 and the praise d3 gets. Imo ds2 feels more like ds1 than ds3 does.




Most common complains regarding ds2 I'v read:
- (1) too linear
- (2) easy bosses
- (3) hollowing as in losing hp when dying repeatedly
- (4) hitboxes
- (5) boss runbacks
- (6) artifical difficulty
- (7) adp / soul memory
- (8) endless healing
- (9) IronKeep elevator, story, poise, pace etc

1) While definitely a step back to ds1, you can still choose many different paths to tackle + great hub + great area diversity. Compare this to ds3 straight line. On my first playthrough of ds3 I searched the starting area for an hour to find a single path, until I finally looked up that you have to teleport to the top of the wall...Doesn't seem to bother anyone, the game still gets absolutely praised.

2) Bosses are - there are exceptions of course - not hard, but same goes for ds1. They are both really similar in that fashion. As in ds1, the area is part of the journey. You can trivialize every FromSoftware game. For example, if you look up a speedrunner tactic, pick the rapier, upgrade and buff it, yeah, the game will be easy...

3) Ds3 introduced the same mechanic, just labeled it different. You gain hp when human instead of losing hp when dying...No one seems to bother. I'm genuinely confused..

4) Just false and has been debunked. Just look up some youtube videos which prove this. I keep hearing this complaint however..Again, I'm soo confused. Feels like people are just parroting a youtuber without actually playing the game.

5) Did anyone complaining even played ds1?

6) I keep reading that exact statement, always worded the same, mostly referring to enemy placements. If you carefully explore and clear the area, I don't see a problem. Just don't expect to be able to run back to every boss without killing a single enemy on the way. Can't count how many times ds3 placed an enemy right behind a corner. But I understand, this is all personal and people have different, equally valid opinions.

7) Absolutely valid. If they would've communicated what adp does, it would've been absolutely fine since you get more souls to spend. Soul memory is just stupid.

8) I also dislike the fact that you can buy the healing stones from the merchant, but I actually do have a similar complaint against ds1. As soon as I could push my estus to 15/20, I absolutely ignored my healing management. I think ds3 did it best.

9) Elevator: Weird oversight and can break immersion. Surprised so many people even noticed. However ds2 does make up for it with how many diverse areas there are. Can't believe ppl genuinely hate this game because of this. Especially considering you have to teleport to your first area in ds3...
Story: ??? I don't know, but the story is great and fits the theme perfectly. Who genuinely dislikes drangleic?
Poise/Pace: Feels like home and similar to ds1. Ds3 basically removed poise and stamina management. You can stagger most enemies with your estoc and roll 10 times in a row while chugging your estus at lightning speed. If anything, I think ds3 should be criticised.

That's it. Wrote way more than initially planned. Seems like I'm emotional too invested and probably quite biased :D.
Anyway, despite the controversy regarding ds2/ds3, we hopefully can agree that ds1 is a great game that can undoubtedly be recommended to anyone liking the genre!

Posted: July 26
I was really excited when I decided to play the Dark Souls series. So many people suggested that I start with Dark Souls III because it’s an amazing game. But I thought let's begin with the first part. If I finish the series in order, then by the time I reach Dark Souls III, the buildup and satisfaction will hit much harder.
Well, enough about Dark Souls III. Let’s talk about Dark Souls Remastered.

Oh boy… what a game.
From start to finish, every single boss fight was both fascinating and frustrating. But the satisfaction I felt after defeating them. that was pure happiness. That’s what true victory feels like.
I completed the game three times to finish all the NPC questlines and unlock every achievement. I 100%ed it. I was so deeply invested in the lore. Every NPC interaction was so interesting. I always made sure to talk to them again after progressing in the game, just to see if their dialogues changed.
And when they did change? I was amazed. It made the world feel so alive. That’s why I kept coming back to them again and again.

Now, let’s talk about the world-building.
You never really know where a path is going to take you. The game doesn’t hold your hand. it never tells you exactly what to do. You have to figure it out on your own. The game encourages exploration. And if you don’t explore, you will miss entire areas, bosses, and NPCs.
I was just goofing around, going door to door, path to path. and suddenly, I stumbled upon a whole new area. That feeling of discovery? Absolutely top-notch. Exploration matters, man.

For me, this game is truly a 10 out of 10.
I got so immersed in the lore that some NPC and boss stories genuinely made me sad. There were moments where I even shed a tear.

As Gwynevere once said,
"Thou hast journeyed far, and overcome much, Chosen Undead."
From the Undead Asylum to the Kiln of the First Flame,
From Praising the Sun to the sorrow of Ash Lake
I wasn’t just playing the game…
I lived it.

It is pretty much just Dark Souls which is good because that game is Peak. What it does change is kinda mid.

Is this game for me?
This game is not for everyone! This game is for people who like a challenge. If you want unforgiving third person combat in a beautiful and mysterious setting this game will be something you love!

•Combat in the game is excellent.
•Game world is interconnected and well designed in almost all aspects.
•Multiplayer is interesting and fun.
•PvP offerings are incredible and rewarding.
•Super unique and unforgettable online aspects.
[Remaster Changes]
•60fps now!
•Player limit increased from 4 to 6!

•Combat (PvE and PvP) has a very high skill floor.
•Some areas (Demon Ruins and Lost Izalith) are unpolished.
•For PvP stat allocation for optimal setups is very limited.
•For PvP Many weapons are worthless.
[Remaster Changes]
•For PvP changes relating to shields make fighting them annoying

Of the Soulsborne games this one has very strong PVE, very strong PVP, and indisputably the best world. While I would rather have PTDE, since this is the only one you can buy I would still say get it.

Product received for free

**Started playing, got trapped in hell.**
Dark Souls Remastered is that infamous, brutal game known for stress, endless deaths, and broken controllers — only this time it’s back looking sharper and running smoother to torment you even better! Lordran remains the same dark, complex, and mysterious world that never spells things out, filled with enemies, traps, and bosses just waiting for you to make a tiny mistake and destroy you.

But here’s the magic: with every death, you learn something new. The game forces you to be patient, think carefully, and learn from your mistakes. When you finally defeat a boss after countless tries, it feels like you’re a hero — like you saved the whole world just by slaying a rusty knight with a tiny sword.

The music, heavy atmosphere, and level design are still iconic, leaving scenes stuck in your head long after finishing. The remaster makes the experience much smoother and more enjoyable, especially with 60 FPS and higher resolution.

**Downsides? Yeah, the controls still feel stiff sometimes and some animations have that PS3 vibe.**

Amazing world design in the first half (mostly). I was surprised seeing how well the levels connected together. Although, you might find yourself not knowing where to go next because there's no indicator nor logs. But you'll probably randomly stumble on the objective anyway. So I suggest that you explore everything and only leave if you are 100% sure you are not supposed to be there or you did everything already.

Things also sort of fell off in the second half. Levels are connected in a weird way and the quality noticeably dropped. It's a mix of fine and, hurt to look at and annoying to play.

Bosses range from boring easy to dodge to fun attack patterns memorizing, with some exceptions being gimmicks bosses (which most of the time sucks)

Quests and npcs can be a pain to deal with, and it is a trend from fromsoft. There's no logs, no indicators. You have to exhaust their dialogues and explore. Sometimes they kinda tell you where to next, but sometimes you just have to pray to god you haven't miss anything in the area.

I'd recommend this as your first DS if you never play one yet. It's going to be hard, but it does get easier if you take the time to adapt, and once it did, you'll be able appreciate other games a lot more imo.

Posted: July 22
Okay, Dark Souls. This classic that has been out for over a decade, time to review it. I'm going to be answering a slightly different question than "Is it good?" That question has been overwhelmingly answered "yes" by the gaming community. This is the game that spawned an entire genre of "souls-like" games. Instead, my question to which I am answering "no" is "does it hold up well?" In a phrase, the reason I say "no" is simple: "Too much jank." Now, cue the flood of "git gud, scrub." Fair enough, although having full-cleared Hollow Knight, Nine Sols, and Armored Core 6, I think I can stave off those critiques to a degree.

When I say "too much jank," I mean that the game isn't just difficult, it comes across as unpolished, hence why it doesn't hold up as well. For example...

1) Many enemies are in narrow hallways, making fighting them unreasonable. In many cases, running by monsters is the most practical thing to do.
2) When refighting a boss, the bonfire is usually a several minute run away, and that's assuming you just run by everything. This makes refighting/practicing bosses needlessly time-consuming.
3) Virtually none of the in-game mechanics are explained.
4) No enemy friendly-fire. (I realize that MOST games are like this, but it's a pet-peeve of mine that a half dozen enemies can all swing their weapon at you and just phase through each other.)
5) Easy NPC permadeath. ( comes to mind. However, I am personally very salty about )
6) Too many time-wasting mechanics. Curse, egg-head, etc.
7) Contradictory multiplayer mechanics. If you need the help from multiplayer, then you are going to be dying and losing humanity, which then means you can't access multiplayer. Also, relying on random summon signs feels like such an outdated method of connecting players.
8) Inconsistent weapon pathing. It feels like enemy weapons can phase through solid objects/walls, but your own bounce off the slightest edge.

Are there plenty of good things in Dark Souls? Absolutely, it is one of the most influential games of modern gaming, with plenty of enjoyable aspects to it that I am sure I don't need to name. It does indeed have amazing challenge, strategy, variability, and the remastered version gave the graphics a much-needed face-lift. But if I am answering the question of "does it hold up well?" then the answer is, not really. For the time, some of these issues could be and indeed were overlooked, but nowadays, you can make a game challenging and even unforgiving without compromising smooth operation.

Hey, if you're here now, you KNOW what Dark Souls is. You KNOW what you're getting yourself into, and I say, more power to you, it's a heck of a game. Just know to watch for the jank.

Posted: July 21
Incredible game.

There are many things lacking, but that's actually what makes it so damn good. You are not spoon fed anything, and are left to figure things out on your own which really helps you appreciate DS1 when things start to click.

Blown away by how great this experience was and did not expect it one bit. You definitely need to adjust to how this game plays and the QOL things that are missing, but once you do, you are rewarded with very enjoyable and almost addictive gameplay.

The camera will be one of your worst enemies to begin with, and will get you killed multiple times, but doesn't take long to get the hang of it. The environments are beautiful and every bit of exploration is worth it in the end. You will die countless times to some really cheeky things, but end up laughing at the fact they actually just did that to you. Some areas are insanely punishing, but become one of the best parts of the game when you finally progress from them. The boss fights are great and while they are not the hardest, you can really appreciate how they play out and the nuances of each one. The Bed of Chaos fight was incredibly unique, and one of the more memorable of them all.

To think how much of a game changer this was on release and how well it still holds up today, you can really appreciate all DS1 has to offer. You won't be disappointed sinking some hours into this masterpiece.

I played through blind and sure, I might've missed a few things here and there, but I wouldn't change the experience that immense trial and error and figuring things out on your own gives you. Definitely will be back for another playthrough to explore the parts that were missed the first time around.

Highly recommend this game.

I highly recommend Dark Souls: Prepare to Die Edition because it has some of the most memorable bosses in gaming history. I’d put bosses like Ornstein and Smough, Knight Artorias, Manus, Father of the Abyss, and Gwyn, Lord of Cinder up against any game ever made. The level design is nonpareil — once you experience how interconnected and thoughtful the world is, you realize it may never be matched again. One of the best parts is the freedom to build your character however you want. You can use giant greatswords, fast curved blades, polearms, or magic — and there’s a wide range of iconic armor sets to match. Experimenting with weapons and armor is half the fun.

The game isn’t truly hard once you learn its patterns — but it does a terrible job explaining that you need to upgrade your weapons to keep up. Many players struggle because they don’t realize a leveled-up weapon does far more than a higher character level. Figure that out early, and the challenge feels fair and rewarding. A few areas are clearly unfinished (like Lost Izalith), but despite that, Dark Souls is a masterpiece of atmosphere, tension, and exploration. If you’re patient and curious, you’ll discover one of the greatest adventures gaming has to offer.

A dark-fantasy RPG, one of the best games ever made. The lore hits hard as it does not spoon-feed you what is going to happen--you have to listen to dialogues and read some of the weapon's description to formulate the story behind this particular event/character. Posted: July 17
(My playtime in this game is mostly on the original PC version, I have around 200 combined hours in this game and thousands if you include the rest of the series, Elden Ring, and Bloodborne)

Dark Souls 1 is possibly the most influential game of the last two decades (influential in a good way, I'm not including Fortnite or Genshin Impact). Sure, Demons' Souls came first, and I love that game dearly, but Dark Souls 1 is a much more refined experience while still managing to maintain an extremely high level of uniqueness and charm that I feel like the later games in the series lost a lot of, even if they're amazing games in their own right.

I think this game is best experienced with an open mind. This series has a bit of a reputation for difficulty but it's honestly way overblown. This game will certainly ask you to be attentive and learn from your mistakes and failures. You will certainly die multiple times in certain areas or against many different bosses. But as long as you keep learning, the actual skill required for this game is not that high.

If you're reading this and you're on the fence of buying it, I encourage you to give this game a go, it's definitely one of my all-time favorite games, and easily the best entry point into Soulslikes IMO.

I came to Dark Souls after beating Elden Ring ((yes i started with Elden Ring)). When I first started playing I really struggled adjusting to the much slower pace compared to Elden Ring. But after time, I got used to the flow of combat and exploration, and I can truthfully say this is an absolutely fantastic game.

The exploration and world design especially in the first half is phenomenal. The second half of the game kinda dips in quality for me ((Lost Izalith especially, it had so much potential but it just feels rushed and unfinished)). There are many areas that are incredibly difficult and annoying to navigate through, I had the most trouble with Tomb of the Giants. However that difficulty makes success all the more sweet.

After defeating Gwyn, I sat as the credits rolled and was filled with a feeling no other game has given me. It was a weird mix of accomplishment yet emptiness, which I think a game like Dark Souls can only do. It is an incredibly depressing and bleak world, and yet you continue on.

One really funny thing that I learned as I played this is that the areas getting to the bosses are much more difficult than the actual bosses themselves. There were multiple bosses that I beat on my first try.

Anyways, Dark Souls is an amazing game, and I highly recommend playing through it.
