Dark Souls is From Software’s first major hit within the now common genre that it defined. The iconic brutal, yet fair approach to gameplay seeps from every corner of this game, and it really is an experience worth having.

There’s something for most every type of player to enjoy here:

Explorers are going to love working their way though the story from Firelink to Gwyn. The lore is also really good. You can skip it and still have a blast, but I loved learning the little details through the environment and item descriptions. There’s also heaps of fan observations and head cannon content that really flows well with the game’s vibe.

Collectors will love the variety of equipment available for the player to try, and will no doubt have a bitter sweet time with the Knight’s Honour achievement.

Competitive players will be happy to learn that the online PvP community is still going strong, showing no signs of dying soon despite newer titles in the franchise. Just stick to levels 60-80 for faster matchmaking.

On the other hand, social and creative types won’t find much here. There’s no designing to do outside the character creator or picking out your drip, and conversations between players are limited to the classic, “Hello” - “Very Good” – “I’m Sorry” – “Help me” – “Thank you!”.

I have to take a moment to appreciate how interconnected most everything is in Lordran. The map is a masterpiece with locations that flow into one another so well. Everything feels so vast, but so connected. Taking that first trip on the Undead Church’s elevator and stepping out into firelink felt so good. Accordingly, the value per gb of storage is insane, the game folder takes up less than 7gb on my drive!

Taking another look at the difficulty, I’d say the learning curve is steep for sure, and in the beginning I’d even say it’s almost malicious toward a new player. The interface is thorough but immediately bamboozled me my first playthrough. Learning anything new can take time since most surprises will often kill you instantly the first time you stumble upon them. Even today, I crash the ♥♥♥♥ out when I get stun-locked or one tapped from a move I’ve never seen before or get catapulted out of bounds by some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ interaction with a Boss’ model near a cliff. I never felt like I was a capable force to reckon with, even at higher levels with the best kit. Dark Souls always takes the time to remind you, that despite being the “chosen undead” - You Are Nobody. But if you can keep it together there’s gold in them hills.

Dark Souls is fittingly steeped in a sort of philosophy regarding the nature of struggle and perseverance. It challenges the player to discover who they want be and how they will act in each moment to realize that person.

No doubt Dark Souls stands as a modern classic, and the remastered edition is the best way to experience it at the time of writing – at 60fps, in 1440P, with ultrawide support!

8.5/10 just... don’t you dare go hollow.

Dark Souls remains one of the most rewarding and masterfully crafted games I’ve ever played - even after multiple playthroughs. Its brutal challenge, intricate world design, and unforgettable boss fights continue to impress me years later. It’s not the hardest game out there, but it demands patience, strategy, and growth. Even now, bosses like Ornstein and Smough or Kalameet still put up a hell of a fight, and the satisfaction of toppling them never gets old. The game's tone - cold, bleak, and isolating - still cuts deep, conjuring that same eerie beauty I associate with Ico and Shadow of the Colossus (but darker). Lordran feels like a decaying purgatory, filled with cryptic NPCs and lurking nightmares. The interconnected world design is nothing short of genius. Every shortcut discovered still feels like unearthing a secret. And thanks to its nonlinear structure, each run offers new routes, builds, and challenges to explore. No matter how many times I return to it, Dark Souls continues to deliver. It’s more than just a great game - it’s a timeless experience that earns its reputation every time I pick it back up.

TLDR: Challenging yet rewarding gameplay and fantastic story and world design make this game an absolute must play. 10/10

My first experience with the Dark Souls series and Soulslike games in general was probably pretty similar to the average person, i had grown up hearing that Dark Souls was the pinnacle of game difficulty and it had this aura of mystique around it for being as hard as it was, with the knowledge that i would get my ass handed to me i first decided to try the game out during covid time as i was looking for new games at the time and hey why not try Dark Souls, i only ever made it as far as beating the Taurus Demon my first attempt at playing (the Hellkite Drake stopped me in my tracks) but something kept pulling me back to it over the years where each time i made it farther and farther, next attempt a few months later it was to the Fang Boar, the attempt after that was to Andre. This time, however, was different. When I returned to Dark Souls to give it my next go through, I decided to really try and lock in and understand the stats and mechanics thoroughly, and what I found shocked me.

I adore this game and this franchise.

For the sake of not rambling on and on forever, I'm going to divide my thoughts into 3 distinct sections. The first will be my thoughts on the remaster, the second, my thoughts on the gameplay, and last, my thoughts on the world-building/design.

Firstly, let's get the elephant in the room out of the way, this is not the best remaster out there. Starting with the positives, the game has seen an improvement from the original 720p in 30 FPS to anywhere from 1080p to upscaled 4k in 60 FPS depending on your hardware, I played in 4k running a smooth 60 frames with HDR enabled, and for a game released in 2011, it looks quite beautiful. The online play features have also seen some improvements to healing and the number of players during invasions/summons. Unfortunately my praise of the remaster ends there because they decided to keep everything else the same, now obviously i understand its a remaster and not a remake so i'm not expecting graphical overhauls or anything but i feel like some further quality of life improvements would have gone a long way in making the experiences as a whole more streamlined, the big things i noticed in my experience was things like quickly ascending ladders not being added or the fact that you have to specifically rest at a bonfire after warping to it if you'd like to respawn there after death, these things were in Dark Souls 3 and i think it would have gone a long way. I will not, however, excuse the lack of fixing the hitboxes (looking at you Titanite Demon).

On the subject of the gameplay in Dark Souls, it offers a feeling quite unlike any other game that came before it, Obviously, this makes sense considering how popular the game and entire genre became but that then begs the question. What about it is so captivating? Well consider the fact that Dark Souls is as much of an easy game as it is an impossible one, the gameplay and items/spells are designed in a way that rewards straightforward pushing with incredibly tough encounters that leave you feeling intensely frustrated as you seethe (haha seath lol) in rage over every second of that runback, and when you do finally beat them you feel like you could eat up all the worlds problems and ask for seconds. But also if brute force isn't your style, try farming for more souls, try casting aural decoy or hidden body, try buying titanite shards and upgrading your weapon, try buying pine resins for buffs, try equipping different rings and armor, all of these things are ways to tip the scales heavily in your favor and it also rewards the player for using their head, Dark Souls has gained a reputation for treating it's players with respect in it's design and it is shines through here. There is NO hand holding in Dark Souls and in a gaming industry filled with dumb unskippable tutorials, Dark Souls feels like it values your intelligence and intuition as a player. This also has the side effect of causing many difference unique experiences among players, we've all had the feeling of tearing through a boss on one save and then being tormented by said same boss on a different save because we're running a different build or didn't do the same prep as before, you say to yourself "this boss is easy i should be able to beat it like i did before!" and throw yourself at it again and again, eventually winning and feeling the triumph all over again. There are definitely some problems when it comes to the gameplay but I've been able to mostly overlook them, Things like only being able to roll in 4 directions and the previously mentioned hitbox issues are the only real problems I can name although I'd personally prefer a bit faster of a mid roll. :p

Onto the world design of Dark Souls, which I can confidently say is my favorite aspect of the game. When I first played the game I never would have expected it to be the type of game to have deep, theory filled lore but I was sorely mistaken. The story is one that is not explained outright to you, but like everything else in this game, pieces are given to you throughout and you are expected to place them together yourself. The main narrative sees you taking the side of the gods in an effort to preserve the fading age of fire although i love how that isn't all there is to it, beating the Four Kings before placing the Lordvessel reveals Darkstalker Kaathe who provides an alternate context to the same narrative you've been hearing throughout the game and allows you to recolor all of your thoughts on what you see, Gwyn goes from being this heroic lord of sunlight who sacrificed himself to save the world as we know it to a disheveled old god who feared the power of the dark so much that he cursed humanity with undeath and the branding of the darksign. I wont go into detail on all of them but i will say that i feel positively about every single area in the game from a design perspective, some of my favorites being Ash Lake, The Lost Izalith, Blighttown, and the Catacombs, playing through the areas themselves can be a different story depending on the build but no matter what i will always adore what the design team was able to accomplish conceptually and visually, descending a giant tree onto a beach of Ash overlooking an endless ocean of archtrees? Hell yes. An underground city (and potentially former swamp :0) completely taken over by the chaos flame and the demons from within it? Sign me up. Every environment from the city of Anor Londo to the painted world within it oozes atmosphere and charm in ways that I haven't seen done by any other game series. Not every area in the game in equally as fun to play unfortunately, The Lost Izalith and Tomb of the Giants specifically feel like they did not have enough time to accomplish everything they had planned for them, i see consistent themes of pretty generic level geometry and spam of one or two very difficult enemy types, evidence of enemies like crag hollows in the files shows that they had planned to alleviate some of these issues but most likely had to cut it before the final release of the game. One area that i do feel went especially underutilized was New Londo Ruins, the ability to go down there immediately made it incredibly foreboding on my first time through and now that i'm very familiar with it, i wish there were a bit more to do there, i like the ghosts as enemies and the whole vibe of the city itself and i would have like to have seen maybe a bit more recognizable landmark ruins aside from just the 2 ghost houses.

My experience with Dark Souls has been amazing overall, ask me at any one time while I'm playing and I'll probably tell you white gritting my teeth that I hate it and its mother and everything it stands for but I know in my heart that I adore everything about this game.

The true getting good was the jolly cooperation we had along the way. :)

I've beaten this game 3 times now. Here is my take:

The first half of this game (up until unlocking fast travel) is perfect, amazing, and infinitely replayable.
Somehow, with each new run, my entire life becomes about dark souls. The lore, the community, the builds, the secrets; it's enthralling. I will be playing this game for the rest of my life.

However, the latter half of this game is grueling, tiresome and a literal list of chores and marathons. A part of this has to do with absurdly cheap bosses and tediously long run back times.

After unlocking fast travel, something about the charm of this game is lost. In the beginning, you are constantly having to think about where you are in this large interconnected world and where the next bonfire is (or may be). This aspect makes dark souls the most immersive experience I've ever had in a video game. Then it changes... Perhaps it's being given the power to teleport, and losing this sense of scale while being encouraged to sit through a loading screen to get to any area you want. Perhaps its that you never feel the same sense of accomplishment after conquering the behemoth that is Anor Londo. Whatever the case may be, by this point in the game, I'm done.

Regardless of whether you stop playing after O&S and obtain the largest of chests, or you see it through to the end and inevitably watch videos on how to parry Gwyn, this game is an absolute masterpiece that everyone should have the chance to experience.

Don't give up. Don't go hollow.

It’s Dark Souls, what more is there to say? I don’t really need to explain how it looks or plays – everyone knows what they're getting into here.

That being said, I came to it without any nostalgia, because I played it in 2020 for the first time so the charm didn’t hit as hard, and honestly, without some guidance on where to go next, I probably would’ve lost motivation early on. The game’s structure is brutal if you're not used to it with no hints on where to go etc.

It actually took me three tries to get through it – but I finally did it, and even did all achievements. And yeah… of course it was fun in the end. It’s a Souls game. You suffer for three hours, hate everything, then finally beat the boss and suddenly love the game again. That cycle is weirdly addictive.

Still, Elden Ring was way more my thing personally – I connected with it more overall. But I’m glad I stuck with Dark Souls Remastered, because despite everything, I genuinely enjoyed the journey. (Also you kinda fall in love with the map layout, that everything is connected in some way, thats pretty cool)

There's nothing I can say about the scion of a generation-defining genre that hasn't already been said. Demon's Souls crawled so that Dark Souls could run so that Elden Ring could fly... while Blood Borne sat quietly in the corner, rocking back and forth, quietly muttering about 'beasts' being all over 'the shop' to itself.

It's hard to call this a 'Remaster' though; definitely not in the same vein as the recent Oblivion one. Some bugs got fixed; this is good and bad. Everything's still pretty much the same for the most part with some minor graphical changes here and there—fog gates in particular. I only bought this because I wanted to re-experience Dark Souls after a massive Nightreign binge (looking at you, Gaping Dragon) and, to be frank, it's not let me down. Just wait for it to be on sale; Bandai usually do one towards the end of the year and you'll pick it up for buttons.

It's a masterpiece. If you've never played it, you should. Yes, it's showing its age somewhat, but like your first car it's not about the looks, it's about the experience.

Dark Souls should be everyone's first car.

However, I've found out that with the occasional looking things up on the internet (there is a lot of good information out there), grinding levels (it is surprisingly fun to kill enemies near a bonfire, return to the bonfire to heal, and repeat) and taking it slow & steady (to not get scared too often), the game is significantly more fun than I expected! And so, far the past week, I keep coming back to the game for a couple of hours every now and then until I've had enough tense moments for that day.

The world of DS1 is surprisingly immersive and wondrous, the enemies are varied (and I've only rung the 1st bell so far) and there's always something new around the corner. I't's very likely I'll finish DS1 in the coming weeks & months.

An amazing game that is definitely worth your time. People who don't play Ds1 because it is "clunky" or "outdated" or "the hit boxes are bad" really missed out.

What sets Ds1 apart from the other souls-games is that this game (mostly) doesn't test your reflexes by sending out lightspeed attacks, causing the player to often panic roll.

Meanwhile, Ds1 (mostly) has slow, well-choreographed, that doesn't show WHEN, but WHERE and HOW. This puts a great emphasis on strategy, patience and how you approach the game.

The graphics may look old but it fits the vibe. It feels mysterious, dark and sometimes, gorgeous even.

Ds1 also has a very good game and map design, the map felt intricately woven, with everything interconnecting together and mind-blowing me when I find a shortcut. It felt real, you had to know your way around as you only got teleporting mid-way through the game, and still you can only teleport to a few specific bonfires.

Great, fun, iconic and memorable bosses *cough* *cough* (not you, bed of chaos) *cough* I love Ornstein and Smough, also Artorias.

I can see what Miyazaki was picturing in his head, and I say that it turned out very good (2nd half was rushed due to deadlines, but if I wasn't told that then I wouldn't have noticed)

Apart from some annoying enemy types and their placement, Ds1 is great from beginning to end.
The achievements were a pain to get.

Enjoyed it a lot.
Praise the sun \[T]/☀️
-Sunbro

I have thrown two controllers in my adult life. Once during Gears of War 3 multiplayer against a team of people teleporting via the power of potato internet and once during Demon's Souls after dying from an ambush and realizing I needed to grind souls to buy healing items again for the 20th time. Dark souls may not be the first "souls" game but it's the first one to not make you lose max health upon death and the first to give you a rechargeable healing flask so you can keep the punishing gameplay without it feeling like you paid $60 for CBT in PS3 disc form. For this reason, I think this is the oldest souls-like I would recommend to those with no nostalgia for it.

I just replayed this for the first time in 10+ years and it was still so damn fun. Chunky sword and board combat, iconic levels, incredible atmosphere, a great sense of discovery, and the trademark punishing cycle of dropping all unused souls when you die. Rediscovering forgotten bosses was amazing and I was genuinely started by some traps and ambushes. This game is so good that it set a standard that for better or worse has become a go-to template for anyone making something with third person melee combat.

With that said, my criticisms of this game are still mostly unchanged from my initial playthrough:





These cons add up to nearly requiring you to look up where to go and how things work online so you don't waste your time or hard earned resources on things that don't matter. I promise once you understand the systems and you're moving in the right direction, it's a hell of a game that holds up all these years later.

I believe every gamer that is interested in swords & sorcery or challenges should play this game at least once. I've been hooked since I played the original on XBOX 360.

The most difficult part of DARK SOULS is the first few hours where the game is nonverbally teaching you how to play by punishing your mistakes harshly. I originally thought this game was some busted PS2 bargain bin game until the combat and movement clicked in my head.

First time players should take it slow, be patient, and persevere. I promise that the payoff is worth it. There is interesting lore, fun loot to discover, and beautiful locations. Some of the sounds and graphics are a little rough around the edges but I think it still has a lot of character.

The achievements could be a little more fun to do. You need to beat the game 2.5 times in order to get all of the achievement without cheats or exploits. Be prepared for around ten hours of grinding for item drops in order to accomplish them all.

There is a highly active player base still so you can fully take advantage of the multiplayer features. Just be careful and maybe avoid being online in the early areas because there are trolls who invade newbies to grief them. There are also some hackers making the game difficult for folks so you might want to research that and use the mods that prevent the hackers from ruining your game.

Posted: July 5
Timeless. I knew the entire layout of the world by the end of the game, all thanks to the top-tier world design. I don't think that a game has done it this well before or even since.

The worst the game has to offer is:
There are rare examples of bosses / boss arenas ranging from pathetically weak to lazy design.
There is one example of a particular zone that Miyazaki even apologised for. Justly so.

The bad made up such a tiny amount of the experience that it almost stands out more, but has never deterred yet ANOTHER new character, ANOTHER new game.

Every step up to and including Anor Londo is the peak of Souls-like gaming. I've completed the game four times at the time of this review, but it has stayed installed ever since my first run, and it will stay installed forever. Never know when that itch will re-emerge, and I'll be fighting alongside Solaire, and saving Siegmeyer once again.

Our fates do seem entwined, don't they?

Playing this game was a really fun and relaxing experience. The exploration is extremely rewarding, for example when you discover a blacksmith or when you realize how some of the levels are linked to the central hub. This game is not perfect, but it is the best game the trilogy.

The combat is quite slow and the enemies attacks are extremely well telegraphed with reasonably sized hit boxes. Generally, the combat is very intuitive. Indeed, with very little time and practice it is easy enough learn the patterns. Every boss is unique and memorable, although some assets were reused it did not feel annoying. I would even say that the DLC bosses are the best boss fights in the entire trilogy.

This game is an absolute masterpiece and I would recommend it to everyone. Also, do not be alarmed by the difficulty. Nowdays, there are a lot of ressources online to help you and you can also ask a question.

Posted: July 4
A beautiful, if flawed masterpiece. Dipping my toe into the franchise with Bloodbourne had me wanting to go back and try the older games. The connected world, the music, the design, the mechanics and the NPCs are all memorable, and even since finishing the play through all I can think of is what my next build will be.
That isn't to say the game is perfect. I refuse to call Lost Izalith anything other than an irritating eyesore, the camera is it's own boss fight throughout most the game, and some of the obtuseness in mechanics like the Curse status, equip load and anything in the multiplayer is unforgivable and actively made me enjoy the game less. The hit-boxes could also have used a second round, as they, objectively, are jank.

Despite this negativity though, I have been unable to stop thinking about Dark Souls since finishing it. I have spent hours on YouTube watching challenge runs from Backlogs, and others of that ilk, seeing all the intricacies that the game has to offer. I want nothing more than to play the game again and again right now, because yes, I felt I needed blue-light glasses to survive the Bed of Chaos fight, but the feeling of beating O+S after trying for an hour is beyond any gaming experience of recent memory.

You start as a nobody, less than a nobody. You try desperately to push forward, but even the weakest skeleton, the smallest rat is enough to kill you. But you keep going, holding on to your last bit of humanity. Then you realise that you’re not alone. You meet many others on your path- some a shell of their former selves; others, like you, cling desperately to a faint hope. As everyone inevitably falls around you, you keep pushing on. You feel the pain after each death, the trembling hands after each victory. Through practice and persistence, you defeat all that stand in your way. Eventually, you begin to face godlike creatures of incredible proportions, and though they initially obliterate you, you beat them too. That's when you finally understand. The game doesn’t want to hurt you with its obstacles. It wants to give you the satisfaction of overcoming them. The game is not about killing all the bosses and reaching the ending, it’s about the journey you take to get there. Don’t let its difficulty discourage you. Keep pushing forward. Because how can the destination be so sweet without the hardships that precede it?

Dark Souls Remastered is my favorite entry in the series. While it may feel slower compared to Dark Souls III, that deliberate pace is exactly what makes it so special. The game doesn’t want you to charge in recklessly it teaches you to approach every enemy and boss with care, patience, and strategy. Victory isn’t earned through aggression, but through persistence and understanding.

The interconnected world design is simply unparalleled. Every shortcut, every hidden path, every descent into darkness feels purposeful and rewarding. The magic of Dark Souls Remastered lies in this truth: if you stay patient and determined, victory will come. so i wholeheartly recommend dark souls remastered whether you are new or didn't play it but played other souls games!

One piece of advice before you begin: don’t start with Dark Souls III. Begin with dark souls Remastered. Dark Souls III was crafted as a homecoming, a farewell to the world of the first game. If you skip ahead, you’ll miss the emotional weight, the echoes of past battles, and the full impact of its legacy.

I would recommend anyone who wants to start with the Souls series to play either this game or, if possible, the original Demon's Souls first. DS1 has restrictions that are not as prominent in the other parts (harsher equipment load, more expensive leveling, more upgrade materials to farm etc.), which are both beloved and hated. The atmosphere and the first traversed levels are an amazing experience, even with all the flaws one could point out.

After Anor Londo, the lategame relies heavily on the atmosphere and is very mediocre gameplay-wise (while Lost Izalith is frustrating, the title of the most boring and worst area in DS1 goes to Duke's Archives).

The DLC has great bosses, but I couldn't enjoy the levels in there very much, except for the Abyss.

Since this game made me replay it multiple times and even try out various challenge runs, it gets an 8/10 from me.

Considered by many to be one of the greatest games ever made and honestly it's hard to disagree, don't let this fool you into thinking it's perfect though because brother it is far from perfect.
This game is unapologetic, you will die and you will die a lot, it really forces you to play the way the game wants you to play. This game wants to teach you, it wants to teach you how to be patient, not get greedy, learn the boss, fully commit to your attacks, and keep persevering . Many tasks seem impossible at first, you won't know where to go, bosses seem too difficult, levels are a bit tricky to understand but in time if you keep throwing yourself at whatever it is you will find a way, and honestly this kind of mindset has helped me a lot in my day to day life. A lot of people would consider that bad game design, I know I did at first.
I mentioned earlier that this game isn't perfect so i'll get to that here. Dark Souls 1 is a clunky game, it doesn't explain anything to you, movement feels stiff, weapon attacks lack oomph, The devs also made some odd choices like the curse status effect which halves your HP and you need to buy an item to remove it, no fast travel so you have to walk your stubby little legs everywhere, pretty atrocious runback on some bosses and on that note some pretty bad bosses. Dark Souls 1 also suffers the curse of FromSoft, they didn't have the time and resources to finish some stuff in the last half of the game leading to areas like Lost Izalith and Tomb of Giants.
With all that said this game still manages to deliver some of the greatest experiences I have ever had while playing video games, that euphoric feeling of getting through an area after struggling for so long is something I wish to feel for every game I play. Dark Souls 1 is more than a game, it's an experience.

It doesn’t invite you in. It leaves you stranded at the edge of a dying world and forces you to claw your way forward. Every encounter is earned. Every inch of progress is a struggle. And yet, nothing about it feels unfair. It teaches through punishment, guides through failure, and rewards only those who refuse to give up.

The remastered visuals give new weight to an already legendary setting. Lordran feels ancient, broken, and haunted. Sunlight pierces fog like a memory of warmth. The ruins are not just places but monuments to lost purpose. The world is dense, connected, and terrifying in its silence.

Combat is pure discipline. There is no room for laziness. Every enemy can kill you, and every weapon feels meaningful. Mastery doesn’t come from stats or shortcuts but from understanding movement, distance, timing. Boss fights are tests of everything you’ve learned, and winning them is unforgettable.

The story hides beneath the surface. There are no cutscenes to hold your hand. You must read between the lines, listen to cryptic whispers, and piece together a shattered past. What emerges is one of the deepest, most tragic mythologies ever created in a game.

Failure is constant. But with each death, you adapt. You learn. You grow. And when you finally triumph, the satisfaction is unmatched. It feels real because it is earned.

Dark Souls Remastered is not just a remaster of a classic. It is a reminder of what games can be when they demand more. It is brutal, honest, and completely unforgettable.

DARK SOULS REVIEW

Dark Souls is a challenging game with a pretty twisted worldbuilding. The choice to hurt or befriend NPCs can have severe consequences, to the point that it can make your journey 10x harder. Overall, the game has difficulty and pretty engaging combat despite being 14 years old. The atmosphere is also lonely, like you have nobody you can trust, and the very people you think may seem very harmless and strong either die or become hollowed and lose humanity. It feels like the only person you can have any ounce of trust in is yourself.

Gameplay



The game being this old and having probably some of the most fun combat systems has a learning curve on dodging at the right time, parrying, and understanding your boss' movements, also to pray you don't fall to any cracks (looking at you, bed of chaos). I had a blast and faced frustration with bosses that distracted me with skeletons while trying to kill Nito or fighting off Ornstein and Smough. I begged God that I had enough flask for their 2nd phase. For a game this old, this shows that games from several years ago still age well in this modern age. Although there are still poor gameplay mechanics like the fact that you get fast travel in the 2nd half of the game because of some lore reason, it is pretty dumb in my personal opinion. I also disliked that you get consequences for defeating bosses like Bed of Chaos, which unfortunately gets rid of some pretty good pyromancy skills that could help you throughout the mid-end game. Then again, it shows that this game ENCOURAGES YOU HEAVILY to explore and get better in your current environment to find any good equipment. The boss fights were engaging, and I've had fun learning and overcoming their movements. It felt rewarding whenever I defeated a boss. But I honestly didn't have much trouble with many bosses; some were easy after you learn how they work, but some bosses are complete BS and are hard to navigate and manage your environment like Ornstein and Smough. Still, even then, they just made me get better at the game with dodging.




The end theme is just gorgeous, "Nameless song" is just so damn majestic and is rewarding after going through so much BS into the game. Souls of Fire is also lovely to listen to, and I like plenty of the OSTs in this game. Sometimes, I stop playing and listen to the OST. It's wonderful, this game playlist has some of my favorite OSTs that I listen to right after beating the game.




Overall, this game is a must-play. Even if you dislike challenging games, summons can be helpful if you're struggling with a boss for a very long time (like days), but even then, I genuinely believe you should fight these bosses solo because it feels so satisfying, like you're on top of the world. I can't wait to play Dark Souls 2 and 3. The game is beautiful overall, and I know this is a remaster, but it looks gorgeous as a remaster and as a game made 14 years ago, from the OST to the quality made here. Sometimes, I wish many companies took notes on Dark Souls with the attention to detail, great worldbuilding, and such gorgeous OSTs.

Dark Souls: Remastered is a punishing yet captivating action RPG that serves as a solid entry point for newcomers to the soulslike genre. With its oppressive atmosphere, labyrinthine level design, and deep combat systems, the game challenges players while offering freedom and replayability. However, some design choices—such as punishing backtracking and a reliance on trial-and-error—may feel unfair or outdated to certain players.

Pros:

+ The dark, oppressive world builds tension constantly, immersing players with a mysterious and haunting tone that feels unique and compelling.
+ Despite its reputation, the game introduces mechanics progressively, helping newcomers adapt to the genre without feeling overwhelmed right away.
+ At the start, difficulty feels earned and deaths feel like the player's responsibility, creating a rewarding gameplay loop.
+ Whether you prefer brute force, magic, strategy, or even cheesing encounters, the game supports multiple approaches to victory.
+ The open-ended map is dense with shortcuts, secrets, and nonlinear exploration, rewarding players for understanding the environment.
+ The abundance of secrets, hidden mechanics, and interpretive storytelling helped foster a passionate community that thrives on shared discoveries.
+ Weight, stamina, parries, dual-wielding, and magic all offer layers of tactical complexity.
+ Both players and enemies can be killed quickly, creating high stakes that reward caution and planning.
+ Items don’t burden inventory space unless equipped, removing frustrating inventory management.
+ Adapting your build to suit specific areas or bosses can be both fun and rewarding.
+ The soul-based progression system keeps players constantly on edge, especially when risking a large number of souls.
+ If you're struggling, you can always grind to upgrade or learn more optimal strategies—optional but available.
+ A clear but impactful player choice determines the ending, with New Game+ offering a more difficult second playthrough.


- In later areas and New Game+, the game inflates difficulty via stat boosts rather than smarter enemy design, which can feel cheap.
- Having to retread long distances just to retry a boss battle adds unnecessary tedium and frustration.
- The world, while rich, can be disorienting, and often requires external guides to avoid missing crucial items or paths.
- Storytelling is vague and relies heavily on item descriptions and community theories, which may alienate players who prefer clear narratives.
- Many mechanics, traps, and enemy placements are designed to kill you once so you learn for next time, which may feel unfair.
- The deliberate combat and movement system demand patience, especially when animations lock the player into actions.
- Playing offline means missing a portion of the game’s unique player-to-player interactions, like messages and invasions.
- With limited materials, upgrading multiple weapons or experimenting with gear is difficult without excessive grinding.
- Dodge rolls offer low invincibility frames, making it less effective for players with slower reflexes.
- Losing all your souls permanently after dying twice can be extremely discouraging and stressful.



*Rating from lowest to highest: C, B, A, S.

My review will probably get criticised by hardcore fans of the franchise but let me explain. I haven't played the original Dark Souls neither the remaster on the past, it was literally my first playthrough and i actually had no idea how to navigate through the game or where to go at all, i was clueless completely so my point of judgement is strictly how i experienced this game as a new player in every way and form. DARK SOULS started for me really strong and I fairly enjoyed the first hour of it until I started realising that there is no guidance on where I needed to go as a new player then it started to fell apart in my eyes really quicky. Long story short I spended a good 2-3 hours on parts of the map that I should not be at as a new clueless player (Catacombs, Ruins etc) I couldn't realise that there was a dark entrance uphill to enter basically the first area which a new player supposed to be at.

For the gameplay itself. Combat in general is super basic but it's understandable for a game of this age,i have played plenty of older games no matter the genre that they felt way better than this but whatever i will just say that this game has the benefit that is old and let it be. You can definitely recognise that you play a souls game developed by From Software even if the combat is simple and straightforward, even the newer Souls games sharing almost everything that this game has to offer, its just that obviously the newer games plays and feel much more refined and solid compared to this. Oh boy let's talk about bonfires..well to put it simple, at times you can play an hour or more without managing to find a new one to rest. If you get killed as a new player that everything is new to you good luck finding your way back through all the enemies to get your souls back or even having a chance to do so. All the areas apart from Anor Londo that was the exception was the worst I had ever played in any game in general, old or new. The amount of unnecessary hustle to traverse through the areas and find your way through them to get to the bosses was the worst in any game I have ever played. The message You Died won't be because the boss you are facing is difficult. 99% of the time it will be because you are trying to survive the infinite map banana peals that are everywhere because Miyazaki enjoys to see you on your breaking point. Sure now that I played and finished the game I can actually navigate much easier since i know where to go or what to do but the whole point is that me as a new player on the game I had a very bad and unpleasent time 90% of the entire game while navigating and there wont be a second playthrough. There wasn't a single part during my playthrough that i actually felt that i need to get good so to speak to beat a boss, there wasn't a single part on my playthrough that i felt a single feeling of accomplishment, it was all about what nonsense the game will do to get me slow down on my progress by having me dieing from something silly like in which part of the map my character will step up on a banana peal and get sended into the shadow realm and how long it will take for me to navigate through every nonsense to get back to the point i fell off or how much the game design will test my patience with this terrible design which made the game extremely unpleasent and postponed the total play time which shouldn't exceed the 10h mark based on the raw ingame content but yet again it took me 32h to complete which felt like forever. In my entire playthrough i was mostly on my personal edge to uninstall the game multiple times or feeling mad because my character fell off the map for the 100nth time because i stepped on invisible obstacles that acting like your character step up on oil while i was falling off the map and having me trying to navigate back to where i was from the bonfire that was 1 mile away only to step on another imaginary obstacle and repeat the process until i "succeed". Dont get me started talking about Blight Town and how terrible this area was. I ended up there exactly after the Gaping Dragon boss fight and lost a bit over 45000 souls which at that time of my playthrough was worth like a milion for me and my character since i was really low levels and the difficulty spike was basically 3x the damage on my character which was also cursed from the basilisks during the sewers session and there was no bonfire near the entrance nor after the boss fight to at least level up my character. I just went there after the boss fight and tried to navigate through the gate and down the ladder searching for the bonfire blindly without knowing the difficulty spike i would face nor the area i was going at. The bonfire btw was in the middle of Blight Town on a bridge which was really really far away and i had absolutely no chance to save these souls and level up as a new player. Sure i could technically go back on the bonfire that wasn't far away before the Gaping Dragon boss fight but i literally couldn't knew the difficulty spike nor what Blight Town as an area to navigate through was. Let's talk a bit about bosses. All of the bosses were super easy and they was extremely disappointing as individual fights to the point that curtain enemies was actually waaaaaay more difficult to beat than the main bosses themselves, mostly the bosses had 2 or 3 telegraphed moves and they was getting clapped in literally 5 to10 hits seconds after the fight start. Sure its not a bad thing to have easy bosses but people was praising over the years how much better old school Dark Souls bosses were and yeah well the truth is that they were garbage bruh nothing more, move on. Literally most of the bosses took me just one try to beat while i had absolutely no clue about their attack pattern, turns out there is no pattern nor a moveset, just keep your distance and let them do something go close swing 2 times and move away until they repeat a move, you won't even need to use your estus literally, minus some cheesy silly bosses that needed a specific pattern to beat them like Bed of Chaos which took 6 tries since i had no idea what to do (Worst boss in the gaming history btw or at least the worst boss I encountered in any game I cared to play). Posted: June 23
9/10.
Dark Souls has a reputation that continues today for being unapologetically difficult.
This has grown to the point where journalists compared anything difficult to it, others tried to imitate it's design philosophies, and Soulslikes are a genre today the same way Doom set the standard for the FPS genre decades ago.

The truth is? It lives up to it, if you don't know what you're doing.
If you've been curious about the game, there's three things you need to know before going in:
- Shields specifically in this game are very useful, to the point where they make strength playthroughs a go-to for newcomers
- Much like Resident Evil, not every fight needs to be taken right away. Take the Zweihander from the graveyard behind Firelink Shrine after the tutorial, and level strength with all your souls until you can dual-wield it. It's slow but it's range and ability to stagger even boss with it's heavy attack make it perfect all game.
- Pyromancy is great as it's not bound to a stat, but don't sleep on sorceries either; I don't give a crap if tryhards think 'your run doesn't count', you're here to enjoy a game and not to blue-ball yourself.

Oh, and you don't know where you're going? That's the point. Dark Souls' design philosophy is the antithesis of The Legend of Zelda games; Back when DS1 released, they guided you at each step, ensure you knew what you're doing, made their designs semi-linear to match this and gave hints at each turn.
Dark Souls intentionally leaves you feeling lost - Lead creator Hidetaka Miyazaki stated that: “Our goal was to allow players to do what they want, define their own goals, make their own discoveries, embrace their own values, and find their own interpretations.” It's intended for you to look at the lore for items with their own stories, explore the beaten paths, find hidden secrets and rewards you handsomely for it.

During the whole adventure, I've usually possesed the Black Knight Sword, the Grass Crest Shield, Mask of the Child, the Ring of Favour and Protection and usually Havel's ring, plus some random light or heavy armour set. Indeed, my build couldn't make my journey any easier, and everything went smooth and manageable, until I met the final lord boss, Gwyn the Lord of Cinder, where all of my missed difficulties piled up on a final point. Posted: June 22
My second stop on the journey through the souls series, this was my first jaunt through this game and wow what an enjoyable ride it was! But I cannot help but feel it was lacking so much of the charm and awesome stuff that dark souls 2 had! I thoroughly enjoyed the content and found it fun start to finish, its a much faster game than 2 though, the combat, and the movements of my own character, this game even took me half the time 2 did!

Class Mechanics
Sorcerer came back again for this playthrough just like it happened in 2, this games spells felt insanely powerful! I was 3-5 shotting most bosses I fought, notably artorius died in 2 CSS and 2 SS. I think the spell diversity in this game is kind of mediocre, due to the ridiculous cast times on heavy soul arrows neither it nor GHSA felt good to use at all, leaving me with essentially my soul spears to contend with. I couldn't even use the spells I wanted to fill out the whole attunement slots though due to this games very strict amounts of spells...

The bosses in this game are very memorable design wise, combat wise I can't say they were all that however... This game had its fair share of pretty boring and just annoying bosses or boss runbacks, Capra demons runback was terrible..!! You'd think they'd learn their lesson on putting dogs in the boss runback sections but nope, three dogs on the way to kalameet!!! -.- Manus was a standout in this game as the first boss to really challenge me in my playthroughs of DS1 and DS2 with DLC! He is fast and rewarded learning his moves a ton! He also had HP and Defenses to make him not just melt to a few soul spears, It took me 3 Dark beads, 2 CSS, and 4 SS to beat him, and thats with the best catalyst (tin crystalizatoin), dusk crown, AND bellowing dragoncrest ring... this guys a tank!! this build and setup mulched most other bosses before they could do much of anything though. I appreciate this game as a baseline for what came after it, despite its kind of boring in gameplay bosses.

I beat manus, and then gwyn at a very low level of 76, at least it felt low! I was 50 INT, 50 ATT, and somewhere around 9 VIT, light rolling once more this made the game feel pretty risky but also pretty forgiving in regards to how I could dodge and when the openings were, which were all usually big enough to cast a CSS into whatever baddie I was up against. The Tin Crystalization Catalyst ended up being my catalyst of choice because the damage it put out when stacked with the other sorcery bonuses is seriously crazy!!

This games zones were big and confusing, blighttown, the catacombs, and the tomb of giants seriously had me pulling my hair out trying to get to their bosses!! It did not help that Nito was a serious beast!! I died a few times to him for sure! I think the enemy density was pretty chill and never overwhelming, except for the catacombs -.- stupid respawning skeletons!! The depths confused me for a while.. and I remember being seriously mald about the lost izalith runback to bed of chaos... BUT in all the zones are beautiful and interesting! I think DS2 has better zones though..

I think that Dark Souls is a great game, and one that everyone should check out if they dig the souls playstyle that elden ring has! It's slow going, but the combat is fun, the exploration feels fun and rewarding, that is one thing that replaying these older two titles has made me realize elden ring lost with it's open world, the nature of shortcuts and intuitiveness that dark souls brings in its first two installments are game defining for them! If I were to rank the games I've played thus far I would put it as Elden Ring / DS2, then DS1, and I look forward to seeing how much I enjoy or dislike DS3 having played it's two prior siblings!

Really can't recommend the game. Many negatives:
- First playthrough feels extremely tedious and unfun.
- Easiest souls-like game I've played.
- When you die, it's usually with ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ deaths, especially on a first playthrough. Especially if you don't know the gimmick of that level (catacomb skeleton respawn, Sen's Fortress traps) or if it's your first time with some easy-death encounters (archers in Anor Londo).

- As a new player, you need far more information than what's explained in the game. E.g. how to use magic and their different types, stats and builds because you can't respec, information on how to progress and where is good to go next, etc.
- Being unable to use mods offline without potentially being banned is a real shame.

Most unfun by far of all souls-like games I've played. It's okay if you ignore the negatives. Though I've enjoyed other games like Lies of P substantially more in pretty much every aspect.

If you're just starting your journey with soulslike games, Dark Souls is the perfect entry point. Even though the game is several years old, it can still grip you from the very beginning and won’t let go until the credits roll.
It’s not a title that holds your hand. Dark Souls teaches humility. Every death is a lesson, every failure brings you closer to mastery, and even enemies you fought an hour ago can still surprise you. But that’s exactly where its beauty lies.
Once you finish it for the first time, it’s easy to fall in love with the series. The moment you realize how the world of Lordran is intricately connected, how shortcuts bring you back to places you’ve already visited you feel like an explorer in a land full of secrets. And the lore? It's wonderful. Dark. Hidden. Nothing is handed to you. You have to piece it together by listening to NPCs and reading item descriptions. But once it clicks, you start uncovering layers you never even imagined were there.
The controls in the remastered version hold up surprisingly well even today. Compared to modern hits like Elden Ring or God of War, Dark Souls might seem more modest… but it makes up for that with deep, thoughtful design. The world feels like a sprawling labyrinth everything connects, and discovering new paths brings joy few other games can match.

Posted: June 21
Dark Souls is one of the most frustrating games I’ve ever played and also one of the most rewarding. At first, I had no idea what I was doing. The game doesn’t explain much, and it throws you into this dark, depressing world where even basic enemies can kill you if you're not careful. But weirdly, that’s part of what makes it so great.

The atmosphere is amazing, haunting and beautiful in a ruined kind of way. And the world design? Absolutely brilliant. Everything is connected in a way that just makes sense the more you explore. Combat feels heavy and precise, and every boss fight feels like a real accomplishment when you finally win.

Honestly, I really enjoyed the first half of the game areas like Undead Burg, Darkroot Garden, and Anor Londo were challenging but felt fair and exciting to explore. But the second half felt a bit rushed and more frustrating than fun at times. Some zones just didn’t have the same care in design, and a few late game bosses felt kind of underwhelming or annoying.

Yeah, it’s hard. Sometimes brutally so. And I get why people bounce off it. But if you stick with it, there’s nothing quite like that feeling when things finally click. It’s a game that respects your time and effort, even if it doesn't always show it kindly. Not perfect, but honestly, it’s a masterpiece in its own rough, unforgiving way.

It's actually kinda crazy how even more than a decade later and with all the bells and whistles of modern gaming, no company has managed to recapture the magic that is spending several hours in one location (Blighttown) and almost losing the will to live, only for you to seamlessly find yourself back in Firelink Shrine with enough Toxic in your body to alchemically conjure a LOL player. It's a game that really does test your patience, even more so than later From titles due to the lack of refined dodging and movement, but it's not so blisteringly difficult in it's core design that you won't be able to adapt to those flaws.

Dark Souls Remastered – A Survival Guide for Masochists!
Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

1. You start the game.
2. You create your character. The illusion of control feels great. It’s fake.
3. You step out of the first area. Reality hits like a brick wall.
4. You realize that every enemy has a personal vendetta against your existence.
5. You die.
6. You try a new strategy.
7. You die faster.
8. You learn patience.
9. You learn patience is useless.
10. You learn patience is actually useful.
11. You die.
12. You start loving the suffering.

Dark Souls Remastered is the perfect example of a game that punishes and rewards you equally. If you crave real satisfaction from progress, Lordran awaits—with open arms… and swords.

🔥 12/10, recommended for anyone who thinks they’re tough! (Don't play if you have rage issues)

Dark Souls – A Review by Donald J. Trump

Let me tell you, Dark Souls—some people say it’s the hardest game ever made. And they’re right. It’s tough. Really tough. Maybe the toughest game ever. But guess what? I beat it. I beat it bigly. Most people can’t even get past the first skeleton. Sad!

Now, the graphics? Not the best. Not like Trump Tower, which is beautiful, gold everywhere, amazing views. But the atmosphere—tremendous. Very dark, very gloomy, very dramatic. Like Washington, but with more dragons.

The story? It’s there if you want it. Very mysterious, like a classified document. You’ve got to work for it. Which is fine, because I’m a worker. The best worker. But most gamers? Lazy! They just want hand-holding. Not in Dark Souls. No hand-holding, no participation trophies. Just you, a sword, and a lot of dying. I died a few times—maybe once, maybe twice. Okay, maybe a hundred. But I always came back stronger. Like America.

Boss fights? Incredible. Epic. Huge monsters. Very aggressive. Reminds me of the debates. Gwyn, the final boss? Great guy. Terrific music. I parried him on the first try—like I parried CNN.

Overall, Dark Souls is a winner’s game. It’s not for the weak. It’s for champions. If you’re looking for something easy, go play Mario Kart. But if you want to be the best, like me, Dark Souls is the game.

10 out of 10. Would invade Lordran again. Believe me.

*NO MAJOR SPOILERS IN THIS REVIEW* This is the best game I have ever played. From a gameplay perspective, DSR does fall behind other FS games such as DS3 and Elden Ring. However, just going off of gameplay is not a fair assessment of DS. The sheer volume of areas in this game is absurd. In a casual first playthrough going in blind a player would need to scour the land of Lordran to find all its secrets. The developers at FS have shown their love while making this game. The thing I love the most of this game is the atmosphere. One moment one could be comfortably sitting at firelink going through which stats to upgrade, and the next you could be exploring the near bottomless world. The way that areas are connected is mind blowing. I'll never forget in my life finding a new way back to firelink after hours of exploring the undead parish. While this game is a challenge, once the player explores this non linear world of Lordran, the items given to the player give several options to defeat the next big foe. Stuck on O and S for too long? Go upgrade your weapon, or even change builds. This game is difficult, but it is one of the most rewarding experiences in gaming. While I got stuck on certain bosses, the sheer thought of completing the next boss and unlocking the next area kept me going. After defeating the next gatekeeper boss, the player will unlock a whole new rewarding challenge to overcome. I will never forget Dark Souls. I genuinely do not believe I will ever find a better game than this. Also I didn't mention it much for spoilers sake, (this will have a couple more minor spoilers) but of all games with NPC's I've ever played I've never loved NPC's as much as the ones in DS. While ER got me close to caring NPC's even once things happened to them only a few made me sad. In DS I feel responsible for these characters and their lives. I still remember going out of my way to save some of these NPC's. I still feel sad each time one of these lovable characters go hollow. Even the ones I hated still make me sad to see go hollow. If you want to experience emotion in a game, play this; not just sad emotion but also amazement. If you decide to purchase DS, just remember, it is meant to be punishing not difficult (generally). Now, Praise the sun \[T]/ and don't you dare go hollow.

9.2/10. Elden Ring is one of my favorite games ever, so I was searching for more of the same: the same, great gameplay; difficult, but rewarding boss fights; and… that’s it, really. Dark Souls basically delivered in that, and to me was preferrable over Elden Ring in several key aspects, though it also shows its age/some strange, early-installment design choices that I was not a fan of. Overall, I agonized over whether to rate this higher or lower than the 9.4 I gave Elden Ring, and settled on lower, mostly because the game is 14 years old, at this point, while Elden Ring visually is still so amazing to me. Much of this review will be a comparison to Elden Ring, which I still believe is the bar for all soulslike games.

I played a light roll dex build focused on two-handing the Winged Spear, fully leveled (no fire/lightning/divine/whatever) with the stamina shield backpack strat. I used the cool light roll ring and the FAP ring, wore light-ish armor (and my Sorcerer’s cap) and never bothered with any ranged attacks/sorceries/anything else, really. Overall, a departure from my Elden Ring build, which also was spear-based but was also a faith hybrid build (which I overall enjoyed more, bringing more complexity to my tactics).

The Good:

The best part of Dark Souls by far is the actual gameplay. The fluid combat and enemy designs were both top-notch, and my concerns that it would be a step down from Elden Ring were mostly unfounded. The inputs and hitboxes, for the most part, were very responsive/accurate, and I never had much to complain about from an actual gameplay perspective. I felt that the level design, too, was mostly good, and was particularly impressed by the innovative, interesting, and varied zones that brought new experiences every time I beat a boss and moved to the next area. I was also pleasantly surprised by how large the game and world were, even in comparison to Elden Ring – my initial impression was that the game was mostly going to be centered around the same castle/area, but the actual world map was about 3-4 times larger than I imagined, and far more varied. I am also a huge fan of the YOU DIED memes and the infamous difficulty – to me, it made the game feel much more rewarding whenever I did make it to the next bonfire or beat the boss. In my opinion, when I did die it didn’t feel as bad, since I imagined a lot of people died in the same spot, and when I didn’t die, I felt like a god gamer (no matter how easy the area/boss might have been in reality). I thought Dark Souls did this better than Elden Ring, even, and I felt that fewer deaths in this game felt unearned. I personally also enjoyed the tighter, more purposeful level design to Elden Ring’s (sometimes empty) open world. I also thought the music, visuals, and overall aesthetic was very cohesive and effective, though I wasn’t the biggest fan of the kind of depressing feeling it gave off.

The Bad:

There’s no getting around it – the game does not look good. I try to give it the benefit of the doubt, since it’s a 14-year-old game, but even something like Skyrim looked much better, let alone Halo 4 or Portal 2. There’s also a lot of what I call “early-installment jankiness”, like the humanity system (which I feel was completely ineffective/pointless/honestly annoying), the kindling/reverse hallowing/invasions stuff (very poorly explained) and some strange design choices (like curling up in front of a crow to go to the tutorial level, or staring at a painting with a doll in your inventory) that almost necessitate having a guide open on your second monitor. To me, the story was also largely incoherent and had no impact on the game, and I had much more fun reading about it on the wiki. The game also kind of loses focus in the second half, with the game on a purposeful march towards Anor Londo for the entire first half, then… you can go wherever you want and there’s no real indication on what to do/where to go? And don’t get me started on how to get to the DLC…

The Ugly (YMWV!!):

I’m sorry, but coming from Elden Ring and its DLC (including beating Consort Radahn week 1 – sorry, just had to flex this) the game is simply too easy. I would say the levels/overworld killed me about as much as it did in Elden Ring and thought it was a fair challenge, but the bosses, for the most part, was a joke. In fact, I can count the bosses that took me more than 2 attempts on one hand: Manus, Gwyn, Artorias (in that order). Coincidentally, those were the only three “aggressive” bosses that were more in the vein of Elden Ring/its DLC boss design, which I personally believe to be preferrable than the slow, shambling, easily dodge-able bosses that permeated the rest of Dark Souls 1’s design philosophy. Obviously, huge YMWV on this, and it may be build-dependent, too, with my build being a far more modern approach to the game than the intended heavy armor, shields out 2011 approach, but I said what I said. Also, the runbacks in this game are brutal compared to Elden Ring’s strategy of putting a Grace next to the boss wall – guess which approach I preferred! Overall, it’s hard to criticize, coming from the latest iteration of the Soulslike genre and playing the granddaddy of them all – like comparing a Ford Model T to a Ford 2025 F-150. I feel like if I were in 2011, I’d be far more impressed!!

And now, the rankings:

Bosses (based on how much I liked them, which is a combination of how hard they were, how annoying/cool they were to fight, and how cool they were – if it’s not on the list, they were forgettable/too easy)

Manus >>>>>>> Gwyn >> the Titanite Demon in Ador Londo in that tiny room (so hard I never beat it) >>> Artorias >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> Chaos Witch Quelaag > Ornstein and Smough (if you kill Smough first) >>> Stray Demon >> (the stupid) Hydra > Priscilla>> Havel >> Four Kings >> Ornstein and Smough (if you kill Ornstein first) >>>> Sif >> Nito >>>> Butterfly >>>>> Gaping Dragon >>>> Dark Sun Gwyndolyn > Demon Firesage >>> Bell Gargoyles >>> Asylum Demon >> Iron Golem >> Seath > Centipede Demon >>>> Ceaseless Discharge >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>Sanctuary Guardian>> Pinwheel >> Bed of Chaos

Similar to Elden Ring/its DLC, where Maliketh/Rellana were my favorite fights, but Malenia/Radahn were harder, I thought Artorias (and to a lesser extent, Gwyn) was the coolest boss in the game, but Manus was by far the hardest.

Best Zone: Duke’s Archives
Worst Zone: three-way tie between Tomb of Giants, Blighttown, and Ash Lake (Valley of the Drakes was hugely disappointing, too, and Sen’s Fortress is up there purely due to frustration at the platforming)

Dark Souls Remastered: A Whole Vibe or Straight Pain?

I loaded up Dark Souls Remastered thinking I’m him… nah, this game humbled me FAST. Like, one sec I’m vibin’, sword out, drip check lookin’ medieval 🛡️, next sec I’m gettin’ clapped by a skeleton that pulled up uninvited. 💀

This game? It's not just hard. It’s built different. The devs were on DEMON time fr 😤 except the 2nd part of the game. Every enemy is like “square up” and you got no choice but to dance. You gotta parry like your life (and 20k souls) depends on it… cuz it does.

The graphics? Lowkey fire 🔥. They remastered that shiz crisp. Everything’s dark and moody like an ex's playlist, but it SLAPS. Sound design? Sheeeesh. You hear a boss roar from a mile away.

And the lore? Hidden AF. You gotta piece it together like it’s a TikTok ARG. NPCs out here talkin’ in riddles like they know the ending of Inception. 🌀

When you beat a boss after dying 57 times? That dopamine hit is ELITE. Straight serotonin uppercut. 👊💥 You feel like a certified GOAT. Legends only.

If you got patience, fast thumbs, and a lil’ masochist in your DNA, Dark Souls Remastered is a W. If not… it’s gonna have you rage quitting and questioning life choices. Either way, it’s a cultural reset. 9/10 would try again. 🔥

Posted: June 15
Dark Souls is a poor name from such a bright and shining star of a game.

Story
Obscure, mystifying, and far deeper than it first appears. Dark Souls sets itself in a medieval fantasy world where souls are tangible things with a multitude of uses and can even be traded freely or stolen. A brief and gripping introduction scene runs players through what is effectively the creation myth of the world. Where once there was a seemingly static world of nothing but grey inert trees and seemingly unmoving dragons; suddenly a fire analogous to the Big Bang theory appeared from seemingly nothing, and brought with it the concept of disparity between opposites. Temperature differences, light differences, and the very concept of life vs. death. New creatures were born from this disparity and found great sources of power referred to as "Lord Souls" within the flame. Harnessing the strength these Lord Souls provided, the creatures rose up and overthrew the dragons, slaying them to extinction and taking charge of the world; giving rise to a new Age of Fire.
When players begin the game, untold centuries have passed since that event, and the great fire is now beginning to subside, threatening to end the Age of Fire if it goes out. Worse still, men and women are becoming cursed with a mysterious "Darksign" upon their flesh, which turns them into a creature known as an Undead. Undead people are incapable of truly dying; continually rising again after suffering lethal injury, and slowly going mad as the years and deaths wear away at their mental state until they turn into soul thirsty Hollows; near feral creatures which seek to murder and steal the souls of others to sate their never ending hunger. Players take on the role of one such Undead, locked away in an Asylum meant to contain their kind for eternity. But after a mysterious knight drops you the key to your cell and gives you a path to freedom, he further explains an old prophecy meant to illuminate the true future and purpose of Undead kind. And so, armed with that knowledge, players set off into the ancient, dilapidated, and dangerous realm of Lordran, to seek out their future and claim it. From that point onwards, just how much of Dark Souls' story a player manages to grasp will depend on their own initiative and attention to detail. There's a massive and fascinating narrative web underpinning everything the player will witness in their journey, but it is obscured and scattered across the entire game in the form of NPC dialogue, short item descriptions, and environmental story telling. Uncovering the truth and twists of Dark Souls feels like being a sort of digital archeologist, and any question you find for which a concrete answer can not be definitively secured, often invites the player to interpret their own based on all the facts surrounding them.

Dark Souls is a third person action RPG that emphasizes skill and timing as much as it does character build and gear selection. Not so much a difficult game as it is rigidly punishing to failure, DS demands a player's full attention as they carefully making their way past traps and deadly opponents. Some baddies are certainly more challenging than others, but at the end of the day, there are myriad of ways a player can improve their own odds if they come up against a wall.
Players can progress their characters in many classic directions: The massive strong man wielding an axe larger than he is. The dexterous fencer flowing around his enemies and prodding them to death. The all powerful mage obliterating opponents from afar. And that's to say nothing of archery, clerics with their god given miracles, flame wielding pyromancers, or even the many in between classes that harness multiple styles to overcome differing obstacles. There are a multitude of weapons throughout the game with a massive variety of different move sets and even special powers or hidden effects. Armor ranges from delicate and dainty silks to stone plate several inches thick. A weight system helps mediate how quickly your character can dodge around enemy attacks while carrying all this gear; encouraging players to either build themselves into slow but sturdy tanks or light and nimble fighters whose entire goal is to remain untouched in combat. Or you can even be a combination of the two somewhere in between. Attack animations are often committed acts. Once you start swinging your sword, your character is going to follow through, so you want to make sure you pick your windows of attack wisely and avoid being greedy, lest you suffer an enemy's reprisal. Certain enemies can be stabbed in the back if you managed to get around them or have their attacks swatted aside for you to then hit them with brutal, high damage riposte attacks. A plethora of damage types helps keep players on their feet both in regard to incoming damage, and how resistant some enemies might be to the damage you're throwing out at them. The world of Dark Souls is sprawling, varied, and delightfully interconnected; often revealing shortcuts between places you've already been when you need them most. Exploration is a key means to finding new gear, upgrade materials, or even entire areas hidden from the main path. And that world is populated with a huge cadre of enemies too numerous to describe. They come in every shape, size, and color imaginable, each with their own attacks, patterns, and AI tendencies. And that's not even mentioning the many bosses through the game who will test your build and sills the most. They're certainly not all perfect. Some enemies have weirdly slippery hitboxes or janky navigation, and one boss in particular is a sin against game design itself, but for every enemy that's a miss, there's thirty more who are a joy to fight.
The game also features a unique online element that helps build a sense of community and pushes back a little on the isolating atmosphere. The ghosts of other players on their own journey will briefly fade into and out of existence. Bloodstains on the ground can be touched to witness a player's final seconds of life, often leading either witnessing a comedic end or a warning of danger to come. Players can write messages both helpful and misleading using predefined words and phrases that will then appear spontaneously in the worlds of others. Summon signs can be set on the floor, allowing others to bring you into their world so that you may assist them in toppling a challenge that's giving them trouble. You can even be invaded by ne'er-do-well players seeking to kill you for your souls and humanity.

A game as ugly as it is beautiful, Dark Souls environments will run you through the filthiest sewers filled with the most grotesque creatures you can imagine, only to then plunge you into a place that is somehow even worse, but then suddenly ambush you with extravagant and sweeping architecture or impossibly ancient and almost alien scenes of beautiful nature. Visual design somehow manages to be both excellent and wholly unique while simultaneously feeling right at home in the most cliche of medieval fantasy tropes. Music is used sparingly and usually comes in the form of overbearing, suffocating choir chants that fit perfectly with the sight of a house sized monster rushing towards you with violent intent. The few songs that break away from this pattern are haunting and memorable. The package certainly isn't perfect. Voice acting is utterly superb. Some of the sound design in particular is extremely weak compared to the heights the rest of the experience, and there are more than a few small graphical oddities; usually geometry seams in the world not lining up and just giving you a view into the void. But overall these are small quibbles compared to the excellence on display.

This game was, amazing. I never played a game where I felt that every level design were making sense and not at all at the same time. Everything is linked to eachother and as for the lore, every player will have its own little adventure as you'll start putting pieces together but not in the same order.

The game is fair. My deaths were my fault, and I really felt getting stronger as I took my time to travel to different zones and search for weapons and equipement. I played a bit of everything, melee, pyromancy, sorcerer, everything works well enough for you to have fun with the classes.

It is now known by anyone who played at least one souls like, but, take. Your. Time. if you expect the game to throw the lore at you, you'll probably complete it without really knowing what's happening, make sure to talk to NPC and to go back to location you already visited as you're maybe missing this one piece of story that could give sense to a whole boss/location.

I am not done with the achievements, seem like you have to do 2-3 runs to get all of them. They're not too bad to obtain, most of them are just story, upgrading weapons and slaying bosses or meeting important NPCs that you'll probably encounter just by roaming around.

When you see/play the game, you understand why the souls franchise is still thriving today, it's a good mashup of gameplay, beautiful sceneries and passive story-telling that really immerse you in the ambience that Dark Souls wants to create.

Posted: June 13
Dark Souls is a journey, in the same way that life is. Your story will be told whether you overcome the challenges and beings ahead of you, or your resolve is shattered and you never login to the game again. There lay the most beautiful part of this game. The moment you create your character you are contributing to the story that has been being told for ages. You will grow stronger, or you will eventually cease and the life that you lived in this world will share the same fate as too many before you. You will go hollow, there with the very weapons and armor and spells that you once wielded so lively in an attempt to challenge your fate. Your hollow form will stand as an obstacle for more undead after you to come, every decision you made will stand there, timeless, and live on without you.

This is a game of duality; heat and cold, life and death, light and dark. Past and present. A life that you live through the character you create that will leave you questioning, and pulling at your mind as you step into a broken world seeking answers. Through the game you will encounter various challenges that will leave you from a range of baffled,(HOW DID THAT HIT ME!?) triumphant, (FIIIINALLY DUDEE EAAAT IT FOUR K-) and awed in the conflicting beauty of a game that so perfectly captures the range of emotions that life itself evokes from you. Thrust into a world and having to learn your bearing. In a way you never have before, you will succeed, and you will fail. You will feel yourself getting stronger and better, just for a new challenge to test the fibers of your fortitude and slam down unto you like the waves on the shore. Again and again, each time learning something to drive you to be better. You will find the answers you seek, and when they are not what you expect you will question why you are doing what you are in the first place. You will evaluate from your characters eyes what it is that you truly desire, others intentions for you cast aside. You will meet a variety of people whom you share this world with and they will cast more perspective on this Undead condition.

And at the end of it all, when your goal is complete. You will turn to the community with your questions. You will learn and pull from our communal library of knowledge and with this newfound information you will plunge yourself into the fray once more.

On a personal note, this game is my all time favorite. Not a second thought in purchasing again as a remaster after playing since the 360 days. You will learn that it is more than a purchase, it is a threshold into the community that loves this universe as I, it is a dream that you experience first hand. I still learn new things about this beautifully ill fated world.

My ONE and ONLY regret, in giving this work of art weeks of my time on this planet, pouring dedication into the story of gods and dragons and men, and the frustration that gave way to euphoria in victory is this: That I cannot go back and do it for the first time all. over. again.

This game made such a deep impression on me that I’ll probably remember it for the rest of my life. Maybe someday I’ll come back to it for NG+ and try out some mad mage build or something wild like that. But that’s not on the agenda for now since I’ve already completed it twice, and each time was no walk in the park. At first, it all seemed incredibly hard and confusing. I’d get frustrated because I couldn’t dodge in time or roll properly, but in the end, I realised I was the one to blame for all my fails. It was even kinda funny, so easy to fall off a cliff when you haven’t slept for 12 hours and your eyes just want to shut from exhaustion. But after a few dozen hours, you start getting the hang of how the game works, and things become... well, almost clear.

The level design, the characters, the lore, just mind-blowing. And the bosses! Especially the ones from the DLC. I still can’t forget the ones that made me swear out loud and jump up in frustration: Manus, Artorias, Ornstein and Smough, the Four Kings, and, of course, Gwyn. They were so brutal that, if you’ve never played, you’ll definitely be cursing them out at every turn. The key here is to not approach them with a cheeky “Hey, Dad!” and then panic when you realise joking with them is a death sentence. No mate, there’s no messing about here, none of that “Hey, let me think for a second” stuff like in casual games. You’ve gotta be 100% in the zone. Every move you make is either a chance or a mistake. If you respect the game and take it seriously, it’ll reward you big time.
