Hello, a PC-only player here.

TLDR. This game is fine and manageable for PC gamers. It feels like an old-school RPG classic with a quite weird art style. If you want immersion, this game might not be for you, as it communicates infrequently through riddles (reading the wiki can help though). While there are interesting bosses, the biggest challenge is finding the bonfire closest to a boss room. So if you dislike running across half a map just to try defeating a boss again, this game may not suit you. The DSR combat involves a lot of dodge-rolling (or abusing boss mechanics while staying in blind spots), which not be your cup of tea. However, if you haven't played a souls-like game yet, understanding the hype might be your main reason to try it.

Despite From Software's apparent disregard for mouse and keyboard players, I decided to give this game a second chance. I must admit that I dislike the art style, not for its appearance but for its logic - like combining a girl's top half with a fire spider or taking a model of an elongated monstrous mouth, attaching a few legs and wings to it, and calling it a dragon. Therefore, I had to adjust my mindset to enjoy the gameplay rather than search for logic. Additionally, the controls, camera, UI, and overall design is tailored for controller users. Usually, a game ported from console to PC raises a red flag for me, but I set that aside to give it a try. I also found it frustrating to see how a character can avoid being hit by a hammer of a size of the Moon by dodge rolling in one place. So in my playthrough, I decided to use dodge rolling only in critical situations and only to cut distances.

After completing the game, including the dlc, the game feels okay, but there is nothing special to it. The lore is nice, but as I mentioned earlier, you need to read wiki to grasp much of it. I appreciated the shortcut system, which connects areas and allows for independent exploration in the first half of the game, giving the game a more open-world feel and making the world itself feel alive. However, this feeling is ruined after unlocking fast travel with the Lordvessel.

Also, I didn't find any big challenges. Things like "do the Sen's Fortress once more if you are low on estus before the boss room" or "go across half of the map to buy a purging stone to remove the permanent curse" felt more like annoyances rather than challenges. Oh, and hiding bonfires behind illusory walls or requiring leaps of faith to reach them is simply a cringe.

The only difficult bosses for me were the gargoyles and Artorias, while the gargoyles' constant fire breath was mostly an annoyance rather than an interesting mechanic. On the other hand, Artorias jumps like a grasshopper and hit like a track (plus his armor is cool). Also I enjoyed the Ornstein and Smough fight, even though it wasn't particularly difficult for me (btw the Smought's armor is sooo ugly - does anyone really like this art style?).

In conclusion, the game is alright but doesn’t even make my top 10. Will I play it again? Could be, but probably if I had an opportunity to fight bosses multiple times and if I had a free respec tool to experiment with builds. Should you play it if you are like me? Posted: June 11
Ive played every fromsoftware's games as well as countless other souls-like games such as nioh, code vein, lies of p, the surge, mortal shell and more. Out of all of these games, this one, in my opinion, is the worst. Yes I get it, its the one that started it all, and honestly, its a great game, but there are so many things I hate about it that I just cant recommend this to anyone. Of course if your a souls fan, you will find an appeal here, but if you've played games like elden ring, dark souls 3, sekiro, etc. there is really no point of playing this. With such better options out there, its difficult to truly enjoy this game. The combat is slow and clunky, the bonfire placements are awful, many of the areas are just an annoying puzzle, there are so many bugs and honestly most of the game is not a challenge (yes i know this is very subjective). I was constantly disappointed by this game at every turn, the bosses are boring and too easy, the areas are bland and boring and the graphics are still preyty bad for a 2018 remaster. Worst of all, they are selling this game for over $50 CAD! You wold be better off saving your money and getting any of the other titles than this. Now with all that said, there are alot of things I like about this game, and it wouldn't be a fair review if I didn't mention these. The interconnected world was something I really liked. Going back to fire link shrine from the undead parish for the first time was awesome, same with draining new londos water and seeing the valley of drakes. This interconnection is unmatched to any game, and it really shines. The sense of exploration is also great, the game has so many secrets. Sadly, those these two things are the only positive aspects of the game, besides that, its very flawed. Each time I hopped on It felt like a chore, I didn't find much of the game very fun, the only good bosses are ornstein and smough and sif imo, the rest are just gimmick bosses or bosses that would have been more fun if they weren't so easy. The anticlimactic bosses made each area feel consecutively more disappointing, and by the time I was at the end game, I just didn't want to continue (I should mention this is my second play through, this is my first play through on pc). The first time I played this game It was new and exciting and there was really nothing like it, but coming back to it with all these new titles makes it completely pointless. Especially running through areas ive already seen without the ability to fast travel till halfway through the game, and even with the fast travel, only a portion of the bonfires you unlock are able top be warped to. Like why make a remaster and just keep every awful aspect of the game in? Like at least let the fans get what they are paying for. This game should be worth 20 bucks, not 50.

Overall, save your time and get literally any other souls born game and you wont regret it, the 27 hours ive put in on this game have been a chore through and through, and I wish I could get that time back.

Overall, its better worth your time and money to play literally any other souls borne game,

Posted: June 10
A friend awhile back asked me to give the Soulsborne series a try, starting with this one. I recall playing DS3 ages ago but too long to retain any knowledge or game skill. So, after a while, I booted up and made my character to give this game a try in a stream with him. Take this as a review from someone who is more or less a brand new beginner to the series.

From the start I felt like this little rodent shivering in the cold from anything that threatened me. Basic enemies that swung once per 3 business days pose a threat at this point and you feel that pressure on you the entire time you engage and scamper past each area with your life clutched. Reaching a bonfire feels earned every single time, be it newly kindled or returning for a refill on flasks. I went for a shield & one-handed build with sturdy armor and every item you get feels as if that ever-engaging pressure against your life is relieved a bit as you swap it out, let alone upgrading your gear with Andre. By the end of the game, I was essentially Hercules with area enemies that once handed my rear to me countless times now barely tickle. The sights you see are so grim yet enthralling to navigate through and don't think the game will have your worst obstacle be a tight corridor -- you will be jumping and rolling for your life through stories-high environments. From the scoldering boils of Lost Izalith to the architecture of Duke's Archives, every location is memorable and gorgeous in its own way. So many times I found myself wide-eyed as I cut around a new corner and it was a big part in keeping my attention glued to continuing.

As for what you face, the game will not hold your hand at all. You will find yourself lost, uncomfortable, or even terrified at times of what's around you and yet it adds to the intriguing edge you tinker on constantly. You consistently feel this intrigue to explore and see what you find. I ran deep into the Catacombs completely blinded on my own curiosity and found two things: one of the best helmets I own and a heart attack. The bosses you will go up against warm you up well I'd say to the difficulty curve. From basic slams & jumps to having to juggle two juggernauts able to obliterate you in record time, you feel that difficulty ramp hard and yet you feel your skill bolster once you topple that one pain in your ass who has been stomping a mudhole in your behind around the clock. That and you just feel like a kingslayer once you do it. A lot of times I found myself going from a sigh of relief to actively jumping out of my chair in hype. Manus, Father of the Abyss, took me 9 hours and two build reformations to take out and on video I got the moment I got the kill. My mic peaked for a solid 20 seconds LMAO

Finally, one aspect I adore about this game is how meticulously teaching every single fight is. Be it from southern-style ass whoopings or from carefully duking it out with an enemy, every individual encounter teaches you something about the enemy/boss. Manus was a GREAT example of this. Died to a flurry of swings? Notice he roars before his enraged swings. Giant arm slammed your health despite you dodging? Notice that you need to dodge as it's borderline connecting with you instead of prematurely. Spams uppercut? Notice how the medium-close range incites specific attacks like uppercut or palm slam. You will learn with each boss that positioning is your friend in manipulating what attacks can be invoked. The biggest devil in the details in that is learning which of those attacks post-dodge are safe to punish/hit the Estus. Some are guaranteed windows for healing/damage while others will pull the rug under you and set you up perfectly for an express ticket back to the bonfire. Greed a la doing damage when it's not safe can and WILL get you killed. You need to study the beast you're facing one-on-one which is key to recognizing "Oh this attack is coming, that's a perfect window to get a heavy in". This song and dance is pivotal to your success in dare I say the series as a whole and my god is that dance wonderfully satisfying.

Going forward in the series, Dark Souls 1 is a VERY strong start to this Soulsborne journey. I personally say this without hyperbole nor the honeymoon phase of just coming out of beating the game but I think this game may be one of my favorite games out there now. Very rarely do games make me go "I wanna immediately do another run with so-and-so build" because my lord will you have that feeling once you are done. Hell, you can beat the whole game more or less by chucking human feces at bosses then poking them with a stick. I'm serious! The entire game is memorable and I'm certain if someone asks me what's in so-and-so location, I can remember the entire layout and the enemies there just from how both enthralling and unique each spot is. I have been wanting a game to intoxicate me like this for such a long time and now that I'm in this stupor, I'm surprised I haven't tasted this series sooner. Only fight frustrated me out of the entire game & DLC and yet after 9 hours, the high I felt on the killing blow made me feel like a Saints fan at XLIV.

10/10 bar none. I loved every second of it.

Posted: June 9
I played this game on console over 7 years ago, it was ♥♥♥♥. I play this game on PC now, it's ♥♥♥♥.

I'm writing this review after finishing the game a second time. I thought it was my favorite, but now I think it's the worst. Every game that came after this from FromSoft was a lot better than this trash.

Good things:

Good connectivity between areas.

Ability to go to most places from the beginning, making it a mix of open-world and linear world progression.

Bad things:

- Most weapons in this game are incredibly weak; you either have to resort to very heavy strength weapons or magic.

- Without using Reddit or guides, discovering the DLC is illogical.

- Bosses are reused in other areas as normal enemies.

- Areas look rushed or unfinished.

- Hitboxes are badly made.

- Stamina management is horrendous.

- Dodging is useless; either walk around or use a shield.

- Big enemies cramped into small spaces result in camera problems.

- Large area wastelands like Anor Londo and Izalith.

- Enemy placement feels unnatural — as if a developer just clicked around the area a few times to spread enemies and left it like that.

- The game is "remastered" although it looks almost the same as in 2011. Bugs, glitches, and mistakes from the devs are still there (wood texture on the railing in Duke’s Archives, for example).

A lot of people defend this game for some reason. I was blinded by nostalgia too after I had forgotten most of the game over seven years. Replaying it now made me realize that this game is the worst of the Soulsborne series.

If you're eager to play this Miyazaki Spaghetticode, try to get it at a lower price, as €40 is way too expensive for this "remaster". I also advise playing in offline mode. I was once invaded by a hacker who made me spawn in the bonfire of the Asylum Demon at the beginning, with no way out. I was only able to escape by manipulating my last bonfire with Cheat Engine and using a Homeward Bone to return to Firelink Shrine.

All in all:

Decent game, bad remaster, and poorly aged. Don’t start this game as your first Soulsborne if possible.

Posted: June 7
Dark Souls is an atmospheric masterpiece with some excellent boss fights strewn about.

You really do feel as though you're making a pilgrimage to a once sacred land that has fallen into that decay and corruption.

In the end, it feels like the first Divinity: Original Sin: an extremely charming and rewarding game that I ultimately cannot recommend anyone to play.
I get the appeal, looking forward to playing the rest of the series, just am at a loss that this series is as successful as it is.

Even after all of the franchise's success the developers did not take this opportunity to finish their game.

Instead of going the route of Knights of the Old Republic 2 and just amputating the cut content to the best of their ability, FromSoftware instead chose to vomit out what they could, and this vomit is almost the entirety of the second half of the game.

There are also so many boneheaded design decisions.

You can't pause...

So many optional NPC quests are nearly guaranteed to be missed without a guide, which I cannot think is intentional.

Another weird thing is that new players seem to be directed towards two of the endgame areas.

I (and others) spent the first several hours of our playthroughs in these end areas due to these being in the natural progression paths with the actual starting area after the tutorial being just a few steps the other way. Progression in these areas is painful, yet strangely feasible, which is weird as many areas after this will make it apparent that you shouldn't be there just yet by either stat checking the player so hard in order to ensure that you are using blacksmiths to upgrade your equipment, or the areas will be literally locked away.

There are many other weird gameplay misses such as some of the earliest bosses having messed up hitboxes, personally wound up spending a good amount of hours mistakenly thinking that Dark Souls did not provide any invincibility frames for its dodge rolls.

Further exacerbating all this are the traps and ambushes lying in wait even after boss areas. This pairs horrendously with the weird decision they made to make this an RPG with potentially lost progress on death mechanics where many save locations/checkpoints are well hidden. After struggling through mazes, traps, and so many enemies it seems weird that you are expected to return to your previous save location before making progress just so that you can level up in a game that does not make sense that you even get have to level up in.

Just so much struggling through an area, get a surprise death, struggle through an area again, eventually kill the boss, die looking for a nearby bonfire by an ambush or introduction to a new trap, die en-route to picking up your EXP = massive loss in progress; so stupid.

Level up system also forces players into very specific builds that all feel extremely different, which is strange as FromSoftware also made the legendary mecha series Armored Core where you are able to just change your playstyle on the fly without any pesky stat requirements and whatnot getting the way of your experimentation

"Dark Souls Remastered – Looks Better, Still Hurts"

Dark Souls: Remastered is proof that no matter how pretty you make it, pain is timeless.

You wake up in a jail cell with zero backstory, armed with a butter knife and the crushing weight of existential dread. Ten minutes later you’re getting bodied by a guy who looks like a moldy fridge with legs. And that’s just the tutorial.

Yes, the lighting is nicer, the textures are smoother, and you can finally see your character’s expression as they get yeeted off a cliff for the 27th time. It’s beautiful. It’s brutal. It’s brutiful.

PvP is back and just as toxic as ever. One moment you’re trying to fight a boss, the next you’re being invaded by someone named ✨xX_BoneDaddy_Xx✨ who parries you into another dimension while wearing nothing but a loincloth and smug energy.

Also, the UI got cleaned up—so you can now clearly read the name of the boss that’s about to ruin your life. That’s helpful, emotionally.

10/10. Would roll off a ledge trying to dodge a skeleton with zero chill again.

Posted: June 5
There is a special wonder for this game that I cannot stop thinking about. While the initial release of it, back in the day on ps3/xbox was much harder, and ridiculous, this game has gone up and high to be one of the greatest gaming experiences ever crafted.

Dark Souls is billed by its fan base as a really hard game, and yes it is. However, this game is about patterns, and not just enemy patterns, your patterns. Your mistakes here are punished, and your victories are rewarded accordingly. It is through adversity that we as humans become stronger as people. This game is a good example of that, because while a couple of sections are a bit ridiculous, the rest of the game is about you, and your willingness to succeed. You will fail, time and time again, but if you learn what's causing it within your playstyle and you address it, you will succeed.

The player is tasked to examine their playstyle, insecurities, and patterns. It is not just about leveling up/min-max/OP weapons, though the latter helps. knowing when to run away, when to be aggressive: Is this run worth me losing all these souls. When you get cocky, the game will humble you, but when you can best it, its downright intoxicating. Trying the same thing that doesn't work will leave you insane.

There are some sections that are downright ridiculous, Blighttown for example, is absolutely really awful if you aren't prepared mentally and tactically. Anor Londo's archers are so wild the first time you encounter them. But reaching that place made Sen's Fortress worth going through. I was just joyed to see such architectural mastery and design.

The boss fights I believe are truly incredible most of the time, especially Dark Sun Gwyndolin, it just felt so otherworldly, even though I didn't have much trouble beating him. This game made me want to know more and more about it, even if I knew that entailed the game becoming more difficult. By the end, I explored and saw so many wondrous and awful things. bed of chaos is at best an annoying boss and getting to it first is the worst part. Similar without the annoyance to Quelaag. Getting to her is atrocious. But wow, it is truly awesome to see such design.

The DLC is decent, however, I wish Artorias' battle was more uh, grand. It felt extremely hollow compared to the emotions you can see in Sif's boss fight.

My qualms with RPGs are mostly based on grinding, and doing that to grind a sword/specific place was really annoying sometimes. That gets worse in DS2. A game I felt had some of the wonder but a lot of frustration and disconnection. With Dark Souls platforming is very annoying, that stands true in DS2 also. Where the dlc become doubles on that.

Overall Dark Souls: Remastered plays great, and it very fun. Come with a plan, and expect to suffer. Getting good is part of the fun. PVP can be fun but annoying when you're in a weird area you are exploring and someone invades you. I highly recommend this game, reading a guide when getting stuck is perfectly fine.

A real treat for dark fantasy fans, architecture, game design, and esoteric lore.

Product received for free

This game saved my life.

I am a 97 year old veteran of the Korean war.

My ex-wife/latinx trans boyfriend and I have a daughter together, and adopted our 16 sons together. They are all in their late 60s.

When we were going through our separation, I found myself lost and miserable. I was self destructive. I got so mad one day from everything spiraling out of my control that I punched some concrete in a moment of overwhelming emotion. That caused me to break my 5th metacarpal in my right hand... my working hand... my games hand.. the hand that I held and carried my children to bed with.. The hand I desperately needed to make sure I could continue to provide.

After learning of the severity of my self-inflicted damage, I was borderline suicidal. Keep in mind that just a few months before this, I was the happiest man with no history of depression or anxiety. I have never had fits of rage, or been one to break down and cry, but I was in a low spot that just really buried me from being able to see the light on the other side.

Having nothing better to do, I searched for a game I could play, ONE HANDED while I recovered. I somehow stumbled upon this game and read some of the reviews. I decided that it had to be worth a shot... I must admit, I didn't beat the game, or play nearly as long as some of you. In fact, I may have only played this game a day or two.. With that being said, after doing so, I had a new found joy and hope for life. I was able to put behind me the pain and suffering that had been cast over me. I was able to experience other peoples joy and happiness. I was able to see the fruits of my "labor". I relaxed for 5 minutes to this music long enough to realize that I was going to be okay.
After coming to that realization, I turned the game off, and I went back to work. It hurt my hand like hell but I was motivated. I stopped feeling so damn sorry for myself, and I became the father I needed to be in that moment, not the weak boy I was behaving as.

Today, I am close friend with my kids mother. We don't fight, or argue, or say hurtful things to each other. We are parents, and friends.

I now have 3 more kids. My third child is, wait for it, ALSO 4 YEARS OLD. The woman I am with was going through a very similar situation at the time of my own separation, and we just stumbled in to each others life unexpectedly. We have been in a relationship for a year now, and are very happy together.

Moral of the story, you never know what life holds in store for you, and if I would have given up when all the odds were stacked against me, I wouldn't be where I am today. This silly little game helped me realize that.

Thank you

There is something inherently loveable and authentic about DS1. It Cannot be found in the other games in the series, i can't put a finger on it but its somehow more 'real' as a game. It's clunky at times, but somehow this feels intended; that it is somehow drawing on the conventions of Dungeon crawler card-based RPG's but putting that experience into a workable but suitable action game framework. I have never been amazing at it, but perseverance got me through first playthrough, understanding got me through second playthough as an intelligence build. I am returning to it for the first time in eight years (2025) as a strength build. I doubt anybody needs this review, but if it means someone goes for this over Elden Ring or DS3 then I have done my job!

TL:DR - Genuinely fun game. It is only as hard as you make it for yourself.

When I first heard of Dark Souls I thought it sounded off putting. I was never really a fan of difficulty in games and always picked the default or "normal" option. When I heard humanity was something you had to grind for it gave me second thoughts (for some reason I can't remember). I played Elden Ring and loved it so it is weird how I was so put off by Dark Souls.

One day my friend started nagging me to play Dark Souls. It came up in almost every conversation for months. When it went on sale I finally caved and bought it. Each time I started it up I was dreading to play it, id play 2 or so hours and call it quits. After a few sessions I started thinking about it in my free time. Brainstorming the lore he drip fed me in calls while I played. And eventually it clicked.

I started really getting into the groove of just playing, killing bosses, and upgrading my gear. I got lost many times but I still had the urge to play. When I was finally nearing the final boss I felt a little sad. I didn't want to end my playthrough already.

I've nagging my other friends about Dark Souls trying to get them into it, like an MLM. I have fallen into the Darks Souls rabbit hole and I don't think I will climb out of it.

Overall i'd rate the game a 6/10. Could be better.

My personal favorite game of all time. I played it on PS3, again when remastered released on PS4. Later on the Switch because playing it on a handheld was incredibly novel. The Switch version ran better and looked better than the PS3 version, so to me it was great.

Here I am in 2025 playing it on my Steam Deck. 1080p 60fps while docked. It's honestly heavenly to play a game like this, at this level of quality, on a handheld device.

I am not majorly into the PVP aspect of the game, but find myself still interested to try different builds to this day, despite having played through the game 4 separate times. I know I'm not hardcore like some players and have never gone past NG +1.

With that said, I know enough about the game and series in general to say that the remaster is excellent, it plays great on the Steam Deck, and if you have never played a Dark Souls game...you owe it to yourself.

Die. Die some more. Learn from your mistakes and become a better, more patient, and more knowledgeable player as a result. Oh. And read the item descriptions for Pete's sake.

10/10.

For patient people

An iconic start to a genre debuting a tale of sorrow, despair and sometimes hope as a brutal but relatable metaphor, also came with long walks and a tedious second half

Very little is told to you and it is okay to look at a guide. Just don't let it drive your entire playthrough, expecting to fly past each zone quickly because that is possible, and the price is turning the careful exploration into an aimless hack and slash with a few bosses throughout, which the game wasn't designed to be

To me this game is fun to talk and research about, not as much to play through

Take your time and go to whatever place you want. If it's too hard, there is probably another way

Also the messages on the ground help you a lot.

Good luck

🌞 DARK SOULS: My First Souls-Like Experience
Time Played: 80+ hours (first run, no spoilers)
🎮 Recommended

This was my first Souls-like game—and it completely changed the way I see video games.

I came into it playing like a button-clicker, no idea what I was doing. No guides, no spoilers. Just me, a rusty sword, and a world that wanted me dead.

It was brutal, confusing, and sometimes unfair. But it was also deep, beautiful, and unbelievably rewarding. Every enemy I defeated, every boss I overcame, felt earned. This game taught me patience, timing, and how to truly learn a game's mechanics instead of just button-mashing through.

It took me around 80 hours to finish my first run. Every moment felt unique—from getting completely lost in Lordran to the joy of finally ringing the second Bell of Awakening. And the music? Chilling. The lore? Mysterious and brilliant. The world? Unlike anything else.

Dark Souls is a masterpiece.
10/10. So good. Changed me as a gamer.

Wow... My nameless journey is over. I can't believe that I made it. Oh Dark souls, where do i start? It was an exprience alright.
First of all, don't jump into this one first. Try something else first, beacuse as much as I loved this game, it has problems.
But first of all, Positives.
Music was amazing. Motoi Sakuraba is amazing.
The game is full of atmosphere, and is quite beautiful(in most areas). Graphics are quite good as well, and the gameplay is also good.
the bad? The sconed half, and SOME enemies and bosses. Capra demon and Bed of chaos can go burn in hell. And those basilliks and bone wheels? They'll haunt me. Luckily, I did watch tips, so I knew those in advance. I suggest that you do so as well, for your sanity.
And it's importnet to understand something. Dying is part of the exprience. So always show patience and try different approches.
So in the end, despite the problems, this game is special. I'll never forget this. Just consider this before you buy.
And by the way, do yourself a favor and skip lost izalith. The 30 humanity cost for the shortcut is worth it. I did, and I'm happy that I did. Trust me, the benefits outweight the cost.
And by the by...

I wish I could love this game. I've never played a dark souls game before, so I was generally excited to play it, despite some of the jank that I was warned of compared to future games.

This game is really difficult. But not in the good way. I could count my deaths to bosses, and it wouldn't even come close to the number of times I've died and lost thousands of souls just getting to them through normal enemies. Throughout these 8.7 hours of playing, there were 4 checkpoints on the mainland. There wasn't a single boss that had a nice checkpoint, not even the tutorial boss, since you still had to go through a couple enemies to get to it. The checkpoints in this game are way too far apart from each other, and there are definitely places to put checkpoints as well. For the fight before the Taurus Demon, you walk up like 3 flights of stairs in a watchtower. This is an entire area away from the checkpoint, and the empty floors here would've been PERFECT for one. I died once fighting the Taurus Demon. I died a couple dozen times trying to get there. That's not real difficulty, that's just frustrating game design.

And don't even get me started on the Capra Demon, which is where I gave up (This wasn't immediately after taurus since i went to the undead parish and nightroot garden before coming back). It has a decently alright journey there, albeit a bit drawn out, however the design of this bossfight is awful. They throw 2 of the most ANNOYING enemies, those undead dogs, at you at the same time a massive demon which hits like a truck is swinging at you. I had maybe 2 attempts that lasted past the first 10 seconds, and it's completely up to luck if you can even get past that from what I've seen. The dogs sometimes spawn at the entrance, other times at the back. Other times, 1 dog to each end of the room. It's a skill issue, sure, but the fact that the white light entrance blocks your view as the enemies get a headstart is ridiculous as well. I had an attempt where I couldn't move at all because the dogs were blocking my movement while the capra demon beat the ♥♥♥♥ out of me.

I was lucky to play the game offline because of what I heard from some other reviews. Hackers can get into your game and kill all of the NPCs that you need to progress, supposedly. Does that sound fair? Does that sound fun? Absolutely not. If I went online and that happened to me I would've quit much sooner.

Armour is also weird in this. If I want to take a normal amount of damage from a boss, I need to wear armour that makes me roll at a speed so slow that dodging the boss is basically impossible in most cases. If I want to roll at a normal speed, I need to wear armour that protects me so much less. It's realistic I guess, but it doesn't make it good. Parrying is also inconsistent, not that it just doesn't work every time with the same enemies, that's just a skill issue, but because I have no idea what enemies can be parried and which can't. There is no indication that you can parry an attack like in most other games (Nine Sols, Metroid Dread, and a lot more) and it really makes parrying less fun. I LOVE parrying and this game does make it satisfying when you stab the guys straight through the chest, but it gets old when you've parried the same enemy 20 times trying to get to the damn Taurus Demon.

One good thing I can say about the game is that it is incredibly well optimised. I didn't expect it to run well on my laptop, and yet I was pleasantly surprised. There are occasional frame drops, but nothing that makes the game unplayable.

In the end: If you enjoy being happy, do NOT play this game. I wish I could love this game, but it doesn't live up to the hype for me at least.

EDIT: I have come back to the game and I've gotta say, it is actually really fun. I recommend finding a build online first and maybe a main weapon, I chose the zweihander and it carried me to the end with a strength and endurance build. Basically do your research into what you wanna be before you play.

I came from Elden Ring and I had a blast. It was however, not without setbacks. About three hours in I was invaded by a hacker that killed all of my npcs, effectively soft locking me. So as far as I know DO NOT PLAY MULTIPLAYER. Honestly though this helped me in the long run as I had to restart and pick a better class. The world is just amazingly interlinked with shortcuts everywhere and paths that lead from one area to the next. You feel a sense of progress each time you play, that is until you reach mid-game or the halfway point. Speaking of that, there were many times were I felt frustrated and wanted to quit. Especially you, New Londo. Or even tomb of the giants. Then there's the magic of the souls game. You re-analyze your strategy,, and approach it again with a level head. It is a test of patience which feels immensely rewarding seeing that text flash across your screen and the souls flow in. There's a plethora of weapons to choose from as well, but I found myself sticking with the claymore for the entire game. So while it might initially feel unfair at first, you begin to learn movesets and change up your strategy. Some bosses like the four kings required me to use a more funny strategy where I slapped on my tankiest armor and shield and just went to town. Other bosses just simply are tests of patience and you must wait and dodge for the perfect moment. Every enemy, every boss only contributes to your growing skill and also power. You increase stat points to fight and hit harder, endure attacks with more stamina and health. You can wield even stronger weapons and empower the weapons you already have. You're not forced to switch off a weapon as long as you upgrade it and its scaling attributes.

In conclusion, I absolutely loved Dark Souls 1. Sure, there were times where I was frustrated but with continued perseverance I was able to push past that wall and defeat whatever was thrown at me. I would say difficulty-wise that this is probably easier than Elden Ring which was my entry into the souls series. Just don't go into it thinking everything is going to be easy and thrown at you. With that you should be fine.

Actually, a parting tip from me: Always and I mean Always look for shortcuts. About to fight a boss? Find a shortcut! ASAP. Some bosses might not have shortcuts but the ones that do you will be very grateful for said shortcut.

TLDR; Yeah, amazing game. Shortcuts are your best friend. Remember that!

And don't you dare go hollow!

Still find myself coming back to this game even years later. It's definitely a fun playthrough despite the jank. Some tips for new players: active poise is broken and thus poise is more important than absorption, "light roll" in this game is medium roll compared to the rest of the series but requires less than 25% equipment load, and stick to the standard weapons upgrade path unless you're going to use a wiki (even then, you can only max upgrade two weapons using the elemental paths in one playthrough). Also beating the final boss automatically starts the New Game+ cycle, so complete the DLC and gather any other missing items first.

That said, I'd recommend playing offline aside from becoming a Warrior of Sunlight (only covenant that has a stat requirement and you can lower it through coop), especially for new players. The sheer number of low-level twink invaders, dried finger stat hacker hosts, and McDonald's WiFi backstabs I've ran into recently is actually insane. Generally, from a PVP standpoint, two players that know what they're doing will devolve to poise-crit fishing as it's incredibly easy to do (you'll quickly understand where the nickname "Backstab Souls Remastered" came from). There were a few fun fights I did run into, but they were few and far between. I mean it was funny to parry the twinks, and lure the stat hackers off ledges, but for the average player these are going to be real annoyances.

Overall, it's fun. If you've played later titles by FromSoftware, it's worth coming back to explore where they got their hype from.

Generally really fun gameplay with a world that feels incredible to explore! As someone playing Dark Souls for the first time after completing Elden Ring (and Shadow of the Erdtree), I was impressed by by how fun its map felt to navigate.

The boss fights can vary a lot in quality, with some being extremely fun while others were boring or frustrating. Highlights for me were Quelaag, Ornstein and Smough, and all of the Artorias of the Abyss fights.

The gameplay itself has a lot of high points but is definitely unpolished. For context, I played with a pure melee build without blocks, parries, or summons. Combat speed is slower than later FromSoftware entries but very methodical. It feels extremely satisfying to master combat by maintaining proper positioning against enemies and learning to spot punish windows. I do take issue with how long using estus flasks can take. Many bosses feel like the developers outright did not consider whether the player would have heal windows during the fight. Additionally, target lock is marred by the fact that it restricts your ability to dodge/run. I played most of the game with target lock off as a result. The fact that you cannot roll while running also can make it frustrating to dodge enemies while disengaging from them. Much of platforming is extremely clumsy and slippery, to the point that it's genuinely comedic at times.

Many enemies and bosses have attacks with excessively large or long-lasting hitboxes. Often my best response to these attacks was playing cowardly by staying in their blind spots or baiting attacks I could punish with my dash attack. By far the worst example for me were Titanite Demons, which have absolutely horrible hitboxes. I also dislike how you can get caught on certain attacks (such as horizontal swings from Taurus Demons Kalameet's head swipe) which proceed to drag you along with the attack and damage you even if your dodge was timed correctly.

Overall, my biggest problem with the game is with "runbacks" to boss fights. On one hand it feels satisfying to learn how to run through an area while avoiding attacks from enemies around you. On the other hand, it can grow very tedious and disengaging, especially for bosses built around being highly punishing. The worst example for me was the final boss, which made me feel disconnected from its tone and completely clashes with how relentless and unforgiving the fight can be.

The story and tone are incredibly well-executed and I completely understand why Dark Souls has had such an impact for that reason. The soundtrack is sparingly used but great, and the themes for Sif, O&S, and Gwyn are masterpieces.

its a no, i wish i could mid-recommend this.
being clear, i do recommend the game itself, just not in this state. i love dark souls and have played it on console since release, 100%ing and collecting everything across across countless play throughs and to me, its one of the best games ever made, defining a genre and hooking me into souls like for the last decade.
got it on steam to play it through side-by-side (offline mode) with a friend and try the new coop mod, after completing that i thought id do another 100% run as i havent on steam yet, for those who dont know, that requires 3 full playthroughs to NG++ with the same character

well the reason i want to mid-recommend this is because of the hackers.
twice during 2 seperate runs i was hacked, not fully computer hacked but somoene came into my game without invasion and killed bosses, teleported me, killed major and key NPC's locking me out of questlines, rewards and even upgrades as one time they killed every blacksmith, im not a streamer or anything so i wasnt sniped or targeted specifically, literally just people out there messing with random peoples whole run for no reason whatsoever, (tell me your parents dont love you, without telling me your parents dont love you)
given the age of this game and fromsoftwares most recent releases and rumours, i dont think this will ever be properly fixed as i found the exact same issue on forums from 3 years ago.

of course i know you can play offline but the online aspect of the game is something i enjoy and personally i feel is a key part of the world building as theres lore explanations for the multiplayer, it adds an extra layer of depth to the game and can be essential for new players in finding secrets/illusory walls/missable items or just reading funny messages, imagine trying to find havels armour without messages or flicking back and forth between open windows for guides, I feel no one should have to play the game offline and miss mechanics, covenant rewards, funny messages and missable items because there's people out there who want nothing but to ruin peoples run.

if you are gonna play this game, get a guide and play offline or there's a specific mod designed to find cheaters, you're gonna miss out on multiplayer summons(you can still summon NPC's), some covenant rewards and player messages, which really sucks because it can really change the game is a good way, but its the only way to enjoy this game now. a real real shame.

Posted: May 25
The game that started it all, being the culmination of everything From Software had worked on before. Truly a masterpiece, that's perhaps diminished a little bit by later games in the series doing things better or in expanded manner, that like any game worth anything, is not a game for everyone.

So who would I suggest this game to? I would not suggest it to a person who enjoys a type of game where you are given a mission X in a place A that you're supposed to go do in a place B where upon completion of the said mission you are given a mission Y that you are supposed to go do in a place C, etc. At the start of Dark Souls it's extremely vague what you're supposed to do and you are never given a straight direction where you're supposed to go. In this game you need to find your own way and explore. That's actually the philosophy of the game in general, I feel, that reaches to the way you are meant to approach different encounters or build your character. The game is also of course famous for being very hard on top of punishing you heavily for your mistakes, so if you're not willing to have to possibly try different sections or bosses multiple times, sometimes making backwards progress, the game is not for you. There's also a lot of obtuse stuff in the game that means that you can, and almost certainly will, miss some content in the game - even the access to the DLC is something that you can miss. There are clues in the game but you have to actively look for them.

So if you are a type of person who enjoys exploration and hard but rewarding games, I highly recommend this game. Later games in the series may do certain aspects better than Dark Souls but it's still absolutely worth playing. I would recommend going in blind and then in later playthroughs follow a guide if you're interested in obtaining all the achievements, for example.

Great Game, But Annoyingly Difficult for My Taste

Dark Souls Remastered is a beautiful and well crafted game with phenomenal level design and subtle environmental storytelling.

There's some jank here and there, but overall it's pretty good.

What ended up ruining the experience for me was the boss runs. Not because they were way too difficult, but the lack of checkpoints near boss fights. Every time you die, you have to run all the way back to the boss area, with all normal enemies respawned.

It's fine the first few times, but when you're new to this genre of game and dying a lot to bosses, it gets really exhausting and not fun.

You can try running past them and avoid fights, but you risk taking damage and wasting your limited supply of Estus flasks (Healing Items).

I wanted to continue playing the game, but the punishing checkpoint system made me give up after a few bosses.

First time hopping into the Remastered version of this game, I played it through exclusively Modder Yui's seamless co-op mod. I dare say, that it was the greatest gaming experience I ever had in my life. As thus, my review is more an endorsement of said mod as the main reason to own the Remaster, than it is for the Remaster itself.

That being said, it is a good Remaster. Ladders and jumping work considerably less well, but it is surreal seeing Dark Souls in true Dark Souls form without much improvements. Perhaps this is a good thing? Visually, the game loses some of the finer touches of the original's angst and grit, but I still find it charming.

It is hard to critique anything about the Remastered game, because it is still Dark Souls and now that the original is delisted, this will be most people's Dark Souls. I will admit, it had been so long since I last played the original, that coming into this was extremely fresh. Especially with lots of meta knowledge and a friend who I served with through every single fight and battle.

Playing this game with seamless co-op honestly makes me almost discard the thesis of Miyazaki's vision for the multiplayer entirely, as much as it is still a complete joy to invade, be invaded or summoned. But truly, playing this game as a full on adventure with a friend is some of the most absurd fun imaginable.

My friend and I had pre-planned this for awhile and eventually decided on Sorcery and Pyromancy builds. His deformed, blue skinned gremlin was none other than Harry Pothead, who would eventually hold many staffs, fire many soul arrows and wear Big Logan's Big Ass Hat, where as my character, Dabby, was a masochistic house-elf who appropriately wore nothing but rags the entire game, as a good Pyromancer/house elf would.

The aura farming of the game, often due to numerous visual glitches that occur due to varying mod synchronicity between the sessions of the games, were hilarious to witness. But honestly, there was just something beyond magical about hunting down every item, every soul drop, getting every sorcery and pyromancy, working our way to level 99 characters with absolutely cracked equipment buffs, fighting every boss, hunting every secret--it was the quintessential Dark Souls experience.

The pain of Blighttown, the difficulty of early reliance on just normal fireballs, grinding intelligence and attunement to become incredibly OP, hunting down rings, hats, crowns, getting bug-headed to gain more pyromancies, grinding humanities, popping humanities, donating humanities to a coven, losing 10 humanity and 90k souls, then eventually gaining them all back through effort alone. Dark Souls is still truly the pinnacle of the series somehow.

Getting invaded by other players, sometimes duos, was utterly interesting. However, in most cases, late game pyromancy instantly toasted them. It was really a joy seeing how some players reacted to a full pyromancy build, the uninitiated always fear what they do not understand.

With Power Within, certain rings and certain headgear, you can easily stack your fire damage to 400%. This is the reason why subsequent Dark Souls games absolutely assassinated pyromancy and fundamentally rebuilt how such a class works. But that is why it is great.

We melted Manus and Gwyn on our first tries. Chaos Storm, Great Combustion and Chaos Fireball, as well as ♥♥♥♥ tons of stacks of fireballs, can get you incredibly far. Especially against bosses with 170% health and damage buffs, to compensate for the persistent second player. And it was fun testing new strategies against numerous bosses, going slow moving tank with Iron Flesh or just risking it all with RTSR set ups and Power Within.

If you only played a melee character, I beg of you, try a sorcery or pyromancy build. There are few things as fun as this in any game and the absolutely cracked status of pyro-builds late game are something to behold. You start this game feeling one inch tall and leave, enkindling the flame, feeling as if you had slayed the untouchable.

Dark Souls gameplay is the more engaging than it will ever be frustrating. There is something rhythmic about it, even when you are running a circuit to get back to a boss fight, running past enemies or risking curses to not have to fight certain enemy types again so soon, the game is truly strategic and provokes the most intuitive parts of your brain.

Manus and Gwyn? Melted on the first try. Artorias? Melted.

But hydras? I have fighting hydras with this build. Firesage was surprisingly difficult too.

But by far, the biggest struggle was Black Dragon Kalameet with no melee capability at all. My friend and I were so close, so many times. But sadly, we had to run off, grab Bellowing Dragoncrest Ring and some funny hats. By the time we came back, Kalameet got completely cooked and it felt so good to triumph over him almost effortlessly.

Of course, there are some enemies that are immune to fire and cannot be defeated, but my friend Harry Pothead dispatched them while I kited them or, by pure misfortune or grand luck, certain bosses simply fell through the map and died. (Once again, the quintessential Dark Souls experience)

We encountered a hilarious glitch with Dark Sun Gwyndolin in the mod, where we repeatedly would defeat him, leave, only to emerge into the same hallway. Each time, he would repeat: "Mark the words of mineself, Gwyndolin! Thou shalt not go unpunished!"

Luckily, we got out of the infinite boss loop by simply abusing homeward bone, which seemingly was infinite thanks to the mod creator's intuitive foresight.

It was great fun, truly. Except for the first trip through Blighttown, the numerous returns to dreaded eras for the sake of getting all pyromancies and upgrading the pyromancy hand all the way, etc etc.

Sen's Fortress is still awful.

The only major glitch we truly encountered was that we were unable to invade Knight Lautrec with the black eye orb. We tried separately, but in his world, everyone just stood there talking ♥♥♥♥.

However, it managed to fix itself and we got we needed. Which was a blessing, given that being unable to avenge Anastacia would have driven us mad.

We also rescued Onion Knight and Daughter by the end of the game, and saved Solaire from getting wormed.

Kicked Patches down a hole, but otherwise, he made it out alright.

As per tradition, we more or less killed Petrus on sight.

Dark Souls... Remastered! If you never played Dark Souls, you have no street cred. Get it now! But really, get it, play it with a friend with the seamless co-op mod, someone who knows what they are doing. And embrace arguably one of the greatest games of our generation. The golden standard of what something with soul (lol) truly is.

Also RIP Sif and Artorias, I cry every time.

*Note: This review contains spoilers for both DS and DS3.*

As someone who was introduced to Soulslike games through Elden Ring, my first venture into Dark Souls was in the form of DS3. It absolutely blew my mind. I never thought I'd find anything like it again. The story telling, world building and atmosphere was immaculate. It remains, to this day, one of my all time favorite pieces of media. When it came to earlier renditions of DS3, I was hesitant to try them - I'd heard many a tale about the dreaded hit box of DS2, and I was worried that the quality of the original game, Dark Souls, would be lacking.

Oh, how wrong I was. This game is life-changing. It is beautiful and painful in a way that must be experienced through your own play through - the spirit of it is wholly intangible through watching videos or reading about the source material (though those are excellent supplementary documents). Not only was this game worth all three play throughs (and will certainly garner more in the future), but it felt so very special to deeply understand the became so clear to me, and I myself felt swallowed by the of these games.

Yes, you should play this game. It is worth every penny a thousand times over. It is a gift, a work of art, and will humble you in ways you never thought possible. As you embark on your journey, friend, be safe out there -- and

You ever get dropkicked by a goat demon in a hallway the size of a broom closet?
I have. Multiple times and the strat is to always to shove my claymore between his huge demonic cheecks.

This game is basically medieval debt collection. You lose your souls, die trying to get them back, then die again because a skeleton did a cartwheel at you. NPCs speak in cryptic riddles and laugh like they just watched you stub your toe. One guy literally lives in a poison swamp for fun.

And yet… there's Solaire. My radiant, sun-obsessed himbo. And the Onion Knight, who looks like a walking kitchen appliance and still has more bravery than me facing a group project.

Every time I thought, "I can't do this," I remembered: somewhere out there, a man in spherical armor is jumping into a pit screaming “I shall assist!” and immediately getting stuck.

10/10. It’s not just a game. It’s group therapy for masochists.

In this review I will be writing about the remastered version of the game. Personally I was a bit disappointed that it did not get the Demon's Souls treatment as I think it would have looked amazing. While I understand the difference between a remake and a remaster I nonetheless think it's a missed opportunity.
I still recommend the game of course, it's THE dark souls. The combat is fun and functional even if clunky. There are many builds and items you can experiment with, although maybe not as balanced as in it's sequels. The areas have a cool interconnectivity and they are not too difficult to explore and enjoy however, some of them are quite tedious, both visually and with the number of annoying enemies. Some of the boss runbacks are also a far cry from the Elden Ring's stakes of Marika.
Overall if you are new to the soul's series and you want to see where it all started I warmly recommend that you try it but don't get frustrated if it's not as fast, take things slow and methodical.

Honestly an awesome game that stands strong even today.
It has a fantastic level design, a lot of rewarding secrets, and it understands the player.

The game forces you to learn the mechanics in clever ways, and punish you hard for skipping steps.
(Early on, I found myself practicing parrying for almost 2.5h straight, just to get some consistency.)

You have to struggle your way forward, with minimal guidance, which can be frustrating but also very rewarding. Strongly recommend to play it blind, without guides and such (at least the first time).

I have never seen a game that forces you to become a better player (not just of DS1 but of games in general) quite like this. There is truly a time before, and after, Dark Souls. (My favorite game so far)

Posted: May 15
Review of Dark Souls Remastered

Dark Souls Remastered is a masterpiece that, even years after its release, remains one of the most outstanding representatives of the action RPG genre. The game, which is a refreshed version of the cult title from FromSoftware, delights in many respects, combining brilliantly designed audiovisual setting, thoughtful world and challenging gameplay into a coherent, artistic whole.

Audiovisual setting

The audiovisual setting in Dark Souls Remastered is a true masterpiece. The architecture of the locations is not only aesthetically pleasing, but also narrative - monumental buildings, such as Anor Londo Castle, overwhelm with their grandeur, emphasizing the player's smallness in the face of the game world. In contrast, cramped, claustrophobic spaces, such as the Tomb of the Giants, evoke feelings of anxiety and entrapment. Locations such as Blighttown, with its dark, overwhelming atmosphere, or the picturesque but disturbing Lake Ash, are memorable for their unique atmosphere.

The music is equally outstanding. The epic orchestral themes accompanying boss fights emphasize their majesty and power. On the other hand, the calm, almost disturbingly soothing melody in Firelink Shrine, the central location to which the player returns most often, contrasts perfectly with the brutality of the world. Interestingly, the music is almost absent for most of the game, adding to the feeling of emptiness and loneliness. The ambient sounds - footsteps on the water, the rustling of armor or the sound of sword strikes - are refined to the smallest detail, adding to the immersion.

World and locations

The world of Dark Souls Remastered is semi-open and ingeniously designed. The player is free to choose which locations to go to first, but the areas themselves are linear, which gives the gameplay the right rhythm. Interlocality, or the interconnectedness of the locations, is very impressive - discovering shortcuts, such as going from the Undead Parish to the Firelink Chapel, gives a sense of cohesion to the world.

The locations are extremely diverse: from the murky depths to the toxic swamps of Blighttown to the majestic walls of Anor Londo. Each has a unique atmosphere and challenges that force the player to adjust his strategy. The only drawback is the placement of the campfires - the save points. They are often too far away from the bosses, which can be frustrating, especially after multiple deaths. While this can be explained as an element of punishment for defeat, which increases the satisfaction of final victory, tougher enemies at closer foci might have been a better solution.

Gameplay and mechanics

Dark Souls Remastered is famous for its high difficulty level, although compared to newer titles like Elden Ring, many bosses seem relatively easy. Most of them, including the final Gwyn, can be defeated in a few attempts, which is sometimes disappointing, especially if you're expecting an epic, multi-phase battle. No boss has a second form, which contrasts with the more complex clashes in other FromSoftware games. Nevertheless, some fights, like with Ornstein and Smoughem, can be a real test of skill, requiring up to a dozen approaches.

The game's mechanics are extremely complex. Combat is based on the stamina system, which forces strategic management of attacks, dodging and blocking. The player has a wide arsenal of weapons at his disposal - from fast daggers to two-handed swords to magical catalysts. Each type of weapon offers a different style of play, making the choice of equipment dependent on the player's preference. Upgrading weapons and armor is complicated, requiring gathering the right materials and planning, which is a challenge in itself.

Character development based on souls, which are lost upon death and must be regained, forces caution and long-term planning. The game does not lead the player by the hand - the lack of clear directions forces exploration and self-discovery of mechanics, which can be as rewarding as it is frustrating.

Narrative

Dark Souls Remastered's plot is subtle, conveyed through item descriptions, NPC dialogue and world design. There is no classic narrative - the player has to piece together Lordran's story on his own, which works perfectly with the sense of loneliness and uncertainty. The mechanic of subtitles left by other players, consisting of ready-made phrases, not only facilitates communication, but also deepens the atmosphere, being an integral part of the narrative.

Technical aspects

In technical terms, the game works flawlessly. During gameplay, I did not encounter any bugs, frame drops or crashes. The only problems are occasional inaccurate hitboxes of some bosses and awkward camera work during fights with large enemies, which can lead to unnecessary deaths.

Summary

Dark Souls Remastered is a game that captivates with its depth, atmosphere and artistic approach. Although it is not without flaws its brilliantly designed world, elaborate mechanics and unique atmosphere make it a must-have title for any game fan. After finishing the game, I have no doubt that I will reach for other FromSoftware productions to immerse myself in their unique world again.

Posted: May 15
Dark Souls was THE gateway drug for me, I'm afraid.
If you find it to your liking, the rest of your life might be ruined in a way. You'll keep searching for that very kind of experience that is rarely present in games, even in soulslike titles. The game makes sure to punish hasty and sloppy players, but generously rewards those who want to take time and methodically explore, both by giving you lots of opportunities to build your character any way you want, and by showing you new and exciting locations as it progressed.

As I've already said in my PtDE review, the design, monster and level planning are fantastic. FROM probably ran out of budget in the last quarter of the game (the drop in quality is noticeable), but that's still tolerable.

Well worth your time.

All that said, if you play online and are unlucky enough, you can still get invaded by hackers, who can break your game by softlocking your progress. The publisher/developer does nothing to address the issue and fix the experience.

Posted: May 13
Nice game, still strugling, I'll come back later with a full review.

Well, I promised to come back with a full review, here it is. I think Miyazaki San had more ideas in mind than he managed to execute, and I'm not even talking about the game being bad from the second half onwards.

Most of the game is disgusting ugly. And it's a medieval fantasy, the maps should be fascinating. Firelink Shrine and Undead Parish are beautiful. The exterior part of Undead Asylum with it's mountain view too, as well as the external part of Sen's Fortress. Kiln of the First Flame is impressive, the perfect setting for a final battle.

Anor Londo is a very interesting, imposing and beautiful place, its gigantic halls convey the idea that the people who lived there were from the most powerful class, at the same time that its uncomfortable emptiness makes us feel sorry for the end that took such a great race... almost like the tale of Ozymandias.

But Blighttown, the Dephts, Tomb of the Giants... disgusting useless, completely disposable. Lost Izalith could have been better, a kind of hellish Anor Londo, but it didn't even come close.

The whole world of the game is sad. The first NPC we find in Firelink Shrine is a drepressed and hopeless knight that's shows how people live in this world. Dark Souls is a post-apocalipse game, there's no hope and, even if you light the flame again in the end, feels like there's no one else more to save.

The variety of builds is impressive and this game deserves relevance just for delivering an RPG with this variety of gameplay while being an action RPG. It's worth the experience, but I don't consider it a good game, and I don't intend to face NG+. It was worth the experience.

Dark Souls is a good game, no doubt about it. The gameplay is pretty simple, but it works, though you can definitely feel the age of the game in some mechanics. Things like hit detection, movement, and camera angles can be a bit rough, and sometimes it feels like the difficulty comes more from those outdated systems than the actual challenge.

I do think the price is a bit too high at this point. For a game that’s been out this long, it would make sense to lower it so more people can jump in without hesitation.

Despite its flaws, it’s a game that left its mark. I could point out a lot of things that don’t work well, but honestly, it’s still worth playing. Recommended, especially if you’re curious about where this whole genre started.

If you have never played a souls game I cannot in good consciousness recommend you to play this game. This game has all the great bones of what has become such a wonderful and lovable franchise. This is fortunately not my very first souls game. I did play Eldenring first so I can see exactly where they started and where they ended and I think they've done an absolute bang up job. Plaguetown is the most unnecessary thing in this game and it's completely unforgiving in the choices that you make. Saving some characters will end up killing others to the point that it can destroy your entire playthrough for a while. Everything is completely obtuse and there is no direction on what to do next period without a guide I would absolutely not know what to do. I did thoroughly enjoy my time playing this game, I cannot lie. However, I could not in good consciousness recommend this to someone if this is their very first soul game. This is definitely a game that will go down in the ages as having lit the bonfire that became the souls franchise and I think that it well deserves to be placed in the top tier of older games that should continue to be preserved and this remastered version does a very good job at that. This is a cruel and unforgiving game. I enjoyed my 28 hours in this game but if I didn't have to go to random areas to find random items like a ring that allows me to walk through water or a ring that allows me to actually even face a boss the game would have probably only been 20 hours long and that's pushing it. What took me so long was after beating the boss in blighttown having to get out of blighttown with no ability to fast travel and having all the bonfires that I needed be unlit because my firekeeper died because I saved a character that you absolutely look like you should save. I will give this game a thumbs up but I do not recommend it if you are not a souls veteran and are not ready to throw down for countless hours and look up a guide to see where to go next. I'm currently a good portion through Dark Souls 3 and have completed 90% of pre DLC elden ring that I am now going back and completing before starting the DLC. I enjoy from software and I have no problem supporting that company.

Это все ещё тот же самый Dark Souls: PTDE, но частично с дсфиксом. Разве что багфиксов толком нет, но чуть получше сделано меню списка ковенантов, где-то текстуры и освещение получше (где-то ещё хуже, чем было).
Если коротко про первые души, то мало что хорошего могу сказать. Сложность, скорее, в том, что есть локации вроде crystal cave, где маршрут занимает около 2 минут до босса, но за счет того, что ты что-то не помнишь (мой случай) или в первый раз идешь, то тебе обеспечены косяки. Игра тренирует внимательность и изучение шаблонов, запоминание маршрутов, поведения боссов и всего-всего, чтобы достичь результата. Очень криво, но, простите за каламбур, душевно. Мне эта часть нравится меньше всего, поскольку она кривая, неказистая, но по-своему уникальная. Тут или люблю/ненавижу или стокгольмский синдром; решайте сами.
В этот раз проходил вдвоём с близким с помощью модификации "Seamless Co-op". Интересный опыт. Да, слегка коряво, есть баги, но кроме как на получение достижений за прокачку оружия и ковенантов, они ни на что не влияют по факту. Было весело, в принципе, я бы и посоветовал на перепрохождение именно этот способ, потому что так куда веселее, чем опять в одного все эти локации зачищать уже в н-ный раз.
Хоть и серия со своей грамотной философией, я бы сказал, уникальной, но гейм-дизайн и кривизна могут истощить тех, кто отвык чему-то учиться в процессе игры. Рекомендую, но с оговорками.

Dark Souls Remastered – My first Miyazaki experience

I went in blind. No wiki, no guides, just me, a rusty sword, and a world that hated me on sight. Dark Souls Remastered was my first step into Miyazaki’s twisted cathedral of pain and beauty… and I’ve never been the same since.

Why You NEED to Play This:
✅ A world that speaks in silence.
No quest markers. No hand-holding. Just broken castles, cursed forests, and undead ruins dripping with mystery. Every step forward feels like uncovering a forgotten legend.

✅ Combat that respects your brain (and punishes your ego).
Every fight matters. Every enemy can kill you. But when you learn – really learn – the dance of dodge, parry, and strike… it becomes poetry in motion. Brutal poetry.

✅ Bosses that live rent-free in your mind.
From the towering knight of Anor Londo to the tragic fall of Artorias, these are not just enemies – they’re stories. Each one a test. Each one unforgettable.

✅ Atmosphere so thick, you can taste the ash.
This world isn’t just dark. It’s sacred. Hollowed. Full of despair and hope intertwined. There were moments I just stood still, listening to the wind, watching the light filter through a ruined church window – and felt something real.

✅ True sense of progression.
You don’t just level up your character. You level up. Your patience, your observation, your resilience. And when you finally ring that second bell… it’s not just a victory. It’s a rebirth.

BUT WARNING:
❌ It will break you.
There is no mercy here. You will die. You will rage. You’ll swear off the game more than once. But if you stay… if you push through…

You’ll discover something few games ever deliver: a genuine sense of triumph.


Dark Souls Remastered isn’t perfect. It’s beautifully flawed. But it’s one of the most rewarding, unforgettable, and strangely magical games I’ve ever played. If you’ve never tried a Souls game, start here – but go in ready to suffer. And to grow.

Because behind all the death, the despair, and the dying screams… is a masterpiece that’s just waiting for you to persevere.

My Score: 10/10 – Praise the sun

All the flaws that this DEmastered version has are forgiven only because the "Seamless Co-Op" mod exists and is so much fun. Otherwise this wouldn't be worth the twenty dollars they're charging for a decade plus old game now that they scumily pulled the original version off the steam store to resell a higher priced inferior product to unsuspecting customers (read: zoomers). So, you should immediately go and get that from NEXUS MODS if you decide to buy this. Don't even take two seconds thinking about it, just go ahead and get it, like, pronto.

(And don't worry if you're computer illiterate the instructions for the mod are extremely clear and everything is easy to install without any frustration at all. It only takes like ten minutes to get everything working properly.)

JUST SOME ADVICE AND A WORD OF CAUTION, THOUGH:
I must tell you that before you play with anyone you first have everyone go through the tutorial until after you get the Estus Flask from Oscar. Then go back to the bonfire and summon your partner(s). If you don't do this either you or your partner(s) won't have the flasks to heal with when you arrive at Firelink Shrine lmao. I learned this the hard way, sadly.

And again, if you're hesitant to get this because you own the original and think that's good enough, or you heard bad things about this version (many of which are true) and decided to stay away from it, I'm telling you right now to go ahead and get this when it's on sale. Then just immediately go and download the mod right away and then play that with your family and friends. I guarantee you won't regret getting this at all no matter the circumstances. I freaking love this so much. I'm kicking myself so hard for waiting this long to try out this mod and go on a grand adventure with my loved one. It rekindled my love for gaming and reminded me once again (I often need reminders over the years it seems) as to why I even played games when I was a child to begin with.

This mod changed the game for the better, that's for sure. Praise the Sun for dedicated modders that use their energy and technical skills to help others. The modding communities for the Souls games are simply unmatched.

Posted: May 9
(Played offline so true hours are roughly ~25-30)
This game is a masterpiece, coming from Elden Ring makes it hard because you wanna go be free and explore. But you have to appreciate how the map is designed here. Any direction you choose to go ends up connecting back to where you've been. As you unlock more doors, getting around the map is easier and so is returning to previous areas.
Besides that, it just feels nice to play what started the whole genre. I can say I wish there were more bosses like Elden Ring has, but I appreciate what we were given here. I enjoy the story as well, and piecing together what's going on there.
This game does feel harder than Elden Ring. No mimic tear to help you; and far, far less save spots. Keep that in mind if you found Elden Ring hard enough as-is - this one will amp up the pain. Very worth it, though.

Posted: May 9
My First Souls game did not disappoint.

After years of sitting on the sidelines and seeing the name FromSoftware pop up over and over again as one of the most respected names in gaming, I finally decided to take the plunge. I finally decided to see what this mysterious, relatively new genre called “Soulslike” was all about. And what better place to start than at its origin?
I was not disappointed.

Dark Souls is difficult. There’s not getting around that. These Miyazaki games are known for their difficulty level and they absolutely deliver. What I didn’t expect is how good the difficulty feels.

Normally if a game is too easy, you breeze through it and may feel unfulfilled. If it’s too difficult, you may struggle and give up. And even if it’s just right, you miss out on that struggle. What makes these game so interesting is that no matter how difficult the game got, I kept wanting to jump back in and conquer it. There’s something about Dark Souls that gives you the urge to just keep fighting until you succeed. That’s the special sauce that so many other games get wrong – a feeling that no matter how tough something is, you can work hard and overcome it. I felt that even more in Sekiro (which I played immediately after this, but more on that in that a separate review).

I died. A lot. I got frustrated. I got angry. I even swore at the screen on occasion. But what I didn’t do was abandon the game. No matter how frustrating it got, it always felt fun and worthwhile.



• The lore is very cool. The story is virtually nonexistent, and anyone who says Dark Souls has some complex storyline is lying to you. But just the way the world is built, with its areas, enemies, and weapons – is incredibly creative. The influences are clear – the game feels to me like a cross between Zelda, Lord of the Rings, and Super Ghouls N Ghosts – but it handles them all with a great ambiance. World-building and level design are fantastic.
• The music is very good, with some truly great moments – particularly the Gargoyles and Gwyn. Sound design is top notch across the board.
• Despite this being a 2011 game with some minor 2018 updates, it looks quite good. It’s amazing how far art design and creativity will get you, even with an outdated game engine. The way you can see the interconnected world and distant areas of the game is beautiful. Anor Londo is a stunner.
• The level design is fantastic. The Undead Parish elevator is truly one of the most memorable moments in gaming I’ve had in a while. Sadly, the level design suffers in the latter half (ie. Lost Izalith), but it’s just a really well-designed game. The interconnectivity gives the game a real sense of physicality, as though this is a real place you’re exploring and uncovering its secrets.
• There are some truly excellent boss fights (especially the Gargoyles, O & S, and Artorias, although there are also some bad ones, like the Capra Demon, and of course Bed of Chaos.



• After 71 hours, I still think the Humanity system is pointless. The foreign invasions are more of a nuisance than anything.
• The game needs more story and more cinematics. The best by far is Gough’s arrow in the DLC, but that kind of thing is very rare in DS. Even the end-of-game cutscenes are severely lacking.
• The NPC storylines are poorly handled. They’re so easy to miss out on, and consequently, it’s so easy to miss out on significant game content. I never even encountered Kaathe. I never got egg infested. I never partook in Alvina’s summons. And without reading about them online, I never would have figured out how to access the DLC (one of the best parts of the game), how to fight Gwyndolin, or even how to reach the Four Kings. People call it “environmental storytelling” but I don’t think that’s accurate. It’s just bad design.
• Runbacks suck. ‘Nuff said.
• Is it just me or is the music from the character creation screen a complete knockoff of the Resident Evil save screen music?
• Perhaps this is a me thing, but I found the RPG elements went too far, with too much stuff left unexplored. For example, there was so much loot in game that I never used or even learned about. I still don’t know what Miracles do or how to use spells or what to do with boss souls or what raw/divine/occult weapons are or what all the summons are (which are pvp, which are NPC?) or what Gravelord Servant summon is or all the poses you learn or so many power ups blows, crossbows, etc… It all felt like it could have used an edited to organize things better and remove some unnecessary elements.



• I encountered some bugs, like the Centipede Demon apparently committing suicide, dragon legs in Ariamis,
• Blight Town is vastly underrated. I enjoyed it quite a bit. The worst area by far is the Great Hollow.
• It’s too easy to accidentally attack shopkeepers and mess things up. Also, you should be able to sell items, as opposed to having to rely on the non-obvious late game mechanics of feedings things to Frampt. I had so many duplicate items that I wanted to get rid of, since they clogged up my inventory.
• Curse is a bad mechanic, and Toxic is too powerful. At least the devs realized to make the former extremely rare and the latter was given to enemies who don’t respawn (the dart guys). Smart decision. The fact that the devs don’t make you replay all of the Bed of Chaos when you die also saved so much frustration.
• Armor is unforgiving. It’s hard to get below 50% weight with decent armor. Later games are supposedly better with mobility.
• The 2 ring limit is insufficient. It should have been 3 or 4.
