Don't let my hours fool you, I played through this game at least 10 times on Xbox. While it lacks the modern day polish of more recent FromSoftware games, this is a "souls-like" game at their peak. I remember struggling to beat the 1st boss when I first played this game at the ripe age of 13 thinking it was going to be like Skyrim. Cut to freshman year of college and I'm jumping out of my chair and audibly cheering as I beat Gwyn for the very first time.

Fallout: New Vegas and Red Dead Redemption 2 are the only two other games that have matched that enthusiasm and satisfaction I felt playing this game. Whether it was finally beating a boss that caused me so much grief or discovering another shortcut back to Firelink Shrine, Dark Souls gave me a sense drive and determination that very few games are able to accomplish.

If you're coming from Elden Ring or DS3 the game can feel a bit sluggish. But once you get past that, the Kingdom of Lordran is waiting to offer you a punishing yet fulfilling experience unlike any game that came out around this time (or even now). When it comes to games I consider a "must play" Dark Souls remains in the top 3 for over a decade.

You don't need me to tell you it's legacy, it's a genre spawning masterpiece that has shook the field of game-design like the dooms and castlevanias of the past.

What I will tell you is that the game is obtuse, and unless you are the kind of person with the spare energy and desire to commit to unplesant and needless struggle, it's best approached with some guidence.

Learn how character work, iframes, carry load and roll thresholds, damage types, stats, upgrades, etc. Learn how to kindle a bonfire, learn how a parry is supposed to work, and then try it now and then until you become confident enough to practise, and while the open nature of the level has a beautifully unique 3d metroidvania design, don't expect an available area to mean it's actually available, you dont begin by entering the catacombs.

If this seems like an absurd amount of buy in for a game that is supposed to be well designed, know this: darksouls doesn't want to be played, and it's brilliance is learning to overcome the barriers of information, skill, and your own psyche to do it anyways.

It's a game about traversing a world that's dying, where people either have, or are about to, fall into depression. There is a reason this game has helped people fight depression, because it teaches you the tools to face unimaginable challenge and hardship, and teaches you that even when you take care of the basics, like your stats and gear, it's ultimately never going to seize to be you having to face your demons, and die over and over again untill you 'git gud'. With a little jolly cooperation for morale.

Darksouls is a game that would be a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ arcade game, but they gave you infinite quarters and said "The game can only be beaten with skill, you can only be beaten if yu resign. Let's see which one gives" and the the game herd the same challenge. The game strives to drain you of everything, you patience, you trust, your hope, your determination, your very humanity, but however hard it tries, when the flesh has been gnawed of your bones, even as a skeleton of a person, you shouldn't ever give up, because if you dig deeper you can allways find meaning n the stuggle, and a reason to keep going, no matter how empty thigs look, don't you dare go hollow.

Darksouls is a good game. Darksouls is also a masterful piece of truly inspired art.

"There was Fire and with fire came disparity. Heat and cold, life and death, and of course, light and dark. Then from the dark, They came, and found the Souls of Lords within the flame. Nito, the First of the Dead, The Witch of Izalith and her Daughters of Chaos, Gwyn, the Lord of Sunlight, and his faithful knights. And the Furtive Pygmy, so easily forgotten. With the strength of Lords, they challenged the Dragons. Gwyn's mighty bolts peeled apart their stone scales. The Witches weaved great firestorms. Nito unleashed a miasma of death and disease. And Seath the Scaleless betrayed his own, and the Dragons were no more. Thus, began the Age of Fire. But soon the flames will fade and only Dark will remain. Even now there are only embers, and man sees not light, but only endless nights. And amongst the living are seen, carriers of the accursed Darksign."

"Only, in the ancient legends it is stated, that one day an Undead shall be chosen to leave the Undead Asylum in pilgrimage.
To the land of the ancient Lords,
Lordran."


You are inflicted with the Darksign, which turns you into an undead. Out of fear, the undead are locked away into an asylum at the end of the world, where they are kept until they lose their minds, and go Hollow. However, you, like some others before, get taken to Lordran, the land of Gods, in order to rekindle Lord Gwyn's flame and save the world from eternal darkness.

You are now entering a world where time is a circle, where legends bleed into reality, and duty is like a chain that binds the soul. This is a realm of Lordran, a forsaken land of gods and men.

The story of the Four Knights of Gwyn begins with a knight, Ornstein, the Dragon Slayer, a name etched into the annals of a forgotten age. Ornstein was no ordinary knight. He was a spearhead of Lord Gwyn's war against the Dragons. His weapon, sharp enough to pierce a stone in scales, and his armor gilded like the mane of a lion, marked him as a predator among men and slayer of beasts. He was a captain of the Four Knights of Gwyn, warriors chosen to carve a new order from the ruins of the ancient world. But, glory is a fleeting mirage. The Dragons fell, the first flame waned, and the golden age of the gods had dimmed into twilight. No Chosen Undead came to claim the Lordvessel.

Duty became a prison, and loyalty a curse. In the world of Lordran, where myths are as tangible as the steel of a cross spear, truth is a shadow, that shifts with the light.

When the gods abandoned the city, Ornstein stood with Executioner Smough, guarding the cathedral and its secrets. Their mission? To test the worth of those who dared to walk the path of the Chosen Undead. Centuries passed, and the cathedral grew silent. Time, like a relentless tide, eroded Ornstein's resolve. He abandoned his post, leaving behind the Leo Ring, a symbol of his knighthood, and sought the Nameless King, a forgotten deity shrouded in storms and shadows. Though, nobody dared to speak his name.

This is a tale of valor, sacrifice and a darkness that devours even the brightest of lights. In this corner of existence, beneath the fading embers of a dying flame resides a knight who's legend outlived his flesh. Sir Artorias, once a paragon of chivalry, now a tragic monument to the upon crumbling foundations. Artorias the Abysswalker. A loyal servant to Gwyn, Lord of Sunlight, and a trusted member of the Four Knights, a fraternity of warriors tasked with protecting a kingdom built on fire and deceit. Artorias was a beacon of hope. When the Darkness swept into the world, it was Artorias who answered the call.

He descended into the Abyss, a churning sea of malevolence born from humanity's untamed soul. But even legends have their limits. The Abyss was no ordinary foe. It was a force that consumed all it touched. Artorias fought valiantly, but his strength faltered. Corrupted and broken, Artorias the Abysswalker became a shadow of the hero he once was. A puppet to the Darkness he sought to destroy. And yet, even in defeat, there was a spark of dignity. Overwhelmed by the Abyss, Artorias used the last of his strength, but not to save himself. With his final act, Artorias shielded his companion Sif from the Abyss, ensuring that the wolf would live to honor his memory. He casted a shield around her, and let himself fall. By the time you encounter him, he's no longer the noble knight he once was. Corrupted, armor corroded, and consumed by rage, he attacks without thought or memory. You are no longer fighting a hero. You're witnessing what happens when you lose against the Abyss.

Artorias's name henceforth passed into legend. The Knight who gave his life to disperse the Abyss, to rescue the Princess of Oolacile.

This is a lie, of course.

The legend of Artorias is a lie. He did not vanquish the Father of The Abyss. He did not eliminate the Darkwraiths of New Londo Ruins. The Abyss is unstoppable. But his legend can only live on with his death. Only his fellow knights knew the truth, but they would take his soul and his secret to the grave. And while Artorias's soul was corrupted by the worst of humanity, it was the best of humanity that redeemed it. And the hero of Oolacile would disappear without a trace, almost as if he didn't belong. For in reality, he didn't.

For centuries, Sif would stand vigil over the knight's tomb, a silent guardian of a legend tarnished by truth. And eons later, deep withing an enchanted wood, behind two locked doors, and a troop of forests protectors, an ancient cat named Alvina speaks the truth. Alvina is keen to downplay the legend of Artorias because heroes and bandits have begun seeking out his grave. They would desecrate his legacy for the secret of the Abyss Walking. And they may do harm to Sif, who in fact, Alvina helped you rescue back when the world was still young.

Imagine what Sif is feeling when you cross paths again years later. Your duty bounds to seek the Abyss, and Sif is duty bound to stop you. One of you will have to die, because Sif cannot give you the covenant of Artorias willingly. Maybe in death, she can find her friend Artorias, again.


He was perhaps a lingering question. Do legends die? Or do they simply wait for their time to rise again?


He did not fight for glory.
He fought with fury, like a beast bound by duty.
Artorias knew he could not save Oolacile,
but he swore to hold back the Abyss even if it swallowed him whole.
He never meant to return.
He walked in, with a sword in one hand, and faith in the other.
It was not a knight who fell.
It was the last light before the darkness.


Dark Souls.

Posted: May 5
I finally tried out the legend itself. My report is...

It's great. Aged poorly in certain areas, but a great time overall. For the right kind of player. This isn't a game for the Animal Crossing or Hello Kitty crowd. No no no. This is a game for another kind of player. Someone that wants to prove a point with a game. Someone that wants to say "oh yeah I beat THAT one". Perhaps someone that has an ego with their game skill. Dark Souls is that.

It is a game that you can grind and become overpowered in. Invest heavily into vitality, put points into endurance and put on heavy armor. Then equip a greatshield and a high damage weapon with decent range and stagger. You have now won the game. High poise (the more you have, the harder you are to stagger), stamina, health and armor make you really hard to kill. You effectively become a boss with how bulky you are combined with your damage and healing. Dodging becomes a thing of the past (mostly). Currently in the middle of Dark Souls 3 and I can see why both enemies and bosses have long attack combos instead of one or two attacks per rotation. FromSoftware learned that making the player dodge more is how you make these games harder.

Dark Souls 1 does have some nonsense level design at points. Anor Londo, or as I call it AnaI Londo has a bs section where you're walking up towards a balcony and there are two archers that shoot giant arrows at you. These arrows don't just hurt; they knock you back hard. There isn't much space to move, since there's a death pit all around the walkway. On top of that, one of the archers is standing in the way of where you have to go, and when you get next to him, he pulls out a melee weapon and shield. Meanwhile, the other archer is still shooting at you.

Then, you have the Demon Ruins, which is your typical lava level. Except, you are forced to take damage, since you have to run through lava to progress. Not just to your hp, but to your gear. You do get a lava resistant ring, but your gear still takes durability damage. Yes, his game has weapon and armor durability, and repairing costs souls. I'm glad that 3 (and maybe 2) made repairing automatically happen at the shrine bonfire. Artificial difficulty that sucks some souls out of the player that could be used for leveling up or buying stuff.

This is both a positive and a negative, but the game is quite open ended. This is great for non linearity, but it becomes an issue for a first time player, especially a blind one (which I WASN'T, thankfully good lord). The New Londo Ruins are right next to the Firelink Shrine. This is an area that you're not supposed to go to until...oh I don't know...three quarters into the game? At the earliest? I went there about halfway through, and then got roadblocked super hard on the boss of the area, which was taking barely any damage from me. I was wondering what was going on, until I looked up what order players recommended tacking areas in. New Londo Ruins was near the end of that list. This is an area right next to the Firelink Shrine. On top of that, there's no resistance in the way of the elevator that takes you down there. AT LEAST the Catacombs has a small group of skeletons (that cream low level characters) in the way of the entrance. New Londo Ruins have nothing in the way. I didn't mention the ghosts inside New Londo, but that's because they're the "easiest" thing in there. Not that a level 2-3 character would do much in the hands of an inexperienced player. Once again, open design is a good thing...when done right. Skyrim for example only has a few things that completely demolish the player at the start of the game. Giants for example, or other enemies that have static levels. Fallout New Vegas is another one. You have an area filled with Deathclaws that will completely tear you apart unless you have broken weapons and skills. NPCs warn you about this area. Not because you walk up to them and press E, but because they run towards you with a warning if you get anywhere near the area. That is good open ended design. This is murky open ended design.

If you're a new player like me, don't be afraid to grind. Grind away. I did, and I enjoyed the game more. I enjoyed the game a lot more when I was able to take 3 or more hits from bosses and large enemies before having to heal. I enjoyed becoming a boss myself with how hard I was to kill. This game is a sandbox. Wear whatever armor you want, use whatever weapons you want, and level as much vitality as you want. Ignore the elitists that say you have to only level strength and wear little to no armor for max speed and dodging. Play how you want.

Get the Zweihander from the graveyard though. Excellent weapon. Swap the light attack (R1 to X) and heavy attack (R2 to Y) to go hand in hand with your already existing muscle memory. Most games have X as light attack and Y as heavy attack on controller. You'll enjoy the game a lot more.

Posted: May 5
This game is frequently described as a flawed masterpiece and I'm inclined to agree. Dark Souls 1 was released before FromSoft really came into their own. Compared to later entries in the series it's far more rough around the edges - bosses and levels typically aren't tests of mechanical skill like they are in later games, but are more often than not puzzles that the developers intend for you to solve in a certain way.

Where Dark Souls 1 shines in a way that no other game in the Souls family has ever recreated is in the world design. Lordran is a complex and convoluted world, each step you take is filled with dread and unknowing. Bonfires are few and far between, with the main method of quickly travelling between areas being unlockable shortcuts. Not a single other game in the series matches the emotional ride you get from feeling completely hopeless to the sense of relief and joy you get from unlocking another checkpoint.

Having glazed "masterpiece" part, I'm now going to talk about the "flawed" part. Dark Souls 1 is an unfinished game. Production was rushed to beat out Skyrim coming out in the same year and the second half of the game really shows that. Lost Izalith is a half-baked map that finishes off with the most comically awful boss in the entire series and Duke's Archive is an annoying repetitive area filled with copy-paste enemies that deal ridiculous damage if they get buffed. There's also plenty of bugs in the game that still haven't been patched out, including a notorious bug where you occasionally fall through the world if you slide down some ladders.

Despite its flaws I'd still recommend DS1 if you enjoy the genre, but if you're new to the series I'd suggest starting off with Dark Souls 3. Dark Souls has a reputation for being hard, but it isn't hard for the sake of hard. It's a game about overcoming adversity and overwhelming odds, and coming out the other side stronger for it.
8/10, a cult classic. It shows its age and it's a bit rough around the edges but it's a one-of-a-kind experience that no other game has quite recreated.

Posted: May 4
Remastered may look cleaner and run smoother, but the gameplay feels hollowed out. This version loses the precision and intensity that made the original unforgettable.

Enemy reactions are often delayed and animations feel broken. You’ll see enemies twitch, misalign, or attack behind themselves. It’s especially jarring when you go for a backstab and suddenly get hit by a flame attack from an enemy still facing forward. This isn’t just odd behavior; it contradicts how the mechanics worked in the original.

Bosses are way too easy. Most go down in 2 to 3 minutes solo, and in co-op they can melt in under 30 seconds. What once felt like hard-earned victories now feel like speed bumps.

The smaller details are off too. Enemies can take 10 to 15 seconds to disappear after dying. Ragdolls either freeze upright or become static props. Even healing feels wrong. Drinking an Estus Flask seems to take 1 to 2 seconds longer than before, which can completely throw off your timing during fights. It’s subtle, but with over 430 hours in the original, it’s impossible not to notice.

To its credit, co-op actually works well now thanks to improved networking. Blighttown also runs smoothly at last. But even with those fixes, it’s hard to enjoy a version of the game where so much feels broken or mistimed.

If you’re new and just want to co-op your way through Lordran, this might be an acceptable option. But if you're looking for the real Dark Souls experience, solo or otherwise, stick with Dark Souls: Prepare to Die Edition. This remaster just doesn't deliver.

Sorry, can't recommend this.

FromSoftware earns my nomination for "Most Incompetently Designed Camera Controls" of all time. They made one camera system and stuck with it for every single game they made, and it is fundamentally flawed in all of them. Note that I am on keyboard and mouse because I have a birth defect in my hands that makes controllers painful to use for long periods of time.

Problem 1: Lack of freedom. The camera is both really close to your character, and is constantly moving around when you do anything. Turning left or right? Camera will automatically follow your movement regardless of your manual inputs. Climbing a flight of stairs? The camera will position itself to look diagonally with the staircase. This will interpose your character between your camera and whatever is in front of you, which includes enemies and/or traps.

Problem 2: The ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ lock-on system. If you ever want to move any direction other than forward while in a fight, you need to use the lock-on system. If you don't, your character will face the direction you are moving instead of toward the enemy. This makes shields unusable without the lock-on system, and makes general combat tedious. If you DO choose to use the lock-on system, you COMPLETELY lose control of the camera. In my 60 minutes of gameplay, I found 2 spots where the lock-on camera interposed a large piece of map geometry (or in one case, a large crow's wing) itself and the action, so I couldn't see what was happening.

It's astonishing how I don't see as many people complaining about this. Am I missing something? I've tried this and Elden Ring, and the camera controls in both games were so abhorrently restrictive that I quit within a couple hours of starting. It's unbearable.

This all started because I wanted to try Elden Ring, but the completionist in me demanded that I play FromSoftware's previous titles first. For my first attempt at Dark Souls two years ago, I didn't look anything up and went as far as I could on my own, since the fanbase insists that it's much better that way. However, I felt so burned out after nearly 15 hours of bumbling through the early areas that when I finally beat Taurus Demon, I put the game down and didn't come back. The game asks a lot of you on all sides, with complexity to every aspect of its design. If you are a less experienced or skilled gamer, or not in the headspace to struggle for fun, it may prove overwhelming to you without any assistance at all.

For my second attempt, after a couple more years of practice with simpler third-person action games, I decided not to be stubborn and allow myself to use the internet. I am thankful to all the players who came before for helping me through this game. Dark Souls trolls you hard sometimes: useless stat options with no respec, cruelly hidden bonfires, unrelenting environmental hazards and status effects, easily missable items and areas, those archers in Anor Londo, whatever Bed of Chaos was... but with a little guidance and commiseration, it's SO FUN. For all your suffering, you feel doubly rewarded when you reach that gorgeous view, unlock that new shortcut, beat that tough boss, and watch your character grow from a prisoner in rags to an absolute unit (I ended up with Zweihander and Havel's armor set!). Along with the satisfaction of completing this game, I feel really excited to put all my newly acquired knowledge to the test in the next one :) I'll make it to Elden Ring eventually lol

Recently 100% it so beat the game roughly 2.5 times so take what I say with a grain of salt.

(SMALL SPOILERS LIKE MECHANICS AND A BOSS)

Simply put, is it a good game? Hell Yes.
-Expectations: Honestly, I thought it would be a little different from what most people were saying, but after playing it for as much as I did it was almost a 1:1 of how people described it. Let me tell you, it is an amazing experience. This was my first ever Soulslike ever and I was so impressed with how everything was. I heard that some areas fell emptier than it should of been because of the rushed development and I can see it, however it barely affects the game in any negative way. Overall the game you see is the game you get, and in my case a little bit more.
-Experience: It feels like one of those training arcs in shows where the teacher is ruthless, but at the end of those relentless training sessions, you feel like an absolute champ. You trained, mastered, leveled up, and prepared for each fight and every win feels deserved. Over time it didn't feel like a mindless fight, (a bit corny) but a dance. It was like noticing details with every swing as you tried to overcome every challenged.
-First Time: Everything does feel overwhelming. During my first playthrough I didn't know if anything was right or wrong, heck I only learned about weight after 20 hours of playing. I do feel that you shouldn't be ashamed of looking up things if you need to, but exploring on your own feels way more fun. There are things YOU WILL MISS on your first playthrough (Example: I didn't know you could use lanterns in the f'in tomb of giants) , and if you ever feel stuck try to find in places you think is unaccusable but actually is. It isn't nice to new players, but you can get through it eventually. Overall when it come to builds I'm the kind of guy who like to make his own builds, but because it's hard to comprehend certain thing it becomes had to actually do so. (ALSO WHERE DO YOU SEE THE USES FOR HUMANITY ON YOUR FIRST PLAYTHROUGH???).
-Style: This game is so up my alley with everything. The story, characters, scenes, feeling of playing, it's just SO GOOD. I do get it is pretty old and I see some parts that just render weirdly, but otherwise it looks great for how old it is. If it doesn't feel like your style though you will see it consistently, so bear that in mind.
-PVP: Ima kms. Seriously though it is a nightmare when you are someone like me with crappy wifi. Invasions are so annoying when all of a sudden the guy teleports behind you and backstabs you- like how??? I feel like if you had good ping it wouldn't be as bad, but for someone like be I'd rather play offline. (This part is a very me issue though)

-Main Complaints: Bed of Chaos. Omg Bed of Chaos. It is horrible. Honestly just cheese it at this point. I wouldn't even be mad. Even if you were doing a challenge run chessing it is only fair. I also find the curse effect really punishing for what it is; I feel like the insta kill was already enough, but having halved health for being bad at the game is just a sucker punch. It also sucks because you also likely don't know the NPC who can cure the curse and the purging stone is pretty expensive early game. (I just remembered) I do think that covenant tasks are tedious however; I wish there was a more fun way of getting certain covenant items.

-Overall: I see why people name this one of the best games of all time. However in my preference there are things that don't necessarily feel bad, but feel missing (Just because of how good it is). You get hints of potential in certain parts but they don't fall flat, but leave you wanting more. The DLC does an amazing job, but honestly I would have loved at least ONE MORE DLC. But I guess that just shows how much I love the game. 100%ing it didn't even feel crappy, I had so much fun grinding everything, and would highly recommend. If you feel like it might be something you like, it 100% is, and if you don't think you'll like it, I give it a 50/50 chance that you might. My only wish is that it was longer honestly, but hey that's why we have DS2 and DS3.

I am def playing on a new account again. Love you devs <3

I was late to the Dark Souls party because I figured it wasn't for me, and hearing people say how great it was made me want to play it less. Years later, I give it a try and I finally get it.

Look, the fact the gameplay is slow but deliberate, tough but fair, etc etc, hardly needs to be repeated a millionth time. What really sold me on Dark Souls is the lore and the world. Given only the smallest scraps of lore and story means your epic journey also becomes a puzzle. Finding some sword, realizing you've seen that name in its description somewhere before and making the link -- that feeling is magical and no other game before or after Dark Souls does it with such mastery.

With so little handholding, the journey you make in Dark Souls feels like an epic quest that you, the player, undertake. It's you alone against a hostile world of misery, and managing to complete your journey feels immensely satisfying.

Posted: May 1
A beloved all time classic for a reason. Albeit a flawed one.

I played and loved the original Dark Souls to death on my PS3 back in the day. I haven't gone back to it much since then. Since then I've played/beaten Dark Souls II, Dark Souls III, Bloodborne, Sekiro, Nioh, Strangers of Paradise and I've played a small amount of Elden Ring. Going back to Dark Souls via this remaster was an emotionally satisfying and nostalgic journey. However no game is perfect and Dark Souls feels like a tale of two halves.

The first half (everything up to obtaining the Lordvessel) is an absolute masterpiece to this day. The level design, the enemy placement, the boss fights, and the environmental story telling/atmosphere are things people planning to develop video games should take notes on. The first half manages to balance being challenging and fun without it getting too frustrating. Yes even Blighttown (Especially in the remaster where the framerate isn't an issue). For new players just don't be afraid of looking up information. It has the system where you can read other player's messages on the floor for a reason.

The second half (everything after obtaining the Lordvessel) felt like a mixed bag. Some areas (Duke's Archive, Crystal Cave and the DLC) were as excellent as the first half. Some areas had a gimmick to them that didn't work that great making them not as enjoyable to playthrough (Tomb of the Giants, New Londo Ruins). Finally some areas just felt rushed and unfinished (Demon Ruins and Lost Izalith) with a terrible boss fight (Bed of Chaos is my least favorite thing I've done in any FromSoft game I've played). No other FromSoft title I've played had any section that felt as rushed (including Demon's Souls 2009). Does this ruin the game? HECK NO.

As far as Dark Souls' difficulty if you've played and beaten newer souls-like games then you'll be surprised how not difficult it is (especially if you consistently upgrade your weapon). Only some of the boss fights in the DLC put up a meaty challenge for me (Bed of Chaos doesn't count). So if you're looking for the Dark Souls experience and want to start somewhere easier than this is safe bet (one of the rare franchises that got more challenging later on). As for the remaster it's a basic quality of life update and isn't a nightmare to deal with like the original pc version.

Even with Dark Souls' lowest lows (which I critiqued out of honest love for the game) it is still very much worth playing and Dark Souls wouldn't be Dark Souls without them. As I said in the beginning it's a beloved all time classic for a reason.

Dark Souls Remastered is not just a touch-up of a classic—it’s a reaffirmation of why this game became a legend in the first place. From the moment you awaken in the Undead Asylum, it's clear that this is a world that offers no apologies, no hand-holding, and absolutely no mercy. And that’s precisely its magic.

The remastered version enhances the original's haunting beauty. Textures are sharper, lighting is cleaner, and running at 60fps across all platforms makes every dodge roll and sword swing feel more responsive. Lordran has never looked or felt better—its gothic architecture and decaying landscapes now bristle with even more oppressive atmosphere.

Gameplay-wise, Dark Souls Remastered preserves the deliberate combat and punishing difficulty that defined the original. Every enemy encounter demands respect; every boss is a test of patience, pattern recognition, and nerve.

That said, this remaster doesn’t add much beyond polish. No new content, no tweaks to broken mechanics like the inconsistent hitboxes or occasionally wonky targeting system. It’s a faithful update—warts and all. Some may argue it’s too faithful, especially given how later entries improved on many of these systems.

Still, for both series veterans and new players curious about where the Soulsborne phenomenon truly began, this version is the best way to experience the original Dark Souls. It’s a remaster that understands its responsibility: not to rewrite history, but to preserve and sharpen it.

Rating: 9/10

The first time I played Dark Souls I actually didn't like it very much. It was my first 'souls-like' game; a term describing a style of third person action RPG with a particular play style and difficulty curve which, indeed, owes its very namesake to Dark Souls.

I didn't like it at first, but it wasn't entirely because of the difficulty curve. The game has a bleak artistry, set in a dying world where themes of inevitable death and decay and loss are paramount. Maybe I wasn't in the right headspace at the time, but it turned me off and I set the game down for at least a few years before randomly returning to it.

And when I did, perhaps my headspace had changed. As an older gamer now, I couldn't help but randomly think of Dragons Lair, an old laserdisc arcade title which would broadly be called a kind of "FMV Game" today, in which the core gameplay loop is entirely about the trial-and-error of memorizing the correct sequence of commands that need to be put in exactly at the right times in order to progress the game and story.

That's a little of what Dark Souls reminded me of, that second time I'd picked it up. It -is- an RPG, with experience points and stat mechanics and a kind of 'leveling up' or growth progression, but it is simply a different kind of RPG and a different kind of game. So different that it needed a new kind of language to describe it, and that language decided on 'souls-like'.

Dark Souls is a game about the casual brutality of a dangerous game world with odds stacked precipitously against you in every aspect and in all enemy encounters. And yet it is also a game about tasting failure again and again, each time coming back just a little more informed until at last you can succeed. It's multiplayer components are often asynchronous and can be a source of great joy and frustration as antagonism and cooperation play out in turn. It has an engaging story told non-linearly, with world building and plot detail presented often very little in the narrative body of the game progression itself but instead told through environmental and character design, or in snippets on examining items.

It's bleak artistic styles and decaying world that once left me feeling depressed and uninterested in the game now challenged me to find an unseemly beauty hidden desert-like amidst it's barren facade.

It's a masterpiece of game design, and I'm glad I had the second chance to recognize it for what it was. If only all games could be so grossly incandescent.

Posted: April 30
The game that established a genre.

Dark Souls holds a special place in my heart, never before had I experienced a game as this one and I'm afraid no other game could recapture the level of greatness I felt playing through it.

The lore, the gameplay (although refined) can't be matched, the clunkiness of combat compared to later Fromsoft Souls only serves to further provide a sense of powerlessness where even a tiny mistake could cost you your life, cementing your frailty among the denizens of Lordran. However, just like you could die from missing an attack, so too can your enemies fall before your might, having learned their moves and/or improved your gear. It's all about overcoming challenges and pushing through towards your goal in any way you can.

I have gone through 6 playthroughs already, trying different builds and challenges. I have even completed an SL1 run. The talk about this game's difficulty is vastly overblown, just like any other game, you can succeed if you learn as you play, not just bashing your head against the wall expecting something to change without you changing aswell. You will have a good time as long as you understand how this adventure is not just about the finish line, but the journey that you, as a player, go through, overcoming adversity and coming out the other side victorious, glorious, proud, seeing your efforts pay out. There are a lot of ways that you can mirror Dark Souls' teachings into real life.

There is a reason this piece of art has been labeled as the "Ultimate Game of All Time", you are cheating yourself of an experience by not playing this game.

Just don't go hollow out there.

Posted: April 27
It feels almost foolish attempting to write about Dark Souls for the first time some fourteen years after its release. What is left to say? Actual tomes have been written, their words pressed into the pulp of long-dead trees and preserved for the ages. Countless games have tried to borrow its soul. It is a monument now, a foundation stone buried deep beneath the landscape of modern gaming, its echoes felt everywhere. Soulslike.

Of all its many triumphs, it is the world—Lordran—that stands the tallest. A place of crumbling ruins and forsaken gods, where time itself seems to rot. With few exceptions, every path is bound to another, every distant bell tower and fetid swamp stitched into a single, decaying whole. In the early hours, there is no fast travel, and there should not be. You must walk the dying earth yourself, bleed into its soil, and learn its sorrow by heart. When a door swings open onto a familiar place you thought forever lost, it is not merely convenience—it is revelation. Everything is connected.

To stand at the Altar of Sunlight, gazing out across all that you have endured, with more yet ahead, or to glimpse the broken ruins of Lost Izalith and the primordial roots of Ash Lake while stumbling through the pitch black of the Tomb of the Giants—these are moments that anchor you to this world. That remind you of what is at stake. Of how small and desperately lost you are.

It is easy to miss the beauty of Lordran, for it wears a mask of cruelty. It demands your suffering, your patience, your will. In other games, death is a punishment. In Dark Souls, death is doctrine. It is the first lesson, and the last. A missed dodge. A broken shield. A warning unheeded. Every failure is a voice calling back to you from the dark. Every shard of lore, every withered sword or broken ring, weaves another thread into a story written in blood and ash.

Listen, if you can.

Stay safe, friend—and don't you dare go hollow.

This game really does live up to the hype. The soundtrack and environments immerse you immediately. The combat is simple to understand and has a high skill ceiling. My only complaint is the inclusion of the "run-backs" when you fail a boss. They encourage you to actively AVOID interacting with the game by figuring out the best way to run past all the enemies before the boss room. In the beginning of my time playing this game I would fight every enemy in order to practice the combat and grind souls. Around halfway through it became a chore. I would run past everything that wasn't a side or required boss unless I needed to grind out some levels. If a mechanic makes a player NOT PLAY THE GAME, then it is a bad mechanic. Just put a bonfire outside the boss room, that's all it takes to fix this. And no, it doesn't "ruin the difficulty" of the game, it just makes it less tedious. There's a difference. Despite this, I would 100% recommend the game.

A Masterpiece Reforged:

The story is quiet but profound—a world falling apart, full of gods, ghosts, and forgotten legends. Every weapon feels like it has a past, every NPC a tragedy. The world design is genius, looping back into itself like a haunted maze, with Firelink Shrine as its beating heart. It’s not just hard—it’s meaningful. A game about cycles, choice, and what it means to keep going.

From the moment I woke up in the Undead Asylum to the first time I stepped into Firelink Shrine, Dark Souls Remastered felt like stepping into a ruined cathedral of storytelling—ancient, cryptic, and achingly beautiful. This isn't just a game; it’s a pilgrimage through a decaying world still clinging to purpose. And no matter how many times I return, the world of Lordran continues to astonish me with its artistry, depth, and mournful brilliance.



For those willing to embrace its difficulty, its ambiguity, and its deliberate pacing, Dark Souls Remastered offers an experience like no other. It doesn’t hold your hand—it holds up a mirror. And what you see reflected might just change the way you look at games forever.

10/10. Praise the Sun. 🌞

Posted: April 23
Dark Souls One is absolutely incredible. I never played the original, and I'm in awe of how amazing this game is. I'm not really a souls-like veteran. I've played Elden Ring, Sekiro, and Lies of P. I liked all of them quite a bit, but there's something about this game that really stood out to me. The level design, the story, the environments... nothing else like it.

There are a few things that definitely show their age. The Hydra's are a pain in the ass to chase down each head, some enemy's pathing was very odd, and things weren't always clear as far as where to go (how the hell did people find that they needed to go through random invisible walls!?), but every step of the journey was insanely well crafted.

I'll admit, it took me three tries to start this game. The first time I refunded it. The second time I didn't understand what the hell I was doing wrong and quit. The third time, I pushed through, and I'm so glad I did. It's impressive the detail of this world with how long ago this game was made. Every single enemy, item, and environment piece is deliberately placed. Some of it is annoying, but every day, I slowly made my way a little further.

I recommend playing as blindly as possible, but definitely don't be afraid to look stuff up. I started with the master key and ended up in some areas I definitely should not have been in. I pushed through them and it made a lot of bosses underwhelming afterword.

Regardless, this is a masterpiece of a game. On to DS2 next.

I have mixed feelings about this game...

Positives:
- Mostly peak level design
- Fun main questline
- Cool mechanics (kindling, reverse hollowing, etc.)
- Good NPC voice acting and engaging questlines

Negatives:
- Bosses often felt either too simple and plain or frustratingly difficult (coming from an Elden Ring player)

Overall, I'd say the game is a positive experience, but I don't think it's something I'll revisit anytime soon. Newer titles like Elden Ring and Sekiro avoided a lot of the bullcrap this game had (mostly runbacks), which makes them more replayable (atleast in my opinion). Some areas and bosses made me want to bite every key on my keyboard, eat them, then ♥♥♥♥ them out in such a way that the letters spell: "♥♥♥♥ this game." Other than that—good game.

Posted: April 17
Revolutionary and Legendary Game, But Heavily Flawed

Dark Souls might be one of the most revolutionary games ever made, and it is absolutely worth playing, however, being one of the first of its kind unfortunately lead to a rushed development, something that is very noticeable and sadly results in a disappointing second half after a near flawless start. If you have any interest in this game I highly recommend going in blind as there's no better way to experience the magic of Dark Souls.


Combat in Dark Souls is what you'd except. You have a weapon, and you can roll to dodge, run, and attack, all of which use up stamina. The key to success in Dark Souls combat is being able to manage your stamina and position yourself in areas where you can avoid attacks and get your own damage in. It's a punishing system that will punish you for every mistake but that's what makes it great. Learning enemy patterns, mastering your weapon of choice, and learning to better manage your stamina takes time but the payoff is so satisfying. Bullying enemies that once seemed impossible is such a great feeling and true sign of growth as a player. Bosses are true tests of your skills and overcoming them is immensely satisfying.


The world design of Dark Souls is truly hit or miss. As a whole the way the entire game's world is connected with shortcuts and countless secrets is perhaps its greatest accomplishment in terms of game design. However certain areas in the world are truly miserable to navigate and I don't mean just because they're difficult. One late game area in particular decides that you don't need to be able to see where you're going. It's pitch black with bottomless holes and enemies around every corner. What. A. Fun. Time. Other areas require extremely long and tedious boss runbacks. Maybe this is a skill issue, or a me issue but I don't find these fun at all. They're frustrating and tedious. While many areas have secret shortcuts that reward exploration, others do not. As a whole though the world of Dark Souls is beautifully dark and hauntingly beautiful. Piecing together where everything is and how to navigate it is an amazing experience that few other games can replicate.


In conclusion Dark Souls is a game that for all its flaws is still an incredible experience and is absolutely worth playing, even if just to see where the series started (sorry Demon Souls but you're not on Steam, what do you want me to do?). While the second half is definitely weaker, it still has its moments of magic that make the whole game worth playing through. I wish I could speak to the DLC included in the remaster but the game instalocks you into New Game+ after beating the final boss of the base game, which I didn't know so sorry, and be weary of that. Probably one of the best games ever made. It's not flawless, as there are tons of mods that seek to make the game more balanced and fair, however, even without it, it does so much right. That being said, it certainly is an acquired taste, as the lack of any form of guidance, and the lack of explanation for many elements of the game can be confusing, but if you either take the time or look into aspects of the game online, you'll get through everything eventually. This is certainly a game that requires you to sit down and play it, as you can't always just turn your brain off and spam the attack button. If you're looking into trying this game out, I recommend you take the time to learn how to play, such as understanding how equipment load works, the difference on light and heavy attacks, and when to sit back and when to punish enemies. If you try to bum rush the enemy, you're likely going to get punished for it. Patience is rewarded. Also, if you're looking for a deep narrative game with a clear story, I'd suggest either skipping this game, or imagining that you're playing as a character that reacts to the world's events, and, in a way, make your own story. There isn't too much outside of what you make as a story for you to engage with aside from combat, so if that's a turn off, I'd suggest turning to online lore videos.

Revising my review: My friends wanted to see the rest of the game, so I completed it. TL;DR play a more modern souls game unless you're a completionist. And if you value your time, just look up a walkthrough so you aren't spending hours just trying ti figure out what to do next.

My original review was a little long, so I wanted to make it more efficient for you to see if it's your jam or not.

The good parts:
1. Graphics are alright. Charming in their janky way.

2. Combat is a bit janky, but since everything is janky you get used to it. The only rage-inducing part for me was that it would queue actions like world of warcraft, so you could press "dodge" a bunch of times while you were unable to dodge, and then later it would try to dodge three times.

3. I think the hint system is a lot of fun, including the troll ones where they'll be like "invisible wall here" and there's nothing. Or the one that disses a lame boss with "Weakness: Anywhere" which gave me a chuckle.

The bad parts:


I admit about halfway through the game I had to get to guides not for the boss fights, but to just figure out where to go next.



Dark Souls, and souls games in general aren't really known for having a great plot, so just watch a youtube video about it and pretend the game actually tells that story.



There's a number of sections, even if you have an optimal walkthrough guide (which I didn't), that you're just holding down sprint trying to get to the next section of the game.

There's fast travel later on, but it only applies to a small set of locations, and most of them are completely useless (dead end boss rooms).

The bosses I fought were okay, sometimes buggy, but it was the runbacks that were just demonic. Some bosses that I would've liked much more are deeply negative to me just because of the runbacks rather than the actual fights.

Posted: April 15
This might be my favorite game of all time. It's probably just nostalgia talking, but I love almost every aspect of this game. The music is killer. The map and world design still blows my mind to this day with its creative inter-connectivity. The combat is challenging, but rewarding and rarely feels unfair. Boss fights (for the most part *cough Capra Demon and Bed of Chaos cough*) are incredibly fun and memorable. And despite its age, the graphics are pretty solid.

My favorite aspect of this game, however, might be the lore. Your first play through, you will probably miss a majority of the story due to being overwhelmed by the harsh environment, or because a large portion of it is hidden away. Once you overcome this, you will find that there is some unique, artistic storytelling and world building that brings significant context to your journey through Lordran. The community also has amazing people who have helped bring these hidden story aspects to the mainstream.

While DS3 and Elden Ring are both amazing games in their own right, I always find myself looking back fondly at this influential game. It made me fall in love with the Souls genre and the sense of accomplishment that is felt when you finally overcome a challenge you have been stuck on for hours. The feelings I have when playing this game are unmatched. If you have already played Elden Ring or DS3 and enjoyed them, this is a must play game for sure.

Posted: April 14
This game is very bad. The combat is incredibly clunky. The level design is atrocious. Everything is ugly. It's so dark that you can't see what's going on around you, to the point where enemies and boulders you should be able to easily see and dodge will hit you just because you're running around in the dark.

The enemies aren't balanced at all; they just kill you right away. The controls aren't intuitive. The targeting system is terrible. The story is that a slimy dragon and some evil dark lords killed the other dragons and now you're a zombie in zombie prison trying to find a torch to light. What?

Heed my warning: stay away from this absolute stinker of a game. There is no classic, here. Save your money. I was very disappointed and you don't have to be.

Edit: I wish I could rate this game even lower than I initially rated it. It is without redeeming quality. There are no checkpoints. Off-screen enemies hit you with arrows and bombs all the time (I mean just constantly). The traps get cornier. It is an utterly repugnant experience. I am completely blown away that somebody has ever felt that this might be a good game, let alone "the best game ever." You will leave this game in a terrible mood every single time. It is cursed and evil and stupid.

The original DkS on PlayStation 3 and the Prepare to Die PC port of the PS3 version had problems... lots of problems. Mostly graphics, controls, and performance issues. The Remaster here fixes almost everything wrong with the original PS3/Prepare To Die version and hey Online works now! Online is a feature in the original Prepare To Die version that slowly became unsupported, not to mention the many items you get on your journey only work for online play, such as the Cracked Eye Orb or Sun Medallion from Solaire.

Enough about the old version, I'm gonna tell you DkS 1 Remastered is my favorite game in the Souls franchise to this very day. If you want to experience the original Souls game that inspired an entire genre of games like Black Myth Wukong, here's your chance for $40.


Here's some very important tips before you play Dark Souls 1 Remastered:

-Understand that if you light the fire at the end of the game, good job. Yet if you play the direct sequel, Dark Souls 3, it explains why that wasn't a great idea. The lore in this game is very very well made and any questions you have about Lordran, the kingdom you explore in this game, will be answered in the 3rd Game.

-USE THE MASTER KEY on your first playthrough, unless you are a veteran and have a reason to pick a different gift. There is a very good reason for this, such as if you get stuck in Blighttown and are cursed because you decided to do Ash Lake before Quelaag. There is a shortcut to Firelink Shrine you can use with the Master Key where if you did defeat the Lower Undead Burg, you have access to a merchant in Firelink Shrine that sells curse cures (this just happened to me oof).

-Speaking of Blighttown, if you get lost in the upper levels, USE THE TORCHES, they literally light your way out of or going into the swamps.

-DO NOT FIGHT every enemy ever. Enemies have a huge aggro range and depending on how fast they are, you usually can just run past them.

-THE EASIEST WAY TO DIE in this game is falling. If you need to only slightly tilt your controller's analogue stick to walk slower, DO IT. It will save your life and your souls.

-In order to survive the game and especially after the first Bell of Awakening is rung, USE YOUR STAMINA BAR like an additional health bar. If you are a board and shield player like a knight (which you should pick that class as a beginner), you need to let go of your shield in between the enemies hits. This almost immediately fills your stamina gague and you should think of your stamina gague as a Borderlands 2 shield. If you run out of stamina, enemies can hit you for actual health.

-If you keep hearing bells when you get close to the Undead Parish church, it is to inform you that other players have rung either the first or second Bell and this is in real-time. If you hear a lot of bells going off one after another, there are other human players that you cannot see that are playing the game alongside with you, which is really fun to know!

-This game has a very active online community. From casting soapstones (messages on the floor other players can leave you about traps or false walls) to the ringing of the bells, to even summoning other players to help you defeat bosses... AND THERE IS A PvP MODE. You have to join covenants to access the items that allow you to play PvP mode and in some cases like in Solaire's Sun covenant, you get rewarded for how many other players you kill.

-Always attack from the back except in certain cases where enemies know you're attacking from the back, such as with the Gaping Dragon which swings it's tail when it knows you're attacking from the back. The strategy in this game is to learn your enemies moves while keeping stamina up. Once you learn the moves, you can safetly attack.

-Bonfires are a place of respite but you should resist the temptation of sitting at one if you know it'll cost you a long backtracking journey. For example, if you're grinding souls to buy Titanite Shards in the Undead Parish, don't activate a bonfire over in Darkroot Basin as that is a time-consuming trip back. You should think before you rest at a bonfire you already have lit. For the unlit bonfires, please sit down at them right away. You get the ability to travel between bonfires later in the game, and the more you light, the easier it will be later.

-Bonfires NEED TO BE KINDLED. This was a confusing mechanic to me. In order to do this you need to be A) NOT CURSED, which should only happen to you in the Depths or in the Great Hollow/Ash Lake before you acquire the Lordvessel. B) you must have two humanities on hand, either via the big 02 on the left hand corner of the screen or if you have two or more humanities in your inventory. You must turn into a human first, then kindle the bonfire. Kindling a bonfire only affects that one bonfire and gives you 10 Estus Flasks instead of 5. If you run out of humanity, rats in the Depths sometimes drop a whole bunch.

-TOXIC is not the same as POISON. As soon as you make your way to Blighttown, there are relentless blowdarters that can kill you quickly with TOXIC. You're supposed to farm Purple Moss and the other one with the white flowers in the Darkroot Garden in order to stave off poisonin Blighttown. THE MOSS DOES NOT WORK ON TOXIC. You only need to kill the blowdarters once and they will not respawn, so try your best to not get TOXIC while you pick them off. Before you get to Blighttown, consider spending souls on Bows and Arrows to get rid of them quickly.

-Blighttown is a relentless and unforgiving area some players give up at, but it's designed to teach players about Status Ailments and enemy weaknesses such as Quelaag being weak to lightning because she is a fire-type. If you learn to adapt to Blighttown, it becomes easy so please do not give up on it. I honestly somewhat enjoy being down there.

-Also, you should go back to the Asylum at the beginning of the game right before Blighttown. To do this, you need to use the elevator shortcut from Undead Parish and land on a specific sheet of decayed ceiling... it'll take you many tries. One there, get into the "window" with stairs and you'll find a bird's nest. Make sure you land on the roof where the 2F key is otherwise this tip won't help you. Get inside the nest and AVOID the middle of the room where you fought the tutorial boss, go to 3F, and get the item you couldn't reach at the beginning of the game. You do not have to fight the Stray Demon at the bottom and I don't recommend it until you unlock the Lordvessel. IF YOU DO THIS, you get a ring that allows you to literally walk normally in the BLIGHTTOWN SWAMP, otherwise traversing the swamp will be much more difficult.

-Another Blighttown tip is to buy Soul Arrows from the "fatty" at Firelink Shrine. I call him "fatty" because other players name him that in the online portion of the game via soapstones. You need to buy it with the catalyst, and equip the catalyst, then go to the bonfire to "attune" the arrows to your upper slot. Make sure you have at least 12 INT, which is the candle icon in the player character card. Once you have soul arrows, MOSQUITOES in the swamp area respawn 3-4 times so you need to kill every wave with the soul arrows before proceeding to Quelaag. This makes it much easier to traverse to her Domain for the boss fight.

After getting both endings for this game, and having beaten all 3 Dark Souls games and all bosses, Elden Ring (still haven't beaten the DLC), and Sekiro (still need to get the other ending though),I think it's finally time for a review.
This is quite possibly the greatest game ever created. Everything from the world detail, to the story, to the combat is just perfect. For me personally, the three best things are the story, the interconnectivity of the world, and the music. Even though the story is convoluted and you need to go out of your way to find the lore, it's just so good that it's worth it, or you can just look up a lore video, which is probably the smarter choice. From the little stories like that one dude in Firelink to Arotrias and Solaire's, they're all amazing and almost always tragic. As for the interconnectivity, the fact that 90% of the major areas in the game are directly connected to Firelink Shrine is just perfect game design. The only "bad" areas are at the end of the game, and they're still good by normal game standards, except Lost Izalith. That's actually just bad. But Tomb of the Giants, New Londo, and Dukes Archives are all the worst areas in the game (excluding Lost Izalith) and they really aren't horrible. Yeah, the boss runbacks are bad, but they're still not even 1% as bad as 90% of the Dark Souls 2 runbacks. And the music. The music is just unbelievably amazing, and I'm not even a huge fan of orchestral music. All of the music in the game is fantastic, and other than not having to do it again, the music is the best part of Bed of Chaos. This isn't important, but the 3 best tracks in my opinion are Ornstein & Smough, Sif, and obviously Gwyn. The music just sets the tone for the game so well. Speaking of, the atmosphere of the game is unmatched. The pure feeling of hopelessness, despair, and sadness you feel through 80% of the game is just amazing. That sounds horrible, but it's the opposite. This is because of the handful of respites that you get in the form of lovely NPC's (Solaire and Siegmeyer), bonfires in general, and most importantly, Firelink. Firelink is better than Majula, fight me. Dark Souls just feels so much darker than it's sequel, which makes Firelink a perfect site to just take a break and look around. Back to worldbuilding, it's truly unbelievable how you see areas in the distance that you're going to go to later. Also walking into Ash Lake actually sent a chill down my spine at how awestruck I was. I could never give this game and FromSoftware enough praise for the perfection that this game is. Miyazaki truly is the greatest director.
Final Thoughts/TL;DR: Bed of Chaos bad, Solaire is my best friend, Firelink>Majula, and this is as close to perfect as a game gets.
Final Notes: Plin plin plon

Dark Souls: A Beautifully Brutal Lesson in Self-Hate

Dark Souls isn’t just a game — it’s an experience. Specifically, the experience of being repeatedly and unapologetically bullied by medieval architecture, undead warriors, and the very concept of hope.

You don’t play Dark Souls. You survive it. Barely. Then you die. Then you survive a bit more. Then you roll off a cliff because your fat-fingered dodge was two pixels off. Classic.

The story? Oh, it's there. Somewhere. Probably scribbled on a wall behind a hidden wall behind another hidden wall guarded by a depressed dragon. Every NPC speaks in riddles, metaphors, or what sounds like Shakespeare having an existential breakdown. Meanwhile, you’re standing there in a rusty bucket-helmet, covered in regret and moss.

Combat is tight, rewarding, and feels great — for the five seconds before a 12-foot skeleton dropkicks you into the sun. Boss fights are majestic duels where you die in 0.3 seconds, whisper “okay, fair,” and queue up to die again.

And let’s talk about mimics. Remember how other games reward curiosity? Not here. In Dark Souls, opening the wrong chest gets you eaten alive by a demonic tongue monster. Trust no one. Especially furniture.

The game’s world design is stunning in a “look how beautiful this place is, shame it wants me dead” sort of way. You’ll traverse haunted castles, poison swamps, and hell dimensions… all while questioning your life choices and the sanity of the developers.

Final Verdict: 9/10
Lost one point because I screamed at a goat demon so loud my neighbor asked if I was okay. I wasn't. I’m still not.

Recommended for people who think “fun” is overrated and want to feel something. Anything.

P.S. If it looks like a chest… hit it first. Trust me.

Note: this review is less of a review but more of a warning to anyone who wants to try this game out, whether if you never touched a souls game, or played a few souls games (specifically, the more modern/new ones)
I've played and beat, elden ring, sekiro, and ds3, and whatever dlcs the games can come with.

Anyone who played souls games before can tell you that you will die a lot, specifically to bosses. This will be important for the 2 major issues I will discuss that may make a new player quit.

First of all, boss runbacks. You fight a boss, die, then you have to run back to the boss room. While I haven't played ds1 a whole lot, I've seen many things about how long the early soulsborne games runbacks are, the average runback is 50 seconds (according to the internet) and the longest is 111 seconds. And the run backs aren't even completely safe, there will be enemies in your way, so you could easily get hit and have to waste a few flasks before reaching the boss.

Second, there is a punishment for dying (besides your money dropping at your last death) where after a certain amount of deaths, your # of healing flasks gets reduced (to a certain amount). Idk too much about this system, and ik there is a way to get your flasks back, but then again, I didn't play enough to find out.

This is more of a general statement, but the games qol is significantly worse than the more modern games, here are 2 examples.
1. Fast travel between bonfires (the place where you can rest and do several things) isn't unlocked from the start. I think I heard somewhere that fast travel is unlocked halfway through the game.
2. Lack of boss bonfire. Modern souls games gives you a bonfire (or equivalent) after defeating a main/major boss. That isn't always the case in ds1. When I beated an early game boss (can't remember its name), I looked around for a bonfire but there wasn't any. In fact, the next bonfire was a fair distance away, with enemies in the way. So if I die before finding one, not only will I have to do the runback to that boss room, but also try and find the next bonfire. This could lead to a loss of souls obtained from beating the boss.
(Note: I'm pretty sure most of the bosses have a bonfire after beating them, but if this one boss doesn't have it, who knows how much more could. And its not like the bosses have a short 5 seconds runback like in elden ring)

edit: after some consideration i decided that i plan on retrying the game when it goes on sale

"He who fights with monsters should look to it that he himself does not become a monster." – Friedrich Nietzsche

To call Dark Souls merely a game is a disservice. It is an experience, an ordeal, and for many, an awakening. Playing Dark Souls Remastered is not just about rekindling a classic—it is about revisiting the foundation of a trilogy that fundamentally changed how I interpret storytelling in video games. It is a reminder that not all tales need to be told in upfront exposition, nor should every journey be neatly plotted by quest markers. In an era where games like Skyrim sought to guide the player's hand, Dark Souls did the opposite: it let us stumble, fall, and carve our own meaning from the abyss.

What makes Dark Souls so profound is its refusal to speak too loudly. Lordran is a land of hushed myths, decaying grandeur, and cryptic figures who offer guidance just as often as they deceive. Its story is not delivered through cutscenes or a journal of dictated objectives but through ruins, item descriptions, and the silent suffering of those who still cling to existence. There is no exposition-laden Jarl to welcome you, no noble guild waiting to anoint you as a chosen hero. Instead, Dark Souls looks at you with hollowed eyes and asks, Why are you here? What are you willing to endure?
This storytelling philosophy—where meaning is not handed to the player but uncovered—resonates deeply with Nietzschean existentialism. Dark Souls forces players to find their own purpose amidst the decay. There is no singular right way to interpret the game’s narrative, just as there is no absolute meaning in an uncaring universe. The First Flame will always fade, and the gods are not eternal. To persist is to impose meaning onto a world that has none.
Unlike Skyrim, where every story is spelled out for you, Dark Souls thrives on ambiguity, and in doing so, it fosters something far greater: a living, breathing myth shaped by its community. It is in the shared experience of piecing together fragmented lore that the game’s narrative finds its fullest form. Players dissect every item description, theorize on the nature of Manus and the Abyss, debate whether Gwyn was a hero or a coward. It is a game where knowledge is just as much a reward as victory over a boss, where every new theory reshapes our understanding of the world.
This communal storytelling turns Dark Souls into something transcendent. It is no longer just a personal journey but a collective endeavor, an eternal cycle of discovery and reinterpretation. In this way, it mirrors how real myths survive—through countless voices passing them down, each altering the tale with new meaning.
Returning to Dark Souls Remastered in the wake of the series’ legacy only reinforces what was already apparent in 2011: this game is a monument to the power of interactive storytelling. The feeling of finally besting Ornstein and Smough, the dread of descending into the Tomb of the Giants, the quiet, sorrowful notes of Gwyn, Lord of Cinder’s theme—these are not just moments of gameplay, but moments of personal revelation. They are tests of will, framed within a narrative that allows players to define their own understanding of struggle and triumph.
To play Dark Souls is to wrestle with the abyss and emerge changed. It is a game that does not tell you what to feel but allows you to discover meaning in your own perseverance. It is, in the end, a testament to the idea that the most profound stories are not the ones told to us—but the ones we carve out of the darkness ourselves.

Setting and Atmosphere
Dark Souls is set in the haunting and decaying kingdom of Lordran, a place where the age of fire is fading, and the undead curse has gripped humanity. The world of Lordran is intricately designed, with areas interconnected through clever shortcuts that create a sense of discovery and immersion. The seamless world design is arguably one of the game’s greatest achievements, giving players the feeling of exploring a massive, living labyrinth. The atmosphere is thick with a sense of despair and decay, emphasized by minimalistic ambient music that only swells during boss fights, making those encounters feel epic and intense.

Story and Lore
The story is subtle and deeply layered, told through item descriptions, environmental storytelling, and cryptic dialogues from NPCs. You play as the Chosen Undead, destined to link the fire and prolong the Age of Fire or let it fade into the Age of Dark. The game never holds your hand in telling this story, leaving much of it to interpretation. The rich lore, crafted by Hidetaka Miyazaki, creates a sense of myth and tragedy that resonates long after you put down the controller.

Gameplay and Combat
The combat in Dark Souls is precise, methodical, and punishing. Every encounter feels like a duel, requiring patience, timing, and skill. The game’s signature stamina management system forces you to balance attacking, blocking, and dodging carefully. Weapon variety is impressive, from mighty greatswords to agile rapiers, and every weapon has a unique move set that encourages experimentation. The parry and riposte mechanics are particularly satisfying, rewarding skilled players with devastating counter-attacks.

One of the most iconic aspects of Dark Souls is its boss design. From the imposing Bell Gargoyles to the chilling Four Kings, each boss presents a unique challenge, often testing your reflexes, strategy, and understanding of the game’s mechanics. Bosses like Ornstein and Smough stand out as some of the best in the series, demanding a mastery of both movement and combat flow.

Character Customization and Builds
Customization is extensive, allowing players to tailor their character to their preferred playstyle. You can build a dexterous swordsman, a hulking tank, a powerful sorcerer, or a versatile hybrid. The stat system is complex, rewarding thoughtful distribution to maximize damage and survivability. Magic builds feel drastically different from melee builds, offering a wide array of spells, miracles, and pyromancies that add replayability.

Level Design
The interconnected world design is a masterpiece in itself. Unlocking shortcuts feels like a revelation, and the way levels loop back on themselves is nothing short of genius. Areas like Anor Londo, Blighttown, and Sen’s Fortress each offer unique challenges and aesthetics that make exploration both daunting and exhilarating.

Multiplayer and PvP
The multiplayer component, including co-op and PvP invasions, is seamlessly integrated into the world. Summoning players for assistance or invading other worlds to challenge them adds unpredictability and excitement to the experience. The covenant system also provides incentives for different multiplayer playstyles, from helping other players to hunting them down.

Dark Souls 1 is an amazing game for its time. There is a lot of jank in this game, but you’ll get used to it within a few hours of playing. If you’ve played any other Souls-like game, this one will be no challenge for you, but it will still provide enjoyment. There are lots of cool enemies and bosses, really good and clever level design, and an interesting and varied range of weapons you can use.

There are some things in this game that can be frustrating. One issue is that your weapon has durability and can break, making it unusable in combat. To repair your weapon, you either need to go to a blacksmith or have purchased a certain item that allows you to repair at bonfires for souls. Another frustration is not being able to warp to bonfires during the first half of the game, forcing you to walk around a lot just to get places. Yes, the game is cleverly designed to minimize walking through the same areas, but it’s still annoying and doesn’t make much sense, especially since you can warp to certain bonfires midway through the game.

All in all, it’s a really good game with some jank, but definitely worth playing if you’re into these types of games, since it’s one of the originals.
