Maybe my favourite FromSoftware game, toss up between this and Dark Souls 3.

Very fun RPG, lots of different weapons, which can be upgraded and infused with different damage types. Lots of upgradeable armour. Lots of builds, although basic compared to later editions; ranging from Dex to Strength weapons, daggers to ultra greatswords, then to spells and pyromancy and miracles.

The map is pretty huge and it all intertwines within itself, you feel like you're going on for miles and you suddenly open a door and it turns out to be a shortcut back to a nearby bonfire.

Loads of fun and brutal bosses, the path from beginning to end is satisfying and you can see and feel the progress from every location you visit.

As for negatives, for some or most people I guess, the game can be hard, hard enough to start its own genre of games. Also before this remaster, the previous remaster was called Prepare to Die... and for a reason. Once you learn the patterns or get a weapon with a move-set you like, the game starts to feel a lot easier.

It takes a long while to unlock the ability to fast travel, not much of an issue, unlocking all the achievements in the game meant a lot of travelling back and forth. Its one of those things that you don't really care about, but once you get it and then start NG+ and lose the ability, you start missing it a lot more. Same as going back from Dark Souls 3 back to this one.

I think my biggest negative is the side quest stuff and the NPCs. Very little guidance is offered in game, and all the story and lore is in writing on the weapons and equipment etc. I know some people enjoy the little help the game gives you and the adventure of trying things yourself, but after you've attempted all that and want to find out how to actually progress certain stories, I find myself relying on the wiki guides, I always have to have one open when playing, just to make sure I don't accidently trigger an NPC to move on and die before I finish with them, or to lock out of certain spells and miracles or covenants. Even after completing this several times, I know the path to the end but I can never remember what NPCs are where and when or how they die off.

Posted: April 1
The first entry in the Dark Souls franchise, though the second attempt at this kind of now famous Souls-like genre since 2009's Demon Souls, released in 2011. As the first game in the franchise, Dark Souls needs to do a lot of heavy lifting - in defining what the gameplay systems are, the vibe of the world, characters and story, how levels are designed and interwoven, and possibly most importantly what the genre was going to be after the release of Demon Souls. I believe that Dark Souls gets most of this correct.

The small section of Lordran you visit in Dark Souls is fantastically thought through, with all of its sections all (mostly) interwoven and their placement above, below and beside each other generally well thought out. There are stretches of this game that are fantastic, and I think have never been done better since, with one possible exception in Elden Ring. Checkpoints - or 'bonfires' - are central hubs and safe zones where you level up, repair equipment, sort your inventory and plan where to go next, but these are not as sparse as the Archstones in Demon Souls, but their placements are meticulously done in order to allow for shortcuts to place players back at Bonfires they didn't think were nearby until they managed to figure out the shortcut route back. They're also generally not too far away from main area bosses, so no runback to a boss whose last attempt resulted in a death is quite so egregious. I loved Lordran, and I thought that it generally made sense, and its choice of art direction and lighting helped highlight a world that was already so close to its end, hanging on for dear life waiting for a Chosen Undead to come back and link the fire.

I played Dark Souls after having beaten an emulated copy of Demon Souls earlier in 2025 as an "analysis" of the Soulsborne genre, and having never played these games before, Dark Souls felt like more Demon Souls that was a bit more refined and a bit more confident in what it wanted to be. My first playthrough was as a sword-and-shield (later katana) wielding knight clad in cloth for the lowest weight costs, and my second was a stalwart beast equipped with a halberd and heavy armour. I haven't played this game as a mage, with the exception of a few pyromancy spells on my first character, but generally I never really wanted to. If I was going to deal damage, I wanted to risk taking damage. Dark Souls is okay with this, in fact I reckon it encourages it, and both enemy design, placement and movesets compliment a melee-focused style.

Bosses are quite good for a genre whose on its second time. There are far fewer gimmick fights unlike Demon Souls, and it sets the stage for the franchise and genre going forward. Of particular note in the base game are Quelaag, the Asylum Demon, Gwyn and of course, infamously, Ornstein and Smough. Quelaag is a standardly good boss fight; the Asylum Demon does an opening spectacle fight right; Gwyn is a fantastic end-boss let down only by it being too easy if you parry him, which is a poorly indicated mechanic you can employ on him; and Ornstein and Smough highlight the absolute pinnacle fight of the game (outside of the DLC) that is the peak of the game as it closes out its first half.

Unfortunately, after Anor Londo and Ornstein and Smough, the game's quality drops off dramatically. I don't mine the Duke's Archives nor Seath and think they're generally okay on par with the Undead Parish, but the combinations of the Catacombs into Tomb of the Giants and Demon Ruins into Lost Izalith sink the game down the lowest the series ever gets, and I'm including certain sections of the game's sequel. It is also in these sections that the interwoven nature of the game falls apart: you generally cannot circle back around to a shortcut in any of the last few "wings" of the game and none of them link into each other, unhelpfully aided by the introduction of warping after you collect the Lordsvessel after Ornstein and Smough. Tomb of the Giants might be the worst part of the game due to its awful enemies and awful mechanics of not letting you see anything, but frankly it could also go to Lost Izalith for the sheer audacity of including Bed of Chaos to begin with. A true shame - with more time, thought and money, perhaps that second half of the game could live up to the highs of the first half.

The DLC - Artorias of the Abyss - takes place in a reused section of the base game that I didn't mention earlier. I don't actively think this is bad, actually, and narratively it makes sense. It features the three best bosses in the game: Artorias, Manus and Kalameet. Artorias is the game's highest point, I think it's a better fight than O&S simply because there's only one enemy and I tried for hours against this boss until defeating him; he's a much faster boss than the game's movement system really allows for, but regardless he's so influential that his sheer moveset is parried in other games in the genre. Kalameet is the first of many very good DLC dragon fights in each of the games, continuing on with Sinh in Dark Souls 2 and Midir in Dark Souls 3. Overall, I generally think that it's a great DLC that adds a bit of extra challenge onto the base game.

The 100% requirements for Dark Souls 1 do require getting a character to NG++, meaning you have to beat the game TWO whole times to finish it due to boss soul weapons and items, but with careful planning and some overpowered builds it's not too bad. I finished a character cleaning up all of the achievements I had left from a single playthrough (other ending, all upgrades, all miracles/pyromancies/sorceries/unique items) in about 32 hours, but this did include about 2 hours of farming souls in order to fund all of those purchasing sprees for spells, items and upgrades.

I highly recommend you play Dark Souls 1.

The global phenomenon of Souls games is a weird one to talk about. While a lot of games were, at the time, striving for more accessibility and wider appeal, From Software defiantely raised their flag and released some of the most hardcore and punishing games, that became so popular, it created it's own genre, borrowing it's own name. Whether you like it or not, Dark Souls, and it's sister games, developed by FromSoft, are now some of the most important games in the history of gaming. As someone who tried to get into the Dark Souls back when it was released on PC for the first time, I was confused and didn't understand, why everyone was praising it so much. But I kept coming back, again and again, until I got to the first Awakening bell and understood, that it was too late for me to give up and I had to see it through to the end. Nowadays Dark Souls is one of my favorite games ever, but it has it's fair share of flaws.

The story of Dark Souls takes place in the world of Lordran. Once prosperous land, now ravaged by the curse of the Undead, everything hinges on the burning fire kept alive by the lords of this land. Intro cutscenes will explain you the basics of the lore and what is going on, but everything else has to be pieced together by the player, through dialog with NPCs and item descriptions. In some ways it makes the game feel somewhat aimless at the beginning, especially if you won't pay attention to the friendly NPCs. Without that it's unclear, where do you need to go, what is your purpose, why do you even bother with this impossible quest anyway. But that's kind of the poiunt. I'm not defending this type of story-telling, but at the same time I respect the way that devs decided to approach the inherent design of minimum handholding and maximum player interaction. The lore itself is definetely interesting and led to the entire community trying to figure out ins and outs of everything in it's world, and after all these years, there are probably no more secrets to solve, but in the end you do have a large amount of stories you can read or listen on Youtube, if you are interested or want to confirm some of your own ideas and theories.

The game, in a technical sense itself, is absolute jank, which will lower your overall enjoyment of the game if you came from later games in the series. The remaster that is availabe on Steam is actually a huge improvement, the original game was stuck on low resolution, that made the game look blurry and it had inconsistent framerate for some reason. The remaster actually makes the game look sharp, and the updated effects like fire and spells actually look pleasant, although a bit out of place in an overall gothic setting.
There are also quite a few bugs. You can get stuck on geometry in some places, fall through smallest cracks in the floor, but unable to go through very obvious gaps, ragdolls and physics are all over the place in terms of their behavior, and also one time one of the bosses was missing a limb that I had to attack to kill him, but the hitbox of the attack was still there, so it could hit me with it's attacks, but I had no way to retaliate which made me reload the world again, thankfully I had some homeward bones on hand.
But overall presentation of Dark Souls is pretty solid, even if graphics themselves are dated. Remaster is not perfect, the community made basically the same job for free back in the day on the original port, but it's nice to have an option to play in actually decent resolution with stable frame rate. Anyway, the atmosphere in Dark Souls in actually really, well, dark. The world of Lordran is dying and hopeless, and the last few survivors are clinging to their sanity and losing it and their humanity. The locations are mostly hidden from natural sunlight and even the most grand looking and important location is losing it's flame, it's marble walls and golden arches being a facade. you really get the sense that the end is near, and your quest to link the flame and bring back the age of fir might be the last chance humanity has in this world, but even that has come to question.

I'm not sure I have to explain the gameplay of Dark Souls, since so many games afterwards used it's blueprint, but I will do it anyway.
Playin as a branded by the curse of the undead, your created character can't die for good, and forced to go through cycles of death and rebirth and the nearest linked flame location, the bonfire. The world is inhabited by dangerous and agressive entities and is full of danger, so getting to a new bonfire brings a sense of relief and hope each you get to it.
The marketing of the game really hammers home how "you are going to die over and over again', with the original subtitle of "Prepare to Die" edition just making sure you know what you are getting into. And sure, it's true that you are going to die plenty of times, but I feel like there was missing a sense of hope and defiance that makes the gameplay of Souls-likes work. You die, but get back up. The game does not end at the iconic "YOU DIED" screen. The game ends only if you give up, but each time you die there is very little penalty for death, in fact, I would argue the initial deat h only will make you stronger, since you gain knoledge and experience against the threat you are facing.
And while yes, the game is hard and unforgiving, with careful planning, proper leveling and equipment you are comfortable with, you can easily get a pretty broken build going and trivialise most of the challenge, though you will still be open to cheap tricks that FromSoft really loves to put in their games.
That is what makes the loop of Dark Souls so addicting, yes it can be frustrating, dying to the same boss over and over, getting lost in some cave and falling to your death in the dark, or getting caught in some trap, but you always get back up and get out there. After all, a lot of gamers are really stubborn. All of us had those sleepless nights trying to overcome some challenge, while giving ourselves a few more minutes, a couple more rounds in that competitive multiplayer game, a couple more turms in that strategy game, one more try on this difficult level. But in case of the Dark Souls, it can drag on for the whole game, just need to get to that bonefire, just need to get to that boss, I got a new key, I need to unlock that door Is saw earlier.

Special shoutout to the level-design of Dark Souls. The world is surprisingly smaller than I remember personally. Getting through must of the locations won't take a long time, but it is cleverly desgned with interconnected paths and shortcuts, that make it feel somewhat metroidvania-lite. Later locations can fall into more linear paths, but most of the early locations loop into each other.

Tha being said, some bonfires could be placed better, sometimes going through the same 5 minutes from bonfire to boss while being bothered by enemies along the way can get annoying. And while the world itself is not that big, getting through large paths of it can get tedious, but only halfway through the game you can get the ability to fast travel.

but overall, Dark Souls is a game everyone should play. if it's not your type, that's OK. There is shame in giving up. But the type of challenge that DS provided at the time was unique and it's design and systems are designed in a way that makes you keep playing and made it so addictive, and so influential. Often imitated, but never matched, Dark Souls is a benchmark and a classic, that inspired generation.

As the flagship title that really drove interest with the souls genre into the limelight through its rounds of being "the hardest game ever" across the internet during its launch, it still holds itself as a valuable game within the franchise, with many of the most iconic bosses, characters, and world building that molded the legacy to come within fromsoftware. This game has many places for praise, namely its handling of creating an interconnected world, especially early on, and its dark atmosphere that many games have tried to replicate going on. Nearly all of the bosses feel iconic in their own ways, for good, and for bad, but the bosses all feel memorable for one way or another. uncovering the world feels very rewarding particularly because you cannot fast travel for the first portion of the game. Whilst many see this as a negative, for those seeking to engage deeply in the discovery aspects of the game this feels rewarding as you can rely on your own memory to recall where to go and map out the world for yourself. A common critique I will agree with is that some of the late game areas feel rushed and underdeveloped, with the biggest offender being Lost Izalith with random enemy placements, a TERRIBLE final boss, and certain enemies clearly being cut content re-introduced last minute as a way to "complete" the area. Despite this, this is really the only terrible area of the game, with the other late game areas being very serviceable and fun despite some not enjoying them.

overall this game is great and deserves its praises, especially given how well its aged since its release and I still believe even those that only follow newer titles would still enjoy this game.

1. Absolute silence unless you engage in a boss battle. Not even the sound of the wind. You only hear the sounds of footsteps, growling enemies, etc. Boring. That is not how you create a sublime, solitary ambience. It did not make me focus on the scenery at all. I thought I was playing a beta version of Dark Souls. Ambience 101 is found in Portal 2 and Dead Space 2, which were all released in the same year as Dark Souls. Take note of this, future game developers. Only use absolute silence like an accent colour in your attire.

2. The UI is unbelievably clunky for a 2011 game. I got the same impression from NES games. I don't want to go back to review this in detail.

3. Some attack animations have no impact and are merely slowed down. Souls fans call it "weighty," but there is hardly any weight to SOME weapons, like the second weapon I acquired to destroy the first boss. It's similar to slowly swinging a cardboard sword. I checked every single animation on YouTube just in case, and it seems there is 80% consistency in the quality of the character animations that follow the twelve basic principles of animation like a textbook. Additionally, sound effects are at fault for insufficiently impactful attacks. The weak bass of several attack sound effects is quite disappointing.

4. Common dark medieval fantasy visuals without any twist. It's not bad, but it's definitely not for someone who is looking for something new.

I genuinely wanted to enjoy it. The series is known for satisfaction after beating unforgiving bosses and sublime atmospheres. The exact reasons why I gave it a go. Unfortunately, Dark Souls brutally shattered my hopes and fantasies about the series with its awkward representations. Souls fans would like this, but not for me. FromSoftware developers in the past insufficiently had the technical capabilities to make a video game appealing. If Dark Souls were not the pioneer of the souls-like genre, it would've made plenty of unfortunate workers thrown out to the street. I hope other FromSoftware games are better.

Edit: Just to add one last comment lest Dark Souls fanboys spam "skill issue." I'll make it clear that I have no issue with the difficulty. I even played Nioh 2 before. The overall representation is not clicking for me, that's all. Cheers.

You think you know, but you don't.

You've heard the reviews: "Dark Souls is the hardest game to exist. Dark Souls is so soul crushingly difficult I can't play normal games anymore. Dark Souls separates the casual gamers from the elite."

Ridiculous. Dark Souls is a game for everyone. You will play Dark Souls. You will like Dark Souls. You will "Git Gud". But Dark Souls isn't a litmus test. It's not a bar that separates the wheat from the chaff. It is a transformational experience for the benefit of all of those who enjoy video games. Dark Souls believes in you. It challenges you because it knows you can do better. And you will. Everyone is capable of success in this game. All you need is a willingness to keep trying- a willingness to lose all that you have. Once you realize that everything that you have to lose in this game is all obtainable again; once you realize that there is no penalty to trying your best and failing- that is when Dark Souls truly challenges you to transcend the current limits of your play and evolve into something more.

Many RPGs have a sense of progression. There is a satisfaction, a catharsis to growing stronger as your character gains attributes and statistics and you have the opportunity to fight some enemy that provided challenge much earlier in your playthough, and now dispatching them is mere child's play- but this catharsis transcends merely gaining numbers on a character sheet in Dark Souls. In Dark Souls, it's not just your character that levels up: you do. And as you get better, as you grapple the controls and the inventory system; as you familiarize yourself with the weapon animations and the combat mechanics, you can feel yourself eclipsing the terrors the game faces you with. You begin to feel safe, to feel certain in yourself. There is no danger the game can throw at you and leave you running scared- you have won, by virtue of your persistence. You know the map now. You know where the enemies spawn. You know precisely where to obtain the weapon you love and the resources you need to level it into grandiose proportions. You can roll, parry, and back stab your way out of any combat encounter. You have achieved mastery. This is what makes Dark Souls brilliant --the appreciation you acquire on subsequent playthroughs. Dark Souls affords you the power to play the game however you may like if you will only try. Try and you will prevail. Try and you will be rewarded with the purest sense of accomplishment to grace modern gaming. Link the fire, Chosen Undead. Lordran awaits you.

After revisiting Dark Souls on Steam after many years, I’m reminded why it remains one of FromSoftware’s greatest creations. The challenging (and sometimes janky) combat, intricate world design, and hauntingly atmospheric storytelling make it a timeless masterpiece. The remastered version enhances graphics and performance, though it feels more like a quality-of-life update than a full overhaul. Still, the journey through Lordran is unmatched... every shortcut discovered, every boss defeated, every small victory feels truly earned. Despite a few dated mechanics, Dark Souls continues to set the gold standard for world-building and emotional challenge in gaming. It’s one of the few games I’ve 100% completed, and I’d recommend it to anyone who loves tough but deeply rewarding experiences. Even after more than 14 years, Dark Souls stands tall as the game that defined the “Souls-like” genre and is a must play for any fan of the series.

"You've died." And you don't know why.

Dark Souls isn't a game, it's a psychological test. They don't explain anything to you here, they drop you into a depressing world with a broken sword and tell you, "Figure out what to do before a rat kills you." And the worst part is, it kills you.
You try hard, you improve, you finally beat a boss after trying 30 times, and you think, "Now I'm really getting the game," but no. You never get it. Miyazaki always has another hell prepared.

- Blighttown? The greatest argument against the existence of God.
- Ornstein and Smough? Two cousins who go to the gym together and hate you.
- Dogs in sewers? More dangerous than most bosses.
- Rolling? It's not an option, it's a religion.

11/10 Solaire didn't find its Sun, but it did find a place in my heart.

I've played through Elden Ring, Dark Souls 3, and Sekiro. I also played Dark Souls 1 on the Xbox 360 when I was younger. Even though this game was a blast of nostalgia for me, I didn't enjoy this playthrough. I had moments of joy when I discovered items that they brought over to Dark Souls 3, and through any frustration, I tried to give it the benefit of the doubt.

On March 12th, 2025 I completed all of Dark Souls 3's achievements after 33 hours. I wanted to try another run to get 100% completion and thought Elden Ring would have taken a bit too long so decided on Dark Souls Remastered. This full achievement run took me 44 hours to complete, and I'm honestly just relieved to be able to uninstall the game. Don't get me wrong - I don't regret doing it... and there were several parts of the game I did enjoy. After I fully completed DS3 I felt euphoric. After DS1 I just felt disappointed.

I'll try my best to name the things that annoyed me:

- The inability to warp between bonfires in the early game. (This was made up by the fact that the map design was next level, but still caused a lot of time-consuming runs. Also, you could only warp to the main bonfires of the area, which sucked in a ton of situations)
- The weird attack animations of some weapons. (The Halberds, for example, feel like the heaviest weapons on earth)
- The sheer amount of enemy spam. (I don't know how many times I got to an area I had never seen before and was so disappointed that the "difficulty" of the area was nothing more than enemies placed everywhere of very little variety)
- Base mobs can parry, backstab, AND have grab animations?
- Armor. (I swear unless you are in Stone Knight armor or Havel's armor, bosses and regular mobs will still hit you like a truck. I ran through the first time very easily, but on NG+ and NG++ even the base mobs were hitting me for 80% of my HP bar on 40 vigor with Elite Knight Armor)
- Equipment Load. (Medium equipment load is so incredibly annoying. The roll made you sit there for almost as long as fat rolling. It also felt like the iframes were so brutal. I only had a chest piece on for most of the game because the medium load was that terrible to play with)
- Terrible PVP latency. (Any time I was forced to restore humanity for a quest, I was invaded and hit by magic or a heavy weapon that was more than a roll length away from my character)
- Unclear objectives. (I was following a guide, but had I not? I would have been completely lost about what to do or where to go)
- Bosses. (3 versions of a stray demon, bosses having dogs or skeletons or other base mobs inside the boss rooms, hitboxes going outside of animations, BED OF CHAOS, and boss rooms that didn't fit the size of the boss. They all contributed to me disliking almost every boss fight in this game. Sif is the exception. I actually kind of enjoyed him)

Posted: March 23
I've beaten all bosses several times (DLC too), but I’m baffled how this game became so overrated. I’ll elaborate, but if you just want something better: try Sekiro or Elden Ring. Since the community must be wearing 1-inch thick rose-tinted glasses, I'll focus on reasons the game is bad.

The first thing you need to know: this game will deliberately waste your time. It tries to screw with you, in all ways both subtle and blatant. I wlll not catalog them here, because it would take several pages. But people are usually in a worse mood after playing. You probably will be too.

Second crucial point: the mechanical depth is more shallow than you probably expect. Bosses are riddled with arbitrary moves and janky hitboxes, which you memorize to survive. While the combat is richer than Skyrim’s, that's not saying much. When we “get good” as players, we're not mastering some rich combat mechanics with depth. We're mostly learning the arbitrary quirks specific to that boss, and the boss often becomes trivialized after. This trial-and-error then restarts for the next boss, like trying different keys to a house.
This community like to pretend how “challenging” the game is. But I believe there’s a misunderstanding about what makes things “difficult”. You can make any game seem “hard” by making the rules unintelligible, or blindfolding people. Dark Souls feels more like a memorization game, dressed up as a combat game. Actually, modern souls games have this problem too. But I found it painfully noticeable here. If you’re looking for rich combat mechanics, you won't find it here.

The “platforming” element is trash-tier. We all hate these pointless cliffs and walkways in Sens Fortress. We can all tell it’s just there to waste your time. That’s the sort of thing you’re giving money to here.

The PvP is also disappointing. The meta is dominated by backstabs, which are so broken that your incentive is to go for them whenever possible. You'll get "backstabbed" from all angles, the jank is absurd. And it doesn't help that the community is toxic. You will interact with the sorts who never worked with coworkers or had a successful relationship. It's quite acceptable to just play offline and use guides instead of reading the player tips.

If you do get into this game (like I did), you can skip boss runbacks by copying your DRAKS0005.sl2 save file just before entering the boss arena. FromSoftware themselves have moved away from boss runbacks in recent years. But it's probably better to just stop paying for a game that's so disrespectful to the player's time.

I offer this as a warning, that is not being given adequately by others. This game is currently very overrated. To a level that, I believe, does a disservice to people buying it.

Once you understand the mechanics, you will find that Dark Souls is not as difficult as you might think and is actually a lot of fun to play.

I’ve tried a lot of times to get into the From Software catalogue, but this was my first successful go after quite a few attempts. Unfortunately, there definitely is a moment you have to wait for where it “clicks”, but once you get over that hurdle you’ll find the game—for the most part—to be a blast.

The music is pretty good for a lot of the time, but it is scarce due to the limited budget. The sound design is serviceable, but not particularly memorable.

The artistic direction of the game is still quite beautiful, and I really hope there’ll be a remake in the future. I would love to see Undeadberg in modern graphics, because it’s so gorgeous even with the older ones.

I love the interconnected map, which I think perfectly balances the advantages of the Open World approach of promoting exploration, while keeping it linear enough to avoid how overwhelming that can be.

The game isn’t always fun though, and there is a decent amount of jank that hinders the experience a bit, as well as some truly questionable and just outright unfun bosses—looking at you, BoC, Centipede Demon, and the f***ing Capra Demon —with some absolutely atrocious arenas.

I find the way the NG+ scales is pretty poorly handled. Only the bosses get a substantial health boost, but ALL enemies get a large damage boost. The only reason I was able to survive so well was because in NG+3 when gunning for the final achievements, I was *rock*ing Havel's armour set, and put loads of points into vitality in earlier playthroughs. It doesn't feel like the game gives you enough souls to level up to any reasonable degree for what you'd be following your first play-though.

Dark Souls isn’t just a game—it’s a towering monument of misery and majesty, a story so grand it feels carved from the bones of a dying world. It’s peak fiction, a sprawling ruin of a place called Lordran that pulls you in with its shadowy beauty, like a crumbling castle begging you to explore its secrets. Every step is a dance with death, every corner a painting of despair, all lit by the faint glow of a fire that’s barely hanging on. The fights are brutal poetry—swinging a sword feels like defying fate itself, and dodging an attack is a middle finger to the void.

I grinned like a madman when I toppled the gaping dragon, my heart pounding as I wandered through the ghostly glow of Anor Londo, a city so gorgeous it hurts. The story? A puzzle of broken gods and endless cycles, so deep it could drown you if you let it. It’s a journey that turns pain into something holy—five stars for that alone. But let’s be real: for all its haunting brilliance, it’s still just a warm-up act for Dark Souls 2, the true king of fiction that leaves everything else in the dust.

A fantastic first offering for what becomes a well beloved franchise. Offers a clearer story and direction than those that come after. A simplified combat system, that not without it's flaws gives players a real sense of reward as completion is earned. Great boss encounters around every corner, and never once did I feel I was being punished for exploring as there are plenty of rewards hidden all over it's often not obvious paths. The game is not without its faults; generous hit boxes on enemies make you question how in the world "that attack" hit you. The rolling mechanic that is so often used to save your own life will probably take it just as often as you try to navigate various terrain far off of the ground. And the weapon and armor system that allows you to strengthen your gear will have you plotting, and scheming, and more than likely watching youtube videos to find the last of whatever you're looking for.
Overall, an amazing game, and one that continues to be over the years an absolute MUST PLAY. 10/10

Knowledge Check: The Game

If you don't already know the ins & outs of the various NPC story lines, enemy placements or attack patterns,
you'll suffer until *you do know them*. Attacks are poorly choreographed, enemies are often placed in locations where you can't infer their existence until they inevitably jump out of their hiding spot & hit you - giving you that knowledge for when you get back there.

You can easily miss dialogue, entire NPCs and even the "intended" way of learning about the alternate ending.
Sometimes even the area is obscured from you (darkness / invisibility), so you feel extra safe in your lack of knowledge.

When you *do* know, then the game becomes an open garden with many paths, great experiences and a rich story, neatly packed action and a lot of options in how to tackle each fight.

TL;DR: great game if you have knowledge, cheap design tricks & poor player experience if you don't.

To be modest, this game is life-changing. I was hesitant to play any souls-like until I played Dark Souls because the reputation it has for being unforgiving was super intimidating. After playing through this game, I want to differentiate the reputation and reality of Dark Souls.

Dark Souls IS challenging, often times feeling impossible and daunting, but it is also easier than it appears.

Dark Souls is a game that requires you to use everything you have at your disposal. You can absolutely throw yourself at enemies over and over again, memorizing patterns and brute forcing your way to the next bonfire if you want Dark Souls to feel like the impossible game it has the reputation for being.
Alternatively, the game gives you so many things you can use to make this game really easy. You can farm souls, summon player or NPC allies to help you, upgrade your weapons and armor, and so much more.

You will have to put in the work to make this game possible, and you're then given probably the most rewarding experience in a game. You will feel yourself get stronger: maybe that first boss took you 50 tries to beat, and then that 15th boss only took 1 try. On top of that, you get to experience the most subtle but thoughtful story in gaming. You could easily go through this entire game and not pick up on a single plot point, as the story is found in item descriptions, hidden NPC dialogue, the music, and the atmosphere.

Honestly it's just okay. Like, this game has the potential to be a lot better.

There are some buggy hitboxes, for example coming up against enemies like Royal Sentinels. Hitting them always feels inconsistent, but they aren't the worst, you can always bait them to shield smashing and that's that. It is not the worst.

Some bosses are just outright unfair or just annoying. Ornstein and Smough for example (Yes, I know this boss is amazing to most, but hear me out). Getting Ornstein's spear was honestly near impossible, as he can attack you even whilst being behind Smough. Solaire is awful for this fight, and cannot kill neither Smough or Super Ornstein because of his poor AI, which they could have fixed honestly, but if you aren't worried about his spear then it isn't a bad fight, far more easy. Centipede demon is extremely janky, and it's obvious to tell it is unfinished (Camera locking off randomly, crazy point of views), and the small amount of room given to fight him. As great as they are, DLC bosses don't really feel like they belong. Now, don't get me wrong, they are some really fun, albeit hard fights but far too aggressive instead of being more slow paced like the standard game. IMO, the good bosses were just about everything else, I actually had fun during pretty much the rest of the game & summoning AI works just about fine for everyone except O&S. My favorite was probably fighting Gaping Dragon with knight Solaire. I won't complain about Bed of Chaos as it's extremely easy to beat when you know the strategy for it (a short Google search away).

Some areas of the game are pretty poorly designed. Take, for example, Anor Londo. This place is absolutely magnificent but going in at about level 40 is pretty bad. Rooftop archers & Royal Sentinels are just such a pain, but other than that I did find the rest of it enjoyable (Except for O&S). Lost Izalith gets a lot of flak, but it's not the worst if you're fast and sneak through all the dragon butts. Tomb of the Giants is just ridiculous game design. The place does not have to be absolutely pitch black & it doesn't help that skeleton dogs have to be insanely tricky to deal with. After that, Nito's boss fight is good, as is Pinwheel's. Both are definitely pretty good. Crystal Caves is just poor game design, but it does not have to be. The paths are way too hidden, and the snow flakes don't help very much - if, at all at some times. But I won't hate on it too much, since Seath's extremely easy boss fight makes up for it.

I think my deal with this game is that I want to love it so bad, but it's just hard to. Like mentioned, it has potential to be so, so much better. Will I still be playing? Yes, always! And do I appreciate I was gifted it? Of course.

But, I wish FromSoftware just actually finished the game instead of just overhauling it's graphics, more obvious bugs and adding multiplayer. There's a lot of lost potential here, but this wasn't the game to receive a remaster, but instead a REMAKE while staying true to the original game from what I have seen.

If you're into games that challenge you to the core and reward you for every ounce of effort you put in, Dark Souls Remastered is a must-play.The game takes everything that made the original Dark Souls legendary and enhances it with better graphics, smoother gameplay, and faster load times. Posted: March 8
It’s Dark Souls! Despite its rough edges, it’s fun enough to revisit every now and then. My biggest gripe is how the game is packed with tight corridors and enemy placements that feel deliberately cheap, more about forcing memorization than presenting a genuine challenge. This issue is compounded by how sluggish your character feels, like controlling a sack of rocks, especially if you’re accustomed to the movement in Dark Souls 3, Elden Ring, etc. As a result, traversing many areas turns into a painful slog (Undead Burg, Sen’s Fortress, the first half of New Londo, etc.).

That said, the world’s interconnectivity is easily this game’s crowning achievement. The relatively compact world is a joy to explore, and it's sequels have never quite replicated this since, making it a marvel even today. Unfortunately, the boss design is hit-or-miss. Too many fights boil down to either massive damage sponges with awkward AOE attacks (Gaping Dragon, Centipede Demon) or gimmicky puzzle bosses (Priscilla, Bed of Chaos). For experienced players, they feel either mind-numbingly easy or frustratingly unfair, both ultimately just boring. That said, the standouts are fantastic. Ornstein and Smough, Artorias, Manus, all top-tier fights that keep you on edge and test your combat skills.

A lot of the game’s difficulty, though, depends on your build. Some pyromancies and sorceries can destroy bosses from a safe distance, while something like Havel’s armor lets you face-tank and R1 your way to victory with minimal effort. It’s a slower, more "methodical" game at its core compared to later FromSoft titles, which is both to its benefit and detriment. Overall, a solid 7/10. A great game with bogged down by a slew of flaws.

Posted: March 8
Dark Souls is an incredible game, despite some flaws. It isn’t impossibly difficult, but its challenge is what makes it so rewarding. The game doesn’t hold your hand—you’re left to figure things out on your own, which makes every discovery and victory feel truly earned.



Pros:

A strong sense of achievement and growth as you overcome obstacles.

FromSoftware’s unique storytelling style rewards those who take the time to piece things together.
Engaging combat with real consequences, encouraging deliberate and methodical play.

Multiple ways to approach any challenge, allowing for creativity and adaptability.

Excellent level design in the first half of the game.



Cons:
The game explains very little, so new players may need to look things up. Some insist you shouldn’t, but there’s nothing wrong with it—just try to limit how much you rely on guides to preserve the satisfaction of discovery.

FromSoftware’s storytelling can be vague and inaccessible if you prefer a more direct narrative.

The second half of the game declines in level design quality, and many of the bosses after feel less enjoyable.



Despite its shortcomings, Dark Souls is a rewarding experience that thrives on challenge, exploration, and discovery. If you're up for it, it’s well worth playing.

Dark Souls will go down in gaming history as one of those wild turning points in video games where everything just spun around on a dime. It took a second for that fire to catch, but you've now got a whole genre carrying the torch for this game. It's one of those titles that launches a thousand imitators and you'll find its DNA in games completely unrelated to its mechanics.

I first played on my PS3 after the announcement of DS2. It kicked my ass, I put it down, and I came back a couple months later. It is physically impossible for me to be completely objective about this title and I will fully admit to that. Yes, it's a master piece. But it's got some glaring flaws that look like time crunch and budget constraints were cosplaying as Just Stop Oil protesters tossing tomato soup on it. Even sitting back and beating it in a little over 30 hours, Lost Izalith, New Londo, Pyromancy as magic system, and a few other nitpicks here and there absolutely bog down the experience. But God bless it, I beat this game the first time on the same day I buried my childhood dog during Christmas break of my freshman year in college. It's physically and emotionally impossible for me to not start bawling when I fight Sif or Gwyn, and I feel like somebody pumped me full of pure adrenaline every time I fight Manus.

I don't care what numerical value you want to assign to this game. It's a perfect 10 for me and From is the only developer in the industry that gets my support day 1 of a release. I don't even like dedicated online multiplayer games and I'm gassed for Nightreign. If that's not glowing praise, nothing is. Play Dark Souls. Pitch a big ol' baby fit like I did because the capra demon is BS. Pick the game back up in a couple months. Kick his ass and physically feel the mechanics suddenly click in your mind. Go on a mad pursuit of that high until you set yourself on fire or make an entire species of funky snake men your ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Thank me later.

Upfront I have already played Elden Ring and the DLC and put in well over 400+ hours into the game since it was my first time dipping into the Dark Souls/Soulsborne games from FromSoftware. So coming into this game I already had a skewed perception since I beat the entire DLC with no summons/magic and thought this game would a pushover and boring..... Oh dear lord was I wrong...

Dark Souls: I beat the Game and DLC and onto my first NG+.

The game is a great experience if you have an open mind and pay attention to all the details and hints the game gives you (Why do I keep hearing a bell??? hmmm.....), just remember to write them down or take a screenshot otherwise off to YouTube and the Dark Souls Wiki you'll go to find out what "Fingers : Try Hole" means (sorry that's an Elden Ring nuisance message you see a lot). The combat is streamlined and to the point; you attack, block, parry or dodge to defeat enemies and with various weapons and armour to kit out builds. The world design is amazing with everything from vast open areas with beautiful backgrounds all the way to dark cramped corridors. Sounds design is amazing were in boss battles the music comes in being bombastic to set the tempo and there are areas where a more surreal cool sounds is played to help get you lost in the exploration of the world, and sometimes there is no sound besides your boots stomping and sound of your clanging armour. The story is a deep as you want it to go, you can block it all out and go boss rush or you hive dive into the deep end and get lost in the rich lore, trust me I ended up watching tons of of videos on Youtube since I loved it that much. Repeatability is great since there is new game + feature where you can just replay the game over and over again and the game scales with your level to keep the challenge going. Now onto "CHALLENGE" this game will break you down mentally and physically and ask your to apologize for wasting its time. However, with enough practice, patience and perseverance you can overcome anything in the game (still though... FU Smough and Ornstein) and end up being the person who says to the next person wanting to try Dark Souls "Git Gud".

Side note: There are still some glitches and areas where if you do get stuck it may be better to restart the game but that's mostly just early on in the game. If you worried about the game I'd wait for a Stem Sale and pick it up then.

Conclusion: Game is a Timeless Classic and deserves your love and attention.

10/10

I went into this game blind and I am very glad for it. I typically play metroidvanias, challenging platformers, retro, or puzzle games; Dark Souls took what I love about all of those genres and made what was one of my favorite gaming experiences. Exploring the world and braving the unknown was such a thrill, but there's more reward to be found in replaying the game with different strategies and mastering the many boss fights.

The world is interconnected in a very satisfying way and often gives that feeling one has in a metroidvania when opening up a useful shortcut between areas. Whenever the player feels like they have everything down, the game ramps up and throws something new and wild at you. Dark Souls is punishing and hard by design, so that a player has to really learn the game if they want to succeed. The boss fights are well-choreographed puzzles that flow so well.

So many memories will stick with me from my first run of this game and I've beaten it five more times, as of writing this review.

There's also a ton of awful memories, but that's what makes the game great. Sometimes you just have to die in a funny or wildly unfair way. It keeps you on your toes. There's also Bed of Chaos and there's really not much to do about that.

I cannot recommend this enough. You will know if it's for you, or not, within the first half hour. If not, you can still return it with time to spare. If, however, you enjoyed the introduction of the game, then you are in for a treat.

P.S. I am still terrible at parrying.

Should you try Dark Souls 1 if you have completed the newer games?
Maybe, maybe not. It is 50/50 for me.

I wanted to do a spoiler-free run and check content as little as possible.

What I did not know was that when killing the final boss, the game restarts automatically into NG+.
No prompt, no pop-up, no warning, nothing.
This completely spoiled the feeling of victory, as I had more content to complete.
You also lose all your embers for upgrades.

Accessing the content for the DLC requires progressing to the mid-to-late game.
This means that I will never play the content I paid for; I do not have the patience to do it all over again.

In some aspects, the game has not aged too well.

For instance, there is no fast travel until the mid-to-late game, and some runbacks are *very* long. It is a massive time waster.

Some bosses are buggy, like Smough and Ornstein.

This is not a hard fight; this is a very buggy fight, especially Ornstein.
He sometimes jumps in one direction, freezes, then rushes towards you.

In his greater form, sometimes blocking works, and sometimes it doesn't.
His lance will go through pillars, and his missiles will go through Smough.

The hitboxes in the game can be quite imprecise.
It is a known issue on YouTube, just check 'Dark Souls hitboxes' to know what I am talking about.

Some areas are very mid, like Blighttown or Lost Izalith.

On the positive side of things, the game is big, and the lore is intriguing.
It is also interesting how it laid the foundation for future Souls games.

I would like to say that I think this is a very good game. It's well designed, with engaging and fun exploration of a fascinating world, it has a brilliant aesthetic and every area feels distinct and unique to explore. The sound design is simply beautiful, and I absolutely love the core gameplay. It's exceedingly satisfying to control in a way that few games match for me.

You might thus rationally be wondering why I'm giving this game a negative review, then. I enjoy it, I intend to keep playing it. What's the big issue? Allow me to explain in a sentence.

In order to enjoy this game to any degree like I have described, I had to use a mod program to give myself infinite health and stamina.

This is Dark Souls, so you're almost certainly aware of the game's punishing difficulty. If that's something you enjoy in your entertainment, then by all means I encourage you to play it. But for me, the sheer amount of constant death combined with the distances between bosses and checkpoints or even just regular areas and checkpoints made it so frustrating that I couldn't get into it when trying the game on several other platforms, as well as other games like it. I really want to love these games, but I simply don't find the gameplay loop enjoyable enough to ignore that I die far too often and make far too little progress to consider myself as having a good time. If I die to a boss and have to start all the way back at the start of the area, taking on many enemies that could kill me before I even get the chance to try again, I don't find that fun. I find it frustrating and outright painful.

That's an issue I have with Soulslikes in general - the only one I've never played is Sekiro and I have no reason to think I'd enjoy that one any more than I do the others - but if I could also bring up one smaller issue I have with this game in particular, I think the level design can be a bit awkward in places. Blighttown is especially annoying, mainly because I do not for the life of me understand how you could possibly be expected to make it through that area without constant poison deaths if you're not cheating the game like I do.

Dark Souls of all Dark Souls

I never liked Souls games. As a matter of fact, I was afraid of them. I knew of the hardship and difficulty people used to talk about it in the internet, through memes and articles. This is coming from someone who grew up playing First Person Shooters and Platform Adventure games. Until I got the chance to play the Devil May Cry Series. Which, in my opinion, is not an easy game for first timers needing to master the combat system and eventually it opened the doors for me to try other games similar to Devil May Cry. Years went by and I saw Fromsoftware's proclaimed game ELDEN RING getting a DLC. It peaked my curiosity so I caved in and bought the main game with the DLC. To my Surprise, Elden Ring has become one of my favorite games of all time. Thanks to Elden Ring, I wanted to expand and try out Fromsoft's other titles such as ,. Then, I decided to try out Dark Souls Remastered. Where it all began.

Dark Souls atmosphere really made it unique and interesting. Fromsoft's environment storytelling is phenomenal in this game. Dark Souls world is grim, desolate, brutal, lonely. The atmosphere is heavy and defeating. While Elden Ring sure feels courageous and uplifting with its godly battles. Dark Souls, in the other hand, immerses you in a realm that appears old, heavy, and ruthless. Right from the initial moments, you are met with a feeling of solitude. This is not a world where individuals guide you. It’s an adventure in a deteriorating realm brimming with concealed stories, enigmatic NPCs, and an atmosphere of unavoidable downfall. You get to experience these themes and emotions through the game's many places. From Undead Asylum, Depth, Undead Burg, New Londo Ruins, Catacombs. Even beautiful but eerie looking places such as Darkroot Garden, The Duke's Archives, Anor Lando, and many others.

However, one place conveyed a different atmosphere, and its the Firelink Shrine. Firelink Shrine is the hub world for Dark souls as to Elden Ring's Roundtable Hold, It gives off a feeling of warmth and shelter. You're sorrounded by NPCs, despite with different emotions and agendas, but still wish you as the player to be safe on your journey and hope for you to never go hollow.

Dark Souls Combat has depth but a little jank and I think that's special. There is a lot of weapons and secret weapons you can get. Funny, secret weapons are usually from the boss's tails you have to cut to earn them.

The game is punishing duh
there is no Stake of Marika , so you have to walk all the way back to the boss arena. You exhaust your stamina so fast its insane. Every action comes with a huge stamina cost. The game is punishing and brutal but, like with every souls game, you learn through trial and error. Every inch of progress you make is a small victory. Every boss you kill, you gain great reward and grow powerful. I got humbled trying to play Dark Souls 1 with the experience I got from playing Elden Ring. If you've played Elden Ring, you get to appreciate the QOL and Convenient Features Elden Ring has.

I don't know how to put it, but there’s a haunting beauty in its dark, gothic aesthetic. experiencing Dark souls is sorrowful and grim but in a beautiful meaningful way.

you try to play through the game without any guides. Your first playthrough is the best playthrough you will ever experience. Looking up guides ruins the experience for the first time. I am currently in my 2nd playthrough to get 100% completion.

Even today revisiting Dark Souls still surprises me.
I was actually a huge Castlevania series player at the beginning and played almost every game many times. By the time I heard about Demon's Souls, I realized that the game was filled with a lot of Castlevania elements, and that the maker should have been more or less inspired by the Castlevania series, but at the same time it was also filled with a lot of original gameplay elements and unique thematic styles that stood out to me. While Demon's Souls still had its fair share of flaws, it was full of potential, but unfortunately wasn't well received by Sony at the time, which ultimately led to sales that fell short of expectations, and so From Software and Sony had this not-quite-as-pleasant experience together. It wasn't until two years later (2011) that Hidetaka Miyazaki developed Dark Souls as a spiritual sequel to Demon's Souls, and since then the Souls series has been on a flourishing path of creativity. Today, the Soul series is known to players all over the world, and Soul-like games have become a very popular genre label.
As the originator of the Dark Souls series, Dark Souls 1 has excellent map design, unique gameplay mechanics, a rich worldview and plot background, a unique narrative, immersive immersion, and a variety of colorful ARPG elements. Although there are some minor flaws, the overall finish is very high, and the game system design is very mature, even today's perspective, it is still an excellent masterpiece.
Since then, the Dark Souls saga has officially begun.

Posted: March 1
Don't be put of by the reputation that Soulslike has, this game is incredible and I believe everyone should experience this at least once.

Dark Souls is punishing, but not unfair. The game doesn't hold your hand in any regards, but that makes the feeling of accomplishment even greater. The pure Dopamine you get from beating bosses is euphoric, especially those that you have been stuck on for some time.

The story is captivating in its themes and narration, its subtle story telling is done very well and is supported by the amazingly interconnected world with characters that are a joy to interact with and to encounter. The soundtrack is a masterpiece (Gwyn's theme is chefs kiss). Gameplay mechanics are logical and well-thought-out and reward the player for mastery of the system. The only gripes I had with Dark Souls was that some of the areas felt sort of mundane and obsolete with no lasting flavour and the latter stages of the game felt a bit hasty, but this is partially overwritten by the connectivity of the world and the way the lore ties in. Once you get a hang of core principles of the game it almost becomes second nature, making it very satisfying to parry, dodge and attack at the perfect time. Some moments were definitely frustrating but overcoming them was all worth it and made for an overall very enjoyable experience.

Dark Souls isn't perfect, but comes pretty damn close.

Highly Recommended 9/10

I finished Dark Souls for the first time today…
If you’re reading this, play this game. Finish it, if you stopped at some point, as I did, about 8 years ago. I was living with my family, eight people in a 3 bedroom apartment, and one of the few things I had was my xbox and this game. I don’t think I even got to the taurus demon then! I put it away and didn’t touch it for years, but I came back record and have fun.
This game is life changing. It seems kind of silly to say that about a video game, but it’s true. I was expecting a masterpiece, and I played one. It absolutely has it’s flaws, mainly glitches and odd hit boxes, but don’t let that stop you.
Combat? Exhilarating.
Exploration? Intriguing.
Music? Divine.
Leveling and equipment improvement? Nearly perfect.
Bosses? Awesome! (except bed of chaos)
At first I didn’t care about the NPCs, but then Solaire saved me from a headache with a certain early fight in the game, and I realized I wasn’t truly alone. I still couldn’t save him…
This game’s best moments are breathtaking, almost literally (beating a boss after the 10th try ALWAYS got my heart pumping). The lows are pretty… hilarious. I didn’t have too much trouble in Blighttown, the place I was fearing most, and the enemies in Lost Izalith are goofy (except Bed Of Effing Chaos, screw that fight). There were multiple times I almost gave up on this game, but there’s something special about it, that neither Demon’s Souls nor Elden Ring have. (I’ve played a bit of Elden Ring, and beat Demon’s Souls once before playing this game) There were moments of doubt, of wondering why I was bothering, cursing my loneliness in this brutal world, but then the brief periods of camaraderie, joy, and satisfaction made it all worth it.
Many People talk about Dark Souls as an allegory for depression or grief, and I absolutely see it. These last 5 years have been hard for a lot of people, me included, and this game came to me at the perfect time.
If you actually read this whole thing, thank you! And Good Luck!
PRAISE THE SUN!!!

Posted: February 24
Unfortunately I'm mixed at best. I have a lot to say but I'll just bullet point and try to keep it as brief as I can:

- Way too much running/not enough bonfires. Even after fast travel is unlocked. This is one of my shortest points but I can't overstate that this is the biggest issue, maybe even more than all the others combined.
- Common enemies (as well as a few bosses) are boring. Most just have a few different attack patterns, the ones that don't are either hit or miss (e.g; Blighttown bugs & Demonic Foliage suck ass, skeleton pinwheels and Halo-esque carrier floods are really cool). I developed pattern-recognition when I was 2 I promise you don't need to keep testing it over, and over, and over again.
- WAY too much repetition. The only common reasons to die on your way back to your body is because either you had no business getting there in the first place, or more likely, that you're impatiently running through enemies to get back because you simply don't want to fight them again.
- Area-specific gimmicks are typically awful. Tomb of the Giants making me memorize a route unless I want to use a niche item to make me only half-memorize it isn't fun nor is it difficult, it's just a poor gimmick. Fake Seath existing is random and stupid as ♥♥♥♥, especially the fact that he's reaccessible. No bonfires in New Londo is just a gimmick which only worsen's my above bullet point, and I think the transient curse annoyances and Blighttown gimmick/s annoyances go without saying tbh these ones seem universal.
- It turns out there is such a thing as too open world. I went down into the Catacombs first and despite how hard the enemies were to kill, this franchise has such a reputation for difficulty that I thought it must the right way, not to mention the NPC at Firelink Shrine telling me to "go underground".
- Bosses (especially late-game) are very easy, even if you don't want to go the pattern recognition route you can usually just orbit them and win and the game should not be a self-imposed challenge.
- I shouldn't accidentally hit an NPC then have to pay a quadrillion souls in some far away tower for them to stop being mad at me. I also shouldn't be able to lose out on items permanently from accidentally doing this at the beginning of the game.
- There is a very obvious meta and despite that you don't properly unlock different ways of playing the game until you're halfway through it (e.g; ability to increase poise to a point where it actually changes playstyle, useful spells, etc). I want the game to be balanced to a point where I can play it a certain way which is equal in effectiveness to any other way. Again, the game should not be a self-imposed challenge due to a playstyle choice.
- Game is very clearly unfinished despite being a remaster (yes, I know it's not the same as a remake). You just can't have unaccessible areas look accessible and vice versa in a game which punishes you for death so hard because this results in it acting as a punishment for exploring. Also I'm not sure if the lock-on system is broken or just poor but it's being thrown in here too.
- The game doesn't properly teach some mechanics nor does it properly explain others. Different levels of equip load being by far the absolute worst offender. Having to do math for a peak maneuverability to strength ratio is diabolical.
- Not related to the game and thankfully only a rather small subset of the community, but some people get real high up on their horses about enjoying this franchise because of the difficulty reputation it has, and any and all criticism is met with "skill issue".

This all being said, there is plenty to love about this game and absolutely none of my complaints are unsolvable, so I'm excited for the sequels, but there's no doubt that the bad overshadows the ♥♥♥♥ out of the good, I probably will not finish my NG+ playthrough which I barely started. A lot of the environments, designs, and systems/mechanics are cool. The game feels really good to play. The lore is interesting enough. Gwynevere. The music is peak. The comedy (both community-given, and physical) is great. I don't know if I'll skip straight to Elden Ring or if I'll play Dark Souls II first, I guess it'll depend on how much I think Dark Souls II resolves the issues of Dark Souls I, but yeah, this game was bad for me overall. I'll add to this review if I remember everything else but I think I got the bulk of it.

Posted: February 24
Dark Souls 1 is both one of the best and worst games I've ever played. However, I cannot just give it a thumbs down after how much I've played this game, the first half of the game is genuinely some of the most fun I've had in any video game. Once you're at Anor Londo, it's very clear that the development of the game took a hit. Anor Londo is still relatively fine, though much of the map design felt empty and almost forgotten. Though Anor Londo did have what is likely the best boss fight in the entire game.

Dark Souls also has a message behind everything in the game. Don't give up. The entire point of Dark Souls is over coming everything and how rewarding it feels when you overcome a boss or area that you had trouble in. I found myself almost breaking my controller at times because of the difficulty in some areas, the game was almost always telling me "hey you can't go that way, turn around and try something else" though it'd take me a while to learn that. I kept booting the game back up because I knew I could do it. I knew I could keep moving, the game is telling the player to keep that hope, to keep moving even when everything is hopeless. Dark Souls is about fighting that depression and not letting it over take you.

Now here's the part that I don't want to talk about. The second half of Dark Souls 1 is bad. I'm not hyperbolizing when the second half of the game is one of the worst parts of any video game I've ever played. Many of the teachings that Dark Souls 1 pulls off in the first half, finding ways around bosses if you're not strong enough, learning the right times to dodge, attack and parry, etc. The game throws those out the window. The Four Kings were incredibly annoying to fight, although I got through them on my second try, All I really had to do was tank damage and just kill them over and over. Nito was boring, as all he really did was just annoy you with skeletons and then scream and launch you 6 feet in the air, another boss where all I did was just tank damage and then kill the guy. Seth was alright, he's easy but he's interesting as you can also get the Moonlight Greatsword from him, I love that little kings field reference. The bed of Chaos is really funny but also just stupid easy and boring to fight. Both the Demon Ruins and Lost Izalith both just suck. They feel like the devs just had their kid come in, place the Tarus Demon and Kappra Demon everywhere and leave it at that.

Oolacile was fun. I was way overleveled when I entered Oolacile however I still found it to be fun little side quest to go on, shout out Sif for protecting your owner's sword and grave and always trying to protect those around you that enter so they don't make the same mistake as Artorias.

I would give Dark Souls 1 a 10/10 if the second half of the game had been finished. However, for a game that prides itself on being tough but fair, and then completely dropping off that point, I cannot ignore that. Games like Dark Souls just can't have flaws that huge, The game isn't fun past that point unless you're like me and way overleveled for everything because you spent your time playing through the game. And even then, the game lacks in a lot of areas that it just shouldn't. I really wish Fromsoft would have given this game way way more time to cook in the oven, as it would've likely been the best gave I've ever played. I know the game was unfinished and it's not exactly the developers fault, however I still cannot ignore the large amount of issues the game has.

Dark Souls 1 is a 6/10 game with the best first half of any game I've ever seen. If you have the money to buy Dark Souls 1, please pick it up. It is one of the greatest games of all time.

Dark Souls being the first big name "souls-like" game (at least to really put it on the map) really shows itself here. As this isn't my first souls like game, many of some of the major obstacles that someone who would first encounter in this gameplay style might struggle with, didn't come as much of a challenge. While that being said, the atmosphere and vibe you get from the setting is very grim and really makes this game standout more from the others I've played so far. That's why when you get those more vibrant colors that you're not used to seeing through this dark game it really adds to the spectacle of the scenery.

DS1 gameplay is the bread and butter of from software games. Dodge and hit, parry and block, etc. The real struggle that this game will through at you is the environment and just doing what it can to frustrate you. While I didn't struggle too much with blight town or sen's fortress, you will definitely have some slip ups here and there that will aggravate you. The bosses, especially earlier on, are not going to be the most challenging, getting back to the boss if you die is more of the problem. Some of the bosses however at the end (especially 2 particular in the DLC) did give me some more struggle, but nothing crazy at the end of the day. I will say I still hate bosses with 2 of them at once, DIRL gargoyles and screw you online server for disconnecting me after I beat orn/smough so I had to redo it.

For a first game of this style, it has it some flaws that were obviously improved upon in the later games. But the general gameplay and enjoyment you get from these style of games is definitely still there, and you can still see quite a few things that other games took from. I also really have to highlight on the INTERCONNECTIVITY as there are shortcuts after shortcuts that bring you back full circle that I have yet to experience the same way as sekiro or elden ring. For me, this was about 24ish hours to beat the game once and DLC included (with i'd say at least 90% of the bosses, might have missed some optional ones unsure) but this can definitely take someone who first tries these style of games a lot more. It might take a bit to get into to it your first time, but it's worth it in the end

Dark Souls is easily one of my favorite games in the series, and for good reason. The interconnected world design is brilliant, making exploration feel rewarding and meaningful at every turn. The subtle storytelling and rich lore behind the characters give the world a depth that few games can match, while the soundtrack especially pieces like the haunting music of Ash Lake elevates the atmosphere to another level. Boss fights such as Ornstein and Smough, the Gaping Dragon, Priscilla, and even Gwyn stand out as some of the most memorable in the entire franchise.

That said, the game isn’t perfect. The last quarter of the journey feels unfinished, with areas like the Tomb of the Giants and bosses such as the Bed of Chaos dragging down the pacing. Despite these rough spots, the overall experience is unforgettable. Dark Souls set the standard for the series and remains to be one of the grates I’ll always come back to.

8.8/10

TL;DR: The game is really well made and satisfying with great level design and some of the best world building gaming has to offer. The game falls off pretty hard in the last 1/3 with some pretty obvious cut corners (though the DLC slaps pretty hard), is rather cryptic and janky in places, and has some pretty devious runbacks to bosses, but is not as hard as its reputation would imply. I would highly recommend playing it despite its flawed nature, but would also strongly recommend doing so with the wiki and a YT walkthrough open, just in case.

One of the greatest games ever made. Some of the most interesting and thought out world building in gaming as well as an incredibly satisfying combat and world design. BUT you should definitely temper your expectations and mindset. If you are coming from more modern From Soft games like Elden Ring, DS3, or Bloodborne, this game is very different. The gameplay is much more methodical and patience based rather than reaction based. The game's development was very rushed as well towards the end, meaning many of the areas in the later third of the game, with one standout in particular, can feel both rushed and unfinished. The game also has a fair bit of jank, again, specifically towards the latter third. I also wouldn't go in completely blind. Some mechanics, like weapon upgrading, which is much a much more in depth and complicated process, especially in comparison to Elden Ring, and obtaining boss weapons, require elaboration the game rarely if ever gives. I would recommend that if you are stuck on or confused by something, don't be afraid to look it up. Better to get minor spoilers and know what to do than to be confused and frustrated. As far as online goes, the netcode is pretty ♥♥♥♥, but that applies to literally all From Soft games so...yeah. Just stay hollow and you'll never have to deal with it. The worst thing in the game is the horrific boss runbacks. Once you have a route that you know, you can avoid most if not all enemies or danger, but most runbacks encounter at least one threatening enemy and can sometimes take more than a minute just to get another attempt at a boss.
With that said, I would still say this game rocks. While the last 1/3 can be pretty hit-or-miss, the first 2/3 rock. People have sang the praises of the level design for about a decade at this point so I can't really add much other than to say they aren't lying. I also weirdly enough prefer the way some systems, particularly combat, work in this game than I do the other more "refined" From Soft games. The more plodding and slower pace focuses way more on game knowledge and using that knowledge to make the best decision in the moment, rather than the reaction based fights in future titles. The bosses movesets are also slimmed way down in comparison to other games, which can lead some fights to feel repetitive and lacking, but also makes the more in depth ones feel tighter as it means that you spend less time memorizing how to dodge 20 hit combos that last a whole minute and more on predicting the bosses next action and making tactical decisions.
I also would not be scared of the game's difficulty. I am not particularly awesome at these games so take my difficulty assessments with a grain of salt, but I found this game to actually be pretty easy, especially in comparison to Elden Ring (my most played game of this series, so the one I feel the most comfortable making comparisons to). There are dozens of builds that you can make pretty early on that trivialize most fights, so many you will likely do it by accident if you have any knowledge about how you should be leveling. Most of the difficulty comes instead from some pretty lengthy and the aforementioned boss runbacks.

If you read all of this, thanks for putting up with the text wall, I just really like yapping about this game.

One thing that is incredibly clear when playing this game blind is that this type of game is NOT made to be played blind. The beauty of these games comes from being able to experience the design of the levels and the intricacy of the systems in place, not to discover everything on your own.

An experience I had that made me realize this with certainty was that on my first playthrough (I played through both endings and now I'm writing this review) I went through the entirety of the Tomb of Giants using a skull lantern in my left hand so I could see anything, but apparently if you take a detour in another zone, you can find a helmet that will automatically provide that kind of light so you can actually have a left hand weapon. I obviously had no way to know this without exploring every nook and cranny, and I am not the type to force myself to explore things that don't call out to me. Had I realized what the actual value of these games is sooner, maybe I wouldn't have wanted to tear my hair out during that area.

So with that being said, what I learned from this game is that even if I don't play to be the most meta character and whatnot, there are games that are best enjoyed by learning from the experiences of other players that did the song and dance before me.
