The first Dark Souls is probably the most beginner friendly and unfriendly game of the trilogy at the same time. It’s beginner friendly because it isn’t as fast as DS3 and more consistent in your main defensive option, the dodgeroll, than DS2. On the other hand, understanding stats, why you can’t use a specific weapon effectively and the Inventory UI make the game quite hard to understand for a newcomer. That said, as long as you have someone to explain the UI and show you where to find stat requirements as well as dodgerolls, you are in for a very good game experience, unless you want to play PvP in which case you shouldn’t play this game.

DS1 has the best areas, in the first two thirds at least, because there are a lot of shortcuts, every enemy seems meaningfully placed and discovering secrets feels rewarding. We don’t talk about the last part though. You can take a lot of these shortcuts with the Master Key early on, but I wouldn’t recommend taking it with you on the first play through. It allows you to go into a lot more areas, straying away from the main path and giving you more paths to get stuck on. I think the Master Key was clearly intended for experienced players that want to reach certain items early on.
As long as you follow the main path, the areas aren’t very hard to make progress in. Especially in the beginning traps and several enemies won’t respawn. How do you know you are on the main path? Enemies die within 3 hits or less.

Bosses aren’t really hard most of the time, there are some notable difficulty spikes, but if you played any of the later games you won’t need more than 3-5 tries for each. If you didn’t, they might pose a harder challenge for you, but if you do ~10% or more of the bosses max health per hit it’s easy mode.

Storywise, the game is almost purely lore. If you want to know more, just watch or listen to some of the 1000s of lore videos on YouTube, or try to decipher it yourself if you’d like to do that.

I briefly mentioned that the game isn’t good for PvP, and thats only half true. It’s really hard to get into, with an established meta and people who nolife the ♥♥♥♥ out of this game. The bad Netcode and weird backstab hitboxes won’t result in a good experience for most people, especially those that aren’t already familiar with the Dark Souls trilogy. Some of the multiplayer jank will also translate towards the PvE part of the game which might be frustrating to deal with if you get killed by it, but is also fun to use on enemies if you learn how to do so. 4 directional rolling and limited movement is a stupid problem that should’ve been fixed with the remaster, but wasn’t. Breaking the lock on will allow you to use full directional movement and rolling, but you might loose the enemy and you’ll have to use a clawgrip with a controller. Speaking of which, Mouse and Keyboard are possible to use, but aren’t recommended. The Remaster at least shows the Keyboard inputs, unlike Dark Souls 3.

TL;DR: I think Dark Souls is a very good experience for the first two thirds of the game and the best one for new players of the series, but becomes a drag in the part. Understanding the UI and Inventory is a challenge in itself and even after 100+h I would’ve liked a remade or at least revised version of it. There is a lot of jank that might scare off new players and is loved by veterans. However it's not a good Remaster, there are lots of things that should've been fixed but weren't for some reason. I think they should've given us a proper Remaster with a new UI and bugfixes while keeping the original at least on steam.

My work is finally complete

All of the DS games on steam have been finished (with the grind for the 100% on this game, being soon to come)

Honestly, after the initial mental hurdle of the run backs, they are actually not that bad, considering how much easier the bosses are, and how much more of a patience game they truly are.

The one gripe I have with this game, is that areas I am genuinely should not be in, are literally making me get hard stuck in, just because I were good enough to get deep enough as is, only to then realize that the game doesn't want me to be progressing this certain area just yet.

With the case of the tomb of the giants, I ended up grinding through that area, reaching the dark pit fall areas, and only after having had progressed to the piss wall, had I realized that I were not suppose to be there yet.

While I understand the reason why people defend this approach (and trust me, this is in no way a rant against the darkness of the tomb of the giants, it definitely has its own charm), I just genuinely feel like I kept exploring difficult to access areas, only to run into hard stop segments, where I would be forced to back track, with no clear indication of 1. How was I suppose to know that I weren't suppose to be here?

There are genuinely no NPC's or signs warning of any of this, so you may end up progressing into an area, thinking that you are making progress, only to hit a hard wall that says "You aren't suppose to be here, now go back and figure out where were you suppose to be going to."

Reaching those ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ piss walls kept annoying me so much, as I honestly felt like the game was punishing me for exploring, as I kept not getting any clear guidance, not on where TO GO, but where NOT TO GO.

I'd say the inerconnectedness was a very unique aspect of this game, the run backs weren't as annoying as I had dreaded them being at the beginning of my playthrough, having had faced which, I had genuinely planned to just drop DS1 completely. But once I locked in and began steadily progressing, all went smoothly and it was a nice ride from there.

Dope game, just wish there were more NPC's / clearer dialogue as to what is suppose to be done after whichever part. Especially with the Anor Londo ruins, it took me a millenia to figure out that I were actually missing a key item in progressing that area, as while the wizard on top of the church did give a hint on how to fight the 4 kings, there were no explicit hints to guide me towards what I were missing in being able to survive entering the Abyss.

Again, dope game, needs clear / more hints to better guide you towards natural progression (But honestly, I am genuinely barking at the wrong tree with this one, these are the mf that added an entire DLC cambaign connected to killing some random ass crystal golem in the Duke's archives, and whos excuse for how this was suppose to be naturally figured out by the players, being that they literally included a guide book with the physical version of the game (so if you are playing only digital, you are literally at a natural discovery disadvantage.) )

Again, dope game, fix the clues bro

Old – still gold?

A classic which still holds really well by 2025, even though it was released almost two decades ago. If you’ve never played it but played newer From Software games and now try to decide whether you should try this one or not – my answer would be ‘definitely’. It’s a great experience, the game has its own atmosphere different from other DS’s, and you will learn where FS took off from. I never stumbled upon any bugs, errors or running problems either on PS or PC, Remastered really made it a smooth game even though it’s old.

However, if you’re a fan of newer FS games only, there are some things that have to be mentioned about DS1 in specifics that make playing it a MUCH different experience than let’s say DS3:

1) The gameplay feels much clunkier and less polished. When I replayed DS1 years later, I noticed the huge difference in how my character rolls for example. When I have a target on an enemy, sometimes my character freezes for a couple of seconds when I need to roll sideways, or they roll back/forth instead. In some bosses’ fights such inconvenience can literally cost you a life, like Artorias fight on NG+ (which I ended up winning not targeting A. at all).

2) Not only the gameplay is slower, but the pacing is slower in general. When I’m playing as a light-armored character in DS3 (or in Bloodborne as default), I’m a literal Flash. I’m quick, dexterous and can run miles within a minute. When I’m playing literally naked in DS1, I feel like I’m running underwater. And considering that sometimes I have to run incredibly long distances just to get to a certain bosses’ arena (Bed of Chaos, Gwyn), it feels like a chore just because how long it is. When I hit even light weapons, it feels much slower than anywhere before, while my enemies hit way faster almost in all cases. It’s not really an issue, but you’ll certainly have to get used to it and literally re-train yourself if you’ve never played DS1 before.

3) Speaking of running. You’ll have to go through roughly 40% of the game to get the ability to warp between the bonfires (only certain ones though, not all of them). And even though the game has a huge number of great shortcuts (and better level-design in general which makes all the areas connected), you’ll really feel playing it tiring after some period of time due to constant underwater running required. I can literally play any other FS games for hours and not get tired, but with DS1, I often finish my daily sessions within one or two hours just because how drowsy I get while being in the need of covering long distances at slow tempo.

4) Adding to the point above: while going for the platinum in my re-walkthrough on PC (which requires fully finishing the game 2.5 times), it really turned into a snoozefest for me lol. As I played in on PS years ago, going for the plat was really easy as I could remember. So if you play the game for the first time, you’ll be really interested to explore and engaged in it. But when you know the game too well already? Man, replaying it really puts me to sleep. And even though it’s an objectively great game, I like it the less from all the other FS projects. I know DS3 better than my parents too, I put around 400 hours into it and I still would play it again over DS1 which I gave about 100 hours only.

To sum it all up: DS1 is an old game, and playing it won’t feel as free and smooth as the newer FS projects. It will require much more time and dedication from you, and certain aspects of it will have to click with you before you’ll start to enjoy it (or, well, at least tolerate it). In any case, it still is and will always be worth playing even after years later.

The first time I tried this gem, I didn’t get it. I knew it was going to be a hard game, but dying has never frustrated me—I was excited for the challenge of defeating real bosses. I reached several areas, rang both bells, and by that point, I thought I had the “Dark Souls experience.” But I wasn’t engaged. I couldn’t understand why this franchise was so highly praised.

After about 10 hours of playtime, I set it aside for several weeks.

Then, out of nowhere, I found myself thinking about it again. I decided to give it another shot, but this time, instead of opening the Wiki every time I found a new item, I just kept playing. I revisited earlier areas, interacted with NPCs I had met before, traded with them, found powerful items, tweaked my armour—and without realising it, I was having a ton of fun.

During my first attempts, I was focused on mastering the combat, rushing from boss to boss, and dying over and over. But when I came back, I started paying attention to the world itself: the setting, the mechanics I had dismissed as unimportant, the environmental storytelling. I was playing for enjoyment rather than just to finish the game.

And that, I think, was my mistake. I originally saw Dark Souls as a challenge rather than an experience. I knew the lore was deep and fascinating, but I hadn’t considered it as part of something greater—I saw it as just an excuse to pit the player against brutally difficult enemies. I was very wrong.

Every aspect of this game—its level design, art direction, sound design, gameplay, and memorable characters—makes it something truly unique. I wouldn’t call it a walk in the park, but the difficulty isn’t the main focus; it’s a feature that enhances the experience rather than defining it.

So in many ways dark souls 1 is pretty much what people usually say about it: it is a difficult, often frustrating, but also beautiful and exciting game, that absolutely justifies why it became its own subgenre. It is also literally quite dark and there are souls in it, so the name checks out.

But it is different in an unexpected way, which is why i can't recommend it. Before starting, I kind of assumed that the challenge of the game would come from the difficulty of enemies or unfair leveldesign, but that is not really the case. Most opponents have very straightforward abilities and there are a decent amount of safe points. In that sense the enemies are not really different from other games, only that they might have slightly higher stats and are in a dark environment. The difficulty for me came not from the enemies but from the way the game refuses to explain core concepts about how to become stronger and play the game right.

As someone who has never played a darksouls game before, i hoped that the first part would be a good start, but there was not really much of an introduction. Stuff like: What is the point of your humanity level? or why are there random other players invading my game? or how exactly do i upgrade my weapons beyond a certain point? Those are all things that are never really explained, even though they probably should. And i feel like that lack of information is where the difficulty comes from. A good example for that are the Catacombs, where skeletons revive endlessly. The solution for this area is to use a divine weapon, which stops them from reviving, but the game never tells you that. I know it because I googled it, but there is no explanation in the game about what specifically divine weapons or any weapon type actually do. And that kind of information gap leads to frustration.

Interestingly enough the game approaches exposition about game mechanics in the same way it explains the lore. At the beginning there is a rough overview but then never goes much into details. For the game mechanics that means you will learn your basics movement set but the rest is on you to figure out. For the lore that means you get some narrated cutscenes (which are genuinely great by the way), but then the worldbuilding basically stops. Of course there are countless unique locations and creatures, but i honestly did not learn much about them.

I completely understand that the game wants the players to figure stuff out themselves but then there must also be a way in the game to get that information. Dark souls just explains way less than other games. If the goal of the game is to create a challenge then the challenge should be based on the skills of the player and not on lacking unknowable background information. So my personal experience was quite downgraded by that minimalistic approach to exposition. Therefore i cant recommend it, but recognise that if you already have some background information the game can be fun.

Posted: February 15
The Timeless Mastery of Dark Souls: An Unparalleled Gaming Experience

Few games have left as indelible a mark on the landscape of the gaming industry as Dark Souls. Released in 2011 by FromSoftware and helmed by the visionary Hidetaka Miyazaki, Dark Souls is not merely a game—it is an odyssey, an arduous yet rewarding pilgrimage through a world steeped in sorrow, despair, and an ineffable grandeur. It stands as a beacon of masterful game design, challenging yet fair gameplay, and a narrative that revels in subtlety and ambiguity. To reduce Dark Souls to just a difficult action RPG would be a grave disservice; it is, at its core, a transcendental experience that redefines what it means to truly immerse oneself in a virtual world.

A Masterclass in Worldbuilding

One of Dark Souls’ most awe-inspiring qualities is its intricate and interconnected world. Lordran, the setting of the game, is a labyrinthine tapestry of locales, each one brimming with environmental storytelling that transcends the need for expository dialogue or cutscenes. The grandeur of Anor Londo, the melancholic ruins of New Londo, the treacherous depths of Blighttown—each of these locations is meticulously crafted with an unparalleled sense of place. The way these areas seamlessly connect to one another, forming a cohesive and immersive world, exemplifies a level of craftsmanship rarely seen in video game design.

The game’s verticality and geographical coherence further cement its immersive nature. Players will often experience moments of revelation as they unlock shortcuts or reach vantage points that reveal the vast expanse of Lordran, instilling a deep appreciation for the world’s design. The sense of discovery in Dark Souls is one of its most intoxicating elements; each pathway, each hidden door, and each shortcut imbues the game with a sense of wonder that few other titles can emulate.

Combat: A Symphony of Precision and Consequence

At the heart of Dark Souls lies its combat system—a brutally methodical yet deeply rewarding ballet of swordplay, parries, and dodges. Every encounter, no matter how seemingly insignificant, demands patience, strategic foresight, and unwavering concentration. The weight behind every swing, the precise timing of a roll, and the razor-thin margin for error imbue the combat with an intoxicating sense of consequence.

Unlike many contemporary action RPGs that lean toward fast-paced hack-and-slash mechanics, Dark Souls forces the player to internalize enemy attack patterns, assess situations carefully, and choose their engagements wisely. The diversity of weaponry, from the nimble dexterity of curved swords to the raw, devastating power of ultra greatswords, allows for an astonishing range of playstyles. Every weapon feels unique, and mastering one requires dedication and understanding, reinforcing the game’s overarching theme of perseverance and growth.

Furthermore, Dark Souls refuses to hold the player’s hand. It places the onus of learning squarely on the individual, rewarding those who take the time to understand its mechanics and punishing those who rush blindly forward. The difficulty, often cited as a barrier to entry, is instead a tool through which players derive a profound sense of accomplishment. Each victory over a formidable boss, each hard-fought encounter with a menacing adversary, feels earned, making triumphs all the more exhilarating.

Narrative Through Obfuscation: A Story Woven in Shadows

Unlike traditional RPGs that rely on extensive dialogue and cutscenes to convey their narratives, Dark Souls embraces a far more enigmatic approach. The story of Lordran and its decaying world is told through fragmented item descriptions, cryptic NPC dialogue, and environmental storytelling. The game does not spoon-feed the player its lore; instead, it encourages curiosity and deduction, rewarding those who take the time to piece together its intricate web of history, betrayal, and fate.

This narrative approach makes Lordran feel like a living, breathing world that has existed long before the player’s arrival and will continue to do so long after. The ambiguity fosters discussion, leading players to pore over every minute detail in search of understanding. This sense of mystery transforms Dark Souls into more than just a game—it becomes an intellectual pursuit, a puzzle that exists beyond the confines of its code.

Characters such as Solaire of Astora, Artorias the Abysswalker, and Siegmeyer of Catarina are not just NPCs; they are fragments of a larger, tragic tapestry, each with their own motivations and burdens. Their cryptic dialogue and often melancholic fates only add to the overarching theme of despair that pervades Lordran. There is an unparalleled depth to the world of Dark Souls, one that reveals more with each successive playthrough.

The Sound of Silence: A Hauntingly Beautiful Soundscape

The auditory design of Dark Souls is an exercise in restraint and potency. For much of the game, silence pervades, amplifying the sense of isolation and unease. However, when music does emerge—most notably in boss fights—it serves as an overwhelming force of emotion. Composed by Motoi Sakuraba, the game’s soundtrack perfectly encapsulates the grandeur and tragedy of its world.

The haunting, choir-laden themes of battles such as Ornstein & Smough or Gwyn, Lord of Cinder, resonate deeply, infusing each encounter with a sense of finality and gravity. The lack of persistent background music elsewhere in the game forces the player to focus on the ambient sounds—the distant growls of unseen monstrosities, the crackling of torches, the rhythmic clang of armored footsteps—all of which serve to enhance the immersive quality of the experience.

A Legacy Cemented in Greatness

The impact of Dark Souls on the gaming industry cannot be overstated. It spawned an entire subgenre—the Soulslike—which has influenced countless games, from Hollow Knight to Nioh to Elden Ring. Its emphasis on environmental storytelling, challenging yet rewarding gameplay, and player-driven discovery has set a gold standard that few have managed to replicate.

More than a decade since its release, Dark Souls remains as enthralling and impactful as ever. Its community thrives, its influence persists, and its legend endures. It is a testament to what gaming can achieve when developers trust their audience to engage, learn, and overcome. In the annals of gaming history, Dark Souls stands as a monolithic achievement—a beacon of uncompromising vision, artistic excellence, and unrelenting challenge.

To those who have ventured through Lordran, who have stood before the abyss and emerged victorious, Dark Souls is more than just a game. It is an experience, a trial by fire, and a journey of unparalleled depth. It is, in every sense of the word, a masterpiece.

Note : I played this for the first time a long time ago. When i first played this, it was my first souls-like ever and it was a gifted game so I had no clue what I was in for. I have since played it so much that I have even attempted hitless and managed to go down to 6 hits in the whole playthrough. I have never played nor do I plan on playing it PvP so this is not part of this review (or of my total 320 hours of gameplay...)

Dark Souls
What is this : RPG, with unforgiving combat involving melee weapons and various magic type. You WILL die.... A lot.
95/100
Death : 351 (1st playthrough only, start to the ending, all bosses, no NG+)

Perfected : Yes
Recommended : Yes
Controller support : Yes...people who play this with a keyboard are crazy...
Replayable : Yes, many times
Price : 50$ rarely goes in 50% sale for 25$
Worth to me : 50$ easily

PROS :
* Rich and beautiful world/setting
* Very varied environments
* So many enemy type with their own movement, AI, attacks, etc
* Bosses are most of the time an amazing challenge experience.
* So many ways to play the game
* Doesn't hold you hand
* Farming souls can help, but not needed
* NG+ lets you try the game with all your equipment after beating it for extra challenge and fun
* DLC included and amazing
* Optionnal area and bosses

* Blightown
* Parries (I hate all sort of parry mechanics in all games :) it's too OP)
* Non-newbie friendly lore : SO MUCH lore, but barely any explanation, dialogue, ways to find out except with fan made youtube video (Check VaatiVidya)
* Parkour : some items require parkour to reach, but this game was not designed for parkour jumps...
* You can only play it as a "first playthrough" once. The whole concept of "dying to learn how to win" means once you figured out how to beat a boss, you will likely forever hold that knowledge and the experience of a 2nd playthrough will never be the same. You'll never get that same adrenaline rush of FINALLY beating THAT boss... that is until you play dark souls 2 and 3 for the first time :)
* coop is finicky AF. I actually recommend not even considering it. attempting to figure it out and make it work will make you not want to play the game.

* People tend to say this is extremely hard. I disagree. I think it is simply a very different kind of game that does not suit everyone. I like to call it a "Puzzle game". The bosses are unforgiving, yes, but not hard. All of their attacks are predictible with animation, and even, sometimes, you can easilly find a way to trigger a specific attack with positionning or actions you take. This is a very analytical game where you have to observe your ennemy and learn from your defeat. Yes you will die, as the game is intended for you to learn through your mistakes and surprises with the bosses triggering new attack with phases of the combat. As shown by Team Hitless , with enough learning, you can beat this game without getting hit once. Not that hard :)

one of my favorite games of all time that I have played countless times across multiple platforms. The remaster has some new minimal additions for convince sake that may bother veterans to the series and some humorous bugs introduced. Fixing frame rate issues that affected Blighttown and making coop with friends much more simple with a password system was so nice and worth it alone but this still feels far from a "remaster". They really should of taken more time to fully remaster the game and flesh out areas that were rushed or cut from the original release as well as improve the graphics. overall since the prepare to die edition was delisted this is currently the only option to play dark souls 1 as it stands, and it is far from a bad option as the specific additions I highlighted above are enough to prefer this option despite still disagreeing with delisting the original release. I heavily recommend trying ds1, it may feel a bit hard to adjust to certain aspects coming in from Elden Ring or ds3 but ds1 is a classic and has some of the most interesting level design I have ever seen in the way that its essentially a open world game bridged together with branching paths and short cuts that breaks the norm of what open world has to be. Its my personal favorite dark souls game of the three, with things I like more with each game independently, but for me this is where it all started and where I fell in love with souls games. 11/10 certified hood classic would "try jumping here" again.

Posted: February 9
One of my favorite games of all time. The themes, the atmosphere, everything. It all blends so well together. Even the little things, like how the game is mostly silent, give the music so much more impact when it plays. The game really deprives you of a lot, but it's still fair, which, in all honesty, makes it so much more rewarding when you conquer a challenge.

The way the areas connect so well is honestly astonishing. The moment I knew I'd be hooked on this game was when I unlocked the shortcut from the Undead Parish back to Firelink Shrine after a long grind to the top Bell of Awakening. Just the sheer feeling of familiarity, which not a lot of games provide, was really soothing. I've really come to appreciate this type of level design. It really reminds me of games like some of the Resident Evil titles or The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time, though I think they very much perfected the formula in this game.

Though getting 100% completion was a bit of a grind, it wasn’t all that bad, and I honestly enjoyed it.

This is a game I will revisit from time to time. It's honestly not too far off to compare it to the ever-recurring "Minecraft phase", since its replayability is very high.

All in all, the game is perfect in my eyes. It's not for everyone, it is a bit difficult. However, it is very rewarding, and the gameplay is excellent. In my opinion, it is a must-play.

The only downsides I can think of are some of the most horrendous run-backs I've experienced in a game. Got used to them after some time though and didn't really mind them too much.

TLDR: Dark Souls will always have a place of nostalgia for me, but that being said, the clunky combat is starting to show its age when you have the superior fluidity of Elden Ring. Great version for the performance enhancements.

PROS:
➕Legendary game world design


NEUTRALS:
🔹Tying humanity to PVP is annoying
🔹Dark vibe of hopelessness
🔹Ornstein and Smough is my worst DS boss ever and they can DIAF

CONS:
➖Combat is VERY rough when coming from newer games like Elden Ring
➖Audio issues - occasional monster move won’t have sound (silent dragon breath)
➖I hate “Double keybound” movesets, like having kick bound to light attack makes me accidentally kick all the time. Jump button on run/roll is just a fun slot machine on what my character will do.
➖Narrative sucks

Absolutely love the game world setup and how everything is intertwined so well.

While peak at the time of release, games like Elden Ring have really shown the age of OG Dark Souls combat.

60FPS consistent. This version is probably the best the game is ever going to look.
Tons of issues with mobs/corpses falling through the environment.
i9-13900KF | RTX 4090 | 64GB RAM | 1440p @ 240hz | Windows 11 installed on SSD

Definitely some audio issues, but overall, still decent.

Average replayability - NG+ allows you to enhance/expand your build and replay again, but no ability in game to try a different build.

This is my 4th run through of DS1. I’ve completed DS2, DS3, Bloodborne, and Elden Ring, all at least once. So when I returned to the original, I was not prepared for how…..clunky and just plain awful feeling it was. While true that it does run nice at a consistent 60fps, and the graphics, while still pretty bad, have some noticeable enhancements like reflections on the environment, DS1 is definitely starting to show how old it is.

After about the first 10 hours, the DS bug bit me again. I was thoroughly enjoying it, and I think that’s a great testament to the classic feeling of DS and how it forever changed the gaming landscape after its release. The “Souls-like” genre began here (You could argue Demon’s Souls….but Dark Souls kinda exploded the popularity).

So what makes it so fun? I think it comes down to the challenge, the builds, and the personal growth that you experience. I remember when I first played DS1 and kept dying to every boss and even normal enemies like black knights. This playthrough I sped pretty quickly to Anor Londo only getting stuck at O&S (the bastards). It’s this growth that makes the game so worthwhile - to see your skill improve as you conquer challenges.

So overall, yes, I recommend DS Remastered. It’s still a good game with some nice under-the-hood performance tweaks, but damn is this gameplay getting old. I think that Elden Ring is the overall better experience, so while I would recommend this to experience the origins of DS, It’s becoming an ever tough sell due to its age and general clunky gameplay. It's important when you play this to put yourself in the year 2011 as the more you forget about FromSoft's newer and more refined gameplay, the better time you'll probably have.

2. Good graphic design
3. You don't lose Max HP upon death, like in DS2.
4. Running doesn't consume as much stamina as in DS2.
5. For a 2011 game it's good, but the Remaster from 2018 isn't good enough in my opinion, since many of the horribly designed aspects of the game remain.

Cons:
1. The story and lore is hard to fathom by only playing the game.
2. The game doesn't explain how anything works. After playing through both DS2 and Elden Ring before DS1, I can only imagine how horrible of an experience one would have if this was their first Souls-like game.
3. The camera when locked on enemies is horrible, forcing you to only roll in 4 directions. This makes the combat incredibly clunky. In addition, when locked on, your character has a tendency to miss the enemy even when standing right in front of them.
4. You can't teleport from one bonfire to another until mid-game, which makes the game an exhausting running simulator. If you reach a dead end in an area, for example if the boss is too hard since you are underleveld for the area, you have to run for 10-15 minutes depending on where you want to go. With very confusing and frustrating area layouts as well, it takes even longer. When you FINALLY get the ability to teleport, you can only teleport to certain bonfires, still forcing you to spend all off your time running!
5. You run slowly, and the character runs even slower the higher equipment load you have!
6. Many bonfires are terribly placed throughout the game, forcing you to run and run even more! One example is when you want to go to the blacksmith in Anor Londo. There is a bonfire relatively close to him, but you can't teleport to it, so you have to use the bonfire in the start of the area, and run for 2 minutes every time you want to see him.
7. There are several boss run-ups which are 2-3 minutes long, again making the game a running simulator.
8. Many of the areas are designed like a maze, being horribly confusing and unfun. The bonfires are often few and hard to reach, so if you die, you have run around for 5-10 minutes to remember the layout and get back to where you were.
9. Most of the bosses are boring and easy compared to the other gamges, which I guess is understandable since its an old game, and considering the horribly long run-ups, I'm happy they were easy.
10. Several areas are purposely designed to be infuriating, exhausting and draining, with traps, incredibly many enemies, poison you have to run through, and confusing layouts. Some of the areas are also incredibly dark, especially one of them, making it very hard to see where you're going. Frustrating layouts are seen in all of the FromSoftware games, but considering the low amount of bonfires in DS1, you have to run for so long through the same exhausting areas to continue where you left off if you die.
11. Screen Shake! Even if you turn screen shake off in the options, there is forced screen shake on most bosses and large enemies. Sometimes it gets so bad it's hard to see what's happening mid-combat, further infuriating the player, at least me.
12. DLC accessability! To access the Artorias DLC, one has to follow a wiki guide to understand what to do to. Also, if you plan to do the DLC after beating the final boss, you can't! This is because you are forcibly thrown right into NG+ after finishing the final boss, forcing you to start over and play through the game again if you want to go there.
13. Being forced into NG+ after beating the final boss screws you over if you planned on doing anything in your save afterwards.
14. Pyromancy is useless! The spells have unironically 2 meter range, making them useless in 99% of all scenarios.
15. it's practically impossible to find out what to do with boss souls without looking it up.

Btw, I'm sure there's other pros and cons I've forgotten!

All in all, it's a good game in some aspects, but it's not enough. There are just too many horribly infuriating aspects and design choices in this game for me to enjoy it. I didn't have any fun at all in my playthrough compared to in the other FromSoft games. All of the negative aspects add up and become too much, just making me want to quit the game and do something else. The only reason I finished the game was because my friends keep saying the game is good, so I hoped to enjoy it after I really got into it, but I didn't.

I feel crazy for having this opinion since everyone hates on DS2, but to me DS1 is far worse.

Posted: February 5
So you've clicked on Dark Souls again, looking through the reviews curious to see if something can convince you to buy it on this sale.

There are games, movies, shows, life events, achievements, etc that hold a special place in a person's life. Things that happen that divide a person's life into a "before" and "after" .. Dark Souls is the one game where I can divide my video game playing life as a before Dark Souls and after Dark Souls. Ever since playing this game all the way through, every game after it gets compared to the feelings this game gave me.

Exploration, this one has it in spades. Just walking around a new area is a wildly exciting and scary thing to do. Not knowing whats around the corner or if you can trust that wall. The traps, the tricks, the design of what the game is putting in front of you and how your eyes are directed. Its here.

Combat. This was my introduction to the souls-like combat system with dodge rolling and counters. Nobody can match From Software here because they put in the legwork to give unique animations to several *classes* of weapons. The halberds work, the spears work, the short swords work all because the level of effort was there. THIS was the beginning of the foundation that has built into DS3, Sekiro, and Elden Ring. The boss design and unforgiving gameplay started here, this is the ground floor and if you play the series in order, you can see the evolution of boss and enemy design.

Story. Its here, you have to find it. If you're not familiar or used to how the story and world is delivered, itll be frustrating. Pick up a new item? There's a chance its got some story attached to it, and its on the item description. Every character has a role and is expressing some part of the theme. And the theming of these games is where it really shines. Its not really interested in telling a narrative.. that's YOUR job, its your story and its unfolding right before you. No, the game is more interested in conveying a theme when its trying to say something.

This is far and away worth the price it goes on during a sale. Put the time in, put the legwork in, and you'll be rewarded with one of the best gaming experiences of all time and you'll be introduced to several games that came in its wake and outdid this one.

Dark Souls is truly one of the best games of all time. Despite Demon's souls being the first from the "soulsborne" genre, Dark Souls pushed the initiative and ultimately began one of the greatest videogame series ever.

The world is nearly flawless, mainly in the first half, but it's still awesome in the second. The bosses are, even though not the hardest, among the most well-known and iconic in gaming history. The atmosphere this game is set in is only matched by Bloodborne and the characters are among the most praised. This game is slow, but fast at the same time. The combat is forgiving at the most part, although intimidating at first.

It is only on you how you approach the difficulty this game presents. You can use simple melee builds, overpowered magic builds or something else, anything goes.
And don't even get me started on the lore. If you think that dark souls are only about slaying big bosses, you couldn't be more wrong.

And it's extremely polished. The whole game, dlc included, is only about 6GB.

Whenever I feel overwhelmed by something in real life, I find myself replaying dark souls once again. I couldn't recommend this game more. It is one of my all-time favourites. Easy 10/10.

Coming from 2024 Elden ring back to 2011 DS1 is a huge change i simply cant get on board with. Rolling, attacking, parrying, and blocking just feels slow, and choppy, facing enemies that can backstop, jump attack, charge, walk and attack through walls with minimal defense and a 3 second stop and heal animation makes the game feel impossible to beat unless you're on your defense 100% of the time.
This is not a relaxing game, this is not a fun game, this is not a smooth game. This is not a game you can sit back, relax, and enjoy for a while.
This is for people looking to relive childhood in 720p and looking for one of the hardest gaming challenges they can face. you will get frustrated, look up guides, summon help, have to stop grind levels, upgrade and come back stronger, because every boss will be a struggle that will make you want to close the game.
A super challenging game that defined the "Souls like" Genre. You will die, you will hate yourself, you will refund
Personally not for me, i just cant see myself constantly dying and running back to the boss room for 5 minutes fighting mobs along the way and genuinely having fun doing so.
If you want a game with a challenge thatll piss you off and give you tons of joy after beating an impossible boss: This is for you!

This game is the equivalent of a persistent teacher/professor in school, originally you hate them for being so challenging, you are annoyed that they seem unfair. But then, one day it clicks and you stop losing. Areas that used to take hours now take minutes, you learn from your mistakes and absolutely crush it. You look back on your teacher and appreciate their persistence and challenge because it made you a better player. Dark Souls doesn't hold your hand when you pick it up for the first time, you are faced trial by fire, but then you are offered aid by way of in game help from characters you meet along the way or knowledge imparted to you by friends such as "Did you know about this shortcut?" or "That boss can actually be cheesed in ____ way". Posted: February 1
The game that has started the trend... This is my second souls-like game after Elden Ring and I feel like this game made me a veteran in this genre. I understand all the hype. Jump into the game after accepting that it is a rather hard and unusual game that will take your time to learn about it. Well anyways,

First of all, I think this game is pretty artistic. Like I felt really excited to be around in this world, it was mostly pleasurable. It was fun to wander around and explore, or just to look at despite this game having older graphics. Plus I understand why people liked the Dark Souls map. It is brilliantly designed and everything is connected. Even if it has been a while since I played this game, when I remember playing it, I feel no gaps between anywhere I went. I guess I can describe it as "complete". Despite not being an open world game, this game gives you the opportunity to explore a lot. Okay I guess I can tell that some stuff is absurdly hidden I still loved it mostly.

Let's talk about combat, the main part that probably most care about. When I was progressing through the game, I though "Hey, this feels just like Elden Ring :D!". Then game smacked me with more combat options that I needed to do. Game kinda forces you to use them all throughout the game. Sometimes you realize you need to parry, sometimes you just use bows, sometimes you have to backstab them. Learning these are hard, but after that you kinda enjoy it while looking for the enemies attack pattern. You can get creative whatever you use. I must say though, it feels so much slower comparing to Elden Ring. But I kinda find a beauty in that. I like breathing rooms to analyze what to do and change my plans. Since movements are slower too it is also more pleasurable to dodge an attack that you see. It just balances itself.

The thing I probably loved about this game is items! Items actually have meanings in this game. You see, there are ♥♥♥♥ ton of different enviromental dangers you need to get through and it is way too hard without some items. You kinda realize that you are doing something wrong in these areas. Walking in the water is too slow? There is an item for that. Too much poison around? Get poison res equipment, they make a lot difference. Bro deals too much lightning damage? Just get light res. equipment and it will become a decent damage. You get your poise up and hey, now you are actually hard to stagger, unlike other games where no matter what your poise is %90 of the enemies just stagger the ♥♥♥♥ out of you. You can shape your game experience however you want with the items you make an effort for. This also boosts your desire to explore.

If we get to the part where I need to write, there isn't much the write to be honest. They are using the same blueprint for all of their games and its okay. I liked Elden Ring lore, I liked this too. It was interesting. Though I can say I mostly played for exploring and combat. If there is a strong point about it's lore, maybe I could say there are really lovely NPC's. Everyone know Solaire but the others are not less amazing than him.

I'll be fair now, there are really some places of the game where it is total ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ and just unnecessarily troubling. But you can just get through them you know, and I was kinda okay with it since I was just excited and just trying to survive.

Overall as a souls game, I loved it totally. It was more on the chill side but was still hard. I loved the whole world and exploring items, and use items lol. I also think that it would be a great game to be "first souls experience" with guides of course. Some people might not like it after games like DS3 or Elden Ring, since it paces a lot slower. But in my opinion game gives it's taste fully since you kinda digest it to get through it lol.

O chosen undead, you are left with the burden of a collapsing world on your shoulders. Fret not, keep trying and do not go hollow!

Just beat the game on my first playthrough and thought I'd give a proper review.
A little background, I've had the game since forever but never got into the first time around. What finally made me go back to revisit it was Elden Ring, and finally grasping From Software games, how they work etc.

Coming from Elden Ring, which is a really polished game with beautiful open environments and stepping into Dark Souls was definitely a change. From some aspects, it hasn't aged well, but that's mainly on the graphical side of things.
Level design is brilliant overall, even though it's a little too much focus on slim ledges with the risk of running off an edge and falling into oblivion. And jumping - I miss jumping!
I can really see why Dark Souls started its own genre and people fall in love with the games.

However, this game is rough. And I don't mean difficult - I mean rough around the edges. Where Elden Ring shines, with focus on tough boss fights and interesting enemies, Dark Souls is sometimes just annoying. Certain fights or "breakpoints" to get to the next part of the map were simply irritating, and mainly due to the placement of respawn-points.
It's not difficult, challenging or adding to the experience to spend 3 minutes running back to a fight where 2.5 minutes were spent waiting and riding an elevator - just add a bloody fire at the bottom of the elevator as well as on top.
Or why not place the closest fire to one of the end bosses a thousand miles off so you have to run back for 5 minutes to do a 5 second attempt at a boss who just slapped you into a hole to die. I'm looking at you, The Bed of Chaos. I love a good boss challenge, but The Bed of Chaos was the only boss I looked up the tactics for as I couldn't be bothered with the corpse run - it just wasn't fun.
Coming from Elden Ring where pretty much every challenging fight had a Grace or a Cross right outside the door, it left a sour taste in my mouth. It's not "hard" or "challenging", it's just poor game design.
(And I'm sure there are hardcore fans out there who enjoys waiting for elevators or running the same distance over and over, but I'm not one of them.)

Now the game is also a lot more punishing in other areas - I think I accidentally smacked vendors, smiths and NPC's at least 5 times which had me running off paying for my sins a number of times. Bye bye Spider Lady who I accidently oneshotted.

I definitely enjoyed the little trip into Dark Souls, and I'll head on to the next chapter now. I'm more Elden Ring than Dark Souls 1 in my preference for comforts of life, but it was still a nice little adventure.

A shame the final boss was a pushover :D

This game is a timeless masterpiece and is by far the most creatively inspired out of the trilogy. So much was sacrificed in DS2 and 3 in the name of convenience that I don't think they can really be considered proper sequels, even if they are still great and enjoyable games. I don't think it's perfect necessarily but I do think this is one of, if not the greatest game ever made and my praise for it probably can't be contained within the character limit of this review, so I'll just say that if you care about video games at all then you should really play Dark Souls.

Oh, and if you're wondering about the remaster, it's... fine, mostly. After the notoriously awful PTDE PC port, being able to run it at 60 FPS by default is nice, but I don't really care for some of the changes to lighting and VFX they made (especially the bonfire's flame, what an awful nonsensical change that was). Also they put an extra bonfire in the catacombs for some reason. It's a small change but Dark Souls's adherence to a 1 bonfire per area rule most of the time is a huge merit to the game's level design. It's the only one they added though, so it's fine. With that said, if you want to experience Dark Souls right now, Remastered isn't going to ruin your experience at all and I can comfortably recommend this version of the game as a huge fan of the original.

I will always love this game. It accompanied me through hard times.

Personal feelings aside, though, this *is* a very good game, and one I find myself coming back to once in a while. Each time I do I discover something else, from a new character to a new boss to a new location or ways to reach said location.... this game is something else and I definitely recommend it to anyone that is interested in trying one of the Soulsborne games.

Also, the community revolving these games is truly magical. There are plenty of people that have made tons of videos and guides that can help you understand the game and solve problems you might be encountering with the mazes or the enemies, along with people out there that play the game and are always willing to help you battle difficult bosses. Yes, there are those that frequently invade your world and hunt you down, but those are inconveniences that you can avoid by playing as Hollowed or Offline, going Online only when you need help.

That being said, this game is a beautiful experience that seems to find its way into people's lives on that moment when they need it the most.

That certainly was my case.

Maybe it's your time now....

Definitely a perfect entry point into the Dark Souls trilogy. I happened to play the trilogy for the first time backwards, starting with Dark Souls 3, and doing this was good in the sense that I was eased into the older slightly more jankier mechanics of Dark Souls 1 & especially 2, but the story connections and references I feel are much more embraced if experienced from the beginning of it all; Dark Souls 1.
As an avid player of the souls-like genre I had the easiest time in this game, which also made it much more relaxed and immersive when compared to the fast paced combat of Dark Souls 3 or Elden Ring. Some remarkable strengths of this remaster is the masterfully tailored map design, with numerous intricate pathways one could follow and discoverable shortcuts that can prove massively beneficial to your quality of life (throughout the game, it is presented to you just how well connected every single area of the game is to one another). Combat is methodical, but not boring, and can prove quite a challenge depending on what your play style is and what sort of enemy you're up against.
A lot of the community recommends going into these games blind and exploring everything for yourself. While I partially agree with this, I see no shame in players who resort to reading articles and watching videos to help guide them through the game, as this game is phenomenal and should be enjoyed by whomever, however they want (for example, you may want to research how humanity works before diving in if its your first time).

"Be safe, friend. Don’t you dare go hollow."

I played Dark Souls on Xbox back when it came out. I've been captivated by it ever since.

Lordran is a decadent realm where ruins prevail. A place of nostalgia and haunting beauty. The landscape takes centre stage, menacing, shadows of what once was. Now a bleak world, perpetually on the edge of doom, slowly dragging you into its suffocating darkness. Yet, you can find glimmers of humanity and moments of peace between the shadows.

Hope is scarce. The atmosphere is oppressive. You hear nothing but footsteps, clashing weapons, groans and the pain of death. Only in the heat of boss battles does the soundtrack shine through, but the music is never meant for you. You are a nobody.

From the beginning, you are shown your place. You are no hero. You are no saviour. You might find faith, or hope, to keep you going. But ultimately you are pure determination in a forsaken world where the only option is to stand again, to fight once more, to sink, to rise, to strive... again, again, again, again, again.

It only takes a single spark to ignite a flame…

Dark Souls is more than a game – it’s an unforgettable journey. It’s a vivid and breathtaking adventure that challenges you and rewards you in equal measure. At its heart, it’s a story about human potential and resilience.

You start as a hollowed, unarmed, and defenseless character trapped in a prison cell – a reflection of the emptiness within. You have no idea what lies ahead, but every step forward brings a sense of wonder, challenge, and discovery.

The game masterfully blends contrasts: where there is darkness, there is always light. Its level design is nothing short of genius – interconnected, immersive, and alive. Each location feels like a real place, rich with atmosphere, secrets, and stories to uncover. The soundtrack amplifies the mood perfectly, while the enemies and bosses are not just obstacles but memorable and meaningful encounters.

Contrary to its reputation, Dark Souls isn’t unfairly hard – it’s a test of your patience, adaptability, and determination. The game doesn’t hold your hand, but that’s what makes it so rewarding. Exploring its mysterious world, facing your fears, and overcoming seemingly insurmountable challenges creates a sense of achievement few games can match.

The story? It’s magnificent. But it’s not something to be spoiled or explained – it’s something to be experienced.

Dark Souls doesn’t just challenge you to fill the Lordvessel with souls; it challenges you to grow, adapt, and fill yourself with determination.

Verdict: 10/10 – A masterpiece that stays with you long after the credits roll.

Posted: January 26
Decided to finally give the original a go. I've beaten DeS, BB, DS3, ER, racked up maybe 1000 hours across all titles. Absolutely adored every title, yet couldn't get into DS2, try as I did. I'm about 37 hours into DS:R now and I'm loving my time with it. It's a very different experience, much slower and more methodical. It's clear now what trajectory FromSoft went on when they iterated on this formula for future titles. Now, I'm a simple man, I always gravitate towards STR/DEX/Quality builds. I just like rolling around and smacking things with my two-handed greatsword/katana/whatever. That appeal isn't quite there with DS due to the slower pace and the way rolling isn't quite as fluid. But instead of turning me off, it has inspired me to go full sword and board, shielding up and thinking more about my positioning when dealing with mobs. And I have to say, I absolutely love it.

Oh and the exploration is top tier. I didn't realise just how linear DS3 really is until I played this. The way the different levels are interconnected is amazing game design, very clever, and I'm actually a little sad that we didn't see a similar approach with later games.

So, that's my essay on DS after 37 hours game time. If you're considering this game but are unsure - maybe due to many of the QOL features being missing that came later in the series - I say go for it as long as you can appreciate the slower, more methodical pace.

Highly recommended.

If you listen to the gaming community every month or so a new masterpiece hit the shelves and twice as often the worst game ever record is being broken.
But this is a classic in the best traditiion. It takes what works from older rpg's and transforms it into something new, creating a new identity for itself that immediately influenced so many other publishers that the "souls-like" genre was created. If you're a gamer and haven't played this, you're not.

For rpg's I will always see baldur's gate I and especially "U7: the black gate" as my personal favorite milestones, but this game belongs in my personal picks for games that are important.

Is it perfect, gods no. The "story" is a concoction of lotr, elder scrolls and whatever Alice inspired fever dream is responsible for putting walking mushrooms and talking cat amongst it's characters.
But the level design is a masterclass in level design, the fighting (except archery) becomes intuitive after a short period and as a result you can focus on the strategy tackling opponents and the game instead of trying to figure out 60 ways to point your sword in the right direction.

I only wished DII was just as good. Fortunately for me, DIII plays just as well as this one does. Still if only for the magnificent level design, give this one a go. It's worth it.

A masterpiece!

It was my first contact with the souls-like genre and it couldn't have been any different. I recently replayed it so I think I can give it a fairer review.

Nowadays, there are problems such as the robust and less fluid movement, the problematic areas due to the level (Anor Londo and the Catacombs mainly), but even so, these problems can't spoil the magic of this game, containing incredible worldbuilding, very well-crafted sets and builds and memorable Bosses.

Playing the Remastered version, I don't think we have any problems with the visuals, even though it was released a long time ago, I believe it has an aesthetic that remains current and an incredible sound work, with a special focus on the Boss Fights, which each battle has an incredible atmosphere, largely due to the soundtrack, especially the battle against Gwyn, which, in addition to being a very bold move by Fromsoftware, has one of the best soundtracks of any game.
I think it's only fair to mention the NPCs such as the beloved “Onion” and the knight Solaire, who make the experience of being in a world completely dominated and commanded by demonic or mythical beings, a calmer and more hopeful experience!

Incredible worldbuilding and fun gameplay!

The game is definitely difficult but honestly I feel like it's not as bad as most people say. If you're having trouble, take some time to look at the tools you've been given. Do you have a weapon that can deal a more effective damage type? Do you have consumables that will give you buffs or extra heals? Are there allies you can summon for that boss fight you're having trouble with? If a situation doesn't feel right, examine the notes and bloodstains on the floor to avoid ambushes or get help with secrets. You might even get some hints on how to take down a boss more easily. This game doesn't have to be overwhelmingly difficult if you don't want it to.

The story in the game is very understated as well. If all you do is listen to the dialogue and watch the cutscenes, you get a very incomplete picture of the game. In order to get the entire story, you need to pay attention to the items you pick up; their names and descriptions, and even the spots you find them can give lots of context about the world around you. The enemies you find in the world can also hint towards the history of certain areas. If an enemy or boss feels out of place in a certain zone, there's probably a lore reason for that. Try looking through the bits of lore you've found if you want to figure out why!

Another great thing about this game is how interconnected the zones are. As you explore through the world, you'll slowly be finding different exits and shortcuts through certain areas that make going through a zone the second time much easier. You won't ever be given a map of the world, but the developers did a great job of putting memorable visual landmarks at each of the more important locations and branching pathways that makes it easier for you to remember both where you are and where you need to go to get somewhere. After running through the world for a while, you'll end up being able to navigate it so well you won't need a map.

Overall, this game, and many of Fromsoft's other games, require a lot more effort from the player than most games to really get enjoyment out of them, both in gameplay and story. There's lots of intent in every design decision made and the developers expect the players to put in a bit of work to really enjoy things, instead of just sleepwalking through the entire experience. Some people don't like that and it's perfectly fine not to, but this kind of game design is why the Dark Souls series became so popular in the first place. There's truly something special about a game asking for some real effort from the player, at a time when games like Skyrim were coming out that basically held the player's hand the whole way through. Please consider trying the game out, even if you're not a fan of "difficult" games.

Posted: January 23
Dark Souls is one of my favourite games of all time. I first played it close to 14 years ago, when it was not about “gitting gud”, it was about an entire community of befuddled players trying to muddle their way through the game without being kicked off a ledge by a disgruntled skeleton, scythed in half by a banshee, or killed by a certain immortal scaleless dragon.

So yeah, it’s one of my favourite games, but there are a lot of frustrating memories there, too (I’m looking at you, Bed of Chaos!). To be honest, I probably wouldn’t have recommended it to everyone when it first came out, but now? With the smorgasbord of guides and builds out there? Irrevocably.

There’s been plenty of discourse about how hard these games are, and why there are no difficulty settings. I thoroughly disagree. Want an on-the-edge-of-your-seat survival horror experience? Play without any guides – hell even play offline to avoid the thousands of glowing messages telling you where to go. You’ll work up more of a sweat than a ten mile bike ride.

Want to pancake your way through everything without ever needing to heal? Pick up Zweihander in the first thirty minutes and go to town. Want to stand there and bash things with a big club without ever being knocked down? Grab Havel’s Armour, Ring, and cast Iron Flesh. I can guarantee you’ll die more playing Mario than Dark Souls if you play like this.

But that’s the beauty of it. Thanks to its still-thriving community, Dark Souls can be as easy or as hard as you’d like, as exploratory or as guided, too. This certainly takes away some of the charm, but not the fun.
This remastered version still holds up well today (especially with a few mods), and the inclusion of the DLC is, in my opinion, absolutely essential.

So go in eyes blind, or eyes wide open. Take in the sights. Bop a few serpent people on the head. Kill the blacksmith. Jump of a cliff. That’s the beauty of these games. You can play them however you want, and either way, they’re great.

DARK SOULS™: REMASTERED brings the notoriously challenging & influential action RPG to modern platforms with improved visuals & performance. It's a game that defined a genre, known for its intricate level design, punishing difficulty, & cryptic lore. While the remaster doesn't drastically change the core experience, it's the definitive way to play this classic.

You play as the Chosen Undead, tasked with undertaking a pilgrimage to link the First Flame & stave off the Age of Dark. The story is told through environmental storytelling, item descriptions, & cryptic conversations with NPCs. It's a world rich in lore & mystery, encouraging players to piece together the narrative themselves.

The gameplay is characterized by its demanding combat, which emphasizes timing, positioning, & stamina management. Every enemy encounter is a potential threat, & death is a common occurrence. However, overcoming these challenges is incredibly rewarding, creating a strong sense of accomplishment.

The interconnected world of Lordran is a masterpiece of level design. Each area is intricately connected to others, creating a sense of exploration & discovery. Unlocking shortcuts & finding hidden paths is a key part of the experience.

The remaster improves the game's visuals with higher resolutions & smoother frame rates. While it doesn't drastically overhaul the graphics, it makes the game look significantly better on modern hardware. The online multiplayer has also been improved, making it easier to connect with other players for co-op or PvP.

However, the remaster doesn't address all of the game's flaws. Some of the gameplay mechanics, such as the inventory management & some of the boss fights, can feel a bit clunky by modern standards. The game's difficulty can also be a barrier for some players.

**Pros:**

* Intricate & interconnected world design! 🗺️
* Challenging & rewarding combat! ⚔️
* Cryptic & fascinating lore! 📖
* Improved visuals & performance in the remaster! ✨
* Enhanced online multiplayer! 🧑‍🤝‍🧑

**Cons:**

* Very difficult & unforgiving! 😭
* Some clunky gameplay mechanics! 🤔
* Can be frustrating for new players! 😫

**Verdict:** 9/10. DARK SOULS™: REMASTERED is the definitive way to experience this groundbreaking action RPG. While it doesn't reinvent the wheel, the visual & performance improvements, along with the enhanced multiplayer, make it a must-play for fans of the series & newcomers alike. It's a game that demands patience & perseverance, but the sense of accomplishment you feel after overcoming its challenges is unparalleled. Prepare to die. A lot. 🔥

I bought into the hyper surrounding Elden Ring and fell in love with the game which led to me going back and experiencing FromSoft's original hit. I maintain that Dark Souls 3 is the best entry point into the series (it's what I started with), but the other two games are nice if you just want to feel something after the pure dopamine that is the final boss of DS3's Ringed City DLC.
I think most people agree that the best part of this game is the first half (the start through ), but I don't actually love the first half that much to begin with. I think I had been so tuned into loving the bosses of DS3 and Sekiro that it was really disappointing to realize that a lot of the bosses of this game are not that great -- a few iconic moments of the series, sure, but I also think most of these bosses are carried by the intrigue of their lore. It's the world design and moments between bonfires that I think has the bulk of this game's charm (brave opinion, I know); they really know how to get your imagination going. Of course, even this exploration felt less fun in the second half of the game for me, but at least its cool to see where certain ends of the map trail off to. I dunno. Not in love with this game, but it was interesting while it lasted.

Do I recommend this game? Yes obviously you can see, but do I recommend it to EVERYONE? Absolutely not.

Like every other souls game and this one being the entry to the series (yes i know demon souls exists) dark souls is at its core an ultimate test of persistence, mental endurance and patience. It is NOT for everyone and at times this game will not be fun at all. The formula is simple, you encounter hard and difficult areas or bosses >> you struggle to conquer said challenges >> you get rewarded for that. Considering its difficulty being absolutely mind breaking especially for new players, the reward can feel high and addictive but the lows can also feel like really low.

One of the strengths and weakness of dark souls comes from its absolute level design and world building. I call it a weakness too because at times, the maze like level design and the vastness of the semi open world can feel very overwhelming combined with its difficult variety of enemies and overall difficulty of the game, it can heavily discourage players which is what happened to me in my first playthrough awhile ago until I got it gifted and gave it another try. But its level design and vastness of the world is also its strength. It allows you to play the game at your own pace. You aren't forced to do anything, you're simply thrown out in the world, told what the "main" quest should be and there you go, fend for yourself. Hence the result can be a very different playthrough than other peoples. Interconnectivity is so impressive I genuinely couldn't believe this game was 10+ years old. After fighting through an area for hours, i find a shortcut that leads me all the way back to the starting area.

Nonetheless, if you're a masochist this game is perfect for you or if you wanna put yourself through an insanely mind breaking challenge go get it, as hard as it is, it is also as rewarding.

Posted: January 15
Base game - 5/5
Artorias of the Abyss DLC - 5/5

This is a classic. There's not much to say that hasn't already been said over the years - this is one of the greatest games of all time. It's not the most popular title FromSoftware has released, but it is definitely their most defining one - both for the company and the genre.

It has a seamless, very immersive open-world design, and even though this world is not revolutionary in design nor particularly big, they've made such a great metroidvania type interconnected world where you have the freedom of choosing multiple path progressions which in itself drives you to explore the world completely independent of the natural game progression. The only parameter that determines which path you should take is your own skill and knowledge of the game. Levels are designed with a lot of shortcuts and hidden passages to discover which makes multiple repeated visits to the same area fresh and interesting - the levels loop back on each other in ways that in hindsight seem so obvious, yet you somehow never notice it. I mean you always get that jaw drop when you realize that you can go from that Undead Parish elevator back to Firelink Shrine. Then they do it with Darkroot Basin to Valley of the Drakes or the other way to the Valley from the New Londo Ruins once it's drowned. It's just level design at it's best. However, this is partly gone with the Lordvessel. After that, the level design, even though it's still really good, heavily depends on warping between bonfires rather than connecting them like it does in the first half of the game, so it definitely feels a bit rushed. That's why I believe nothing else comes close to the brilliance of the level design in this game prior to Anor Londo.
DARK SOULS™: REMASTERED 

Even though the camera controls show it's time, combat doesn't. It's still highly impressive and truly brilliant when you come to think of it - they've given you very simple standard RPG stats, but they combined it with mechanics such as poise, critical hit multipliers, staggering effects, different weapon types with one-handed/two-handed stances, magic etc., and on top of that you have great block, parry, dodge and roll mechanics. What you get is a great variety of viable weapons, and combining this with a really good variety of different enemy types, it makes for an immersive experience, but even more important, highly replayable one. Yes, the combat might seem weighty and slow if you're coming back from DS3, or Elden Ring, but this game makes you utilize your critical thinking and mechanical smoothness rather than relying on your memory and rhythm. You WILL certainly eat your mistakes.

This game's reputation is notorious for having a stressful grind with overly-difficult bosses, but I would say that the truth is far from that. All bosses have their strengths and weaknesses, they all give you opportunities to capitalize on damage or avoid unnecessary damage, and it's up you to learn and master these techniques. Some bosses truly become a walk in the park after you do understand these. Yes, there is always a high possibility that you could be going into a fight underleveled, but this game gives you complete freedom to discover and toughen-up your character. Deaths are necessary and they are supposed to teach you lessons, so there's no point in stressing out about it really. In it's own way it's rewarding and very addictive. The runbacks from the bonfire to the boss fight have always been excessively time-consuming in Dark Souls games - I won't ever encourage them being as long as they are, and this combined with pretty long preparations in case you break your weapon or if you get cursed certainly is very annoying and it builds up on the reputation of this game which is already considered pretty hard. But you just have to love these boss fights - Ornstein and Smough, the Four Kings, Gwyn... they are all incredibly unique and just iconic at this point.

The multiplayer aspect of the game is fantastic. Why don't more games have a simple mechanic such as leaving messages to other players to warn them about something or give them hints/point their attention to something (or sometimes just simply bait them into death). It's interactive in the simplest way possible, yet this simple mechanic has connected the community in such a wonderful way. Wherever you enter a Dark Souls discussions, it's going to be full of references to these messages. Next to that, you have the black phantoms that invade your world to create havoc and the white phantoms that will help you in your journey as you summon them. These can be both NPCs or other players too. PvP though? It's really not balanced.

The lore is deep and very interesting, even though it might seem confusing and unexplained. To be true, the story of Dark Souls is very cryptic, and you will certainly need to reach out onto the internet at times, but this exploration means understanding the story. It is not "set in stone", it's not told through cutscenes, the story of Dark Souls is told through your actions entirely. All the characters you meet and all the items you collect are there to guide you.

And the DLC... Man, I f*cking love Artorias. Fighting Artorias makes you feel like everything you played through made you for that exact moment. There is no room for error. That's when you finally realize why everyone relied on him to save them. Through the game, you will hear the legends of Artorias fighting the abyss, and ultimately losing to this abyss with him being consumed to it. The DLC builds up onto the main story really good, and it's quite supposed to, as this content was meant for the original game which was cut short in order to meet the release date. The new area is great, and the new boss fights are great, with some very haaard boss fights (Kalameet and Manus). They are the toughest bosses in the game and a true test to your patience.

This striking gothic art direction is S-tier, with both gorgeous and frightening scenes, and the game has a great sound design with really nice soundtracks. Some of these sound effects will get stitched on to your brain for the rest of your life. And the piano in the final fight... After all the struggle through the entirety of the game that was dead silent and plain dreadful, that piano was a cherry on the top. Everything about this game's art direction is just masterfully elegant.

Play it. If you started it and you just can't see this game being good, return to it later. I swear, this game will just click with you one day and you'll understand it all then.

DARK SOULS™: REMASTERED is the definitive version of a game that has redefined action RPGs. This remaster delivers everything fans love about the original, with enhanced visuals and smoother gameplay, making it an even more immersive and intense experience. From the challenging combat to the hauntingly atmospheric world, every moment feels meaningful.

The difficulty is undeniably tough, but that’s part of what makes Dark Souls so rewarding. Every hard-earned victory, whether it’s defeating a challenging boss or overcoming a difficult section of the game, fills you with a sense of accomplishment unlike any other. The combat is deep, satisfying, and requires precise timing and strategy, making each fight feel earned.

The world design is masterful, with interconnected areas that encourage exploration and reward curiosity. The storytelling, told through the environment and subtle clues, is rich and captivating, leaving you with a sense of mystery and wonder as you uncover the game’s lore.

DARK SOULS™: REMASTERED is a fantastic game that remains as engaging and relevant as ever. The experience is challenging, but each success makes it all the more rewarding. It’s a perfect example of how a game can blend difficulty, depth, and storytelling to create something truly special. A true masterpiece deserving of a perfect 10/10.
