Dark Souls is more than just a game, it’s an odyssey of challenge, discovery, and triumph that has earned its reputation as one of the most influential titles in modern gaming. Developed by FromSoftware, this spiritual successor to Demon’s Souls introduced the world to the dark, brutal, and intricately designed kingdom of Lordran, captivating players with its unforgiving difficulty and deeply rewarding gameplay.

At its core, Dark Souls is about learning, growth, and perseverance. Every enemy, every boss, every inch of Lordran is a test of your skill, patience, and determination. Death isn’t a punishment but a lesson, urging you to adapt, strategize, and try again. The combat system, characterized by its weighty, deliberate feel, demands precision and mastery. Each swing, roll, and parry carries consequences, turning even minor skirmishes into thrilling encounters.

The world of Lordran is a masterpiece of interconnected design. From the moment you set foot in Firelink Shrine, the game encourages exploration, rewarding curiosity with shortcuts, hidden paths, and treasures. The seamless way areas like the Undead Burg, Blighttown, and Anor Londo connect creates a sense of place and discovery that few games can match. The atmosphere is oppressive yet mesmerizing, with crumbling ruins, foreboding forests, and majestic yet decayed architecture painting a picture of a world on the brink of collapse.

The storytelling is subtle and enigmatic, unfolding through cryptic dialogue, item descriptions, and environmental clues. Instead of spoon-feeding a narrative, Dark Souls invites players to piece together its lore and themes. It’s a story of cycles, despair, and fleeting hope, where the fates of gods and mortals intertwine in tragic ways. The ambiguity gives the world an air of mystery, making every revelation feel earned and impactful.

Boss battles are the game’s defining moments, each presenting unique challenges that test your reflexes, strategy, and resilience. Whether it’s the iconic encounter with the towering Iron Golem, the haunting beauty of the Abyssal Artorias, or the relentless aggression of Ornstein and Smough, these fights are unforgettable, blending spectacle with substance.

The game’s mechanics emphasize personal growth. The bonfire system serves as both a sanctuary and a risk-reward mechanic, while the leveling system allows you to tailor your character to your preferred playstyle. Whether you wield a hulking greatsword, delicate sorceries, or a trusty shield and spear, Dark Souls offers a wealth of options for creative builds.

The haunting score by Motoi Sakuraba underscores the emotional weight of the game, with somber melodies accompanying moments of quiet reflection and thunderous orchestration heightening the intensity of boss encounters.

Dark Souls is not a game that holds your hand, and its difficulty can be intimidating for newcomers. Yet, for those willing to embrace its challenges, the game offers a sense of accomplishment and mastery that few others can match. It’s not just about beating the game, it’s about conquering yourself, pushing through frustration, and emerging stronger on the other side.

For its groundbreaking design, unforgettable world, and deep, meaningful gameplay, Dark Souls remains a titan in the gaming world. Whether you’re a first-timer or a returning champion, venturing into Lordran is an experience that will leave an indelible mark on your gaming journey. Prepare to die, and to fall in love with the beauty of the struggle.

After having played every SoulsBorneKiroRing game (except for bloodborne and demon's souls) Dark Souls 1 still has to be my favorite. The first half of the game is amazing. From the very start you have many options of how to progress. With your only goal given is to ring the bells of awakening a new player might find themselves exploring areas that are way more difficult than anything they should be doing, but it doesn't matter. Even if you go to a difficult area early, there still will be upgrade materials and weapons that you might use for the entire rest of the game. These branching paths at the start of the game also help for replayability. You can get many different weapon types from the very start of the game without even killing a single enemy which is great for specific challenge runs or just a normal playthrough with a weapon type you haven't used before.

However in the later half of the game there is a noticeable decline in quality there is still branching paths because you can choose between whichever lategame objective you want to do first, but these areas these areas themselves are often very unpolished due to time constraints in the development team. There is still some really cool parts in the second half that I always enjoy, but they don't really make up for some of the things that are still present.

So in conclusion: The overall map design is amazing in the first half, allowing you to reach many areas very early and allowing for much variety in the way that you tackle the game on repeat playthroughs. However the second half is very unpolished, there are still some really cool parts, but a lot of it feels kind of painful. I don't care though, I can look past the second half and enjoy the entirety of the game for the masterpiece that it is and I hope that this review might convince you to play the game and enjoy it as much as I have and probably will in the future

A Journey Through the Abyss!
Dark Souls is an unforgettable odyssey into a dark, mysterious world teeming with danger, despair, and triumph.
From the moment you awaken in the "Undead Asylum" to your final battle, this game challenges you, humbles you, and rewards you in ways few others can match.
The world of Lordran is a masterpiece of atmospheric storytelling.
Every ruined castle, crumbling fortress, and shadowy catacomb feels alive with history and secrets, waiting for brave players to uncover them.
The game doesn’t spoon-feed you its lore, you piece it together through cryptic dialogue, item descriptions, and the haunting environment itself.
It’s a level of storytelling that respects the player’s intelligence and curiosity.
Combat in Dark Souls is methodical and rewarding.
Each swing of your sword, dodge, or parry feels deliberate, and the weight of every action makes victory all the sweeter.
The game’s iconic boss battles are a highlight, with each fight pushing you to learn, adapt, and overcome.
Whether it’s the towering Bell Gargoyles or the oppressive presence of Ornstein and Smough, every encounter leaves a lasting impression.
The difficulty is, of course, legendary, but it’s not just hard for the sake of being hard.
Dark Souls teaches you patience, resilience, and the joy of learning from failure.
Every death is a lesson, every bonfire a moment of reprieve, and every hard-fought victory a triumph that fills you with genuine pride.
The interconnected world design is another standout feature.
Lordran feels like a living, breathing place, with shortcuts and paths looping back in ways that are both surprising and ingenious.
It’s a design philosophy that encourages exploration and rewards those who pay attention to their surroundings.
Beyond the mechanics and design, Dark Souls is an emotional experience.
It’s about perseverance in the face of overwhelming odds, finding light in the darkness, and forging your own story in a world that offers no hand-holding.
For those willing to embrace its challenges, Dark Souls offers an unparalleled adventure that lingers long after the credits roll.
It’s a testament to the power of games as art and storytelling, and it stands as one of the most impactful titles of its generation.

This game is amazing. The controls are nice and allow for a satisfying and rewarding combat experience. The overall world design is definitely my favourite among the other soulsborne games that Fromsoft has ever released as every area feels connected with each other in some way, allowing for some really rewarding exploration and discovering some neat shortcuts.
The bosses are very fun to fight (except Bed of Chaos. ♥♥♥♥ Bed of Chaos) and feel very rewarding to beat. The Oolacile expansion is a very neat addition to the game (even tho it's very complicated to get to), featuring a whole new area to explore and 4 new bosses, each more challenging and fun than the last. The implications this DLC has on the story are also very nice to observe (paints Sif in a whole new tragic light). The music of the game is phenomenal, something that remains consistent in every soulsborne game.

I'd say this game is a solid 8.5/10. A must play title for someone that is looking to try a soulsborne game for the first time and also if you want a good challenge. Has a lot of replay value as you always find something new upon replaying the game. It's one of the games that I revisit the most and I think it'll be the same for you as well.

At first I didn't like the game so much, coming from Dark Souls 3 I thought the game was quite boring and outdated. However, it is different enough from DS3 that it has its own charm. The environments are really well connected and it feels almost open-world, although it is probably more comparable to the recent Tomb Raider games or the post 2018 God of War games in terms of how the world is set out. There's very minimal storytelling, but it is told through various interactions. Boss fights can seem annoying at first, but you will know how to beat them after a couple of tries. I don't think that the game is really that hard, but it can be annoying. For example, at the "Four Kings" boss fight, there are no bonfires in the New Londo Ruins area, but it doesn't take that long to get to the boss from the Firelink Shrine area (about two minutes). There are a lot of areas in the game that are not as visually appealing as any area from DS3, and I would say that the combat is not as good as DS3 either even though it is functionally the same. Estus takes longer to drink, which can make its use much more strategic than in DS3. There are loads of items to find throughout the environment, including rings that give you certain abilities, and so on. Overall, I do find that the game can get boring at times, which is why you should probably take regular breaks, but even then, you'll regularly be thinking of coming back to retry a certain area or continue the game or try the boss one more time. It is worth buying, but it is not as good as Dark Souls 3 (the only other Fromsoft game that I've finished as of now).

Posted: January 7
TLDR: I wanted to like this game. It's story is very interesting, the world design and 3D metroidvania looping map style is cool. Being able to look in the distance and go to a place you see later on is also cool. I also love the music. However, the most important part of a game is not any of those things (though they all are still very valuable to an experience). The most important part of a game is gameplay, and the gameplay here is awful. Not enemy difficulty, but the crummy camera and the awful and frankly, in my opinion, lazily designed controls keep me from enjoying this game. Skip it, start with Sekiro or Elden ring. They have their flaws and laziness too, but no where near as frustrating as this game.

Long Review: The lethargic, unresponsive, and poorly implemented controls and camera interactions keep this game from being great. The dodge and run controls are bound to a single key. Here are some examples.

So, you want to dodge? Well press dodge and wait for the game to understand that you want to dodge the guy swinging a sword at your face. Dodge delay occurs, you get smacked anyway and now have wasted valuable stamina (yes, even when on low armor encumbrance).

You want to run away? Hold on to the dodge/run key for dear life and hope your character get's the hint to run away from a demon dual wielding greatswords.

You want to fight a guy in a tight corridor? Well tough luck buddy. Your character has not heard of thrusting and will only ever swing in a closed space, getting stunned because they hit a wall. While the enemy you're fighting doesn't suffer any penalty and can go loco on your character.

You want to get closer to the enemy to hit them? Well tough luck chum you just triggered the kick action (bound to a movement key and your attack button...yunno, the main keys you use in a fight) and will now get ganked.

Wanna Kick a guy and break his parry stance? Too bad, so sad. You didn't time it perfectly so now you hit him and get parried, then ganked as your drunken character tries to mosey off of the ground.

Wanna parry someone and get the upper hand? Sorry, even though your shield obviously made contact with the sword coming at your gut, you didn't do it when the game wanted you to. So now The sword will phase through your shield and you die.

How about this. Do you want to backstab someone for an easy kill? Or maybe you parried them and they're stunned. Once again, sucks for you. You didn't stand in the exact right spot and the game has no toggle option for "backstabbing" so you don't get the easy hit.

That's enough of my angry ranty examples. These control flaws, as I've said, ruin this game for me. I have no idea what happened to fromsoft's control scheme designers, but the controls from armored core 1 (a ps1 game with no joysticks) had better camera movement and controls than this game. If you haven't played armored core 1 (it's great if you can look past some of the jank, highly recommend) that is a very bad thing. To give a better example, it feels like I'm playing this game with "that one annoying cousin/brother" who won't stop trying to grab the controls out of my hand. Every time I try and do something, he comes over and starts pushing buttons and yanking on the joysticks. Whereas AC1 (and frankly all of the ps1 AC games) just felt like you're playing fetch with a smart dog who's just a bit old and dopey and sometimes needs a hand.

The camera in this is very frustrating. In elden ring the lock on was awful, but the base third person camera was mostly great. In this game, you can thankfully turn off some of the annoying camera reset features. But, not the ones for strafing. If you dare strafe, whatsoever, the game will yank your camera away from you, and no, you can't keep looking at the enemy you want to, as the game physically won't budge while strafing. This is an annoying hindrance and I don't know why it was coded into the game. When you run backwards, sometime's the camera also gets screwed up. The game just won'te let the camera be, and allow the player to worry about it.

The combat controls, and I mean it, feel like an old geezer who's half awake while standing on a banana peel. The delay caused by having dodge and run bound to one key, and being unable to be separated, make a significant impact on combat. There is no excuse for why they aren't separated, or able to be. Let alone in a remaster. The parrying is very unrealistic to me and feels less like a skill check and more of a "guess the parry window" check. I find it's better just to take the shield hit and let the enemy get stunned from that. The lock on system is also very flawed overall, and is a necessary evil in combat. Unfortunately, even in their latest title, it seems fromsoftware haven't quite figured out how to not make an annoying lock on system, even after 8 games with similar mechanics and 14 years of time. The lock on is the only thing that let's you move away from an enemy while still facing them, and is damn near key to consistently landing your hits (as there isn't a cursor on the screen that lets you tell your character where to hit). This lock on will also prevent any and all camera movement, so if you're in an awkward spot in a corridor or happen to be in a bad spot, it'll be tough to see.

Combine the jank of the camera with the jank of the combat systems, and you have one nasty experience. I cannot in good faith recommend this to anyone who likes and/or expects semi-responsive control schemes. What I will say is that you'd be better off skipping this game, the original demon souls, and DS2 and going to the more recent titles like Elden Ring or Sekiro (maybe DS3 but I have yet to try it. Hoping it's good). Elden ring especially has some glaring issues (especially in the end game areas, which were lazily put together in terms of enemies) but they are still vastly superior experiences.

I am saddened to give this review, as I've always wanted to try DS1. I quit the game on my first play through because the camera was so frustrating. I decided to try and give it another chance but it is still just as frustrating. Enough to where I cannot say how hard the enemies are, or who was my favorite to fight. I had no issues with the enemies, since I spent more time fighting the controls than the actual enemies in the game. Don't buy into the hype like I did.

There's no strecthing how hard this game tanks the second you complete Anor Londo and reach the backend of the game.
There's still quality to be found, plenty in fact, but the Tomb of the Giants is awful, Lost Izalyth even more so, and some of the other areas, though not as egregious, also don't really set off the amazement and wonder the earlier areas did. For a game whose main appeal was how perfectly planned and structured it was, often interconnected in the most fascinating way, to abandon that once fast travel becomes a possibility is nothing short of a calamity.

With that being said, were it not for those dips, this could very easily have been the single greatest game I'd ever played, and that cannot go understated. The DLC also put some good effort into improving my final impressions of it, even if Kalameet proved well beyond my patience. The lack of handholding is always a blessing, truly selling your place in this world as a miserable ant liable to be crushed at each and every step. It's a deep meditation on resilience and hardship told entirely through gameplay and game design--which is already enough to consider it a Berserk adaption. Even the multiplayer elements contribute: no one's ever truly alone, and their mistakes can be your teachers as much as your own.
Summons and invasions too were super fun mechanics to deal with. Always gonna cherish facing off against another player, him starting by taking a bow, only to then absolutely wreck my ♥♥♥♥, before finishing with yet another bow, just to keep it classy. Love it.

It's a stunning game. A stunning adventure in the most ideal senses; something akeen to a spiritual experience more than an enjoyable ride, that its follow-ups do not seem quite as interested in trying to replicate. Though that much is understandable, as through its first thirty hour strecth, this was lightning in a bottle; to think you can catch it twice is blasphemous.

After playing Elden Ring and Sekiro I was curious about the game and series that started it all. I think it's up there with the best decisions I've made in my life.

The map of this game is better than any game I've ever played. It's so intertwined and interconnected every corridor can lead you to anywhere within the map and it all makes sense in a normal Euclidean plane (No cheating with portals etc.). You'll climb a ladder for like half a minute but it WILL connect to the place you were at earlier. It's like they challenged themselves to make the map as compact as possible yet it's still so huge. And the sights are so beautiful I find it hard to explain. This one's definitely something you'll have to experience for yourself.

The boss fights themselves are great except a few but nothing to write home about in my opinion but that doesn't mean they aren't great fun, especially the DLC bosses.

The DLC is a nice short little adventure that I enjoyed thoroughly and I recommend anyone that liked the base game to give it a shot.

Most people find that the latter half of the game is empty with a lot of running and not enough content compared to the first, I will agree that they're emptier in terms of content but I will say that I enjoyed them at least as much as I did the first half of the game. The sights towards the end of the game trump the areas you're in at the start for sure.

This game is 5 stars, 10/10 whatever other max award I can give it. Amazing.

General Opinion
Amazing game. An incredible introduction to the souls franchise with great lore/story, well-made mechanics and strenuous difficulty (albeit the difficulty sometimes is caused by the wrong reasons). Out of 10 - a solid 8. If I were told to play again i would but only to relive nostalgia but if it was to get all the achievements again then no,

Particular Likes & Dislikes
I really love the intricacy they've put on Dark Souls 1. The cohesion and thought put into each area to one another is something I've simply never seen before in a game and I find it unfortunate that later stages of the game could not live up to the initial areas in terms of ingenious game design and level efficacy (This is due to the rushed production of the game btw). I do however, want to critique that Dark Souls 1 difficulty stem less from the mobs and bosses, and instead is made challenging by other factors (such as the environment, walk-backs, requirement to do research by exploration and trial and error, confusion). Although, this may seem intended and at cases is imply a "get good" scenario, I would like to point out specifically to the bosses "Bed of Chaos". "Seath the Scaleless" and "Gravelord Nito" which are simply difficult because of the ludicrous walk-backs, questionable level design like invisible floors or complete darkness and the possibility of not even reaching the boss or defeat the boss due to falling specifically. Unlike Anor Londo (where its still rather acceptable because the walk back isn't that bad and the bosses are actually a skill check), the bosses previously mentioned are hard because of a lack of quality in the game itself.

Should you buy?
Yes. Ab-soul-utely. This is a really good start to a souls game and far more friendlier than Elden Ring (imo) because its slower paced, simpler than modern souls games and gives new members of the community the chance to feel extreme unnecessary hell rather than a difficult boss fight. This is vital to teaching patience and determination to these fledglings as both are core requirements to long-term souls enjoyment. Dark Souls 1 is also the more acceptable earlier souls games compared to Demon Souls and Dark Souls 2 where the previous problems are far more extreme (from what I heard). Dark Souls 3 is also a much more fun alternative but I find Dark Souls 1 unique game features as something to greatly appreciate as it won't ever be replicated in any other souls games.

TL;DR: If you like Dark Souls franchise and you don't have the original version then I recommend this one. Otherwise, you can mod the old version to look even better than this one for more less the same content.

Even though it is Dark Souls 1 (DS1), it is a second Souls-Like game and replaying it made me feel more confident and content easier than anticipated. How so? In essence, after playing Dark Souls 3 and Elden Ring as well as some other Souls-like games, DS1 seems far easier combat wise. However, certain aspects of the game are very old school - initially, player doesn't have teleportation/warping between bonefires and has to go everywhere by foot. This might feel tedious. At certain point player unlocks the ability to teleport/warp but only between certain bonefires.

Areas are more self contained and backtracking is not that present. But the design of certain areas might be quite challenging for certain players and given the (now staple of the series) limited information, player might end up in situations where they can't progress and now know what to do next (as an example entering the Abyss to fight the 4 Kings boss). For such scenarios, players can find many guides online how to overcome these challenges as it felt that what to do is more difficult than combat itself. Then again, maybe I am becoming a normie in souls-like genre.

Boss fights are not particularly difficult. There are some 2 mandatory odd fights which are not intuitive and player has to perform certain steps in order to kill a boss (but for that too there is a guide online).

I was playing a sorcerer and while spells are simplistic and mostly rehashed same thing, they are very effective. Paired with weapon that has at least A scaling with intelligence is a good combo for victory. Of course, there are other builds just as effective.

All in all, I had a fun experience with the (second) game that became a legacy in gaming industry. Even though graphics are dated, I would still recommend to play it.

Posted: January 3
This game is so so A$$!
Wait, hear me out!
It's a great game, a fantastic one even, but it is so incredibly a$$ to its player, I have rarely seen such a thing, closest I can think of would be IWBTG.
If you never played a Soulslike, I DON'T suggest this game, because it will just needlessly frustrate you. Play Elden Ring or some other game instead, only play it if you have masochistic preferences and want to make a checkmark.

Still here? Okay, let me elaborate then. The game is hard, yes, but that's not my main gripe with it, by far not. I've played my fair share of hard games. But this game, it specifically wants to f*ck you in particular. So many things are designed just to despise you. Enemies placed at points that will simply kill you the first time you walk by, just for the sake of it. Traps and spots to specifically shoot you in the back without any possibility to know before. Deliberate architecture and situations to make you scream "what is that unfair bull$hit??!".

This game toys with your emotions, and rarely in positive ways. It takes your preconceived notions and experiences about games and takes a giant dump onto it just because it can. It deliberately incites frustration and impatience to lead you to make worse decisions. And the patience, oh man, this game just doesn't respect your time. It spits on it and then laughs at you getting mad for it. The concept of an interconnected world where you have to plan your route to efficiently get to the spot that gives you difficulty so you can actually try again is fun at first, but it very quickly becomes a nuisance. You ever felt annoyed when a savepoint wasn't right before a boss or you couldn't just press a single button to retry instead of having to reload manually? Yeah, this game puts that to shame, it doesn't respect you, it doesn't respect your time, or your skills. It becomes a pure try&error at some point, you throw yourself into the grinder headfirst repeatedly getting 10 seconds of interaction for 5 minutes of getting there just to try out if you actually can block that one move and instead getting oneshot by it. It's extremely knowledge-based, the learning curve is very steep and no two enemies behave the same, the game constantly forces you to relearn interactions and adapt. And that's great! But man, at some point I just want to actually try something out, not having to jump through all the hoops I already mastered. 70% of this game is just running. Impatience is pure killer, and this game knows it. It's great when things work out, and you feel just cheated of your time when they don't. And you try again, and again, and again... and it's that what makes it both great and $hit at once.

But there's a lot more issues I have with this game that are less about the way it treats you. A lot of things are very clunky, the game doesn't tell you so many things (wait, I can actually pov aim with the bow???!!) and there are like 20 ways you can irredeemably ♥♥♥♥ up your game and items and story to absolutely miss unless you consult the wiki. It is barebones in that regard, which is also why I don't suggest it for someone who never played a Soulslike. There is the incentive to try out things yourself, but just because of the time investment that would require, it doesn't feel to pay off and you just check the wiki instead. Lot of such games, which in itself isn't a problem, but I feel this game could have easily dealt with a lot of those things.

And the camera, OH MY ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ GOD THE CAMERA. This is actually the worst camera I have ever seen in any game, and I must have seen hundreds of bad ones. You can rotate it around freely, but if it touches an enemy or level architecture, it zooms in to not land in the models. Great in idea, absolutely awful in practice. At times you can absolutely see nothing because the camera just says no as you are stuck in a tight spot. You try to actively avoid tight spaces just so the camera doesn't pull out your nails all at once. And you can lock onto enemies, but then because they moved around a centimeter your camera touched that beam above your head and suddenly you no longer see them and die instantly because they swinged just then. It is so so bad, you feel like playing against the controls instead of against the enemies. Often it's just easier to not use it at all because it can hose you so hard at random points.

The input buffer is ridiculous, it is cool that you have to commit to things like a roll, but it's way too high. You can press roll twice in a second and your dude will do two rolls for 10 seconds. Not the worst of the game, but feels very unresponsive at times. The controls are also kinda whack, your jump is on the same button as your run, which makes it really awkward to use properly. There is no reset option for the camera, so often you just run into the void hoping it works out. The backstab range seem often very discolated from the opponents actual backs and arbitraty. Weird hitbox interactions can happen, you can die simply because you stood at the wrong place at the wrong time and the game decided to work against you in that moment.

This is a great game, that rightfully got all the praise, there are enough reviews of why that is so, and I won't mention them. But it's not just a diamond with rough edges, but those are covered in $hit and Ebola as well. It is suffering, and a great point of that suffering is NOT because it is hard. I can suggest it to anyone who doesn't mind those things and enjoys getting backstabbed repeatedly, and seemingly enough people don't mind. But don't think this game will give you a pleasant complete gaming experience or simply respect you, steer away very very far from it in that case.

I first need to say two things about Dark Souls before we continue;

First and foremost, this is my favourite game of all time

Second, my favouritism is totally marred by nostalgia, for this game is not without fault

This being said, I will recommend this game to anyone with even a vague interest in souls gameplay or medieval fantasy rpgs as (understandably so) Dark Souls is the blueprint for so many other games striving for the same genres.

Dark Souls’ core gameplay is refined in later editions, with 3 being the most accessible and most pleasing to actually play, but there is an undeniable charm to the roots. I adore the jank, and I miss the humanity system. There are many intricacies to the game and its mechanics hidden away that tie into the discovery and mystery of the world as well.

Above all else, Dark Souls stands out in the series for its esoteric world. Theres an aloofness to the story, the characters and overall presentation that feels so unique. It is a style I feel was lost slowly over the series - 2 being a fairy tale worked well, but 3 felt too structured.

“The flow of time is convoluted” says Solaire. This little line justifies, explains and shrouds the entire story in such a perfectly effortless way. A line we take for granted throughout the adventure. The dreamlike vagueness of the game only being elevated by the stylised visuals that still hold up all these years later.

My real, and only problem with the game is that there is a clear dip in quality about 3/4’s of the way through. A look behind the scenes explains why this is, with strict deadlines throwing everyone into a spin. However, this dip only appears worse than it actually is because of how well crafted every other moment throughout has been. It makes you appreciate the choices in design that had been made, that you didn’t notice, to make the experience a fair and tough challenge. In a sense, the game suffers from its own greatness.

If left to ramble, I could eventually find the words for how delightful this game is in every regard. But I also think part of playing this game is figuring it out for yourself (like boss souls weapons for example).

If you are yet to play DS, and have played other instalments in the series - you’ll develop a new found appreciation for the journey FromSoftware have undertaken. If you stick with it too, you might just find the most mysterious and intriguing world FS have ever made.

Posted: January 2
Peak Souls Remastered.

The interconnected map in Lordran adds to the depth and discovery of the game with most areas linking back to the bonfire at Firelink Shrine. Although the graphics definitely appear dated from a modern perspective (or when compared to DS3), Lordran is still able to maintain its visually appealing yet grotesque aesthetic. Anor Londo remains one of the most beautiful, yet challenging, areas in Lordran with its grand Gothic architecture and monumental buildings towering above the player at any given time. In contrast, Blighttown is a vertical labyrinth with its visually unstable, descending scaffolding that leads directly into a poisonous swamp. You also get the joy of toxic dart shooters the entire way down.

In terms of bosses, Great Grey Wolf Sif, Ornstein and Smough and Knight Artorias were among my personal favourites due to their challenging movements and attacks as well as their expansive lore. Some bosses only took me a couple of attempts, others made me consider therapy. The only bosses I personally disliked was the Bed of Chaos due to how poorly designed it was (and just Lost Izalith as a whole) and the Four Kings due to the ridiculous amount of health they would have in NG+. Other than those two, each boss felt well-placed, well-designed and equally as challenging.

DS1 provides an unforgettable experience which is only further enhanced by its beautiful soundtrack and unique theme for each main boss. DS1 is considered a fairly difficult game, especially with some bosses that may take dozens of attempts to finally beat and some areas that may take you hours to get through. After all, it is the very game that spawned the entire "Souls-like" genre. In terms of playtime, DS1 will take you about 60-70 hours to complete if you include exploration, side quests and the DLC. If you plan to get all achievements like I did, then you're looking at about 100-120 hours. In conclusion, DS1 is a masterpiece and I'd easily give it a solid 9/10.

Praise the Sun! Don't you dare go Hollow!

I played this quite a lot when it first came out, and didn't care for it much. Playing it again for the first time in 12-14 years and it feels exactly the same as any other game that has ripped off this style since. Mediocre to the core.

The game's story doesn't seem to have as many overt occult and freemason references as the other "fromsoft" games, which is at a point of parody at this point. Nothing compared to bloodborne or elden ring. Still insidious though, I can just tell that the way it is presented is catnip to a very specific kind of ever-impressionable person. Only really offensive if you really know your stuff, I don't even notice it at first blush sometimes.

The whole experience is so nihilistic and the controls are so poor, that it feels like I am playing a "neuroatypical" depressed person playing an rpg. Every movement feels like it needs to first be signed off and rubber stamped before anything happens. The story is ridiculous and reeks of a man whose single defining moment in his life was "peaking" during his "early teenager" emo / theater kid phase, and stuck there for 30 years. Considering the appearance of personality of miyazaki in interviews, this is 100% the case.

miyazaki has said that his style of gameplay is to make the player feel his depression, as the same empty frustration you feel when playing is how he sees every moment of his life. LOL

I would not be shocked one bit if this game played no small part in both the "doomer" and occult culture among "specific" communities of shut-in gamers of today. Just mimicking what they see.

When dealing with atheists / satanists in the recent past (say pre-2015), I could tell very easily they were first "radicalized" by the final fantasy series. I can tell equal mixtures of "soulslike" and anime in more modern ones. Ideological maladies are able to be traced even easier than physical ones after all. This confirms my hypotheses hinted at here (that I can't expound on much on this platform), because the themes here have a specific rancid odor that is very common. The fact that half of the comments on wikis for this series are from clear 10-15 year olds, and those comments are going back over a decade, it's easy to trace ideological lineage to the bloodthirsty monsters I have to deal with online that are in their 20's.

As for the game itself, it was an interesting experiment.

When sony reminded miyazaki that they were only giving him money for an exclusive game just to fill out marketing advertisements on the failing ps3, and that they knew there is no chance miyazaki would have a chance to sell any real copies due to his reputation as a hack, miyazaki deep down knew they were right.

miyazaki took sony's advice to "make the game you always dreamed of, because it's the only chance you will ever get" to heart and so took his game design quirks up to 11.

miyazaki likes hard games because he says if he can conquer a hard game he feels he can conquer his depression. demon's souls was harder than any previous game he had made though. In-fact, miyazaki admitted he balanced the game around 3-player co-op but didn't tell anyone.

miyazaki likes sparse stories because his fondest memories were playing Western games on his pc as a kid, and had not one clue what the text or dialouge meant as he did not know english. demon's souls was even more obtuse than any previous game he had made though. The irony being that demon's souls did not have a coherent story whatsoever, in other words there was nothing to find in the first place.

miyazaki also was a huge early-adopter of social media, and wanted his game to make reference to micro-blogging and mass-messaging like we would see with myspace or MSN / AIM / IRC chats or twitter feeds or facebook walls. Make it amusing by limiting speech as well for "memeable" moments.

All of this was not just done for fun though as miyazaki had a plan: make the game so hard, obtuse, and shareable that people would just have to post online about it and start wikis and discussion forums just to handle basic functions of the game. This boosts the game's "engagement," and could be used as metrics in place of sales to negotiate another major publisher deal.

miyazaki is a cunning businessman pretending to be an "auteur."

Of course, demon's souls DID become the "cult classic" with ridiculous "engagement" that miyazaki planned out, but sony refused a sequel.

miyazaki then decided to remake demon's souls as another original ip called "dark souls" (ie, this very game), and namco was willing to publish based upon the extensive word of mouth that demon's souls had.

The meteoric rise of dark souls came with a common bit of games industry collusion. A female "games reviewer" at ign was hired in the marketing department of namco-bandai and this was to be her first client.

Upon playing the game, she had nothing nice to say and so came up with a proper advertising goblin ploy: say the game is "hard on purpose," say the story is "only for the most intelligent," and seal it with a tagline kiss like "PREPARE TO DIE." Basically watch any expose on corporate advertising and realize the games industry is a solid twice as corrupt if not more!

Said "games reviewer" turned marketing girl then pulled strings to have an "exclusive" at her old employer ign where a number of male reviewers were tasked with taking turns playing the game for the first time on camera, with the marketing girl mocking their manhood like a dominatrix when they died in game.

Nerds, unable to fight off the urge to peacock, and unsavvy enough to fall for every corporate manipulation tactic in the book, bought game in droves to "prove themselves" to a random, near-middle-aged, female-on-the-internet with an attitude problem that they have zero chance of ever meeting.

If this game is to be remembered for one thing, it will be that this marked a turning point in exposing just how easily it is to manipulate the common person into doing things they hate and saying whatever you program them to.

The underground backlash against the open, legitimate brainwashing in the "souls" community played no small part in inspiring the "gamegate" community 2-3 years later. This really brought dissident communities out in the open, and hundreds of previous groups who were in hiding for decades began to make friends with the "recently awoken" gamers. Then when the disaffected paleocon "tea party" and disgruntled libertarian "gamergaters" congealed around 2015 with a number of popular dissidents from the 1990's at the helm, they found a then unpopular-but-outsider presidential candidate Donald Trump as their chosen figurehead. It's all been a snowball since then.

I was there for it all, in the trenches, doing God's Work among men who didn't want to be ruled by evil any more.

If for little else, I can thank this game for being a key part of that series of strange events. Whatever other flaws I can overlook because of that.

Okay, so here's the thing: I downloaded Dark Souls Remastered because I heard it's, like, the "hardest game ever," and I thought, "Oh, bet. I’m totally going to crush this and flex on my friends." Spoiler: I did not crush it. The game crushed ME.

From the moment I walked into the cursed land of Lordran, I was greeted by a delightful collection of enemies who clearly never learned the concept of "personal space." Like, I'm just trying to vibe and level up, and suddenly I'm getting mobbed by a bunch of dudes with axes. Thanks, I really wanted to see how long I could last before literally dying—IN THE FIRST FIVE MINUTES. That’s on me, though, I should’ve known when they said "prepare to die," they weren’t joking.

The combat? Oh honey, don’t even get me started. It’s like the game is designed to humiliate me in the most painful ways. I’ll dodge, roll, parry, and still end up face-first in a puddle of my own soul. But, like, I get it. Dark Souls isn’t just a game. It’s an experience—a brutal, heart-wrenching, soul-crushing experience. You think you're ready to face that boss? Nah. That boss is ready to humble you so hard, you’ll question every decision that led you to this point. Like, WHY DID I CHOOSE A KNIGHT, THIS SWORD IS SO SLOW AND I CAN’T EVEN READ THE GAME’S INSTRUCTIONS?!

Also, shoutout to my poor little character who looks like they’ve never seen the sun and probably lives on a diet of instant noodles. At this point, I just want to take a break, but I can’t. Because there’s a dragon that keeps breathing fire down my neck like it’s a TikTok roast, and I’m trying to figure out how to get my soul back from a skeleton who’s WAY too into his job. Chill, bro, it’s just a game.

Finally, the community? 10/10 for chaos. Every time I think I’ve beaten a boss, some random player shows up and leaves a message that’s like, “Try jumping here”... and then I jump off a cliff. Classic. Gotta love it. Really makes you feel connected to your fellow tortured souls.

So, would I recommend this game to other e-girls? Honestly, if you have a lot of patience, a high tolerance for humiliation, and a penchant for breaking controllers, Dark Souls Remastered is for you. It's like that toxic ex who treats you like trash, but somehow you still keep going back for more because… I mean… look at those graphics? 😩

TL;DR: 10/10 would recommend if you enjoy suffering in the name of epic gaming moments. But seriously, someone please help me.

Dark Souls is everything a video game should be. Although it has some confusing design choices that can be frustrating for new players, it is still ultimately very successful at what it set out to do. Create a melancholy, oppressive, atmospheric adventure game about exploration and growing stronger in a hostile world that wants to see you dead at every turn.

It honestly reminds me a lot of Metroid in that aspect. Especially when it comes to more specific things like the interconnected world design where you enter a place, leave that place and find a new one, then find a seemingly hidden or out-of-the-way path that will take you right back to where you started. Not to mention nearly every area having something new to look at and see with a diverse array of enemies and locations that don't feel like they fall into the video game archetypes catalogue.

Even though FromSoftware has certainly gotten better at their craft over the years making these kinds of games, it is still important to play their older attempts at the formula and appreciate just how much the fundamentals are still there. Dark Souls is well worth your time, and money, and for anyone who is new to the series I can't find any other game I'd recommend more than the very first Dark Souls.

Posted: December 30, 2024
I'm bad at this game but from a design perspective it's simply fantastic. The idea that anyone could dislike these games simply because they are tough to beat boggles my mind. The score- and the battles that the pieces are composed for- work hand-in hand beautifully, and they communicate the nature of the battle perfectly. The enemies in every area are, for better or worse- perfectly tailored to that area, and each of them have their own details to them that I highly recommend looking at, if not in game (I'll grant that some areas are overwhelming and quite difficult to take in these details) then online pages.

I do wish there were a more 'casual' setting, but it has been a decade and I'm not a rage gamer who's going to call a game bad just because I struggle with it. I play slow-paced management games. This is a game not of my skillset or depth. I can recognise that.

The messaging in the souls series makes me feel very conflicted- the nature of the world that the game presents and my own personal belief in how things should be conflict heavily, and that dissonance is hard for me to escape in a game so intertwined with it. On one hand, I love it; it's a fantastic setting and I think it does a lot with it, thoroughly considering every aspect of what would occur in a setting that operates in this manner. On the other, it leaves me intensely uncomfortable; which is the purpose, more often than not, to drag the player out of their comfort zone and show depravity and all it's adorable (and not-so-adorable) subcultures.

Just because a game aims to make someone uncomfortable, however, does not directly imply that they have to enjoy being made uncomfortable. By no means is this a bad thing either way. It just means that if you find yourself uncomfortable or put off by something in the beginning, I would not recommend playing this game much longer, because it does get worse.

Relatedly, I have so few hours in this game myself because I find the multiplayer... unsettling. I play offline. I have been told that, on some levels, this means I am missing out on a little bit of content, and if I am to be frank, I don't really mind. The parts of the game I am missing out on due to my quirk of personality are not so grand that I find them a necessity. By my nature I abhor being forced to interact with people on servers against my will- this is nothing on the game's part, from what I have seen of the online functionality, it seems well thought-out and designed.

10/10 game, I intensely dislike this game but by no means is that a bad thing. You would not judge a fish by it's ability to climb a tree, and likewise, you would not judge a tree for not accomodating a fish.

Rating: 10/10

FromSoftwares Dark Souls remains a landmark in gaming, setting the gold standard for action RPGs with its challenging gameplay, meticulously designed world, and a story that unfolds like a puzzle. While the series boasts several exceptional entries, the first game stands out for one crucial reason: its lore.

The world of Dark Souls invites players into the fallen kingdom of Lordran, a land steeped in mystery, decay, and forgotten glory. Unlike many games that spoon feed their narrative, Dark Souls delivers its story through cryptic dialogue, item descriptions, and environmental storytelling. This design choice encourages exploration and rewards players who piece together its fragmented history.

The lore of Dark Souls is unparalleled, weaving themes of cyclical fate, ambition, and despair into its narrative. Characters like Gwyn the Lord of Cinder and Artorias the Abysswalker are not only legendary figures, but tragic symbols of a world succumbing to entropy. The games brilliant world building ties every location, enemy, and artifact into the overarching mythos. For example, Anor Londos majestic splendor contrasts sharply with the bleakness of Blighttown, reflecting the fading light of a once glorious age.

What makes the lore in Dark Souls superior to its successors is its originality and restraint. The first game doesnt rely on the established expectations of a franchise. Instead, it introduces a universe that feels fresh yet ancient, where every mystery has layers and every revelation deepens the tragedy. This uniqueness ensures that the lore doesnt just enhance the experience, it defines it.

Despite its punishing difficulty, Dark Souls is more than a test of skill, its a journey of discovery. Its a game that demands patience, curiosity, and a willingness to engage with its world on a deeper level. Lordran isnt just a setting, its a character, whispering its secrets to those who dare to listen.

In conclusion, Dark Souls is a game where the challenge lies not only in combat but in uncovering the truth of a world teetering on the brink. Its lore, especially in the original entry, elevates it beyond a mere game to a work of art. If you havent experienced the haunting beauty of Lordran, now is the time to take up the mantle of the Chosen Undead.

Don't let the hours played mislead you. I originally played the Souls series on Xbox before firmly making the jump to PC, and across the trilogy, I have a couple hundred hours.

With Dark Souls 1 specifically, what is there to say that hasn't already been said? People produce 1-2 hour long video essays about how it saved their life, how it changed gaming, how it gave them the courage to dodge roll on past their abusive relationship, etc., etc.

But for as sarcastic as I'm being about the whole "Dark Souls changed my life bit"... it kinda did for me too. It has been a while since I played it for the first time, and since then I've been introduced to a lot of different games that really push the medium. But without a doubt, Dark Souls was the first game I played where when I rolled credits, I thought "no, this is a work of art". It gave me a new perspective on what gaming could be and I've been chasing that high ever since.

This game allowed me to branch out of my comfort zone of fantasy, action, rpg, sword game into others I never would've ever given a second look because of the philosophical undertones they provided (Outer Wilds, Disco Elysium... games like that).

If for some reason you've never tried the Souls series, do yourself a favor and try it now. Maybe it won't change your life and help you overcome depression... maybe you're coming from Elden Ring and you're worried about how clunky the 2011 combat must feel at this point. All valid. But you'll never know until you try. And then you'll try again. Because I know you won't go Hollow.

Posted: December 28, 2024
The world is awesome and wonderfully imaginative and the moment to moment gameplay is simple yet almost infinitely mutable in such a way that almost every fairy tale fantasy can be fulfilled. Quite simply the game is of high quality, it is clear that a lot of effort was put into quality assurance at the most baseline level.

Getting stuck in the catacombs was one of the most fun and challenging world puzzles I've ever experienced. No other game I've played, perhaps with the exception of Hollow Knight, has managed to fulfil my wish for a world that's expansive and free enough, to let me fail, not only in moment to moment gameplay, but in world exploration. The fact that some of the most crucial parts of what makes this game so good are completely hidden to someone that plays the game in an unmindful way is what puts it leaps and bounds beyond the mountains of imitators that followed its success.

Well worth a playthrough. If you're on the fence, I would suggest pushing yourself a little, at least until you start being able to build an understanding of the world, before putting this game down for good. However if you aren't impressed by that point, the game might not really offer what you're looking for, which is ok. Also feel free to put the game down and move on to another in the series if the quality of the game starts to feel a little thin towards the latter half. This game is most polished in the first half.

I never made review on this game even though I have more than 2k hours (through xbox) But, after another playthrough, I can tell you this is a masterpiece. This is, and I say this with humility, the first game that ever made me cry. This is a very complex lore once you put the pieces of the puzzle in place you start to feel sorry for who you kill through the game and start to realise why they were acting like so...

Enough with my emotions. This is very difficult game but that's where I found it interesting. The more difficulty you have the more satisfied you're going to be once you finally kill a boss. This is the kind of game where you need lots of patience and you're keen to learn mechanics.

Lore wise. Trust me, you might need the help of the community to understand every pieces of the lore since it's very complex. But take your time to understand your environment, talk to everyone. Do the game again. Go on a different path/covenant NPC's will react differently depending on the convenant you are and/or you will unlock new NPC from new covenants.

This is, also, the kind of game you wish you could play as if it was your first time. So if this is your first time playing this game. Do me a favor. Do not go check any video or anything. Feel the struggle because you're going to miss it :)

Anyway, I think this game is worth every penny and I highly recommand this game. If you're going to buy the game I wish you the very best gameplay. Make mistakes, kill npcs (except solar knight) and do not ever not praise the sun.

Posted: December 26, 2024
I think people have forgotten just how important Dark Souls is ever since Elden Ring has overshadowed it. Nobody had ever played anything like Dark Souls before. It literally changed the gaming landscape in the way something like Castlevania or Mario 64 changed it - no game made after this will not be in some way impacted by it, that is how important it is. Demon's Souls came out before this, yes, but it wasn't nearly as popular as this game was and was still very prototypical. Sure, the controls are janky and the gameplay is crude and convoluted, but that is all part of it's charm and what made it such a special, unique experience. Dark Souls boasts some of, if not the best level design of any game ever made, and the lore and storytelling was state of the art and would spawn so many theories, so many stupid video essays that it is completely unfathomable. Boss fights in modern gaming? Forget all about that if not for Dark Souls popularizing it. It is a shame that FromSoft was obviously rushed towards the end of the game where the quality drops off, but for the majority of the game it is truly something incredible that will never be replicated again. Seriously, if you have not played any of From Software's games, start here. I know just starting at Elden Ring is very tempting, but I wholeheartedly think starting from the beginning and building up will give you a truly unique experience that you will hopefully treasure forever. Highly recommend, changed my life and how I view games forever.

Goodness me, this game at 60 FPS is both a blessing and a curse.

Character movement and camera controls feels responsive and smooth--allowing the player to react and adjust a lot more quickly during combat situations. But, I will say that this game hasn't exactly aged perfectly in the gameplay department:

The combat system is a lot more rigid than I remember. Every action your character performs--dodging, attacking, casting a spell--it locks you into the full extent of the animation, no take-backsies!
It makes me appreciate just how far FromSoftware improved their gameplay over the years.
At any case, it's one thing to be careless by button-mashing in this game, but it's a whole other story when enemies' hitboxes are still active well after they've finished their attack animations.
Even when it appears that an enemy has finished their attack, and the coast looks clear, the player can still get attacked for rushing in too soon. It's relatively difficult for me to say whether it's the player's fault or the game's fault; I believe that the game was intended to run at 30 FPS in order to mask this unfortunate detail.

Despite that issue, Dark Souls: Remastered remains an exceptional game with atmosphere and subtle storytelling that still manages to captivate me all these years later. Every little item the player picks up is a piece of the game's larger narrative, and I really wish that the player could teleport between bonfires at the very beginning of the game :3

Still very enjoyable! Take your time learning how to upgrade weapons and armor efficiently!

--

Update: As of December 2024, I've beaten this game. I must say, there could've been more bonfires right before boss fights, I think that would've saved the player's time instead of backtracking ^^;

I’ve Always Loved Dark Souls, Flaws and All

Then, there was fire. Re-experience the critically acclaimed, genre-defining game that started it all. Beautifully remastered, return to Lordran in stunning high-definition detail running at 60fps.

Story
During the Age of Ancients, the Everlasting Dragons ruled until the First Flame appeared, bringing light, dark, life, and death. Gwyn and his allies used its power to defeat the dragons and begin the Age of Fire, while the forgotten Furtive Pygmy faded into legend. As the flame waned, Gwyn sacrificed himself to prolong the Age of Fire. Now, near its end, humanity suffers from an undead curse tied to the Darksign, causing endless resurrection and eventual madness known as hollowing.

Gameplay
Dark Souls Remastered is a third-person souls-like action role-playing game. Combat is a core part of Dark Souls, deliberate and punishing, requiring precise dodges, parries, and stamina management. It includes melee, ranged attacks, defensive maneuvers, and magic. Melee weapons include swords, spears, maces, greatswords, and other fantastical arms, while ranged options feature bows and crossbows. Defense involves shields, armor, dodging, backstepping, and parrying. Magic—sorceries, miracles, and pyromancies—uses limited casts replenished at bonfires rather than a mana system. All combat types have trade-offs and can be enhanced through leveling, smithing, consumables, rings, and new equipment.

Bonfires are central to the gameplay, serving as checkpoints throughout the world. Resting at a bonfire heals you, refills Estus Flasks, and allows leveling up with acquired souls, attuning magic, and repairing or upgrading equipment. However, resting also respawns all enemies, except bosses, mini-bosses, and defeated NPCs. Upon death, you respawn at the last bonfire rested at. Dying causes you to drop all collected souls and humanity at the death location, which are lost permanently if not recovered before dying again.

Another key mechanic is the humanity system. You can be in human or undead form. Dying in human form reverts you to undead, and you can restore humanity at a bonfire using a humanity item. While human, you can summon allies but are also vulnerable to invasions by other players or NPCs. Humanity can be acquired in various ways and grants bonuses and resistances, encouraging you to maintain it, though progression is still possible in undead form.

NPC Summons are cooperative NPCs specifically available for boss fights. They appear as white summon signs and often require meeting certain conditions—such as completing parts of their questlines—before they can be summoned. Once available, their signs typically appear just outside the boss fog gates.

The game offers strong replayability. After finishing, you can start New Game+, where enemies and bosses have increased health and damage. This continues up to NG+6, after which scaling stops, though you can continue playing on NG+.

The remaster includes Artorias of the Abyss DLC, adding new areas, bosses, and lore to the game.

Dark Souls features online multiplayer for both co-op and PvP. In co-op play, players place a summon sign that becomes visible to others using a matching item; interacting with it brings the summoner into their world until the area boss is defeated, the host dies, or the summoned player dies. In PvP, summon signs can be used to challenge others to duels, or players can use special items to invade other worlds.

Dark Souls offers a wide variety of enemies and bosses, each with distinct attack patterns that require careful observation and strategy to dodge and counter. Every enemy has unique moves, gear, and specific weaknesses and resistances, keeping combat engaging and varied.

Bosses are the true test of the Dark Souls experience. Bosses are powerful, challenging foes. Each boss is unique and has varying attacks, weaknesses, and strategies to defeat it. For the most part they are well designed. The DLC has the best bosses in the game for sure.

Set in the cursed kingdom of Lordran. Exploration is a core mechanic. You are encouraged to proceed cautiously, learn from mistakes, and discover alternative paths. Dark Souls features a large, interconnected world linked through a central hub, allowing you to explore various areas freely once prerequisites are met.

The game features a good variety of environments, many of which have become legendary, such as Blighttown and Anor Londo. Most areas are well-designed, with each room feeling meaningful to explore and little unnecessary space.

The remaster offers smoother gameplay and improved lighting. Enhanced effects, including better ambient occlusion and water reflections, make areas like Firelink Shrine and Undead Burg more immersive and atmospheric.

Every enemy has a distinctive set of sounds—grunts, roars, weapon swings—that communicate their size, aggression, and attack patterns. Audio cues are essential for combat; a giant’s stomp, a dragon’s growl, or a sorcerer charging a spell signals danger and timing windows.

The story in Dark Souls is good but can be confusing and hard to follow. You need to pay close attention to every NPC and exhaust all dialogue to understand the narrative and side quests. Like other FromSoftware games, figuring out what to do and when can be tricky without an online guide. Progressing too far without completing certain steps can permanently forfeit a quest, and there’s no journal or quest log to track your objectives. This lack of guidance can be frustrating, and it’s one of the few aspects of the Soulsborne formula I dislike.

The second thing I dislike is the co-op system. You can summon a player to help defeat a boss, but afterward they return to their world. To continue together, you must complete each area and boss twice—once in your world and once in your friend’s. It would be better if world progress, like defeated bosses and collected items, were shared and saved.

The third thing I dislike—actually, hate—is PvP. You can’t turn it off, so other players can invade your world unexpectedly. It’s often laggy and unbalanced, and in my opinion, PvP has never worked. The only way to avoid it is to play offline, which is difficult if you want to use co-op.

The combat isn’t flawless, with issues in hitboxes, targeting, spellcasting, and dodging at times. Still, for its time, it was excellent and remains very good. Many newer souls-like games are far worse.

Some bosses are just terrible. Regardless of the game’s age, I can’t understand the design choices behind certain bosses and arenas. Bed of Chaos is one of the worst I’ve ever encountered, both in boss design and arena layout.

The few negatives—like side quests and co-op limitations—are far outweighed by the game’s many strengths. Dark Souls is a good game, especially when played solo, where completing it feels like a far greater achievement than relying on co-op assistance.

Dark Souls is a challenging game that punishes mistakes—but in a fair way. Success depends on learning enemy and boss patterns and improving your skills. Most deaths are your fault, not the game’s, requiring practice and perseverance.

When I first played it over 10 years ago, I was completely blown away and fully immersed. If I played it for the first time today, I probably wouldn’t be as impressed. At the time, Dark Souls and its predecessor Demon’s Souls were innovative, kicking off a new genre of challenging games. Even after 13 years, Dark Souls remains fun and challenging, especially after taking a break from it. I just played through it again with a friend, marking my 23rd completion (24 if you count his world too), if I count all the playthroughs, both Xbox and PC. It’s without a doubt a favorite game.

After experiencing the rise and evolution of the souls-like genre, it's hard to call this game anything other than revolutionary. The gameplay is famously brutal, but it is also simple and fair enough for anybody to understand. What I think is truly brilliant about this game is that death is only punishing if you give up. There is a huge variety of builds and weapons you can use, so it's up to you to find what works for you.

I also want to harp on the inter-connectivity of this world and how satisfying and thoughtful it is. Many games beyond this quickly give you access to teleportation without much forethought, but this game focuses heavily on unlocking shortcuts and allowing you to find new or alternate paths to get to new locations. There's just an inherent sense of accomplishment in these subtle points of progression that is difficult to replicate.

Review after recently playing all 3 Dark Souls games in order.

Dark Souls 1 may look old and clunky, but damn, the first half of the game is one of the best gaming experiences ever! It's basically a 3D metroidvania. The level design is 100/10.

The whole game is very atmospheric. I really liked how dynamic it is. It goes from being very dark and claustrophobic to open spaces that fill you with awe and wonder, then back to narrow, treacherous paths where one wrong step can send you falling to your death. It's super immersive.

Talking about the gameplay, I actually really enjoyed the slower, "clunkier" combat here. I played Elden Ring before this and other souls-like games like Lies of P and Sekiro, and the slow combat in Dark Souls 1 feels impactful and decisive. You can feel the weight of the weapon and your swings.
But there are two problems with it: weird hitboxes and simple enemy ai. It doesn’t impact the overall experience too much, but it’s there.

And if you're coming from newer FromSoftware games, don’t expect this game to be very challenging. 99% of your deaths will be from gravity, not the enemies :)

All in all, a huge recommendation to play this game. It’s a masterpiece!

Don’t give up, skeleton!

P.s. Runs perfectly on a Steamdeck OLED.
P.s.p.s. Some of my achievements bugged, so keep this in mind if you hunt achievements.

Posted: December 17, 2024
Dark Souls is likely the game that had the largest impact on me as an adult, and truly helped me mature into the person I am today. Bloodborne was my first Fromsoft game, but Dark Souls was the first Fromsoft game where I'd say I actually had the full experience. I distinctly remember going into the game knowing about its reputation as a difficult game, going to the graveyard outside Firelink, fighting the skeletons, and thinking, "Huh, these guys are tough. Oh well, this must just be how the game is." I then proceeded to go down to Vamos's bonfire and get stuck for multiple hours in the bonewheel pit trying to get back out of the Catacombs. It was a spiritual experience. Another memory of mine was fighting Gaping Dragon for the first time, where I managed to kill it on my first try. I was so incredibly thrilled I managed to do it on my first attempt, and while it might seem silly in retrospect, it is a memory I will always hold close to my heart. Fast forward to the end of the game, and I was somehow still not aware of weight thresholds and roll types. I was fighting Gwyn with full Stone Knight amor, Black Knight sword, and fatrolling everywhere. After many attempts I suddenly came to the realization that there was in fact different dodges bases on the weight of your character and equipped the Gold-Hemmed Black set with a Reinforced Club and proceeded to beat him using that. Since then, I've replayed Dark Souls so many times I've honestly lost count. After playing the Souls series solo for years, I managed to get a friend to play with me, and despite it being her first rpg, she stuck it out all the way to the end. Today, it is a tradition of ours to take friends of ours new and old and guide them through the entire series as a kind of bonding experience. I think at this point we've played the series with upwards of twelve people, and I don't intend to let that stop any time soon. To review the game more critically, it's moment to moment gameplay can feel kind of floaty compared to later entries in the series, however where the game truly excels is in its interconnected world design and layout. If you know where to go and what to do, the game truly opens up, leaving you free to explore the areas out of "order". You can easily beat the game in thirteen bosses, and even less if you're acquainted with some of the easy skips and glitches. The bossing experience is rather easy in this game, at least compared to the rest of the series, however that does not make it any less satisfying to go through and experience them on repeat playthroughs. Dark Souls also shines in its build variety and weapon diversity. Having upwards of one hundred different weapons and extremely customizable upgrade paths, every playthrough will find a way to be unique. There are also many obscure and niche mechanics that not many players will interact with outside of those who truly dedicate themselves to the game. Things like Gravelording and Miracle Resonance are not likely to be interacted with by most players, but for veterans they act as ways to diversify and enjoy playthroughs after hundreds of hours of playtime. Unfortunately, Dark Souls NPC quest design is not very friendly to newcomers to the series, and I believe this to be one of its biggest flaws. There are many interesting characters that are a really strong addition to the game, but most players are unlikely to finish their questlines as you often have to go way out of your way to explore and re-explore areas to find every quest stage for most NPCS. Another "flaw" of Dark Souls is that the second half of the game does not quite live up to the first half. While I have learned to love it, I can see why many people take issue with it, as places like Lost Izalith and the Crystal Cave are not as polished as areas like Undead Burg and Anor Londo. Dark Souls remains my favorite Souls game, and I will always encourage people to play it.

Posted: December 17, 2024
What I love most about Dark Souls 1 is its unique atmosphere and the brilliant level design of the first half of the game. The feeling of exploring Lordran for the first time, discovering interconnected shortcuts, accessing new areas without any loading screens, and realizing that everything makes geographical sense is simply magical. Locations like Undead Burg, Parish, Depths, Blighttown (as hated as it is), Sen’s Fortress, and Anor Londo form an almost perfect sequence—each area has personality, mystery, and a constant sense of discovery. It’s the kind of design few games manage to achieve.

Then comes the second half, and this is where Dark Souls 1 shows its cracks. After Anor Londo, it becomes clear that the game loses pace and quality. Areas like Demon Ruins, Lost Izalith, and Tomb of the Giants feel rushed, less inspired, and with less care in their design. Lost Izalith is almost a meme: a huge, empty map with dinosaurs copy-pasted around, full of wasted space. Demon Ruins repeats enemies in a tiring way, and Tomb of the Giants relies on extreme darkness as artificial difficulty.

Additionally, some bosses in the second half don’t have the same impact as those in the first—many are too easy, recycled, or simply forgettable. The game, which starts as a masterpiece of exploration and atmosphere, ends somewhat unevenly.

Even so, Dark Souls 1 remains an incredible game. The first half is so good that it carries it through even its weak points.

Posted: December 16, 2024
A great game that changed the way a lot of people viewed games as a medium. Dark Souls is a shining example of how earned satisfaction beats pandering to the player almost every time. With that being said, it is probably one of the most overrated games out there lol. I think you should go in with no expectations and let Dark Souls engross you with its own merits rather than force it to live up to the hype that has been generated by the countless reviews and video essays that exist regarding it's quality. All you need to know is that there is enjoyment to be had here. Look up guides if you want, as I think the game is way more fun when you know what you're doing, but play how you want to.

As a final bit of spoiler free advice, I recommend not summoning random players as some will use cheats to instantly kill all enemies and npcs in an area to try to ruin your save. The anti-cheat here is basically nonexistent and can be circumvented in about 10 minutes if you catch a ban. Use the community discord or SummonSign subreddit for finding coop partners. PvP invaders can also cheat to kill NPCs but aren't as common and you can just play as a hollow if you want to avoid them.

update: Hacking has ramped WAY up, even more than it already was. I recommend the Dark Souls 1 PvP Overhaul mod, even if you do not PvP at all as it can be used just for it's anti-cheat functions. The mod can be found on NexusMods and an install guide can be found on the Illusory Wall channel on youtube.

But in all seriousness, Dark Souls game is a game that will always feel like home for me. I've been drawn to its difficulty after seeing it take the internet by storm as the "World's hardest game". After playing both Prepare to Die Edition and the regular game on Xbox 360, I'm glad to have the chance to play this gem again.
Known for its difficulty and truly setting a new bar for games of its kind, Dark Souls is a game about conquering and overcoming obstacles no matter how high they may be. Whether it's trying to survive the trek to the boss or facing the next boss, death is inevitable and you will die. A lot. This game can easily test your patience and your sense of perseverance so pick yourself up no matter how many times you fall or go hollow trying. For those that are still trying to complete this game: Keep trying, I believe in you! Every failure is an opportunity to come back stronger and once you've finally succeed where you've constantly failed the feeling is unlike any other.

Posted: December 16, 2024
This is my first From game, I finally gave in and decided to see what it's all about. To be fair, I've played numerous 2D Souls-like titles such as Ender Lilies, Death's Gambit and Salt and Sanctuary. I was actually surprised how blatantly those had games directly copied the leveling and weapon scaling mechanics from Dark Souls. So I was immediately familiar with some of the more obtuse systems in the game that new players probably have difficulty comprehending. Basically, this is a great game, it's much larger in scope than I had anticipated, to the point that the massive scale of Elden Ring doesn't seem like such a leap from it's predecessors. But my main take away is that this game isn't that hard, at all. 90% of my deaths in the game have been falling/platforming related. In my first playthrough I think the only boss that I had to fight more than 10 times was Bed Of Chaos, and the majority of those deaths were that stupid thing knocking me into one of holes it created, so again, death by falling. In NG+ I had to fight Ornstein and Smough about 20 times because I was being stubborn about changing my gear, I finally made a set that looked really badass but the lightning defense was trash. Eventually after learning the fight well enough my defense didn't matter and that felt pretty gratifying. My main issue with the gameplay is that Dash and Roll are the same button, and the game wants Jump to be the same button too! What? I don't know if this is the case in other versions of the game, but if I could reconfigure the controls so Dash could be a trigger button I wouldn't have to manipulate the R3 camera with my index finger while running and evading with my middle finger, "The Claw" we used to call it in og Monster Hunter. Speaking of Monster Hunter, I can't use the lock-on in those games, and I can't use it in this game either unless I'm continuously whiffing and it's absolutely necessary. I also think the MH series has acclimated me for the slower, more deliberate combat, so I felt right at home in Lordran. Visually, this game is a work of art, it's atmosphere is palpable, and it reminds me of playing Shadow of the Colossus for the first time. I can't think of another game where I found myself constantly exclaiming "I hate this game" and coming back for more, over and over and over.
