Posted: December 15, 2024
TL;DR - A good version of the original game with some questionable QoL changes. In some areas it does great whilst in others it went too far or not far enough. The core gameplay remains amazing as it did back in 2011.

Pro's:

- Combat system is challenging and rewarding. As with other Souls games, it's like a dance. Learn the enemy/boss attacks, time your dodge's, manage your stamina and respond accordingly. It feels very rewarding once the combat style clicks.
- Lots of customisation options for the player. Build variety is great.
- Interconnected world and locations are all great. Anor Londo is particularly breathtaking and still looks great.
- Lots of lore to uncover.
- Many characters to (optionally) meet. Some of them are great (Solaire, Siegmeyer and Sieglinde standout).
- Played perfectly fine without issues on PC and Steam Deck.
- NG+ encourages repeat playthroughs. Enemy health and damage scales up and may require the player to approach things differently.
- Sound design is great. Enhances the ambiance and atmosphere of the world perfectly. Love the credits song in particular.
- Artorias of the Abyss DLC is included.

Cons:

- Combat can feel a little stiff with 4 directional rolling if you are coming from a later release with omni directional rolling.
- Bed of Chaos. Feels like luck 90% of the time.
- Some players may need to look to external sources like Youtube to learn of the great lore/story.
- Unable to respec like in later titles. Multiple builds will require multiple characters/playthroughs.

The below are to do with the remastered versions quality:

- Finally able to change native resolution and runs at 60fps without the use of mods
- Missing some additional enhancements in the options menu that would have been nice.
- Password matching to make it easier for jolly co-operation
- Some art style changes may/may not be to everyone's liking e.g. bonfire.
- Lighting seems a bit borked. Some characters seem too shiny
- Everything is brighter on the whole. Whilst this does increase visibility in some areas, it detracts from the original vision of the game.
- Not offering much in the way of benefits over a fully modded PTDE version of the game, which was removed with online multiplayer shutdown in order to entice players over to this version. Bit of a slap in the face to owners of the original, regardless of the original discount that was offered with some of the objective issues the remastered version has.

The game is really fun, it has its flaws in the second half of the game unfortunately. There are some rushed areas and admittedly some boss fights are more on the annoying side than hard. The run backs to bosses can be annoying as well but try not to let that take away too much from the experience. It is still a very fun experience and has a good variety of builds you can play as.

So yes, the game will be hard on your first go through. You will very likely die a lot. Like it will feel like you are banging your head against a brick wall trying to progress. But that's where the real joy comes from. Breaking down that wall and overcoming a tough challenge. You will feel so great afterwards giving you that push to keep on moving forward. You will die, but you will learn as well. Learn what works and what doesn't work. Every attempt at a boss will bring you closer and closer to beating them. The game isn't brutal beyond belief though. If you find a challenge is too much to handle on your own, call upon others to come to your aid for some jolly cooperation. It's only as hard as you make it to be.

If you do decide to try this game, then whatever you do, don't you dare go hollow

This replay was one I wanted to undertake for a long time, because Dark Souls to me despite all it's comparatively unpolished gameplay elements to later souls games was the one that'd always immerse me the most. I wanted to see if that magic was still there for me. Out of that fear of maybe too much immersion if proven right and sinking disappointment if proven wrong, I stalled it for all these years. But the stars were just aligned and the excuses were strong enough that I finally caved in. And boy what a cave it was. It's one of the few games where for me the later experiences (second half) despite being awful was able to be overcome and maybe romanticized to an extent. This level of craftsmanship in level design and is rare in games and nigh extinct in the game's younger siblings. Dark Souls 1 will never be replicated, neither in its impact then nor in it's status as an icon in gaming culture.

Using this replay to learn how to use a controller was the best decision of my life.

Posted: December 13, 2024
After before forced to traverse all inches of Lordran to get all the achievements I can safely say that, while it has its flaws, this is one of the greatest games ever made.

Combat feels powerful and every hit intentional, level design is phenomenal aside from a couple late-game areas, and there are so many little details and secrets that make the world feel alive.

Much more strategic and a lot slower paced compared to newer FromSoft games, so it won't be too much of a challenge if you are coming from games like DS3 or Elden Ring, but it does have difficult moments.

Dark Souls is a masterpiece, and if you haven't played it already you definitely should as soon as you have 20-25 hours to dedicate to a first playthrough.

Be safe, friend. Don't you dare go Hollow.

If you're like me and you've been avoiding this game out of fear, don't. You're missing out on one of the greatest video game experiences of all time. I'm late to the party, but this game changed me. It changed my perception of games as a storytelling medium and even my perception of self.

The atmosphere sucks you in, the environments are carefully crafted, the storytelling is subtle but focused, the NPCs are memorable and tragic, combat is challenging, your every move has weight and purpose, the lore is deep and serves the narrative. The game never holds your hand, not through the story nor through the encounters. Despite that, you feel it pushing you on regardless; urging you forward. This is what game design should be.

I'm not a "hardcore" gamer, you don't have to be to play this. All you need for this game is the drive to keep going. You will die, that's okay. What I love about this game is how it hides its core message in its own gameplay: never give up. Humanity's greatest strength is our ability to pick ourselves up when we've fallen. You only go hollow when you stop trying.

I almost beaten the game and DLC. This title is weird, sometimes it delivers awesome moments, packed within awful (and more frequent) ones. It seems online most people are eaten by nostalgia and remember how surprising it was to return to Firelink through the elevator. I understand how that would impact someone who tried this game back in the early 2000s. But if you are a reverse veteran and played ER or DS3, it just won't land the same way. You want to know what you WILL remember? The run backs, the 4 directional roll when locked on enemies, the lack of tp early in the game, and the horrendous UI.

The base game is a C, but if you use Save Organizer to skip the run backs, make use of the wiki and video guides, and download a mod to unlock tp early in the game, it becomes a B.

I can't recommend this game, unless you are an absolute pain-lover maniac like myself and want to experience the souls-genre from its origins. The origin is rough, and hidden within it is a gem, but you won't see the gem in this game, for that you'll have to experience future games.

Posted: December 6, 2024
7/10.
Dark Souls’ reputation precedes itself to a degree I’ve never seen before. The premise of a difficult game is so synonymous with Dark Souls that they are nigh inseparable. Not dissimilar to an urban legend, the legends of Dark Souls’ difficulty are inflated with every passing year, reaching the status of unbeatable challenges requiring unmatched discipline and skill. However, this premise is somewhat divorced from the truth. Dark Souls’ is not about the challenge. Dark Souls’ is about exploring the cruel land of Lordran, uncovering its history, and finding your place in this Age of Fire.

Exploration in Dark Souls is split into two categories; before you acquire the Lordvessel (Allows teleportation between major bonfires), and after. The first half of Dark Souls is airtight in consistency, challenge, and fun. The web-like interconnectivity of the world is breathtaking. Bonfires, the oases in the unforgiving world of Lordran, are an infrequent blessing. Frequently there will only be a single bonfire covering a massive area. This allows for excellent moments of revelation as you spend hours exploring thinking you are miles from a bonfire, only to find a ladder you can kick down to reach your previous bonfire. You now have a quick passage to this area which took so long to reach before, while also gaining a greater understanding of the layout of the region. Undead Burg’s ladder beneath the Hellkite bridge comes to mind, but examples of this are all over the first half of the game. No matter how isolated you feel, there is always something familiar waiting closer than you expect. Firelink Shrine, this game’s hub world, is full of these connections. You can reach nearly every location in the game quickly from Firelink Shrine without any teleporting, because the world was designed with an extreme importance on interconnectivity, and gives the journey an incredibly refreshing and organic feeling.

The second half of Dark Souls takes a massive hit in quality. Instead of one world with many connected regions, you now have to journey to the corners of Lordran to defeat the 4 Lords and claim their Lord Souls. These regions have no connection with the rest of Lordran, and some are lazily constructed with poor enemy placements and rushed pacing. These four areas are the Dukes’ Archives, Lost Izalith, The Catacombs, and New Londo. While some of these areas are good on their own, their isolated nature severely detracts from their quality compared to the previous regions. However one stands out from the rest in the worst way possible. Lost Izalith is the worst designed area I have ever played. The majority of this area consists of wide open, flat areas with dozens of enemies placed with seemingly no purpose or thought. It is undoubtedly the lowest point of Dark Souls, and is almost designed to be as anti-fun as possible. The worst bosses in the game also comprise this area, so I look forward to never returning.

Speaking of bosses, you will encounter several of them throughout each area, and these are what have given Dark Souls its reputation. However, aside from a few standouts like Gwyn, Ornstein & Smough, Quelagg, and Seath, I was underwhelmed. Dark Souls 1 bosses are not this immaculate, life-changing experience the community seems to think they are. The quality ranges from offensively bad (Bed of Chaos) to very strong, but by and large they are just fine. This is why I say that Dark Souls is not about the bosses, it's about the world.

The DLC, Artorias of the Abyss, is the exception. Artorias the Abysswalker is a perfect boss fight. He has an excellent design paired with the most unforgiving moveset of the entire game. Factor in his lore which adds not only to the tragedy of Artorias, but to the hopelessness the player has towards the coming conflict. Artorias is the culmination of everything great about Dark Souls’ combat, and is a heavenly fight.

On a more sour note, Dark Souls does not explain the rules of the game well. The tutorial does not teach the player about many core mechanics of the game, such as how to parry, how you take extra damage when running, or how you can only roll in four directions when locked on. It never teaches you what humanity does or what being hollowed means. Plus, the bits of information that are given to you are shown all at once, which is an ineffective way to teach something. By the time you need to execute the commands you learned, you will likely have forgotten some of them.

NPC questlines have the same understanding issue. Figuring out what even is a quest is the hardest part of a souls game, and is in severe need of a change. Fromsoftware needs to revamp their quest system. The fact that you need to have the wiki up constantly just to make sure you don’t accidentally progress too far to destroy the quest progression is beyond ridiculous.

The music was shockingly forgettable and bland. I was expecting some grand orchestral accompaniment for these fights but the music is either boring or, in some cases, actually bad. The xylophone solo in Seath’s theme is the goofiest inclusion to a boss I can think of. Sounds like the intro to a cartoon network show. Gwyn’s theme is the exception though. A delicate piano ballad that subverts the whole idea of a boss theme. A legendary musical ending to an otherwise boring compilation of tracks.

Overall, the exploration of Dark Souls starts out fantastic and drops off towards the end, but the end is where the boss quality increases as well, so it all balances out in some way. The crushing atmosphere and deep history of Lodran is fantastic. If not for the poorly designed regions of the 2nd half of the game, this could have been an 8-9/10, but Lost Izalith and the gradual loss of a cohesive world was a brutal blow to Dark Souls’ quality.

Posted: December 6, 2024
What else is there to say about the most famous "souls" game that hasn't already been said? Not much.

If you stumbled upon this title then it means you've already heard about it and know what to expect.
It's brutally pure to its core: you either get good or you die. A lot.
The story and lore is depressing and obscure like the enviroments you explore. A lot of it is hidden behind item descriptions which was a very innovative storytelling approach at the time. The game doesn't serve it on a platter, neatly displayed like with other action games. You have to figure things out for yourself, make assumptions, connect the dots. There are no tips, minimap or NPCs telling you where to go, no invisible safety barriers to keep you from falling to your death, no indications whether an enemy is stronger or weaker than you. You make the choices where to go next and often pay for it.

The game is simple yet brutal and unforgiving... and it's the reason why you keep coming back to it.

9 out of 10 because some enemies are just cheap and you need to cheese them but they often cheese you too so it's fair game.

For years now I have been debating getting into Dark Souls. I am so, so, so glad I did. It hooked me almost instantly. This is one of the most solid gaming experiences in the world, even the grinding aspect isn't so bad. It doesn't really make you that angry either like I was worried it would do.

It's almost like magic how well you get to know the areas, for as intricate as they are.

Art style is 10/10, gameplay is 8.5/10, story is 11/10. The whole game has a particular sense of humour and you'll be laughing alongside it as long as you keep your head. It will humble you, and also give you a sense of pride. This game is infamous for a reason. Immense game.

It's your second time releasing this game for PC and you still can't be ♥♥♥♥♥♥ to let us bind a separate kick button. The port also runs like ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥- if you're playing on a steam deck or a weak PC, your performance will decay over time, and repeatedly necessitate rebooting the game. Some game actions, like parrying, or being struck by a weapon, will tank your framerate to less than 10 FPS. You cannot play online on a steamdeck, because if your framerate dips low enough, you will be kicked to the login screen, as your FPS isn't good enough to play online. Dark Souls 1 itself is maybe even perfect game mechanically until you get the lord vessel, and then you progress through a series of more and more irritating and underbaked zones. Do yourself a favor, get the Lord Vessel and then play a different Souls game. This is not a good port and the devs don't deserve your money for it.

And once I got the hang of the gameplay loop, I was progressing much faster than in the beginning. Not only am I glad I experienced this game for its own sake, but it has also increased my confidence to leave my comfort zone in general! If, like me, you waited forever to play this game- it is absolutely worth a try.

Dark Souls Remastered breathes new life into the legendary title that revolutionized the action RPG genre. Whether you're a seasoned fan or a newcomer, this remaster captures the essence of what made Dark Souls a masterpiece while providing a smoother, more polished experience.

Graphics and Performance
The remastered visuals are a noticeable improvement, with enhanced textures, lighting, and a stable 60 FPS that significantly elevates the atmospheric beauty of Lordran. Iconic locations like Anor Londo and Blighttown feel more immersive than ever. The dreaded frame-rate issues that plagued the original game, particularly in areas like Blighttown, are now a thing of the past, making exploration much more enjoyable.

Gameplay
Dark Souls' signature combat system, known for its precision and challenge, remains as engaging as ever. The remaster retains the core gameplay mechanics that demand strategy, timing, and patience. Boss fights are still the highlight, offering thrilling encounters that are both frustrating and deeply rewarding.

The addition of password matchmaking and dedicated servers for multiplayer make co-op and PvP more accessible and stable, revitalizing the community aspect of the game. Improved online mechanics ensure that summoning allies or invading other players is seamless, adding a new layer of excitement.

Sound and Atmosphere
The haunting soundtrack, ambient sounds, and eerie silence in certain areas perfectly complement the dark, foreboding tone of the game. Every sound is meticulously crafted to heighten the tension and immerse players in the melancholic world.

Accessibility
While the game stays true to its "Prepare to Die" philosophy, the improved performance and visuals make it easier for new players to appreciate the depth and artistry of Dark Souls. However, it still demands persistence and a willingness to learn from failure, which is part of its enduring charm.

Conclusion
Dark Souls Remastered is a brilliant update to a game that has left an indelible mark on the gaming industry. It provides a fantastic opportunity for fans to revisit Lordran and for newcomers to experience a landmark title in its best form. If you’re ready to test your mettle and explore a dark, beautiful, and unforgiving world, this remaster is a must-play.

Rating: 9.5/10

Posted: December 3, 2024
A very good game somewhat hindered by inconsistent quality and wasted opportunities. This review stems from an offline playthrough without the use of summons or guides, and minimal knowledge of the game from external sources.

Exploration
Dark Souls is first and foremost an exploration game, and in this aspect, it reaches the point of brilliance at times. It has numerous areas with varied themes, intricate layouts full of secrets and connections to adjacent zones that actually make sense in the world, without loading screens, all enhanced by detailed skyboxes where you can observe the places you've already visited in the distance.

The environments and enemies you encounter are consistently tough and sometimes feel like the game is outright trolling you, especially in offline mode, where player-placed signs and bloodstains won't be there to warn you of impending danger. However, a lot of this game's appeal lies in learning each section through failure until you manage to get past the obstacles thrown at you.

Unfortunately, when getting close to the end of the game, quality falls off drastically. Some zones practically resemble open, low-poly skyboxes with random copy-pasted enemies scattered around, and love to waste your time in unfun ways. So much less care was put into their overall layout that I can only assume they were rushed to meet publisher deadlines.

I also wish there were better ways of stopping a new player from going into late game areas too early. Getting through particularly difficult zones just to face doors you cannot open at the very end was one of the worst parts of the game for me. It also led to me being overleveled later on due to everything I had to kill to survive.

The combat is not this game's focus and as a result is not particularly groundbreaking for better or worse. Mindless rolls, aggro cheesing and facetanking attacks seem to be sufficient to get through most of the game, which is fun enough but feels like it could've been something more. Environmental obstacles and enemy positioning are a much more elaborate challenge than the direct fights themselves.

Bosses range from OK to outright terrible. Some of them barely get to do anything against a player that just spams attacks, while others have very poorly executed puzzles. The best bossfights it has to offer are simple skillchecks that rely on how fast you can react to their movement or how good you are at parrying. With few and mostly bad exceptions, their mechanics generally do not require smart or strategic use of your dodges, blocks, weapons, or the environment, which seems like a huge wasted opportunity. In some cases their attacks are poorly telegraphed and damaging colliders and visuals do not always match.

In addition, the instances where where it was necessary to use any of the many consumables the game throws at you were few and far between, and the stat requirements made it very difficult to try out new pieces of gear. Not only do you need to invest your souls into a stat to use a weapon you might not even like, but the grind required also turns many of your hard-earned rewards into yet more inventory clutter you won't get to use in a long time.

The UI is very clunky to navigate with mouse and keyboard as it does not make much use of their advantages. There is a lack of tooltips, search bars or drag and drop functionalities which would significantly speed up navigation and inventory management.

I welcome the lack of features such as objective markers or other ways of holding your hand, but some things are sorely missing from it and would, in my opinion, make for a better overall experience. It does not explain how stats work in any useful detail (equip load thresholds being one of the worst offenders) and does not track your past progress at all, something I would have appreciated as my spatial memory is not the best. I had to take notes to remember where I'd been and couldn't make effective use of map shortcuts I had already discovered.

This also makes the (surprisingly interesting) story very hard to follow, as it is learnt through item descriptions and disconnected pieces of dialogue which sometimes also contain key info about your progression.

I was pretty disappointed to see the supposed 'remaster' of an old game on PC still capped at 60 FPS and with no option to increase the FOV. Mouse and keyboard controls are janky and the game took control of the camera against my will from time to time, particularly when walking on sloped terrain. The world still had numerous holes to the void and some nasty kill barriers and invisible walls. And most important of all, the unfinished parts are still unfinished.

Content in Artorias of the Abyss is among the best of what Dark Souls has to offer. The zones are a huge step up from the final few in the base game, and the bosses are much more mechanically interesting. I definitely recommend giving it a go as it comes included with the remaster. It is accessed in-game so make sure to visit it before you're done with your save.

This game for sure would have been fun for me if the controls were good and the game wasn't so delayed. It reminded me of many older games from even around the time I was born in the early 2000's. I wanted to like this game since I enjoyed other games like Bloodborn, Sekro (I forget how to spell it), Lies of P, and other soulslikes. I just can't with how wonky all of the controls are. If you buy this game, expect to not only fight the game's very difficult enemies, (which is expected of course), but also fighting the game mechanics itself. Dodge rolls were heavily delayed on both keyboard and controller. Sometimes it would make me stop running despite me not releasing the button, and then sometimes jump off edges and die. The game tended to lag here and there, which I've never had an issue with before. This is a brand new $1000+ pc that I've had no issues with, so I'm going to assume it's just this game. Also, the camera would sometimes jerk around despite me not even touching the camera button (joystick) since I was locked onto an enemy/boss. I feel bad dissing this game since I know its grand legacy, but I just can't play games that I have to fight the game itself to actually play it. I'll probably stick to newer games from now on after this.

A Masterpiece Remastered – Dark Souls™: REMASTERED

Dark Souls™: REMASTERED is a definitive revisit to one of the most iconic and challenging games in modern gaming history. FromSoftware has masterfully polished the original while preserving its soul-crushing difficulty, intricate level design, and deeply rewarding gameplay.

Visual and Technical Enhancements
The remastered visuals breathe new life into Lordran, with enhanced textures, improved lighting, and smooth 60 FPS gameplay on most platforms. This upgrade makes exploring the hauntingly beautiful and interconnected world even more immersive. The game feels fluid, and the improved stability eliminates many of the frustrations from the original release, such as infamous areas like Blighttown, which now run seamlessly.

Timeless Gameplay
At its core, Dark Souls remains an expertly crafted action RPG. Combat is precise, strategic, and punishing—requiring players to learn from their mistakes and adapt to each foe's unique patterns. The variety of builds, weapons, and spells ensures endless replayability. The feeling of defeating a boss after countless attempts is unparalleled and remains one of the game's standout aspects.

Multiplayer Improvements
The updated multiplayer mechanics and dedicated servers have revitalized the online experience. Co-op, invasions, and duels are smoother than ever, making PvP more balanced and engaging. The addition of password matchmaking enhances co-op with friends, a welcome change for both veterans and newcomers.

What Makes It Special
Dark Souls™: REMASTERED isn’t just about gameplay; it’s an emotional journey. The cryptic story, pieced together through subtle environmental storytelling and item descriptions, encourages exploration and curiosity. The despair and triumph players feel are echoed in the hauntingly beautiful soundtrack, which enhances the atmosphere and boss encounters.

For Newcomers and Veterans
For those new to Dark Souls, the remaster is the perfect entry point. It maintains the essence of the original while offering modern quality-of-life improvements. For veterans, it’s an opportunity to relive the masterpiece in its best form to date, with a polished experience that honors its legacy.

Verdict
Dark Souls™: REMASTERED is more than a game; it's a rite of passage. It challenges you, frustrates you, but ultimately rewards you in ways few games can. Whether you’re stepping into Lordran for the first time or returning to rekindle the flame, this remaster is an essential addition to any gamer's library.

Rating: 10/10

If you have time to commit to games, more than an hour a day, and you are willing to enter with a patient mindset, definitely try this gem out.

The first time I played this game, I dumped about 12-20 hours and was in utter despair. The game is very unforgiving, but only if you don't "play" the game. There's no real tutorial or anything, but any questions you might have are locked away in NPC interactions and item descriptions. By speaking to all of the NPCs/merchants/blacksmiths, you'll be able to discern what exactly you need to do and what everything does.

If you prefer to skip all dialogue and ooga booga your way through the game, like I did for so many hours and on so many playthroughs, Reddit is a massive help and the community will guide you all the way. There aren't any questions that haven't been asked that you won't find an answer for.

Enough with the warnings and "how to play".

This game is not a hack and slash. It can become one depending on how well you know all there is to it, but patience is key. I attempted the normal hack & slash route and was beaten to a pulp when starting out, but by coming back to the game and just persevering a few minutes/ hours more, It's almost as if something starts to click. Once you find the rhythm, it is such a fun and addicting experience.

One of the main reasons to keep persevering is the dopamine spikes these games give you. It is incredibly satisfying to finally clear that boss or move on to the next area. The "difficulty" also adds to the replayability of the game. I've finished the game about 7 times now, excluding NG+ runs (New Game plus, increases enemy HP and damage per plus) and am able to constantly feel the urge to come back.

All in all. Keywords for DS1 or any of the souls genre, patience and perseverance is key. Enjoy and remember to praise the sun.

It took me 10 years to finish the Prepare to Die Edition. Back in 2014, I abruptly stopped at Gwyn's fog gate and never managed to complete the fight. Now, in 2024, I got pulled back in with the Remastered version.

This game is a gem. While players often associate Dark Souls with its difficulty and jank, in reality, the game is deep—almost poetic. Aiming for 100% achievements requires you to play the game 2.5 times, giving you plenty of time to absorb the untold lore and feel the weight of the world, the setting, and the struggle.

The final boss, Gwyn, is often depicted as an easy or meme-worthy fight, but it’s much more than that. The battle itself feels intimate, almost melancholic, with the haunting piano melody of Gwyn, Lord of Cinder underscoring the fight. Interestingly, the melody is played only on the white keys of the piano, symbolizing Gwyn's desperate avoidance of the darkness he fears. It’s not a triumphant showdown but a sobering confrontation with a god clinging to the remnants of his power and purpose. Gwyn’s aggressive, relentless attacks convey desperation more than dominance, as though he’s fighting not just you but the inevitable passage of time.

Dark Souls is a masterpiece that transcends its reputation, weaving a narrative of solitude, agony, and survival into every aspect of its design.

10/10

So now for the real review: It took me some time to get used to the slower pace of combat and movement. And not gonna lie, the 1st 6 or so hours were pure frustration. Constantly walking across the entire early map was exhausting, and I also got stuck in Blightown without a way to level up my weapon ("there's no way I'm going back up and down again") so I assume I fought Quelaag underleveled, since I was dealing 65 dmg per hit. Nevertheless I remembered her entire moveset and and beat her, blah blah blah.

Highlight for me was probably Anor Londo, which is where I was finally able to upgrade my weapons and character to a reasonable level (which ended up making him stronger for some other areas later on). O&S bossfight was at 1st annoying but later I played more patiently and it payed off.
I also didn't find the 2nd half to be that much of a falloff as ppl usually say, but I usually don't tent to pay attention to such thing unless I really look deeper into it.

Now the game definitely left a strong and positive impression on me and I will probably be paying this game another visit in the future, just like DS3 or ER.

First off, I want to say that I wish this is the 1st game that I played of the series, because its' age is showing, and bunch of my issues stem from that. I'm sure I'd have much less frustration without having the newer games to compare it to. Am also aware that without this game we wouldn't get the ones like DS3, ER, etc.

Terrible choices of when it comes to game design, such as not being able to fast travel from Bonefires for the 1st half of the game = not being able to upgrade weapons while in certain areas. This issue isn't like complaining ''why does my stamina drain when not being chased by an enemy like in ER", because they give u the option to fast travel, just later in the game.

This game just isn't made for fighting multiple enemies at the same time. The combat is extremely slow (which takes getting used to) which isn't the problem for fighting but with everything else: the character takes forever to stand up or when staggered. Not being able to heal while moving or on ladder creates a new layer of difficulty to this and also the delayed healing effect (by that I mean it feels like my hp is restored not when I drink the flask but when I put it back in my pocket).

Some bosses are honestly terribly designed when it comes to combined factors such as runback+arena+boss itself. (for ex. getting poisoned before the bossfight or fighting a boss with 2 dogs in the space smaller then an average bedroom).
Interesting side effect of that is that beating those bosses feels better then how I felt beating bosses in newer games.

Also why does the character, when hit, moan like someone's sucking him off...?

I hated Dark Souls and most soulsborne games for years (excluding Sekiro, I always loved Sekiro.) Recently I decided to give soulsborne games another chance. After finishing Bloodborne I became concerned that no other game could give me such an engaging first playthrough. Enter Dark Souls. The interwoven world is spectacular, Giving you the ability to see later game locations early on, such as the ability to just barely see blighttown from firelink shrine. This connection of the world brings a level of "immersion" to the map that is nearly unrivaled. The NPCs near the shrine will tell you where to go and what to do if you listen closely but the use of firelink shrine as a hub was very intelligent on FromSoft's part. If you decide to venture too far in one direction from firelink even in the beginning of the game it can DRASTICALLY change your experience. Instead of ending up in undead burg you might just make it to the deadly catacombs or even the valley of the drakes a very short while into the game. This not only allows for player choice but also for a level of depth to its exploration that is nearly unrivaled despite opting for a more metroidvania style level design as opposed to the open worlds that games these days are so often plagued with. The boss fights are mostly fantastic aside from a couple of awful gimmicky fights. The worldbuilding and story are fantastic albeit, a touch confusing to a souls game newcomer. One recommendation and one word of caution for anyone who might be playing this for the first time in 2024: I recommend googling Dark Souls Re:Remastered. However, while using this I have noticed that my achievements are broken, some sessions i get achievements others it's like i don't earn them at all. I will give Dark Souls Remastered an 8/10 and say, if you play this it may ruin most other RPGs for you. I know it has for me

It took me five long years to defeat one of the early bosses of the game—the Capra Demon in the Lower Undead Burg. I finally killed him somewhere at the beginning of this year armed with a spear in one hand and a shield in the other, chicken-poking him for what felt like the hundredth or maybe even thousandth time until he finally perished. For me, this is what the game is about — a test of perseverance and tenacity.

This game made me fall in love with gaming all over again. Its subtle storytelling, ingenious world design, and difficult boss fights made me realize how beautiful games can be. Almost every location is intertwined with another, and I was never tired of gasping in amazement whenever I discovered a new shortcut that connected everything to the starting location.

Honestly, once I acquired the Lordvessel, the game felt a little less exciting. But in reality, it's just a chance to appreciate other aspects of the game, like progressing with covenant ranks and engaging with other online functionalities.

I have not yet played other Souls-like games, but this one will definitely remain my most favorite, just for the experiences it gave me.

This game has a certain sense of loneliness that I found utterly enthralling. It, at times, feels like walking through a town that has faced a huge war and has been abandoned. The music, when it's there, fits so wonderfully and underlines this feeling. Until, of course, you get into a bossfight and the music turns from feeling lonely to feeling dangerous and exciting.

However, I do enjoy the lack of music as well. This is especially nice in the darkroot garden. It feels good to just stop and listen to the different sounds.

As for the way the game looks, I find it visually rather ugly which is why Darkroot Garden was such a breath of fresh air. Seeing some green after being surrounded by brown and beige was a damn relief.

I find that the world in this game does a really good job looping back in on itself (e.g. the elevator from Firelink to Undead Parish which transitions nicely to Sen's Fortress, multiple entrances to Blighttown, etc). I'm impressed with how balanced the game is as well, I was mainly a strength build but I used pyromancy here and there and I can see basically any build having a decent shot at getting all the way through.

The only thing to note if you're coming off of other souls games is that this one feels incredibly slow by comparison. Most enemy attacks take a full second or more to execute, giving you more than enough time to plan your next move. Although, the upside to this is that heavy weapons are much more viable.

8/10 given because some bosses feel a little too gimmicky, to the point where you either don't even have to try, or you have to deal with a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ mechanic and your build doesn't even matter (looking at you, Bed of Chaos). Also, I realize this is an issue solved in the later games, but you cannot warp between bonfires until after the halfway point of the game. The developers remedied this by adding lots of shortcuts between areas, but when I got the ability to teleport I felt like a weight was lifted off my chest. I really don't think the game would suffer too much from allowing teleporting from the start. It's mostly just a nudge to keep moving forward instead of running back to Firelink where it's safe and cozy.

running around the map and watching the player character do it isn't a great experience, and much like older FromSoftware titles before this, navigating feels less like a humanoid running walking and jumping, but more like a very small mech or robot following user instructions. Simple actions like running a couple meters then jumping can be fear inducing because of this robotic movement. besides that one boss and its run, this is the only major criticism i can think of after already being invested in DSII for 50 hours.

Also, after 80 hours of Dark Souls the gameplay cycles became rather obvious where they felt repetitive and had me lost a couple times because I refused to believe that I had to just do this same thing again for the 30th time. at least its fun when you do though!

Much like in Evergrace, learning and navigating the layout of the map and connecting all the pieces that make the whole of it is a rewarding process on its own, not even mentioning the reward of leveling up all the stats infinitely, which makes that experience an achievement itself.

this paragraph is solely about the Remastered version's implementations.
Most altered effects like the bonfire and item lights seem disconnected from the rest of the textures and assets, especially those unchanged.
how the engine handles material effects seems changed aswell, giving less shiny reflectiveness to all things overall and coupled with the altered scene colors, makes things look rather dull, flat and lacking in contrast, like a 2013 instagram filter. The 60 fps enhancement is working better than forcing Prepare to Die Edition to run at 60, but the occasional ememy jump warp homing at superspeed still remains, but its not the biggest deal.
overall id say for all things remastered, the benifits outweigh the side effects.

Dark Souls is a moderately pretty but stunning at few times and plain at other times game, that felt good to play through and invest in.
FromSoftware's many ventures into a dungeon crawler type full control 3D game really paid off with this series and is currently the final evolution of the genre as i see it. so yeah pretty cool

The lore is interesting and while I don't think its as good as the other games I've played there are still 2 more Dark Souls games I have to play that will expand on it even more which I am looking forward to. Overall I had a good time playing this and was pleasantly surprised by it. Glad that I chose to start with this instead of skipping right to DS3.

Dark Souls Remastered: A Timeless Classic That Rewards Patience and Adaptation

Dark Souls Remastered is a game that takes everything you think you know about video games and flips it on its head, in the best possible way. From its hauntingly atmospheric world to its intricate gameplay mechanics, this game is a masterpiece that demands attention, patience, and a willingness to learn from your mistakes.

The Story: Deep, Mysterious, and Intriguing

Dark Souls doesn’t handhold its players. The story is told through subtle environmental cues, item descriptions, and cryptic NPC dialogue. While the plot is not directly spelled out for you, the more you explore, the more you begin to piece together the tragic history of Lordran, a kingdom cursed by the undead curse and eternal darkness. The game’s lore is deep, engaging, and open to interpretation, which makes every discovery feel like an achievement in itself.

Gameplay: A Perfect Blend of Challenge and Reward

The core of Dark Souls is its combat, and it is brilliant. The game doesn't rush you into battles; instead, it encourages you to think, time your attacks, and learn from each enemy encounter. The difficulty is infamous, but it's not punishing—it’s fair. Every enemy can be defeated, every obstacle can be overcome, but it requires patience, timing, and a methodical approach. This game isn’t about button-mashing, it's about precision. The slower pace of combat might be jarring for players used to faster games, but once you get into the rhythm, it becomes a meditative experience of constant improvement.

Small Details: Mastery of the Craft

The small details in Dark Souls are what make it truly special. From the quiet crackling of a distant bonfire to the subtle variations in the design of the weapons and armor, the world feels alive. The interconnected map is a marvel of level design, with shortcuts and hidden secrets everywhere. There's satisfaction in revisiting areas after unlocking shortcuts, and that feeling of progression is priceless. Every nook and cranny seems carefully crafted to draw you deeper into its world, and you’ll often find yourself rewarded for your curiosity.

Characters and Dialogue: Rich in Mystery

The characters you meet in Dark Souls are unforgettable, even if some of them are brief. Their dialogue is steeped in mystery, and often, their motives are unclear. You’ll meet strange, sometimes tragic figures, each with their own goals, all adding to the game’s emotional weight. There are no obvious "good" or "evil" characters—everyone is caught in the same cycle of suffering, and it’s up to you to decide who to trust, and who to leave behind.

Patience and Adaptation: The True Core of the Experience

What really sets Dark Souls apart from other games is the way it teaches you. At first, you might find yourself dying over and over again, unsure of what went wrong. But each failure is a lesson. The game doesn’t hold your hand—it lets you learn from every mistake, forcing you to adapt to the ever-changing challenges. This builds a sense of resilience in the player, and as you begin to understand the mechanics, enemies, and rhythms of the game, you’ll find yourself becoming more capable, patient, and strategic. It’s a rewarding feeling when you finally defeat that boss you struggled against, and it’s a testament to how well the game teaches players to adapt and persevere.

Conclusion: A Must-Play for All Gamers

Dark Souls Remastered is a game that pushes you to your limits, but it also gives you the tools to succeed. It rewards patience, encourages adaptability, and immerses you in one of the most hauntingly beautiful worlds ever created in gaming. If you enjoy games that challenge your intellect and reflexes while offering a deep and immersive experience, Dark Souls Remastered is a must-buy. Be prepared for frustration, but also for the immense satisfaction of overcoming it. The journey through Lordran is unforgettable, and the lessons learned will stay with you long after the game is over.
